topics.title: Indian Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indian Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Indian Energy Scenario** refers to a comprehensive, multidisciplinary study of the present state, structural composition, generation capacity, consumption patterns, resource availability, policy framework, and future trajectory of primary and secondary energy resources within the Republic of India. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PERUT637, this topic forms the foundational module that contextualizes the demand-supply equilibrium, energy security challenges, and the transition pathway from a fossil-dominant energy portfolio to a sustainable, low-carbon economy.

The term encompasses quantitative indicators such as **installed capacity (in Gigawatts, GW)**, **per capita energy consumption (in kilowatt-hours, kWh)**, **energy intensity** (energy consumed per unit of GDP), **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)**, **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio**, and **primary commercial energy mix**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT637 Module 1):** Students must be able to map India's energy resources, classify them into commercial and non-commercial categories, interpret installed capacity statistics, evaluate per capita energy consumption against global benchmarks, and articulate the role of central agencies (CEA, MNRE, MoPNG) in shaping national energy policy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of India's energy ecosystem as a **massive, multi-fueled kitchen** serving a household of 1.4 billion people.

- **Coal** is like the traditional firewood chulha (stove): cheap, abundant, but smoky and polluting.
- **Oil and Natural Gas** are like LPG cylinders: efficient and clean for cooking, but expensive and imported, leaving the family vulnerable to global price shocks.
- **Renewables (Solar, Wind, Hydro)** are like solar cookers and biogas plants: free fuel from nature, but weather-dependent and require upfront investment.
- **Nuclear** is like an induction stove: powerful, dense, but politically sensitive and requires strict safety protocols.

The "kitchen's" total capacity is the **installed power capacity** (~430 GW), and how much it actually cooks depends on the **Plant Load Factor (PLF)**. The "meal per person" is the **per capita energy consumption** (~1,250 kWh), which is one-fourth the global average. The challenge is to shift from imported fuels (LPG/petrol) to indigenous, clean fuels (solar/biomass) while feeding a growing population and expanding industrial base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Benchmark Constants for KTU Exams:**
> - **1 GW = 1,000 MW = 1,000,000 kW = 1,000,000,000 W**
> - **1 TWh (Terawatt-hour) = 1 Billion kWh = 10⁹ kWh**
> - **1 Mtoe (Million tonnes of oil equivalent) ≈ 11.63 TWh**
> - **1 BTU = 1,055 Joules; 1 Quad BTU ≈ 1.055 × 10¹⁸ J**

### 1.3 Categorization of Energy Resources in India

| Category | Renewable | Non-Renewable | Examples |
|----------|-----------|---------------|----------|
| **Primary Commercial** | Hydro, Solar, Wind, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal | Coal, Lignite, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium, Thorium | Coal (205 GW), Solar (85 GW) |
| **Primary Non-Commercial** | Firewood, Cow-dung cakes, Agricultural residues | — | Traditional biomass (still serves ~25 crore rural population) |
| **Secondary** | Green Hydrogen, Biodiesel | Electricity, Refined Petroleum (Petrol, Diesel, Kerosene, LPG) | Refined fuels at consumer end |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of India's Energy Ecosystem
India's energy landscape can be decomposed into five interlinked layers, each governed by distinct ministries and regulatory bodies:

1. **Resource Layer (Upstream):** Geological reserves of coal, hydrocarbons, uranium, and the in-situ potential of solar irradiance (4–7 kWh/m²/day), wind (300 GW onshore potential at 120m hub height), and hydro (~145 GW economically exploitable).
2. **Conversion Layer (Midstream):** Power plants (thermal, hydro, nuclear, renewable), refineries, coal gasification units, and bioenergy plants. The aggregated output is the **Installed Capacity**.
3. **Transmission and Distribution Layer:** The national grid (One Nation-One Grid) operated by Power Grid Corporation of India Limited (PGCIL), interconnecting regional grids at 765 kV HVAC and ±800 kV HVDC.
4. **End-Use Consumption Layer:** Industrial (≈42%), Domestic (≈24%), Agricultural (≈17%), Commercial (≈9%), and Transport (≈8%).
5. **Policy and Regulatory Layer:** Central Electricity Authority (CEA), Ministry of Power (MoP), Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG), Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC), and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).

### 2.2 Operational Logic: How Energy Planning Works
- **Step 1 — Demand Assessment:** Utilities (state DISCOMs) submit long-term demand forecasts to CEA based on GDP growth, electrification rates, and industrial expansion.
- **Step 2 — Resource Allocation:** CEA prepares the **National Electricity Plan (NEP)** matching projected demand with planned capacity additions, ensuring the **Reliability Standards (Loss of Load Probability ≤ 0.2%)**.
- **Step 3 — Capacity Addition:** Through competitive bidding (for renewables) or tariff-based competitive bidding (for thermal), capacity is awarded to developers.
- **Step 4 — Operations and Dispatch:** The merit-order dispatch principle dictates that plants with the lowest marginal cost (renewables first, then nuclear, then thermal) are dispatched first, subject to grid stability constraints.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Expression | Description | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---------------------|---------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)** | $\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Electrical Energy Consumed}}{\text{Total Population}}$ | Average electricity used per person per year | kWh/person/year (India: ≈ **1,250 kWh**) |
| 2 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{\text{Primary Energy Supply (toe)}}{\text{GDP (Million USD at PPP)}}$ | Energy required per unit of economic output | toe / Million USD PPP (India: ≈ **0.20**) |
| 3 | **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** | $\text{CUF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (MWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (MW)} \times 8{,}760 \text{ hours}} \times 100\%$ | Effective utilization of a plant | % (Coal: 55–70%, Solar: 18–25%, Wind: 30–40%) |
| 4 | **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** | $\text{PLF} = \dfrac{\text{Units Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours of Operation}} \times 100\%$ | Used for thermal plants | % |
| 5 | **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | Annualized growth over n years | Decimal (e.g., 0.05 = 5%) |
| 6 | **Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P)** | $\text{R/P} = \dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Years of reserve left at current extraction rate | Years (Coal: ≈ **140**, Oil: ≈ **14**, Gas: ≈ **20**) |
| 7 | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Input}}{\text{Output (tonnes or units)}}$ | Industrial energy efficiency metric | kWh/tonne, MJ/kg |
| 8 | **Energy Demand Projection** | $E_{n} = E_{0} \times (1+r)^{n}$ | Future energy demand after n years at rate r | TWh or Mtoe |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion of Toe to kWh (commonly tested):**
> $$1 \text{ toe} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Understanding the Indian Energy Scenario is not academic — it underpins:
- **Investment Decisions:** Private IPPs (Independent Power Producers) like Adani, Tata Power, and ReNew use scenario analysis to site new plants.
- **Grid Stability Studies:** Load frequency control and spinning reserve calculations (typically 5% of peak load) depend on the fuel-wise installed capacity mix.
- **Carbon Accounting:** India's NDC (Nationally Determined Contribution) under the Paris Agreement targets 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP by 2030 (from 2005 levels) — directly derived from Energy Intensity metrics.
- **Policy Formulation:** The **Pradhan Mantri Sahaj Bijli Har Ghar Yojana (SAUBHAGYA)** scheme aimed to electrify all un-electrified households by 2018–19, requiring energy demand projections of the form $E_{n}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Problem 1: Per Capita Electricity Consumption Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
India's total electrical energy consumption in 2023–24 was approximately 1,620 TWh, and the population is estimated at 1.42 billion. Calculate the per capita electricity consumption. Compare it with the global average of 3,300 kWh and identify the gap.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Total Energy into kWh.**
Since $1 \text{ TWh} = 10^{9} \text{ kWh}$, the total consumption is:
$$E_{\text{total}} = 1{,}620 \text{ TWh} \times 10^{9} \frac{\text{kWh}}{\text{TWh}}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 1.62 \times 10^{12} \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Population into numerical form.**
$$P = 1.42 \text{ billion} = 1.42 \times 10^{9} \text{ persons}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Per Capita Formula.**
$$\text{PCEC} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{P} = \frac{1.62 \times 10^{12} \text{ kWh}}{1.42 \times 10^{9} \text{ persons}}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Division.**
$$\text{PCEC} = \frac{1.62}{1.42} \times 10^{12-9} \frac{\text{kWh}}{\text{person}}$$

$$\text{PCEC} = 1.1408 \times 10^{3} \text{ kWh/person}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PCEC} \approx 1{,}141 \text{ kWh/person/year}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Gap with Global Average.**
$$\text{Gap} = 3{,}300 - 1{,}141 = 2{,}159 \text{ kWh/person}$$

$$\text{Gap Percentage} = \frac{2{,}159}{3{,}300} \times 100\% \approx 65.4\%$$

**Conclusion:** India's per capita consumption is approximately **34.6% of the global average**, indicating significant latent demand and a strong rationale for capacity addition under the National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP).

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 2: CAGR of Installed Capacity

**Problem Statement:**
India's installed power capacity grew from 1,71,000 MW in 2010 to 4,30,000 MW in 2024. Determine the Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of installed capacity.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables.**
- $V_{\text{initial}} = 1{,}71{,}000 \text{ MW}$ (Year 2010)
- $V_{\text{final}} = 4{,}30{,}000 \text{ MW}$ (Year 2024)
- $n = 2024 - 2010 = 14 \text{ years}$

**Step 2 — Apply the CAGR Formula.**
$$\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values.**
$$\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{4{,}30{,}000}{1{,}71{,}000}\right)^{\frac{1}{14}} - 1$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Ratio.**
$$\frac{4{,}30{,}000}{1{,}71{,}000} = 2.5146$$

**Step 5 — Take the 14th Root.**
$$\text{CAGR} = (2.5146)^{\frac{1}{14}} - 1$$

Using logarithms:
$$\ln(2.5146) = 0.9224$$

$$\frac{0.9224}{14} = 0.06589$$

$$e^{0.06589} = 1.0681$$

**Step 6 — Subtract 1.**
$$\text{CAGR} = 1.0681 - 1 = 0.0681$$

$$\boxed{\text{CAGR} \approx 6.81\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Interpretation:** India's installed capacity has more than doubled every ~10 years, growing at a robust 6.81% CAGR — significantly outpacing the global average of ~2.5%.

---

### 3.3 Worked Problem 3: Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF) of a Solar Plant

**Problem Statement:**
A 100 MW solar PV plant in Rajasthan generated 220 million units (MU) in 2023. Calculate its CUF.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data.**
- Installed Capacity $C = 100 \text{ MW} = 100{,}000 \text{ kW}$
- Annual Energy Generated $E = 220 \text{ MU} = 220 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh}$
- Hours in a non-leap year = 8,760 hours

**Step 2 — Apply the CUF Formula.**
$$\text{CUF} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{installed}} \times 8{,}760} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**
$$\text{CUF} = \frac{220 \times 10^{6}}{100{,}000 \times 8{,}760} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute Denominator.**
$$100{,}000 \times 8{,}760 = 8.76 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**
$$\text{CUF} = \frac{2.20 \times 10^{8}}{8.76 \times 10^{8}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{CUF} = 0.2511 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{CUF} \approx 25.11\%}$$

**Interpretation:** A CUF of 25.11% is excellent for Indian solar plants, exceeding the national average of ~19% and the standard 8,760-hour computation baseline. This indicates high solar insolation and well-maintained modules.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Indian Energy Reserves vs. Production

| Resource | Proven Reserves | Annual Production | R/P Ratio (years) | Strategic Implication |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Coal** | ≈ 360 billion tonnes | ≈ 950 MT | ≈ **150** | Abundant; "Energy Security Backbone" |
| **Crude Oil** | ≈ 600 MMT | ≈ 30 MMT | ≈ **14** | Heavy import dependence (~85%) |
| **Natural Gas** | ≈ 1.4 TCM | ≈ 32 BCM | ≈ **20** | Import dependence ~50% |
| **Uranium (U₃O₈)** | ≈ 82,000 tonnes (low-grade) | ≈ 400 tU | Limited | Three-stage nuclear program with Thorium |
| **Thorium (ThO₂)** | ≈ 963,000 tonnes (monazite sands) | Negligible | Effectively infinite | Future fuel for Advanced Heavy Water Reactors (AHWR) |
| **Hydro Potential** | 145 GW (exploitable) | 47 GW (installed) | N/A | 32% harnessed; 68% remaining |
| **Solar Potential** | 750 GW (assessed) | 85 GW (installed) | N/A | <12% of assessed potential tapped |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A: India's Installed Power Capacity Mix (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following schematic represents the functional composition of India's electricity generation infrastructure, mapped by source and reported capacity (approximate, 2024-25 figures).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Installed Capacity: 430 GW]:::root
    A --> B[Fossil Fuel: ~205 GW 48%]
    A --> C[Renewable: ~190 GW 44%]
    A --> D[Nuclear: 8.2 GW 2%]
    A --> E[Hydro: 47 GW part of RE]

    B --> B1[Coal: 200 GW]
    B --> B2[Gas: 25 GW]
    B --> B3[Diesel: 0.5 GW]

    C --> C1[Solar PV: 85 GW]
    C --> C2[Wind: 47 GW]
    C --> C3[Hydro Large: 47 GW]
    C --> C4[Biomass: 10 GW]
    C --> C5[Small Hydro: 5 GW]

    D --> D1[Pressurized Heavy Water: 6.8 GW]
    D --> D2[Light Water VVER: 2.0 GW]
    D --> D3[Boiling Water: 0.3 GW]

    classDef root fill:#1F4E79,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef fossil fill:#C00000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    classDef re fill:#00B050,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    classDef nuc fill:#7030A0,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    class B,B1,B2,B3 fossil
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,E re
    class D,D1,D2,D3 nuc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Hydro (47 GW) is double-counted in the **Renewable** total and the **Hydro** node — it is conventional large hydro, classified as renewable per MNRE definitions. The actual non-hydro renewable capacity is ~143 GW, approximately **33%** of the total installed base.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B: Energy Supply Chain Flow (Block Diagram)

This schematic maps the upstream-to-downstream flow of energy in India, from resource extraction to end-use consumption.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Coal Mines]:::source
    R2[Oil and Gas Fields]:::source
    R3[Uranium Mines]:::source
    R4[Sun Wind Biomass]:::source

    C1[Thermal Power Plants]:::convert
    C2[Refineries]:::convert
    C3[Nuclear Reactors]:::convert
    C4[Solar Wind Farms]:::convert

    T1[National Grid 765 kV HVDC]:::transmit
    T2[Petroleum Pipelines]:::transmit

    E1[Industry 42 percent]:::enduse
    E2[Domestic 24 percent]:::enduse
    E3[Agriculture 17 percent]:::enduse
    E4[Commercial 9 percent]:::enduse
    E5[Transport 8 percent]:::enduse

    R1 --> C1
    R2 --> C2
    R3 --> C3
    R4 --> C4

    C1 --> T1
    C4 --> T1
    C3 --> T1
    C2 --> T2

    T1 --> E1
    T1 --> E2
    T1 --> E3
    T1 --> E4
    T2 --> E5
    T2 --> E2

    classDef source fill:#2E75B6,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    classDef convert fill:#FFC000,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    classDef transmit fill:#7030A0,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    classDef enduse fill:#00B050,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
```

```mermaid

    S --> P1[Industry 680 TWh 42 percent]:::ind
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Agriculture consumes 17% of electricity but contributes only ~4% to GDP — an implicit subsidy of nearly ₹1.5 lakh crore per year through free/subsidized power to agricultural pumps. This is a critical driver of DISCOM financial distress and a central concern in tariff reform debates before the CERC.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B]** Differentiate between **commercial** and **non-commercial** energy sources with two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Commercial Energy Sources (1 Mark):** Energy sources that are traded in the organized market and have an established pricing mechanism. Examples: (i) Coal, (ii) Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) (Any two).
- **Non-Commercial Energy Sources (1 Mark):** Energy sources collected from nature or agriculture, not bought or sold through formal markets, and traditionally used in rural households. Examples: (i) Firewood, (ii) Cow-dung cakes, (iii) Agricultural residues (Any two).
- **Distinguishing Feature (1 Mark):** Commercial sources are measured, metered, and taxed; non-commercial sources are unaccounted, free, and cause indoor air pollution due to incomplete combustion. Non-commercial sources still meet the cooking energy needs of approximately 25 crore Indians.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **per capita energy consumption** and state India's approximate value.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** Per capita energy consumption is the average electrical energy consumed per person per year. It is calculated as the ratio of total annual electrical energy consumption of a country to its total population. The standard unit is kilowatt-hour per person per year (kWh/person/year). Mathematically, $\text{PCEC} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{P}$, where $E_{\text{total}}$ is in kWh and $P$ is the population.
- **India's Value (1 Mark):** India's per capita electricity consumption is approximately **1,200 – 1,250 kWh/person/year** (FY 2023-24), which is about one-third of the global average (~3,300 kWh) and roughly one-tenth of the USA (~12,000 kWh).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Installed Capacity (GW) with Energy Generated (TWh).** They are dimensionally different. Always include units in every step.
> 2. **Avoid confusing CUF (Capacity Utilization Factor) with PLF (Plant Load Factor).** PLF is used for thermal plants that can operate continuously; CUF is used for renewable plants with variable output.
> 3. **In CAGR problems, ensure the exponent matches the year difference.** A common error is to use $n=12$ instead of $n=14$ for a 2010–2024 problem.
> 4. **Energy Intensity questions must specify the denominator** (GDP at constant prices or PPP). Most KTU questions expect PPP for cross-country comparison.
> 5. **Do not write vague answers like "India is facing energy crisis."** Examiners expect quantitative reasoning and identification of specific bottlenecks (e.g., DISCOM losses, import dependence).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set A]**

**(a)** Discuss the present installed power capacity scenario of India. Categorize the capacity by source (thermal, hydro, nuclear, renewable) and present approximate GW figures. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a numerical example, explain the concept of **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)**. A 50 MW wind farm in Tamil Nadu generated 1,20,000 MWh in a year. Calculate its CUF and comment on whether it is above or below the national average for wind plants (~30%). (7 Marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **per capita energy consumption** and **energy intensity**. Why is India considered energy-poor despite being the third-largest electricity producer globally? (7 Marks)

**(b)** India's installed capacity grew from 1,70,000 MW in 2009 to 4,10,000 MW in 2023. Calculate the CAGR. If the same CAGR continues, what will be the projected installed capacity in 2030? (7 Marks)

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Part B (Internal Choice)

#### Solution to Question A (a) — Installed Capacity Scenario (7 Marks)

India's installed power capacity as of late 2024 stands at approximately **430 GW**, making it the **third-largest** globally (after China and the USA). The source-wise breakup is:

| Source | Installed Capacity (GW) | Share (%) |
|--------|-------------------------|-----------|
| **Coal (Thermal)** | ~200 | ~46.5% |
| **Natural Gas** | ~25 | ~5.8% |
| **Large Hydro** | ~47 | ~10.9% |
| **Nuclear** | ~8.2 | ~1.9% |
| **Solar PV** | ~85 | ~19.8% |
| **Wind** | ~47 | ~10.9% |
| **Biomass/Bagasse** | ~10 | ~2.3% |
| **Small Hydro** | ~5 | ~1.2% |
| **Diesel** | ~0.5 | ~0.1% |
| **Total** | **~430 GW** | **100%** |

**Key Observations (2 Marks):**
- Coal still dominates at ~46.5%, reflecting India's indigenous abundance.
- Renewables (excluding large hydro) constitute ~32%, with solar leading.
- Nuclear remains under-developed at <2%, despite the three-stage program's vision.
- The non-fossil capacity (renewables + nuclear + hydro) is ~52%, exceeding fossil capacity for the first time in 2024.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b) — CUF Calculation (7 Marks)

**Concept Explanation (3 Marks):** CUF is the ratio of actual energy generated by a power plant in a year to the maximum possible energy that could have been generated if it operated at its rated capacity for the entire 8,760 hours. It reflects the **operational efficiency** and is a key performance indicator (KPI) for renewable energy plants because they depend on weather variability.

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- Capacity $C = 50 \text{ MW} = 50{,}000 \text{ kW}$
- Annual Generation $E = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ MWh} = 1.2 \times 10^{8} \text{ kWh}$

**Calculation (2 Marks):**
$$\text{CUF} = \frac{E}{C \times 8{,}760} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{CUF} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{8}}{50{,}000 \times 8{,}760} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{CUF} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{8}}{4.38 \times 10^{8}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{CUF} = 0.2740 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{CUF} \approx 27.4\%}$$

**Comparison and Comment (1 Mark):** The computed CUF of 27.4% is **below** the national wind average of ~30% but within the typical operating range (25–35%). It indicates a slightly underperforming year, possibly due to low wind speeds (monsoon deficit) or extended maintenance outages.

---

#### Solution to Question B (a) — Per Capita Consumption & Energy Intensity (7 Marks)

**Per Capita Energy Consumption (2 Marks):** Defined as the average electrical energy consumed per person per year. India's value of **~1,250 kWh** is among the lowest for any major economy. Formula: $\text{PCEC} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{P}$.

**Energy Intensity (2 Marks):** Defined as the energy consumed per unit of economic output (GDP). Formula: $\text{EI} = \frac{\text{Primary Energy (toe)}}{\text{GDP (Million USD at PPP)}}$. India's energy intensity is ~0.20 toe per 1,000 USD of GDP, which is high compared to developed economies (~0.10), indicating an **inefficient economy** that is not yet decoupled from energy use.

**Why is India Energy-Poor Despite Being the 3rd Largest Producer? (3 Marks):**
1. **Population Denominator Effect:** With 1.42 billion people, the absolute generation gets diluted. China's per capita consumption is ~6,000 kWh, more than four times India's.
2. **Aggregate Technical and Commercial (ATC) Losses:** DISCOM losses of ~18–22% (against a target of <15%) reduce delivered electricity.
3. **Subsidized Agricultural Consumption:** Free pump sets waste energy without accountability.
4. **Energy Poverty in Rural Areas:** ~5% of households still face supply quality issues despite 100% electrification.
5. **Industrial Inefficiency:** Old technology in MSME sectors (e.g., inefficient motors, boilers) inflates specific energy consumption.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b) — CAGR and Capacity Projection (7 Marks)

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $V_{\text{initial}} = 1{,}70{,}000 \text{ MW}$ (Year 2009)
- $V_{\text{final}} = 4{,}10{,}000 \text{ MW}$ (Year 2023)
- $n = 14 \text{ years}$

**Step 1: Apply CAGR Formula (2 Marks)**
$$\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{4{,}10{,}000}{1{,}70{,}000}\right)^{\frac{1}{14}} - 1$$

**Step 2: Compute Ratio and Root (2 Marks)**
$$\frac{4{,}10{,}000}{1{,}70{,}000} = 2.4118$$

$$\text{CAGR} = (2.4118)^{0.0714} - 1$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(2.4118) = 0.8806$, then $\frac{0.8806}{14} = 0.0629$, then $e^{0.0629} = 1.0649$.

$$\boxed{\text{CAGR} \approx 6.49\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 3: Project to 2030 (2 Marks)**

From 2023 to 2030 is **$n = 7$ years** at the same CAGR.
$$V_{2030} = V_{2023} \times (1 + \text{CAGR})^{7}$$

$$V_{2030} = 4{,}10{,}000 \times (1.0649)^{7}$$

Using $(1.0649)^7$: $\ln(1.0649) \times 7 = 0.0629 \times 7 = 0.4403$, $e^{0.4403} = 1.5531$.

$$V_{2030} = 4{,}10{,}000 \times 1.5531$$

$$\boxed{V_{2030} \approx 6{,}36{,}770 \text{ MW} \approx 637 \text{ GW}}$$

**Interpretation:** At 6.49% CAGR, India's installed capacity is projected to reach **~637 GW by 2030**, aligning with the government's target of 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 (declared at COP26, Glasgow, 2021).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] India is the **3rd largest electricity producer** globally but ranks low in per capita consumption (~1,250 kWh vs. world average of 3,300 kWh).
- [x] **Coal dominates** at ~46–48% of installed capacity, but **non-fossil capacity crossed 50% in 2024**.
- [x] **Per Capita Energy Consumption** = Total Energy / Population. Always convert TWh to kWh ($1 \text{ TWh} = 10^9 \text{ kWh}$).
- [x] **Energy Intensity** = Energy / GDP at PPP. Lower EI = more efficient economy.
- [x] **CUF** = Actual Energy / (Installed Capacity × 8,760). Used for renewables. PLF is used for thermal plants.
- [x] **CAGR** = $(V_f / V_i)^{1/n} - 1$. Use natural logarithms for accuracy.
- [x] **1 toe = 11.63 MWh = 11,630 kWh**. Master this conversion.
- [x] **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio:** Coal ≈ 150 years, Oil ≈ 14 years, Gas ≈ 20 years. This is why India is a net importer of oil and gas.
- [x] **Sector-wise Electricity Consumption:** Industry (42%) > Domestic (24%) > Agriculture (17%) > Commercial (9%) > Transport (8%).
- [x] **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR)** Phase 1 has a capacity of 5.33 MMT (Vizag, Mangalore, Padur); Phase 2 will add 12.5 MMT.
- [x] **Renewable Targets:** 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030; net-zero by 2070 (announced at COP26).
- [x] **Key Policy Initiatives:** SAUBHAGYA (household electrification), UJALA (LED distribution), FAME II (EV adoption), PM-KUSUM (solar pumps), National Green Hydrogen Mission (5 MMT by 2030).
- [x] **Central Agencies:** CEA (planning), MNRE (renewables), MoPNG (oil and gas), CERC (central tariff regulation), BEE (efficiency standards).
- [x] **Three-Stage Nuclear Program:** Stage 1 — Pressurized Heavy Water Reactors (PHWRs); Stage 2 — Fast Breeder Reactors (FBRs); Stage 3 — Thorium-based Advanced Heavy Water Reactors (AHWRs).
- [x] **One Nation-One Grid** is synchronized at 50 Hz across the entire country, enabling cross-border power exchange with Bhutan, Nepal, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka.
- [x] **Green Hydrogen** is produced via electrolysis using renewable electricity; India targets 5 MMT annual production by 2030, replacing grey hydrogen in refineries and fertilizer plants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** World Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
The **World Energy Scenario** is the systematic quantitative and qualitative assessment of global energy demand, supply, consumption patterns, resource availability, energy mix, and the geopolitical, economic, and environmental forces that shape the production, distribution, and utilization of primary and secondary energy across nations. It establishes the macroscopic backdrop against which all energy engineering decisions (technology selection, policy design, sustainability planning) are evaluated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The World Energy Scenario topic anchors the student’s understanding of *why* renewable and non-conventional energy systems are critical. KTU examiners frequently test numerical data interpretation (consumption share %, R/P ratio) and the categorization of reserves vs. resources.

### 1.2 Key Energy Metrics (Standard International Units)
The following are the universally accepted standard units:

- **TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent):** 1 TOE = $41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$ of thermal energy.
- **MTOE (Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent):** $1 \text{ MTOE} = 10^6 \text{ TOE}$.
- **BTOE (Billion Tonne of Oil Equivalent):** $1 \text{ BTOE} = 10^9 \text{ TOE}$.
- **Quad (Quadrillion BTU):** 1 Quad = $1.055 \times 10^{18} \text{ J} \approx 25.2 \text{ MTOE}$.
- **Exajoule (EJ):** $1 \text{ EJ} = 10^{18} \text{ J} \approx 23.88 \text{ MTOE}$.
- **Barrel of Oil Equivalent (BOE):** 1 BOE ≈ 0.146 TOE (based on $5.8 \times 10^6 \text{ BTU}$ per barrel).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Global Energy Bank"
Think of the Earth's energy resources as a **large bank account** with two ledgers:
- **The Vault (Reserves):** Energy that is *proven* and *economically extractable today* (e.g., operating oil wells, accessible coal seams).
- **The Promissory Notes (Resources):** Energy that is *theoretically present* but requires new technology, higher prices, or more time to access (e.g., deep-sea methane hydrates, hydrogen from seawater).

The **World Energy Scenario** is simply the annual "financial statement" of this bank — showing deposits (production), withdrawals (consumption), and the years of balance remaining (R/P ratio). If withdrawals exceed deposits of new reserves, the account goes bankrupt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Hierarchy (KTU Terminology):** Energy is universally classified as **Primary Energy** (found in nature: coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium) and **Secondary Energy** (derived from primary sources: electricity, refined gasoline, hydrogen, steam).

### 1.4 Global Energy Consumption Snapshot (IEA-aligned Approximations)
- Total Final Energy Consumption (TFC): ~ **420 EJ/year** (≈ 10,000 MTOE).
- Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES): ~ **600 EJ/year**.
- Share of fossil fuels (coal + oil + gas): ~ **80–82%** of TPES.
- Share of renewables (modern hydro, solar, wind, bio): ~ **13–14%**.
- Share of nuclear: ~ **4%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **India-specific context (important for KTU):** India is the **3rd largest primary energy consumer** globally and the **2nd largest importer of oil**. Per-capita energy consumption in India (~ 0.6 TOE) is far below the world average (~ 1.7 TOE), signalling huge growth potential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the World Energy Scenario

**Pillar 1 — Demand Side (Consumption)**
- Driven by **population growth**, **GDP per capita**, **industrialization**, and **urbanization**.
- Captured by the **Energy Intensity** metric (energy used per unit GDP).
- KTU Key Insight: Developed nations have *low* energy intensity (efficient) but *high* per-capita consumption. Developing nations show the opposite.

**Pillar 2 — Supply Side (Reserves & Production)**
- Quantified by **Reserves (R)** — proven + economically recoverable.
- Quantified by **Production (P)** — current annual extraction.
- The critical metric linking both is the **R/P Ratio** (Reserve-to-Production Ratio) — also called the *Static Depletion Index*.

**Pillar 3 — Transition Side (Sustainability)**
- The shift from **carbon-intensive** (coal, oil, gas) to **low-carbon** (solar, wind, hydro, nuclear, green hydrogen) energy systems.
- Driven by the **Paris Agreement** target of limiting global warming to **1.5 °C above pre-industrial levels**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the R/P Ratio
1. Identify proven reserves of an energy source (e.g., oil = 1,700 billion barrels).
2. Identify current annual production (e.g., 30 billion barrels/year).
3. Apply the formula: $\text{R/P} = R \div P$.
4. Result represents the *number of years* the reserve would last at *current extraction rates*, assuming no new discoveries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** "If a country has X tonnes of coal reserves and produces Y tonnes/year, find the lifetime of the reserve." This is a guaranteed question in Module 1 every KTU cycle.

### 2.3 Energy Density Hierarchy
A core concept in any energy study is comparing *how much* energy each source provides per unit mass or volume:

| Energy Source | Energy Density (MJ/kg) | Energy Density (MJ/L) | Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uranium (fission) | $8.0 \times 10^7$ | $1.5 \times 10^9$ | Primary |
| Hydrogen (LHV) | 120 | 8.5 | Secondary |
| Natural Gas (LHV) | 50 | 0.04 | Primary |
| Diesel | 45 | 36 | Secondary |
| Crude Oil | 42 | 37 | Primary |
| Coal (Bituminous) | 32 | 28 | Primary |
| Li-ion Battery | 0.5 | 1.5 | Secondary |
| Wood (dry) | 16 | 10 | Primary |
| Water (100 m head) | 0.001 | 0.001 | Renewable |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Definition of Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil Equivalent | $1 \text{ TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ}$ | TOE = Tonne of Oil Equivalent | GJ |
| Energy Conversion | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | Standard SI conversion | MJ |
| R/P Ratio (Static Lifetime) | $\text{R/P} = R \div P$ | R = Proven Reserve, P = Annual Production | years |
| Energy Intensity (EI) | $\text{EI} = E_{\text{total}} \div \text{GDP}$ | $E_{\text{total}}$ = Total Energy Used, GDP in \$ | MJ/\$ or TOE/\$ |
| Per-Capita Consumption | $E_{\text{pc}} = E_{\text{total}} \div N$ | N = Population | TOE/person |
| Share of Source *i* | $S_i = (E_i \div E_{\text{total}}) \times 100$ | $E_i$ = Energy from source *i* | % |
| Growth Rate (CAGR) | $C = (E_f \div E_i)^{1/n} - 1$ | $E_f$ = Final, $E_i$ = Initial, n = years | fraction |
| Capacity Factor | $\text{CF} = E_{\text{actual}} \div E_{\text{ideal}}$ | Actual vs ideal energy output | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Heating Value (Combustion) | $Q = m \times \text{HV}$ | m = mass of fuel, HV = Heating Value | kJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Energy Density — Why It Matters:** Hydrogen has the highest energy *per mass* of any chemical fuel, but the *lowest* per volume. This single fact is why hydrogen storage/compression/liquefaction is one of the hardest engineering problems in the energy transition.

### 2.5 Reserves vs. Resources (Definitive Distinction)
- **Reserves:** Proven + Recoverable + Economically viable *at today's price and technology*.
- **Resources:** Total estimated occurrence, including inferred, undiscovered, and currently uneconomical deposits.
- **Reserves** ⊂ **Resources** — reserves are a strict subset.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** The terms "reserves" and "resources" are *not* synonyms. KTU explicitly tests this distinction. Reserves = bank balance; Resources = total potential earnings including lottery tickets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Unit Conversion Chain
A frequently asked numerical problem: *"Convert X MTOE into EJ."*

**Step 1 — Define the base unit (TOE in Joules).**
From standard thermodynamic reference:
$$1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 2 — Express MTOE in Joules.**
$$1 \text{ MTOE} = 10^6 \text{ TOE} = 10^6 \times 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 4.1868 \times 10^7 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert GJ to EJ.**
$$1 \text{ EJ} = 10^{18} \text{ J} = 10^9 \text{ GJ}$$
Therefore:
$$1 \text{ MTOE} = 4.1868 \times 10^7 \text{ GJ} \times \frac{1 \text{ EJ}}{10^9 \text{ GJ}} = 0.041868 \text{ EJ}$$

**Step 4 — The universal conversion factor.**
$$\boxed{1 \text{ MTOE} = 0.041868 \text{ EJ} \approx \frac{1}{23.88} \text{ EJ}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**
*Convert the world's annual primary energy supply of 600 EJ into MTOE:*
$$E = 600 \text{ EJ} \times \frac{1 \text{ MTOE}}{0.041868 \text{ EJ}} = 14{,}331 \text{ MTOE}$$
$$\boxed{E \approx 14{,}331 \text{ MTOE/year}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: R/P Ratio (Static Reserve Lifetime)
**Step 1 — State the conservation premise.**
At a constant production rate, the time to deplete a known reserve is:
$$T = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Worked numerical example.**
*Given: Crude oil reserves of a country = 5,000 million tonnes. Annual production = 150 million tonnes/year. Find the static lifetime.*
$$T = \frac{5000 \text{ Mt}}{150 \text{ Mt/yr}} = 33.33 \text{ years}$$
$$\boxed{T \approx 33.3 \text{ years}}$$

**Step 3 — Modification for growth in production.**
If production grows at an annual rate $g$ (CAGR), the reserve lifetime becomes the *Dynamic* lifetime, derived by solving a geometric series:
$$R = P_0 \sum_{k=0}^{T-1} (1+g)^k = P_0 \cdot \frac{(1+g)^T - 1}{g}$$
Solving for $T$:
$$(1+g)^T = 1 + \frac{R \cdot g}{P_0}$$
$$T = \frac{\ln\!\left(1 + \frac{Rg}{P_0}\right)}{\ln(1+g)}$$

*Numerical example with $g = 0.02$ (2% growth), $R = 5000$ Mt, $P_0 = 150$ Mt/yr:*
$$Rg \div P_0 = (5000 \times 0.02) \div 150 = 0.6667$$
$$(1+g) = 1.02$$
$$T = \frac{\ln(1.6667)}{\ln(1.02)} = \frac{0.5108}{0.0198} = 25.79 \text{ years}$$
$$\boxed{T_{\text{dynamic}} \approx 25.8 \text{ years (vs. 33.3 years static)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 2% production growth rate shortens the effective lifetime by ~7.5 years. This is precisely why KTU and IEA always warn that R/P ratios are *optimistic* upper bounds for fossil fuel planning.

### 3.3 Derivation: Share of a Specific Energy Source
**Step 1 — Define share mathematically.**
$$S_i = \frac{E_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Numerical example.**
*A nation's TPES comprises: Coal = 300 MTOE, Oil = 250 MTOE, Gas = 150 MTOE, Renewables = 100 MTOE, Nuclear = 50 MTOE. Find the share of fossil fuels and renewables.*
$$E_{\text{total}} = 300 + 250 + 150 + 100 + 50 = 850 \text{ MTOE}$$
$$E_{\text{fossil}} = 300 + 250 + 150 = 700 \text{ MTOE}$$
$$S_{\text{fossil}} = \frac{700}{850} \times 100\% = 82.35\%$$
$$S_{\text{renewables}} = \frac{100}{850} \times 100\% = 11.76\%$$
$$\boxed{S_{\text{fossil}} \approx 82.4\%, \quad S_{\text{renew}} \approx 11.8\%}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Per-Capita Energy Consumption
**Step 1 — Formula.**
$$E_{\text{pc}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Example.**
*India's TPES = 900 MTOE; Population = 1.4 billion. Find per-capita consumption.*
$$E_{\text{pc}} = \frac{900 \text{ MTOE}}{1.4 \times 10^9 \text{ persons}} = 642.86 \text{ TOE/million persons}$$
Expressed in kg per person:
$$E_{\text{pc}} = 0.643 \text{ TOE/person} = 643 \text{ kg of oil equivalent per person}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Global Energy Snapshot (Approximations)
| Region | TPES (EJ) | Per-Capita (TOE) | Fossil Share | Renewable Share | Energy Intensity (TOE/\$1000 GDP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| World | 600 | 1.7 | 81% | 14% | 0.18 |
| China | 145 | 2.4 | 87% | 11% | 0.23 |
| USA | 95 | 6.8 | 78% | 17% | 0.16 |
| India | 36 | 0.6 | 89% | 9% | 0.20 |
| EU | 70 | 3.0 | 65% | 25% | 0.11 |
| Africa | 40 | 0.7 | 75% | 22% | 0.30 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 World Energy Classification — Top-Level Architecture

### 4.3 Decision Framework — World Energy Scenario Drivers

### 4.4 R/P Ratio Visual — Static Lifetime Concept

```mermaid
    resA --> resB
```
