> **Interpretation for Kerala / South India Context:** Tier-2 (ECBC) is enforced by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency. Tier-3 GRIHA is preferred for government and PSU projects in Kerala due to MNRE affiliation, while IGBC is preferred for private commercial buildings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Green Building Standard and list any four major international or national rating systems. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Green Building Standard is a formalized set of performance benchmarks used to evaluate the environmental sustainability of buildings across their life-cycle. (1 mark)

The four major rating systems are: (2 marks — 0.5 each)
1. **LEED** (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) — USGBC, USA
2. **BREEAM** (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method) — BRE, UK
3. **GRIHA** (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment) — MNRE, India
4. **IGBC** (Indian Green Building Council rating) — CII, India

*(Alternative: WELL, EDGE, Passive House, ECBC — any four accepted)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Energy Performance Index (EPI) and write its formula with standard units. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** is a normalized metric expressing the annual energy consumption of a building per unit built-up area. It enables comparison across buildings of different sizes. (2 marks)

$$\text{EPI} = \frac{E_{\text{total annual}}}{A_{\text{built-up}}}$$

Units: **kWh/m²/year** (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks in Part B by writing only definitions without quantitative examples, omitting units, or failing to present comparative tables. Always include a **comparison matrix** for any "compare and contrast" question — it conveys structured thinking and is highly rewarded.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the categories and certification levels of the LEED v4.1 rating system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

LEED v4.1 (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) is the latest iteration developed by the **U.S. Green Building Council (USGBC)**. It evaluates projects across **7 categories**:

| Category | Max Points |
|---|---|
| Location and Transportation (LT) | 16 |
| Sustainable Sites (SS) | 10 |
| Water Efficiency (WE) | 11 |
| Energy and Atmosphere (EA) | 33 |
| Materials and Resources (MR) | 13 |
| Indoor Environmental Quality (EQ) | 16 |
| Innovation (IN) + Regional Priority (RP) | 11 |
| **Total** | **110** |

**Certification Levels (based on points earned):**

| Level | Points Range |
|---|---|
| Certified | 40 – 49 |
| Silver | 50 – 59 |
| Gold | 60 – 79 |
| Platinum | 80 + |

**Key Features of LEED v4.1:**
1. **Performance-based approach** — emphasizes measured outcomes (e.g., actual energy use) rather than prescriptive design.
2. **Building Performance Institute (BPI)** monitoring after occupancy.
3. **LEED O+M (Operations & Maintenance)** for existing buildings.
4. **Carbon tracking** integrated into EA credits.

*(7 marks — 2 for categories, 2 for certification levels, 3 for features)*

---

**(b) Compare LEED and GRIHA rating systems with respect to their origin, weightage, certification levels, and applicability in India. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | LEED | GRIHA |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | US Green Building Council (1998) | MNRE + TERI, India (2007) |
| Weightage: Energy | ~33% | ~28% |
| Weightage: Materials | ~13% | ~20% |
| Weightage: Water | ~11% | ~14% |
| Certification Levels | Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum (4) | 1-Star to 5-Star (5) |
| Mandatory in India | No | Required for Central Govt buildings |
| Local Material Credits | Limited | Strong emphasis on local & regional materials |
| Cost of Certification | Higher (international fees) | Lower (subsidized) |
| Applicability | Global portfolios | Indian climatic & cultural context |

**Conclusion:** While LEED offers global brand recognition and consistent benchmarking, **GRIHA is better suited for the Indian context** due to its alignment with local climate zones, material availability, and government mandates. For Kerala-specific projects, GRIHA's emphasis on monsoon-adapted design and tropical climate zones provides a more relevant framework.

*(7 marks — 4 for comparison table, 3 for conclusion & applicability)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the Indian Green Building Council (IGBC) rating system and its certification levels in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Indian Green Building Council (IGBC)**, operating under the **Confederation of Indian Industry (CII)**, was established in 2001. It offers rating systems tailored to diverse project typologies in India.

**Key Rating Categories under IGBC Green New Buildings:**

| Category | Max Points |
|---|---|
| Sustainable Architecture & Design | 22 |
| Site Selection & Planning | 15 |
| Water Conservation | 19 |
| Energy Efficiency | 24 |
| Building Materials & Resources | 14 |
| Indoor Environmental Quality | 6 |
| **Total** | **100** |

**Certification Levels:**

| Level | Points Required |
|---|---|
| IGBC Certified | 50 – 59 |
| IGBC Silver | 60 – 69 |
| IGBC Gold | 70 – 79 |
| IGBC Platinum | 80 – 89 |
| IGBC Super Platinum | 90 – 100 |

**Specialized IGBC Rating Tools:**
- IGBC Green Existing Buildings (O+M)
- IGBC Green Homes
- IGBC Green Factory
- IGBC Green Healthcare
- IGBC Green School
- IGBC Green Township (large integrated developments)

**Notable Feature:** IGBC strongly rewards projects that include **on-site renewable energy generation** and **net-zero water** strategies — particularly relevant for Kerala's solar and hydro resources.

*(7 marks — 2 for introduction, 3 for categories and levels, 2 for specialized tools)*

---

**(b) Explain the Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) of India: its scope, applicability, and compliance pathways. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)**, developed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the Energy Conservation Act (2001), was first launched in **2007** and updated in **2017**.

**Scope and Applicability:**
- Applies to commercial buildings with a **connected load ≥ 100 kW** OR **contract demand ≥ 600 kVA**.
- Covers building envelope, HVAC, lighting, and electrical systems.
- NOT applicable to residential buildings (separate Eco Niwas Samhita applies).

**Key Compliance Parameters:**

| Parameter | ECBC Threshold |
|---|---|
| Envelope U-factor (walls) | 0.35 W/m²K (Composite Climate) |
| Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR) | ≤ 40% (with shading) |
| Lighting Power Density (LPD) | ≤ 9.0 W/m² (office) |
| Equipment Efficiency | IS 1391 / BEE star rating |
| HVAC COP | ≥ Baseline as per schedule |

**Three Compliance Pathways:**

1. **Prescriptive Path:** Each component meets minimum prescribed values.
2. **Trade-off Path:** Allows underperformance in one component to be compensated by overperformance in another (e.g., better envelope for worse HVAC).
3. **Whole Building Performance Path:** Simulated energy use must be ≤ ECBC baseline by at least 25%.

**Star Rating Scheme (BEE):**
The **ECBC star rating** awards 1 to 5 stars based on the percentage reduction of EPI over the baseline (similar to Derivation 3.1 above).

**Status in Kerala:**
Kerala's Energy Management Centre (EMC) is the state-level nodal agency. ECBC compliance is now mandatory for new government buildings in Kerala under the KIIFB-funded projects.

*(7 marks — 2 for scope, 3 for parameters, 2 for pathways and Kerala context)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Green Building Standards:**

- ✅ Green Building Standard = third-party verified rating system with quantitative thresholds.
- ✅ Six universal pillars: Site, Water, Energy, Materials, IEQ, Innovation.
- ✅ **LEED** = USGBC; levels = Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum.
- ✅ **GRIHA** = MNRE+TERI India; levels = 1-Star to 5-Star.
- ✅ **IGBC** = CII India; levels = Certified to Super Platinum.
- ✅ **BREEAM** = UK; uses Pass, Good, Very Good, Excellent, Outstanding.
- ✅ **ECBC** = Indian mandatory code for commercial buildings (≥100 kW).
- ✅ **EPI** (kWh/m²/year) is the single most critical metric across all standards.
- ✅ % Energy Savings = (EPI baseline − EPI proposed) / EPI baseline × 100.
- ✅ Embodied Carbon (EC) = Σ (Energy × Emission Factor) — measured in kgCO₂e.
- ✅ SDA (Spatial Daylight Autonomy) > 75% earns maximum LEED points.
- ✅ Tier-1 (Voluntary International) → Tier-2 (Mandatory National) → Tier-3 (Voluntary National).
- ✅ Kerala context: GRIHA preferred for government; IGBC for private; ECBC mandatory for commercial.
- ✅ Always present comparative answers in **tabular form** for full marks in KTU.
- ✅ Mention units explicitly — a missing unit can cost 0.5–1 mark per question.
- ✅ Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` in any markdown table formulas.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute Examiner Trap:** Students frequently confuse **GRIHA** (MNRE, government-backed) with **IGBC** (CII, industry-backed). Remember: **GRIHA = Government**, **IGBC = Industry**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Lamps and Their Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Lamps and Their Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Electric Lamp

An **electric lamp** is an artificial source of light that converts **electrical energy** into **visible radiant energy (luminous flux)** through the controlled excitation of a suitable material (filament, gas, vapor, or semiconductor junction). The performance of any lamp is judged by how well it converts the *watts drawn from the supply* into the *lumens reaching the working plane*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT637 – Module 1):**
> *"Types of lamps and their efficiencies – Incandescent, Fluorescent, CFL, Mercury Vapour, Sodium Vapour, Metal Halide, LED – comparison of luminous efficacy, life, CRI, and applications."*

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics for a Lamp

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **lumen (lm)** | Total quantity of visible light emitted per second |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **candela (cd)** | Luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle |
| Illuminance | $E$ | **lux (lx)** | Luminous flux incident per unit area ($1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$) |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_L$ | **lm/W** | Lumens produced per watt of electrical input |
| Lamp Life | $L$ | **hours (h)** | Average useful operating hours before failure |
| Colour Rendering Index | CRI | unitless (0–100) | Ability to reproduce true colours of objects |
| Correlated Colour Temperature | CCT | **Kelvin (K)** | Warmth or coolness appearance of the light |

### 1.3 The Master Performance Parameter: Luminous Efficacy

**Luminous Efficacy** is the single most important figure of merit for energy-conscious lighting design.

$$\eta_L \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Luminous Flux emitted}}{\text{Electrical Power consumed}} \;=\; \frac{\Phi \,[\text{lm}]}{P \,[\text{W}]} \quad \text{(units: lm/W)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Luminous Efficacy?**
> The human eye is *not* equally sensitive to all wavelengths. It peaks at **555 nm (green-yellow)** under photopic vision. Therefore, a lamp radiating heavily at 555 nm appears brighter than one radiating at, say, 800 nm (deep red), even when both emit the same total *radiant* power. Luminous efficacy already weights the radiant output by the eye's spectral response curve $V(\lambda)$, giving an *honest* measure of perceived brightness per watt.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water-Tap Model"

Imagine the electrical supply as a **water tap** and the useful visible light as a **measured glassful of water** you actually want to drink:

- **The tap flow rate** → Electrical power input (Watts).
- **Spillage (invisible IR, UV, heat)** → Wasted energy at invisible wavelengths.
- **Water that actually reaches the glass** → Useful lumens.

An **incandescent lamp** is like a tap that splashes mostly onto the floor (90% heat, 10% light) — you waste most of the water. A **modern LED** is like a precision tap delivering almost all the water straight into the glass (40–50% light, rest heat at the back). **Higher efficacy = less spillage, more light per drop of electricity.**

This is the foundational reason why energy-efficient lamps (LED, sodium vapour) dominate the modern lighting market and why KTU emphasises their study in the Energy Systems course.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Broad Classification of Artificial Light Sources

All electric lamps fall into one of three families based on the *mechanism* of light generation:

1. **Incandescence (Thermal Radiators)** — light produced by heating a body to high temperature.
2. **Gas Discharge Lamps (Electroluminescence via plasma)** — light produced by exciting atoms of a gas/vapour.
3. **Solid-State / Semiconductor Lamps** — light produced by electron-hole recombination across a p-n junction (LED, OLED).

### 2.2 Detailed Theory — Family-by-Family

#### 2.2.1 Incandescent Lamps
- **Mechanism:** A tungsten filament is heated to ~**2500–3000 K** by passing current. The filament glows by black-body radiation.
- **Spectrum:** Continuous; rich in infrared → most of the input power (≈ 90%) is radiated as heat, not light.
- **Filament:** Coiled tungsten (sometimes double-coiled) to reduce evaporative loss; bulb filled with inert N₂ / Ar (or vacuum for low wattages).
- **Efficacy:** **10 – 17 lm/W** (very low).
- **Life:** ≈ 1000 hours.
- **CRI:** 100 (excellent colour rendering).
- **KTU Insight:** Almost *obsolete* in modern installations because the energy wastage violates BEE star-rating norms.

#### 2.2.2 Halogen Lamps (Tungsten-Halogen)
- **Mechanism:** Same as incandescent, but the bulb contains a small amount of **iodine or bromine** (the "halogen"). The halogen cycle redeposits evaporated tungsten back onto the filament, allowing higher filament temperature.
- **Efficacy:** **15 – 25 lm/W** (≈ 20% better than standard incandescent).
- **Life:** 2000 – 4000 hours.
- **CCT:** 2800 – 3200 K (warm white).
- **Drawback:** Operates at very high bulb temperature → requires a quartz envelope.

#### 2.2.3 Fluorescent Lamps (TFL)
- **Construction:** Glass tube with **tungsten electrodes** at each end, internally coated with **phosphor**, filled with low-pressure **mercury vapour (≈ 4 Pa)** and **argon** starter gas.
- **Mechanism:** Mercury atoms ionise; excited Hg atoms emit **UV radiation at 253.7 nm**. UV strikes the phosphor coating, which fluoresces (down-converts) to visible light.
- **Efficacy:** **50 – 80 lm/W** for standard T8 tubes; up to 100 lm/W for T5 high-efficiency tubes.
- **Life:** 8000 – 15000 hours.
- **CRI:** 60 – 90 (depends on phosphor blend).
- **Auxiliary:** Requires **ballast** (choke) and **starter** for conventional circuits; electronic ballasts eliminate flicker and improve efficacy.

#### 2.2.4 Compact Fluorescent Lamps (CFL)
- **Concept:** A fluorescent tube bent into a compact shape with an integrated electronic ballast in the base.
- **Efficacy:** **50 – 70 lm/W**.
- **Life:** 6000 – 10000 hours.
- **Replaces** a 60 W incandescent with a 13–15 W CFL — saving ≈ 75% energy.

#### 2.2.5 Mercury Vapour Lamps (MV / HPMV)
- **Construction:** Quartz inner arc tube inside an outer borosilicate glass bulb; filled with mercury + argon.
- **Mechanism:** High-pressure mercury arc; dominant lines at 365 nm (UV) and 546 nm (green) → phosphor-coated bulbs convert UV to red for better CRI ("deluxe white" MV).
- **Efficacy:** **35 – 60 lm/W**.
- **Life:** 16000 – 24000 hours.
- **Application:** Street lighting (older installations), factory floors, parking lots.

#### 2.2.6 Sodium Vapour Lamps
Two sub-types — extremely important for KTU numericals:

**(a) Low Pressure Sodium Vapour (LPSV / SOX)**
- **Mechanism:** Sodium metal vaporises at low pressure; excited Na atoms emit *almost monochromatic* yellow light at **589 nm and 589.6 nm** (the famous sodium D-lines).
- **Efficacy:** **100 – 200 lm/W** (highest of *all* conventional lamps).
- **CRI:** ≈ −45 (very poor — monochromatic; objects appear only in shades of grey).
- **Application:** Highway lighting, tunnel lighting, security lighting where colour recognition is not critical.

**(b) High Pressure Sodium Vapour (HPSV / SON)**
- **Mechanism:** Sodium vapour at higher pressure (≈ 26 kPa). The sodium D-line broadens due to pressure broadening, producing a *near-white* golden light.
- **Efficacy:** **80 – 130 lm/W**.
- **CRI:** 20 – 65.
- **Life:** 20000 – 28000 hours.
- **Application:** Street lighting, warehouse, industrial bays — the *workhorse* of Indian municipal lighting.

#### 2.2.7 Metal Halide Lamps (MH / HPI)
- **Mechanism:** A high-pressure mercury arc with added **halides of sodium, scandium, indium, thallium, dysprosium**. Each halide contributes its characteristic emission line, producing a near-white spectrum.
- **Efficacy:** **80 – 110 lm/W**.
- **CRI:** 65 – 95 (excellent).
- **CCT:** 3000 – 6000 K (cool white / daylight).
- **Application:** Sports stadiums, high-bay industrial lighting, car dealerships, film studios.
- **KTU Pitfall:** Don't confuse metal halide with mercury vapour — they are *different* lamp families.

#### 2.2.8 Light Emitting Diodes (LED)
- **Mechanism:** Forward-biased p-n junction. Electron-hole recombination releases energy as photons with wavelength $\lambda = \frac{1240}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$ nm, where $E_g$ is the semiconductor band-gap.
- **Common Materials:** GaAs (infra-red, 870 nm), AlGaAs (red), AlGaInP (amber/red), InGaN (blue/green/white via phosphor).
- **Efficacy:** **80 – 150 lm/W** (premium white LEDs exceed 200 lm/W in laboratory).
- **Life:** 25000 – 50000 hours.
- **CRI:** 70 – 95.
- **CCT:** 2700 – 6500 K (tunable).
- **Unique advantages:** Directional emission, instant start, dimmable, mercury-free, rugged, no UV/IR.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table before the exam — it covers ≈ 80% of numerical questions asked on this topic.**

| \# | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Luminous efficacy | Direct calculation |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Illuminance on a surface | Photometric design |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$ | Inverse-square law (point source) | Street-lamp spacing |
| 4 | $P_{\text{total}} = n \times P_{\text{lamp}}$ | Total power of $n$ lamps | Energy audit |
| 5 | $\text{Energy} = P_{\text{total}} \times t$ | Energy in kWh | Electricity bill |
| 6 | $\text{No. of lamps} = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$ | Lumen method of design | Illumination design |
| 7 | $\text{Energy saved} = P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new}}$ | Retrofit savings | Energy conservation |
| 8 | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Extra cost}}{\text{Annual saving}}$ | Economic viability | Project justification |

Where:
- $\text{CU}$ = **Coefficient of Utilisation** (0.3 – 0.8 depending on fixture).
- $\text{LLF}$ = **Light Loss Factor** (typically 0.7 – 0.8 to account for ageing + dust).
- $d$ = distance (m), $\theta$ = angle of incidence, $A$ = area (m²).

> **Note on Table Safety:** All mathematical "or" separators use $\vert$ or $\mid$ rather than the bare pipe $\vert$ to keep the markdown table parser intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BEE Star Labelling in India** rates appliances (including lamps) by efficacy — the topic directly supports **energy-audit** career paths.
- **IS 3646** (Code of Practice for Interior Illumination) and **NBC 2016** specify minimum lux levels — students must be able to *back-calculate* the number of lamps from these lux standards.
- **Municipal street-lighting tenders** (Kerala KSEB, PWD) use the inverse-square law and the lumen method to decide the *spacing-to-height ratio* of HPSV / LED poles.
- **Green-building certification (GRIHA / LEED)** credits efficient lighting — direct application of the comparative efficacy table.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Luminous Efficacy from First Principles

Let a lamp consume electrical power $P$ (in watts) and emit a total radiant flux $P_{\text{rad}}$ (also in watts). The remainder $(P - P_{\text{rad}})$ is lost as conductive/convective heat. The radiant flux is distributed over a spectrum with spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$ in W/nm.

**Step 1 — Spectral radiant flux density:**
$$S(\lambda) = \frac{dP_{\text{rad}}}{d\lambda} \quad \text{(units: W·nm}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Luminous flux** is obtained by weighting the spectral radiant flux with the CIE photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ (dimensionless, peaks at 1.0 for $\lambda = 555$ nm) and the photometric constant $K_m = 683$ lm/W:

$$\Phi = K_m \int_{380 \text{ nm}}^{780 \text{ nm}} V(\lambda) \, S(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Luminous efficacy** is the ratio of the luminous flux (lumens) to the input electrical power (watts):

$$\eta_L = \frac{\Phi}{P} = \frac{683}{P} \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \, S(\lambda) \, d\lambda \quad \text{(lm/W)}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering form.** For a lamp with a single dominant emission line of wavelength $\lambda_0$ and fraction $f$ of total power radiated at that line:

$$\eta_L \;\approx\; \frac{683 \cdot f \cdot V(\lambda_0) \cdot P}{P} \;=\; 683 \cdot f \cdot V(\lambda_0)$$

**Step 5 — Worked example (Low-Pressure Sodium).**
For LPSV, $f \approx 0.4$ and $\lambda_0 = 589$ nm, so $V(589) \approx 0.78$:

$$\eta_L \;\approx\; 683 \times 0.4 \times 0.78 \;\approx\; 213 \text{ lm/W (theoretical upper bound)}$$

The *measured* efficacy is slightly lower (100–200 lm/W) because of ballast losses, IR radiation, and imperfect resonance — a key KTU point.

### 3.2 Comparative Efficiency Matrix (Master Table)

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | Life (h × 10³) | CRI | Warm-up Time | Restrike Time | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incandescent (GLS) | 10 – 17 | 1 | 100 | Instant | Instant | Decorative, home (legacy) |
| Halogen | 15 – 25 | 2 – 4 | 100 | Instant | Instant | Showrooms, projector |
| Fluorescent (T8/T5) | 50 – 100 | 8 – 15 | 60 – 90 | ≈ 1 s | Instant (electronic) | Offices, schools |
| CFL | 50 – 70 | 6 – 10 | 80 – 85 | ≈ 2 s | ≈ 1 min (hot) | Domestic retrofit |
| Mercury Vapour (HPMV) | 35 – 60 | 16 – 24 | 40 – 60 | 3 – 5 min | 3 – 10 min | Street lighting (old) |
| Metal Halide (HPI) | 80 – 110 | 6 – 15 | 65 – 95 | 2 – 4 min | 10 – 20 min | Stadiums, industry |
| HPSV (SON) | 80 – 130 | 20 – 28 | 20 – 65 | 3 – 5 min | 1 – 2 min | Street, warehouse |
| LPSV (SOX) | 100 – 200 | 12 – 18 | ≈ −45 | 7 – 15 min | 5 – 10 min | Highways, tunnels |
| LED (white) | 80 – 200 | 25 – 50 | 70 – 95 | Instant | Instant | Universal (modern) |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical — KTU Board Pattern

> **Problem:** A factory floor of dimensions $30 \text{ m} \times 20 \text{ m}$ requires an average illuminance of **300 lux**. The proposed HPSV lamp has flux 22 000 lm per fitting, with CU = 0.6 and LLF = 0.8. Calculate (a) the number of fittings required, (b) the total connected load if each HPSV fitting (lamp + ballast) consumes 150 W, and (c) the energy consumed per day if the lamps operate 10 hours.

**Step 1 — Required total lumens reaching the working plane (lumen method).**
$$E \cdot A = 300 \times (30 \times 20) = 300 \times 600 = 180\,000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2 — Effective lumens per fitting (accounting for utilisation and depreciation).**
$$\Phi_{\text{eff}} = \Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF} = 22\,000 \times 0.6 \times 0.8 = 10\,560 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 3 — Number of fittings.**
$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}} = \frac{180\,000}{10\,560} = 17.05$$

Round **up** to the next whole fitting:
$$N = 18 \text{ fittings}$$

**Step 4 — Total connected electrical load.**
$$P_{\text{total}} = N \times P_{\text{lamp}} = 18 \times 150 = 2700 \text{ W} = 2.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Daily energy consumption.**
$$E_{\text{daily}} = P_{\text{total}} \times t = 2.7 \text{ kW} \times 10 \text{ h} = 27 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 6 — Monthly energy cost (assuming ₹ 7 per kWh).**
$$\text{Cost} = 27 \times 30 \times 7 = ₹ 5670 \text{ per month}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-saving extension (commonly asked follow-up):** Replacing HPSV (150 W) with LED (60 W) fittings that give the same 22 000 lm reduces daily energy to $18 \times 60 \times 10 = 10.8$ kWh — a **60% reduction** in energy and a monthly saving of about ₹ 3402.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Master Classification of Electric Lamps

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ELECTRIC LAMPS] --> B[INCANDESCENT FAMILY]
    A --> C[GAS DISCHARGE FAMILY]
    A --> D[SOLID STATE FAMILY]

    B --> B1[GLS Incandescent]
    B --> B2[Tungsten Halogen]

    C --> C1[Low Pressure Discharge]
    C --> C2[High Pressure Discharge]

    C1 --> C1a[Fluorescent Tube T5 T8]
    C1 --> C1b[Compact Fluorescent CFL]
    C1 --> C1c[Low Pressure Sodium LPSV]

    C2 --> C2a[Mercury Vapour HPMV]
    C2 --> C2b[Metal Halide HPI]
    C2 --> C2c[High Pressure Sodium HPSV]

    D --> D1[LED White Phosphor Converted]
    D --> D2[OLED Organic LED]
    D --> D3[Quantum Dot LED]

    B1 --> E1[10 TO 17 lm per W]
    B2 --> E2[15 TO 25 lm per W]
    C1a --> E3[50 TO 100 lm per W]
    C1b --> E4[50 TO 70 lm per W]
    C1c --> E5[100 TO 200 lm per W]
    C2a --> E6[35 TO 60 lm per W]
    C2b --> E7[80 TO 110 lm per W]
    C2c --> E8[80 TO 130 lm per W]
    D1 --> E9[80 TO 200 lm per W]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Working Principle Block Flow of an LED

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[DC Power Supply Driver] --> stepB[Constant Current Regulator]
    stepB --> stepC[LED Chip p n Junction]
    stepC --> stepD[Electron Hole Recombination]
    stepD --> stepE[Photon Emission at Band gap Wavelength]
    stepE --> stepF[Phosphor Layer for White Light]
    stepF --> stepG[Optical Lens and Diffuser]
    stepG --> stepH[Useful Luminous Flux to Working Plane]
    stepC --> stepJ[Heat Sink Thermal Path]
    stepJ --> stepK[Ambient Air Convection]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Energy Flow Through a Typical Lamp (Sankey-style Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP[Electrical Input P Watts] --> FIL[Filament or Arc or Junction]
    FIL --> RAD[Radiant Output]
    FIL --> LOSS[Conduction and Convection Heat]
    RAD --> VIS[Visible Lumens lm]
    RAD --> INV[Infrared and Ultraviolet Wasted]
    VIS --> OUT[Useful Illumination at Working Plane]
    INV --> W1[Wasted as Heat]
    LOSS --> W1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — Decision Flow for Choosing a Lamp

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Need excellent colour rendering?} -->|Yes| Q2{Application: domestic or office?}
    Q1 -->|No| R1[Choose HPSV or LPSV for maximum efficacy]

    Q2 -->|Domestic| R2[Choose LED with CRI above 80]
    Q2 -->|Office| R3[Choose T5 Fluorescent or LED Panel]

    Q1 -->|Yes and High Bay| R4[Choose Metal Halide]
    Q1 -->|Yes and Stadium| R5[Choose Metal Halide or High CRI LED]
    Q1 -->|Yes and Decorative only| R6[Choose Halogen]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define luminous efficacy and state its SI unit. Why is luminous efficacy preferred over radiant efficiency for evaluating lighting devices?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition:** Luminous efficacy is the ratio of the total luminous flux emitted by a source to the electrical power consumed by it. (1 mark)
> $$\eta_L = \frac{\Phi \text{ (lm)}}{P \text{ (W)}} \quad \text{Unit: lm/W} \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$
> **Why preferred:** Radiant efficiency considers *all* emitted radiation, but the human eye is not equally sensitive to all wavelengths — it peaks at 555 nm. Luminous efficacy weights the radiant output with the CIE photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$, giving a *true* measure of perceived brightness per watt. (1.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Compare Low Pressure Sodium Vapour (LPSV) and High Pressure Sodium Vapour (HPSV) lamps in terms of efficacy, CRI and applications.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | LPSV (SOX) | HPSV (SON) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Efficacy | 100 – 200 lm/W (very high) | 80 – 130 lm/W |
> | CRI | ≈ −45 (mono-chromatic yellow) | 20 – 65 (golden-white) |
> | Application | Highway, tunnel lighting | Streets, warehouses, industry |
> (3 marks — 1 for efficacy, 1 for CRI, 1 for application comparison)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **fluorescent tube lamp**. Discuss the role of the choke (ballast) and starter in its operation. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A drawing hall of size **20 m × 12 m** is to be illuminated to an average illuminance of **450 lux**. Using **fluorescent tube fittings**, each delivering **3000 lumens**, with CU = 0.5 and LLF = 0.75, determine **(i)** the number of tubes required, **(ii)** total connected load if each tube with choke consumes **48 W**, and **(iii)** monthly energy consumption for 8 hours/day operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working of Fluorescent Tube Lamp** *(7 marks)*

A fluorescent tube is a **low-pressure mercury-vapour discharge lamp**. (½ mark)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A glass tube of length 0.6 m (2 ft) to 2.4 m (8 ft), internal diameter 25 mm (T8) or 15 mm (T5).
- **Two tungsten electrodes** coated with **emissive oxide** are sealed at each end.
- The inner wall is coated with a uniform layer of **halophosphate phosphor** (or tri-phosphor for high-CRI T5).
- The tube is filled with **low-pressure mercury vapour (≈ 4 Pa)** and a small amount of **argon or neon** as starting gas.

**Working (3 marks):**
- When the supply is switched ON, the **starter** (glow-switch type) momentarily closes, allowing current to flow through the chokes and electrodes → electrodes heat up and **thermionic emission** occurs.
- The starter opens → the chokes generate a **high inductive kick (≈ 1000 V)** that ionises the argon, striking an arc between the electrodes.
- The arc vaporises mercury; excited Hg atoms emit **UV radiation at 253.7 nm** (resonance line).
- UV photons strike the phosphor coating → phosphor **fluoresces**, down-converting UV to visible light.

**Role of Choke and Starter (1 mark):**
- **Choke (ballast):** A series inductor that limits the arc current to a safe value (lamp has negative resistance). Also produces the high voltage required to strike the arc.
- **Starter:** A bimetallic switch that momentarily closes to preheat electrodes, then snaps open to allow the choke to generate the striking voltage.

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $A = 20 \times 12 = 240$ m², $E = 450$ lux, $\Phi = 3000$ lm, CU = 0.5, LLF = 0.75, $P_{\text{lamp}} = 48$ W, $t = 8$ h/day.

**(i) Number of tubes** (2 marks)
$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}} = \frac{450 \times 240}{3000 \times 0.5 \times 0.75} = \frac{108\,000}{1125} = 96 \text{ tubes}$$

**(ii) Total connected load** (2 marks)
$$P_{\text{total}} = 96 \times 48 = 4608 \text{ W} = 4.608 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Monthly energy consumption** (3 marks)
$$E_{\text{daily}} = 4.608 \times 8 = 36.864 \text{ kWh/day}$$
$$E_{\text{monthly}} = 36.864 \times 30 = 1105.92 \text{ kWh} \approx 1106 \text{ kWh}$$

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **(a)** With the help of a **classification chart**, describe the **construction, working principle and relative merits of Metal Halide, HPSV and LPSV lamps**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A street-light installation has **20 lamp posts** spaced 30 m apart. Each **HPSV lamp** gives 25 000 lumens and consumes **250 W** (including ballast). Calculate: **(i)** the **average illuminance** on the road assuming the road width is 7 m, CU = 0.4 and LLF = 0.7; **(ii)** the **luminous efficacy** of the lamp; **(iii)** the **annual energy cost** at ₹ 6.50 per kWh for 12 hours daily operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction, Working and Comparison of HID Lamps** *(7 marks)*

| Feature | LPSV (SOX) | HPSV (SON) | Metal Halide (HPI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arc tube | U-shaped glass | Ceramic alumina (PCA) | Quartz |
| Fill | Na + Ne/Ar | Na + Hg + Xe | Na/Tl/In halides + Hg + Ar |
| Spectrum | Monochromatic 589 nm | Broadened yellow + IR | Multi-line white |
| Efficacy | 100 – 200 lm/W (2) | 80 – 130 lm/W (1) | 80 – 110 lm/W (1) |
| CRI | ≈ −45 (1) | 20 – 65 (1) | 65 – 95 (1) |

**Working of HPSV (1 mark):** A high-voltage pulse ionises xenon starting gas; arc heats and vaporises Na-Hg amalgam → pressure-broadened Na D-line emission.

**Working of Metal Halide (1 mark):** Halides dissociate in the arc; free metal atoms emit their characteristic spectra → near-white light; halides recombine on cooling near the wall.

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $n = 20$ posts, spacing $s = 30$ m, road width $w = 7$ m, $\Phi = 25\,000$ lm, $P = 250$ W, CU = 0.4, LLF = 0.7, $t = 12$ h/day, tariff = ₹ 6.50/kWh.

**(i) Average illuminance on the road** (3 marks)

Area effectively covered by each post = $s \times w = 30 \times 7 = 210$ m².
Total useful lumens per post = $\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF} = 25\,000 \times 0.4 \times 0.7 = 7000$ lm.
$$E_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}{s \times w} = \frac{7000}{210} = 33.33 \text{ lux}$$

**(ii) Luminous efficacy** (2 marks)
$$\eta_L = \frac{\Phi}{P} = \frac{25\,000}{250} = 100 \text{ lm/W}$$

**(iii) Annual energy cost** (2 marks)
$$E_{\text{daily}} = 20 \times 0.25 \times 12 = 60 \text{ kWh/day}$$
$$E_{\text{annual}} = 60 \times 365 = 21\,900 \text{ kWh/year}$$
$$\text{Cost} = 21\,900 \times 6.50 = ₹\,1\,42\,350 \text{ per year}$$

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Metal Halide with Mercury Vapour.** Metal halide has *halide additives* — write this explicitly in the answer.
> 2. **Writing $\text{lm/m}^2$ instead of lux** for illuminance. KTU specifically marks step-marks for the *correct unit*.
> 3. **Forgetting to round UP the number of lamps** in the lumen method. You cannot install 17.05 lamps — always round up.
> 4. **Forgetting ballast / starter losses** in fluorescent-lamp problems. The 48 W includes the choke; the *tube* itself consumes only ≈ 36 W.
> 5. **Writing "sodium vapour" without specifying LPSV or HPSV.** These are two different lamps with very different spectra and efficacies.
> 6. **Using $E = I/d^2$** when the problem clearly uses the *area method* (lumen method) — always read the question.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Luminous efficacy** $\eta_L = \Phi/P$ in **lm/W** is the master figure of merit.
- Eye response peaks at **555 nm**; photopic constant $K_m = 683$ lm/W.
- Efficacy ranking (low → high): Incandescent < Halogen < HPMV < CFL < HPSV ≈ Metal Halide < LPSV < LED.
- **CRI ranking (best → worst):** Incandescent = Halogen = 100 → Metal Halide (65–95) → CFL/Fluorescent (60–90) → HPSV (20–65) → LPSV (≈ −45).
- **Longest life:** LED (25 000 – 50 000 h) > HPSV > HPMV > Metal Halide > Fluorescent > Halogen > Incandescent.
- Fluorescent lamp = **low-pressure mercury + phosphor**; requires **choke + starter**.
- **HPSV** is the workhorse of Indian street lighting; **LPSV** is used where colour rendering is irrelevant.
- **LED** = p-n junction, $\lambda = 1240/E_g$ nm, mercury-free, instant start, dimmable.
- **Lumen method formula:** $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$ — remember to **round up**.
- **Inverse-square law:** $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$ — used for point-source problems and street-lamp spacing.
- **Energy audit angle:** Always quote the percentage energy saved (LED over HPSV ≈ 50–60%) in retrofit questions.
- **BEE star ratings** and **IS 3646 lux standards** are the Indian regulatory anchors for lighting design.
- **High-pressure** lamps need **3 – 5 min warm-up** and **5 – 20 min restrike** — a frequent MCQ/fill-in-blank.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal System - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal System - Working Principle - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solar Thermal System** is an engineered energy conversion装置 that intercepts incident solar radiation using a specially designed **heat collection surface**, converts the short-wave solar flux into long-wave thermal energy via the **greenhouse / selective absorption effect**, and transfers this heat to a working fluid (water, air, thermal oil, or molten salt) for direct use, storage, or further conversion to mechanical / electrical work.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT637 – Energy Systems, Module 2)**, the working principle is governed by three coupled physical processes operating in series:

1. **Radiative Capture** — Collection of beam + diffuse solar irradiance on an absorber plate.
2. **Photothermal Conversion** — Transformation of photon energy into sensible heat of the absorber.
3. **Thermofluid Transfer** — Convective + conductive delivery of heat to a transport medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to draw and label a **functional block diagram** of a solar thermal system and clearly identify the *direction of energy flow*, the *state of the working fluid* at each node, and the *boundary* between the *collection subsystem* and the *utilization subsystem*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Watering Can" Model

Imagine a black-painted garden watering can left in the afternoon sun.

- The **can (absorber plate)** absorbs sunlight just like a dark surface.
- The **water inside (working fluid)** heats up because the can's walls transfer the absorbed energy inward.
- The **lid (glass cover)** traps the heat — exactly like greenhouse effect — preventing the warm air from escaping via convection.
- If you poke a small **outlet pipe (heat exchanger / storage)**, the hot water flows out for use.

A solar thermal system is essentially this watering can, but **engineered for maximum capture, minimum loss, and continuous delivery** — often with active pumping, thermostats, and storage tanks.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Solar Constant (Iₛc) = 1367 W/m²** — extraterrestrial irradiance at the top of the atmosphere (AM 1.5).
- **Air Mass (AM 1.5)** — reference terrestrial spectral irradiance benchmark.
- **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** — average equivalent hours of 1000 W/m² irradiance per day.
- **Solar Insolation (kWh/m²/day)** — daily integrated energy per unit area (Kerala avg ≈ 4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day).
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**.
- **Selective Absorber Absorptivity (α) ≈ 0.95**, Emissivity (ε) ≈ 0.05.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why α/ε ratio matters:** A good selective coating has **high α for solar wavelengths (< 2.5 µm)** and **low ε for infrared (> 2.5 µm)**, so the surface "looks black to the sun" but "looks shiny to its own re-radiation".

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**STEP 1 — Solar Radiation Incidence**
Short-wave radiation (λ ≈ 0.3–2.5 µm) from the sun passes through the atmosphere with some attenuation (scattering + absorption by H₂O, CO₂, O₃). The ground-level **Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI)** is the sum of Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) and Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DHI).

**STEP 2 — Transmission Through the Cover Plate**
The **glazing** (low-iron tempered glass) has transmittance τ ≈ 0.91 for solar spectrum but is nearly opaque to long-wave IR emitted by the hot absorber — this is the **greenhouse effect**. It also reduces convective losses from the absorber to ambient air.

**STEP 3 — Absorption by the Selective Surface**
The absorber plate, coated with black chrome, nickel-pigmented aluminium oxide, or cermet (cermet = ceramic-metal composite), converts photon energy into lattice vibrational energy → heat. **Absorptivity α ≥ 0.95**.

**STEP 4 — Heat Transfer to the Working Fluid**
Heat flows from the absorber to the fluid tubes (risers) by **conduction** across the tube wall, then by **convection** from the inner wall to the fluid. The collector efficiency depends critically on this step.

**STEP 5 — Transport and Storage**
The hot fluid is either sent directly to the load (e.g., domestic hot water) or stored in an **insulated thermal storage tank** (typically water, with polyurethane foam insulation, U-value ≈ 0.5 W/m²·K).

**STEP 6 — Distribution and End Use**
Heat is delivered for **space heating, water heating, industrial process heat, or power generation** (via Rankine/Brayton cycle in concentrated solar power).

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

| Step | Physical Phenomenon | Engineering Design Choice |
|------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Radiative transport | Tilt angle = Latitude ± 15° for annual optimization |
| 2 | Greenhouse effect | Low-iron glass, AR coating, double-glazing for hot climates |
| 3 | Photothermal conversion | Selective coating (α/ε > 10) |
| 4 | Conduction + convection | Tube geometry, fin spacing, turbulent flow (Re > 2300) |
| 5 | Thermal storage | Sensible (water) or latent (paraffin PCM) |
| 6 | End-use matching | System sizing based on daily load (kWh/day) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{u} = A_{c} \cdot S \cdot \eta_{c}$ | Useful heat gain from collector | W |
| 2 | $\eta_{c} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha) - F_{R} U_{L} \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$ | **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q_{abs} = A_{c} \cdot S \cdot (\tau \alpha)$ | Absorbed solar energy | W |
| 4 | $Q_{loss} = A_{c} \cdot U_{L} \cdot (T_{pm} - T_{a})$ | Thermal loss to ambient | W |
| 5 | $T_{pm} = T_{in} + \dfrac{Q_{u}}{A_{c} F_{R} U_{L}}\left(1 - F_{R}\right)$ | Mean plate temperature | K |
| 6 | $E_{stored} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat storage | J |
| 7 | $E_{latent} = m \cdot L_{f}$ | Latent heat storage (PCM) | J |
| 8 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{c_{p}(T_{out} - T_{in})}$ | Required mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 9 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot \eta_{pump}}$ | Pumping power | W |
| 10 | $Hottel \ factor \ F_{R} = \dfrac{\dot{m} c_{p}}{A_{c} U_{L}}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A_{c} U_{L} F'}{\dot{m} c_{p}}\right)\right]$ | Heat removal factor | dimensionless |

Where:
- $A_{c}$ = collector area (m²)
- $S$ = incident solar irradiance (W/m²)
- $U_{L}$ = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K), typically 4–7 W/m²·K for flat plate
- $T_{in}, T_{out}, T_{a}$ = fluid inlet, outlet, and ambient temperatures (°C or K)
- $F_{R}$ = heat removal factor (0.7–0.9 typical)
- $\tau \alpha$ = transmittance-absorptance product (≈ 0.85)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Sector:** Solar water heaters in Kerala homes (especially post-KSEB solar rooftop subsidy).
- **Industrial Process Heat:** Dairy, textile, food processing industries (60–250 °C range).
- **Solar Cooling:** Absorption chillers (LiBr-H₂O) for air-conditioning.
- **Power Generation:** Parabolic trough, solar tower (400–1000 °C working temperatures).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-world Linkage:** Kerala's MNRE-subsidized solar water heating market is one of the highest in India per capita — direct application of this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Collector Efficiency Equation

This is the **most important derivation** for Module 2 and is a high-yield KTU question.

**Given:**
A flat-plate solar collector with area $A_{c}$ receives incident solar radiation $S$ (W/m²). The absorber plate has temperature $T_{pm}$, ambient air is at $T_{a}$, and the working fluid enters at $T_{in}$ and leaves at $T_{out}$.

**Assumption 1 — Energy Balance on the Absorber Plate**

The net absorbed energy equals useful heat gain plus all thermal losses to ambient.

$$Q_{abs} = Q_{u} + Q_{loss}$$

Substituting standard solar collector expressions:

$$A_{c} \cdot S \cdot (\tau \alpha) = Q_{u} + A_{c} \cdot U_{L} \cdot (T_{pm} - T_{a})$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $Q_{u}$:**

$$Q_{u} = A_{c} \cdot S \cdot (\tau \alpha) - A_{c} \cdot U_{L} \cdot (T_{pm} - T_{a})$$

**Assumption 2 — Heat Removal Factor $F_{R}$**

In real collectors, the fluid does not contact the entire plate uniformly. The **heat removal factor** $F_{R}$ corrects for this non-ideality, defined as the ratio of actual useful gain to the gain if the entire plate were at the fluid inlet temperature.

$$F_{R} = \dfrac{Q_{u,\text{actual}}}{Q_{u,\text{if plate at } T_{in}}}$$

Applying this correction:

$$Q_{u} = A_{c} \cdot F_{R} \cdot \left[ S \cdot (\tau \alpha) - U_{L} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{a}) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $A_{c} \cdot S$ to get efficiency $\eta_{c}$:**

$$\eta_{c} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{A_{c} \cdot S} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha) - F_{R} \cdot U_{L} \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$$

**Final Result — The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{c} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha) - F_{R} \cdot U_{L} \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- **Intercept (when $\dfrac{T_{in}-T_{a}}{S} = 0$):** $\eta_{c} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha)$ — the *maximum theoretical efficiency* (no losses).
- **Slope:** $-F_{R} U_{L}$ — the rate of efficiency drop as fluid/ambient temperature difference rises.
- The plot of $\eta_{c}$ vs $\dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$ is a **straight line** — universally tested in KTU labs.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Mass Flow Rate for a Given Load

**Given:** A solar flat-plate collector of area $A_{c} = 2 \text{ m}^2$ produces $Q_{u} = 1200$ W under peak sun. The fluid is water ($c_{p} = 4186$ J/kg·K). Required temperature rise $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = 20$ K.

**Energy balance on the fluid side:**

$$Q_{u} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $\dot{m}$:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{c_{p} \cdot \Delta T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{1200 \text{ W}}{4186 \text{ J/kg·K} \times 20 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{1200}{83720} = 0.01434 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m} \approx 5.16 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 51.6 \text{ L/hr}$$

This is the volumetric flow rate — directly used in KTU numerical problems for pump sizing.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Removal Factor $F_{R}$ from First Principles

For a tube-in-plate collector with $N$ tubes, fin efficiency $\eta_{f}$, and tube-to-fluid heat transfer coefficient $h_{fi}$:

$$F' = \dfrac{1/U_{L}}{W \left[\dfrac{1}{U_{L} (D + W - D \eta_{f})} + \dfrac{1}{C_{b}}\right]}$$

where:
- $W$ = tube spacing
- $D$ = tube outer diameter
- $C_{b} = h_{fi} \cdot \pi \cdot D_{i} \cdot N$

Then $F_{R}$ is related to $F'$ by:

$$F_{R} = \dfrac{\dot{m} c_{p}}{A_{c} U_{L}} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( - \dfrac{A_{c} U_{L} F'}{\dot{m} c_{p}} \right) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **final HWB equation** and the **definitions** of $F_{R}$, $U_{L}$, $\tau \alpha$. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying each term's physical meaning.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range (Flat Plate) | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------------------------|------------------|
| Transmittance-Absorptance product | $\tau \alpha$ | 0.80 – 0.92 | Optical efficiency ceiling |
| Heat loss coefficient | $U_{L}$ | 4 – 7 W/m²·K | Thermal insulation quality |
| Heat removal factor | $F_{R}$ | 0.7 – 0.95 | Fluid contact effectiveness |
| Efficiency | $\eta_{c}$ | 0.40 – 0.75 (operating) | Real-world performance |
| Stagnation temperature | $T_{stag}$ | 150 – 220 °C | Max no-flow temperature (safety design) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Active Closed-Loop Solar Thermal System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUN(["☀ SOLAR RADIATION S W/m²"])

    subgraph COLLECTION["COLLECTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        GL["GLAZING\nτ ≈ 0.91\nLow-iron Glass"]
        ABS["SELECTIVE ABSORBER\nα ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.05\nBlack Chrome / Cermet"]
        INS["INSULATION\nPolyurethane Foam\nU_ins ≈ 0.04 W/mK"]
        HX["RISER TUBES\nCopper / Aluminum\nTubes Carry Working Fluid"]
    end

    subgraph TRANSFER["HEAT TRANSFER & TRANSPORT SUBSYSTEM"]
        PUMP["CIRCULATION PUMP\nActive System\nη_pump ≈ 0.6"]
        VALVE["FLOW CONTROL VALVE\n+ Check Valve"]
        SENS["TEMPERATURE SENSOR\nT_in, T_out, T_tank"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["THERMAL STORAGE SUBSYSTEM"]
        TANK["INSULATED STORAGE TANK\nV = 100–500 L\nU_loss ≈ 0.5 W/m²K"]
        AUX["AUXILIARY HEATER\nElectric / Gas Backup"]
    end

    subgraph UTILIZATION["UTILIZATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        DHW["DOMESTIC HOT WATER\nT = 50–60 °C"]
        SPACE["SPACE HEATING\nRadiator / Underfloor"]
        PROC["INDUSTRIAL PROCESS HEAT\nT = 80–250 °C"]
    end

    SUN -->|"Beam + Diffuse"| GL
    GL -->|"Transmitted Spectrum"| ABS
    ABS -->|"Conduction"| HX
    INS -.->|"Reduces Edge Loss"| ABS
    HX -->|"Hot Fluid T_out ≈ 60-90 °C"| PUMP
    PUMP --> VALVE
    VALVE --> SENS
    SENS --> TANK
    TANK --> AUX
    AUX --> DHW
    TANK --> SPACE
    TANK --> PROC
    DHW -.->|"Cooled Return T_in"| PUMP
    SPACE -.->|"Return Loop"| PUMP
    PROC -.->|"Return Loop"| PUMP
```

### 4.2 Working Principle — Energy Flow Block Diagram (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["SOLAR ENERGY\nSOURCE\nS W/m²"] --> B1["COLLECTOR\nη_c"]
    B1 --> C1["USEFUL HEAT\nQ_u = η_c · A_c · S"]
    C1 --> D1["STORAGE\nE = m·cp·ΔT"]
    D1 --> E1["DISTRIBUTION\n+ End-Use Load"]
    E1 --> F1["LOSSES\nQ_loss to Ambient"]

    B1 -.->|"η_c = F_R(τα) - F_R·U_L·(T_in - T_a)/S"| B1
    C1 -.->|"60% of Input"| F1
```

### 4.3 Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUTS"]
        S1["S: Solar Irradiance W/m²"]
        T1["T_a: Ambient Temperature °C"]
        T2["T_in: Fluid Inlet Temperature °C"]
    end

    subgraph SYSTEM["SOLAR THERMAL SYSTEM"]
        SYS["PROCESS BLOCK\nη = F_R(τα) - F_R·U_L·(T_in - T_a)/S"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUTS"]
        Q1["Q_u: Useful Heat W"]
        Q2["Q_loss: Thermal Loss W"]
        T3["T_out: Fluid Outlet Temperature °C"]
    end

    S1 --> SYS
    T1 --> SYS
    T2 --> SYS
    SYS --> Q1
    SYS --> Q2
    SYS --> T3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Spec:** The block diagram must show **all four subsystems** (Collection → Transfer → Storage → Utilization) with **clear arrows indicating energy and fluid flow direction**. Reserve space for **control loops** (sensor → controller → pump/valve).

### 4.4 Physical Layout Reference (Text Schematic)

```
        NORTH SKY
           ↑
    ════════════════════  ← Glazing (Glass Cover)
    ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐  ← Riser Tubes (Carry Water)
    │  │ │  │ │  │ │  │
    ╞══╪═╪══╪═╪══╪═╪══╡  ← Absorber Plate (Selective Surface)
    └──────────────────┘
    ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  ← Insulation (Bottom + Sides)
    ════════════════════
           ↑ Tilt = Latitude ± 15°
    ┌──────────────────┐
    │   STORAGE TANK   │ ← Insulated (PUF)
    │   V = 100–500 L  │
    └──────────────────┘
           │
        [PUMP]
           │
        [LOAD] (Hot Water Tap / Radiator)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT637]**
*Define a solar thermal system. List its four main functional subsystems with one-line descriptions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **solar thermal system** is an engineered装置 that captures incident solar radiation and converts it into useful thermal energy for heating applications.

The four main functional subsystems are:

1. **Collection Subsystem** — Captures solar radiation using a glazed absorber plate (e.g., flat-plate or evacuated tube collector).
2. **Heat Transfer / Transport Subsystem** — Circulates the heated working fluid via pump (active) or thermosiphon (passive).
3. **Storage Subsystem** — Stores excess thermal energy in an insulated tank (sensible or latent storage).
4. **Utilization Subsystem** — Delivers heat to end-use loads: domestic hot water, space heating, industrial process heat, or power generation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT637]**
*State and explain the significance of the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation for a flat-plate solar collector.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) equation** expresses the instantaneous thermal efficiency of a flat-plate solar collector:

$$\eta_{c} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha) - F_{R} U_{L} \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$$

**Significance:**
- It is a **linear equation** in the parameter $\dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$, allowing efficiency to be plotted as a straight line for experimental validation.
- The **intercept** $F_{R}(\tau \alpha)$ represents the maximum optical efficiency (zero-loss condition).
- The **slope** $-F_{R} U_{L}$ quantifies the rate at which efficiency drops as the operating temperature rises above ambient — critical for **system sizing and collector selection** in Kerala's tropical climate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each question contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks — KTU 2024 ESE pattern.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw a neat block diagram of an **active closed-loop solar water heating system**. Label all components and explain the working principle with energy flow at each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flat-plate solar collector has the following specifications:
- Area $A_{c} = 3 \text{ m}^2$
- Heat removal factor $F_{R} = 0.82$
- Transmittance-absorptance product $\tau \alpha = 0.88$
- Overall heat loss coefficient $U_{L} = 5.2$ W/m²·K
- Incident solar irradiance $S = 850$ W/m²
- Ambient temperature $T_{a} = 30$ °C
- Fluid inlet temperature $T_{in} = 50$ °C

Calculate:
1. The collector efficiency $\eta_{c}$ using the HWB equation.
2. The useful heat gain $Q_{u}$ in Watts.
3. The required mass flow rate of water for a 25 K temperature rise ($c_{p} = 4186$ J/kg·K).

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Working Principle [7 Marks]

**[1 Mark] Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for reference)

**Components to be labeled (1 Mark each, total 4 Marks):**
1. Solar collector (glazing + absorber + insulation)
2. Circulation pump + control valve
3. Insulated storage tank with backup heater
4. Distribution network (DHW / space heating)

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
- Solar radiation $S$ (W/m²) is incident on the **glazing**, which transmits ~91% of the spectrum.
- The **selective absorber plate** ($\alpha = 0.95$, $\varepsilon = 0.05$) converts photon energy into heat.
- Heat is conducted through tube walls and convected into the **working fluid** (water/glycol mix).
- The **pump** circulates hot fluid to the **storage tank**, where it transfers heat to stored water via a heat exchanger.
- **Backup heater** activates during cloudy days; **sensors** maintain temperature setpoints via the control loop.
- End-use: hot water is drawn at the load tap, with cold return water re-entering the collector loop.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $A_{c} = 3 \text{ m}^2$, $F_{R} = 0.82$, $\tau \alpha = 0.88$, $U_{L} = 5.2$ W/m²·K, $S = 850$ W/m², $T_{a} = 30$ °C, $T_{in} = 50$ °C, $c_{p} = 4186$ J/kg·K, $\Delta T = 25$ K.

**Step 1 — Apply HWB equation [3 Marks]:**

$$\eta_{c} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha) - F_{R} \cdot U_{L} \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$$

$$\eta_{c} = 0.82 \times 0.88 - 0.82 \times 5.2 \times \dfrac{50 - 30}{850}$$

$$\eta_{c} = 0.7216 - 0.82 \times 5.2 \times 0.02353$$

$$\eta_{c} = 0.7216 - 4.264 \times 0.02353$$

$$\eta_{c} = 0.7216 - 0.1003 = 0.6213$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{c} \approx 62.13\%}$$

**Step 2 — Useful heat gain [2 Marks]:**

$$Q_{u} = \eta_{c} \cdot A_{c} \cdot S = 0.6213 \times 3 \times 850$$

$$Q_{u} = 1584.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.584 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate [2 Marks]:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{c_{p} \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{1584.3}{4186 \times 25}$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{1584.3}{104650} = 0.01514 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 0.0151 \text{ kg/s} \approx 54.5 \text{ L/hr}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of a passive thermosiphon solar water heater**. Compare it with an active system in terms of pumping power, cost, and reliability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solar flat-plate collector is tested under standard conditions and the following data is obtained:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Collector area $A_{c}$ | 2.5 m² |
| Solar irradiance $S$ | 920 W/m² |
| Fluid inlet $T_{in}$ | 40 °C |
| Ambient $T_{a}$ | 28 °C |
| Useful heat $Q_{u}$ | 1650 W |

**Calculate:**
1. Collector efficiency $\eta_{c}$.
2. If $\tau \alpha = 0.86$ and $F_{R} = 0.80$, determine the overall heat loss coefficient $U_{L}$.
3. Comment on how $U_{L}$ can be reduced in practice.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Thermosiphon Working Principle [7 Marks]

**Principle (3 Marks):**
A **thermosiphon system** uses the natural buoyancy of heated water to drive circulation — no pump is required. As water in the collector tube heats, its density decreases, causing it to rise into the storage tank (placed **above** the collector). Cold, denser water from the tank descends to replace it, establishing a **natural convection loop**.

**Schematic (2 Marks):**
```
        ┌─────────────┐
        │  STORAGE    │  ← Above Collector Level
        │  TANK       │
        └──────┬──────┘
               │ Cold Downflow
               ↓
        ┌─────────────┐
        │  COLLECTOR  │  ← Below Tank
        └──────┬──────┘
               ↑ Hot Upflow (Risers)
```

**Comparison (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Passive (Thermosiphon) | Active (Pumped) |
|---------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Pumping power | **Zero** (natural convection) | 50–200 W |
| Initial cost | Lower (no pump) | Higher |
| Reliability | **High** (no moving parts) | Moderate (pump failure risk) |
| Tank placement | Must be **above** collector | Anywhere |
| Flow control | Limited | Precise (sensors + controller) |
| Best suited for | Domestic, small loads | Large, multi-load systems |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $A_{c} = 2.5 \text{ m}^2$, $S = 920$ W/m², $T_{in} = 40$ °C, $T_{a} = 28$ °C, $Q_{u} = 1650$ W, $\tau \alpha = 0.86$, $F_{R} = 0.80$.

**Step 1 — Collector efficiency [2 Marks]:**

$$\eta_{c} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{A_{c} \cdot S} = \dfrac{1650}{2.5 \times 920} = \dfrac{1650}{2300}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{c} = 0.7174 \approx 71.74\%}$$

**Step 2 — Determine $U_{L}$ from HWB equation [3 Marks]:**

$$\eta_{c} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha) - F_{R} \cdot U_{L} \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$$

$$0.7174 = 0.80 \times 0.86 - 0.80 \times U_{L} \times \dfrac{40 - 28}{920}$$

$$0.7174 = 0.688 - 0.80 \times U_{L} \times 0.01304$$

$$0.7174 - 0.688 = -0.01043 \cdot U_{L}$$

$$0.0294 = -0.01043 \cdot U_{L}$$

$$U_{L} = -\dfrac{0.0294}{0.01043} = -2.82 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Physical sanity check:** The negative sign indicates an inconsistency in the test data (efficiency should not exceed $F_{R} \tau \alpha = 0.688$). This teaches students that **real test data must lie below the intercept line** on the HWB plot. In a corrected test, $Q_{u}$ would be measured as ≤ 1582 W.

**Step 3 — Practical methods to reduce $U_{L}$ [2 Marks]:**

1. **Double-glazing** the collector (reduces top convective loss by ~40%).
2. **Increasing insulation thickness** at the back and sides (PUF, 50–100 mm).
3. Using **low-emissivity selective coatings** (ε ↓ → radiation loss ↓).
4. **Evacuated tube collectors** (eliminates convective loss entirely, $U_{L} \approx 1.5$ W/m²·K).
5. Reducing **edge losses** with proper frame sealing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Always write W, W/m², kg/s, or °C explicitly. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$** in the HWB equation — HWB uses **inlet** temperature, not outlet. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Not drawing arrows on block diagram** — Energy flow direction MUST be shown with labeled arrows. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Missing the storage subsystem** in the block diagram — A solar thermal system is incomplete without storage. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Substituting °C instead of K** for temperature differences — ΔT in K = ΔT in °C, but absolute temperatures in Stefan-Boltzmann equations must be in **K**. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the energy balance derivation** — Always start with $Q_{abs} = Q_{u} + Q_{loss}$ in the HWB derivation. (–2 marks)
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the auxiliary heater** in practical system diagrams — Examiners expect real-world components. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **solar thermal system** converts sunlight into heat for direct use or storage.
- The **four functional subsystems** are: Collection, Transfer, Storage, Utilization.
- The **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** is the cornerstone formula:

$$\eta_{c} = F_{R}(\tau \alpha) - F_{R} U_{L} \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$$

- **Solar constant** $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²; Kerala average insolation ≈ 4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day.
- **Selective coating** property: high $\alpha$ (≈ 0.95) for solar spectrum, low $\varepsilon$ (≈ 0.05) for IR.
- **Active systems** use a pump; **passive (thermosiphon)** systems use natural convection.
- **Useful heat** $Q_{u} = \eta_{c} \cdot A_{c} \cdot S$.
- **Mass flow rate** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{c_{p} \Delta T}$.
- **Storage** can be sensible (water, $E = m c_{p} \Delta T$) or latent (PCM, $E = m L_{f}$).
- **Block diagram** must show **energy flow arrows** and **fluid return loops**.
- **Efficiency is linear** in $\dfrac{T_{in} - T_{a}}{S}$ — straight-line plot is the standard test method.
- **Typical $U_{L}$** for flat plate = 4–7 W/m²·K; for evacuated tube ≈ 1.5 W/m²·K.
- **Tilt angle** for collector in Kerala ≈ 10°–15° (latitude ≈ 10°N, optimized for annual yield).
- Always specify **working fluid**: water (domestic), glycol-water mix (cold climates), therminol (industrial).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Photovoltaic System - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Photovoltaic System - Working Principle - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** is a solid-state, semiconductor-based renewable energy conversion assembly that directly transforms incident solar irradiance (photons) into direct current (DC) electricity through the **Photovoltaic Effect** — a quantum-mechanical phenomenon first observed by Edmond Becquerel (1839) and theoretically explained by Albert Einstein (1905) using the concept of the photoelectric effect.

The fundamental building block is the **solar cell** (typically a crystalline silicon P-N junction diode of area ~156 mm × 156 mm), which when grouped into **modules**, **strings**, and **arrays**, becomes a usable power-generating unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The photovoltaic (PV) effect is the *only* direct sunlight-to-electricity conversion method. It is distinct from **solar thermal** systems, which use sunlight to produce heat that drives a turbine. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction in Module 2.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaf and Tree" Intuition
Imagine a green leaf on a tree. The chlorophyll inside the leaf absorbs sunlight and uses that energy to perform a chemical "split" — splitting water and CO₂ to produce sugar (stored chemical energy).

A **solar cell behaves identically**:
- **Sunlight** = the energy source (instead of being absorbed by chlorophyll, photons strike silicon atoms).
- **Silicon atoms** = the "chlorophyll" — their electrons get excited and jump from the **valence band** to the **conduction band**.
- **P-N junction** = the "engine of the leaf" — its built-in electric field separates the excited electrons (negative) from the holes (positive), forcing them to flow through an external wire, producing **current**.
- **The wire + bulb** = the "tree's vascular system" that transports the energy (electricity) where it is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants (Standard KTU Values):**
> - Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Elementary charge: **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - Band-gap energy of crystalline Silicon at 300 K: **Eg(Si) ≈ 1.12 eV**
> - Band-gap energy of Gallium Arsenide at 300 K: **Eg(GaAs) ≈ 1.42 eV**
> - Standard Test Conditions (STC) for rating solar panels: **Irradiance G = 1000 W/m², Cell temp T = 25 °C, Air Mass AM = 1.5**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Working Principle of a Solar PV Cell

The conversion of sunlight into electricity occurs in four sequential physical stages inside the semiconductor:

**Stage 1 — Photon Absorption**
A photon of frequency *f* strikes the P-N junction. Its energy is given by the Planck-Einstein relation:

$$E_{photon} = h \cdot f = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$$

The photon is **absorbed** *only if* its energy is greater than or equal to the band-gap energy of the semiconductor material:

$$E_{photon} \geq E_g$$

If $E_{photon} < E_g$, the photon passes straight through (transmission loss). If $E_{photon} \gg E_g$, the excess energy is wasted as lattice heat (thermalization loss).

**Stage 2 — Electron-Hole Pair (EHP) Generation**
An absorbed photon transfers its energy to a valence-band electron, knocking it free and leaving behind a "hole." This creates one **electron-hole pair**. The rate of EHP generation per unit volume is denoted by $G(L)$ and depends on the absorption coefficient $\alpha$ and the depth *x* from the illuminated surface.

**Stage 3 — Charge Separation by the Junction Field**
The depletion region of the P-N junction possesses a built-in electric field $E_0$. This field immediately sweeps:
- Electrons → toward the **N-side** (negative terminal)
- Holes → toward the **P-side** (positive terminal)

**Stage 4 — Current Extraction**
The separated charges flow through the external load, producing a **direct current (DC)** proportional to the incident irradiance *G*.

### The Six-Block Functional Architecture of a Complete PV System

| Block # | Component | Engineering Function |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **PV Array** | Converts sunlight → DC electricity (multiple modules in series/parallel) |
| 2 | **DC-DC Converter with MPPT** | Tracks the Maximum Power Point to optimize power extraction under varying irradiance/temperature |
| 3 | **Battery Bank (Off-grid only)** | Stores excess DC energy as chemical energy for night/cloudy hours |
| 4 | **Charge Controller** | Prevents battery over-charge / deep-discharge |
| 5 | **Power Inverter (DC→AC)** | Converts DC to AC (single-phase 230 V / three-phase 415 V, 50 Hz for India) |
| 6 | **AC Load / Grid** | End user / utility interconnection point |

> [!NOTE]
> **On-Grid vs Off-Grid Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Off-grid (Stand-alone)** → Includes Battery + Charge Controller
> - **On-grid (Grid-tied)** → No battery; uses Net-Metering and requires anti-islanding protection
> - **Hybrid** → Includes battery + grid connectivity

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h \cdot f = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$ | Joules (J) or eV | $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J |
| 2 | Band-gap Energy Relation | $E_g = q \cdot V_{oc}$ | eV or Volts | Open-circuit voltage is limited by Eg |
| 3 | Maximum Power | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Watts (W) | At the knee of the I-V curve |
| 4 | Fill Factor (FF) | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless (0 < FF < 1) | Ideal Si cell FF ≈ 0.80–0.85 |
| 5 | Cell Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$ | Percentage (%) | $G$ = irradiance, $A$ = cell area |
| 6 | Module Series-Parallel | $V_{T} = \sum V_i$, $I_{T} = \sum I_j$ | V, A | Series adds V; Parallel adds I |
| 7 | Temperature Effect on Voc | $V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + \beta \cdot (T - 25)$ | Volts | $\beta \approx -0.3$ to $-0.4$ %/°C for Si |
| 8 | Irradiance Effect on Isc | $I_{sc}(G) = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{1000}$ | Amps | Linear with G |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
Solar PV systems power everything from **1 W calculator cells** to **multi-MW solar farms** (e.g., Bhadla Solar Park, Rajasthan — 2.25 GW). They are the fastest-growing renewable energy source globally and form the backbone of India's **National Solar Mission** target of 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Threshold Wavelength for Photovoltaic Absorption

**Given:** A silicon solar cell has a band-gap energy $E_g = 1.12$ eV. Find the **maximum wavelength** of incident sunlight that can generate an electron-hole pair.

**Step 1 — Convert band-gap energy from eV to Joules.**

$$E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}$$

$$E_g = 1.7942 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

*Logic:* The SI unit of energy is the Joule, so we apply the standard eV→J conversion factor.

**Step 2 — Equate photon energy to band-gap at the threshold condition.**

At the threshold, the photon has *just enough* energy to lift an electron across the band gap. So:

$$E_{photon} = E_g \implies \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda_{th}} = E_g$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for the threshold wavelength $\lambda_{th}$.**

$$\lambda_{th} = \frac{h \cdot c}{E_g}$$

*Logic:* Wavelength is inversely proportional to energy; longer wavelengths (red/infrared) carry less energy and may not be absorbed.

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\lambda_{th} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}) \times (2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s})}{1.7942 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator and denominator separately.**

Numerator:

$$h \cdot c = 6.626 \times 2.998 \times 10^{-34+8} = 19.865 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9865 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$

Denominator:

$$E_g = 1.7942 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**

$$\lambda_{th} = \frac{1.9865 \times 10^{-25}}{1.7942 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.107 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{th} \approx 1107 \text{ nm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Wavelengths longer than 1107 nm (i.e., far-infrared and beyond) **pass through** a silicon cell without generating electricity. This is why silicon is a poor absorber of far-IR light, and why multi-junction cells (GaAs, InGaP) are used for higher efficiency in space applications.

---

### Derivation 2 — Solar Cell Conversion Efficiency

**Given:** A PV module has $V_{oc} = 45$ V, $I_{sc} = 5$ A, $FF = 0.78$. The module area is $A = 1.6 \text{ m}^2$ and operates at STC ($G = 1000$ W/m²). Calculate the conversion efficiency.

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum power output using the Fill Factor definition.**

From the formula sheet: $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$

Rearranging:

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

*Logic:* The Fill Factor is the ratio of the actual maximum rectangle (Vmp × Imp) under the I-V curve to the ideal rectangle (Voc × Isc). It captures how "square" the I-V curve is.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$P_{max} = 45 \text{ V} \times 5 \text{ A} \times 0.78$$

$$P_{max} = 225 \times 0.78 = 175.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the incident solar power on the module.**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2 \times 1.6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{in} = 1600 \text{ W}$$

*Logic:* The total solar energy striking the module surface equals irradiance × area (STC standard).

**Step 4 — Apply the efficiency formula.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{175.5}{1600}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 0.1097 \text{ or } 10.97\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Commercial crystalline-Si modules typically achieve **η = 15%–22%**. Theoretical Shockley-Queisser limit for a single-junction Si cell is **~33%**. Multi-junction cells in labs exceed **47%**.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Comparison of PV Technologies (for KTU Viva/Exam)

| Parameter | Mono-crystalline Si | Poly-crystalline Si | Thin-Film (CdTe) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Efficiency | 18%–22% | 15%–18% | 10%–13% |
| Cost (₹/W) | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Temperature Coefficient | -0.3 %/°C | -0.4 %/°C | -0.25 %/°C |
| Appearance | Uniform black | Blue speckled | Uniform dark |
| Lifespan | 25–30 yrs | 25 yrs | 20 yrs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Block Diagram of a Grid-Tied Solar PV System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[SUN] -->|Photons| B1[PV ARRAY\nMultiple Modules in Series-Parallel]
    B1 -->|DC Voltage Vdc| C1[JUNCTION BOX\nwith String Fuses and SPD]
    C1 --> D1[DC-DC MPPT CHARGE CONTROLLER\nBoosts Voltage and Tracks MPP]
    D1 --> E1[DC BUS Vdc link]
    E1 --> F1[POWER INVERTER\nDC to AC 50 Hz Conversion]
    F1 -->|230 V Single Phase or 415 V 3 Phase| G1[AC DISTRIBUTION PANEL\nMCB and ELCB Protection]
    G1 --> H1[AC LOAD\nLights Fans Motors]
    G1 --> I1[BI-DIRECTIONAL METER\nNet Metering]
    I1 --> J1[UTILITY GRID]
    F1 -->|Anti-Islanding Relay| J1
```

### Diagram 2 — Block Diagram of an Off-Grid (Stand-Alone) Solar PV System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[SUN] -->|Photons| B2[PV ARRAY]
    B2 --> C2[CHARGE CONTROLLER WITH MPPT]
    C2 -->|Charging Current| D2[BATTERY BANK\nDeep Cycle Lead Acid or Lithium Ion]
    C2 --> E2[DC LOAD\nLED Lights and DC Fan]
    D2 --> F2[DC-AC INVERTER]
    F2 --> G2[AC LOAD]
    D2 -.->|Battery Management BMS| D2
```

### Diagram 3 — Internal Structure of a Single Solar Cell (Cross-Sectional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Top[Anti-Reflection Coating ARC] --> Top2[Top Contact Finger Grid]
    Top2 --> P[P-type Emitter Layer\nHeavily Doped Boron]
    P --> J[P-N Junction Depletion Region\nBuilt-in Electric Field]
    J --> N[N-type Base Layer\nLightly Doped Phosphorus]
    N --> Bck[Back Surface Field BSF]
    Bck --> BC[Bottom Contact Rear Metal]
    PV1((Photon 1 absorbed)) --> P
    PV2((Photon 2 absorbed)) --> J
    PV3((Photon 3 absorbed)) --> N
    PV1 -.->|EHP Generated| J
    PV2 -.->|EHP Generated| J
    PV3 -.->|EHP Generated| J
    J -->|Electrons pushed| N
    J -->|Holes pushed| P
```

### Diagram 4 — I-V and P-V Characteristic Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Irradiance Axis W/m2] --> Curve1
    Y[Current I in Amps] --> Curve1
    Curve1[Typical I-V Curve of a Solar Cell] --> MPP((Maximum Power Point Vmp Imp))
    Curve1 --> ISC[Short Circuit Current Point at V 0]
    Curve1 --> VOC[Open Circuit Voltage Point at I 0]
    Note1>Voc decreases as Temperature rises] -.-> Curve1
    Note2>Isc increases linearly with Irradiance] -.-> Curve1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Block Diagrams:** Each block represents a **functional stage** in the energy-conversion chain. The arrows show the *direction of power flow* (not signal flow). In a KTU exam, when asked to "draw the block diagram," you must label every block, show direction, and identify which blocks are **optional** (e.g., battery in grid-tied systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the photovoltaic effect. Mention any two semiconductor materials used in solar cells.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **photovoltaic effect** is the physical process by which a semiconductor material generates a voltage and current upon exposure to light (photons). When photons with energy greater than the band-gap energy strike a P-N junction, they create electron-hole pairs, which are separated by the junction's built-in electric field, producing DC electricity. (2 marks)

Two common semiconductor materials used: (½ + ½ marks)
1. **Crystalline Silicon (c-Si)** — most widely used, Eg ≈ 1.12 eV.
2. **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** — used in space and high-efficiency cells, Eg ≈ 1.42 eV.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)? Why is it necessary in a PV system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**MPPT** is an electronic algorithm (commonly Perturb & Observe or Incremental Conductance) implemented in the DC-DC converter that continuously adjusts the operating voltage of the PV array to extract the **maximum possible power** under varying irradiance and temperature conditions. (2 marks)

**Necessity:** The MPP shifts dynamically with changes in sunlight, temperature, and load. Without MPPT, the array may operate far below its true potential — for instance, a 100 W panel could deliver only 60–70 W. MPPT improves energy yield by **20%–30%**. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each: **(a)** and **(b)**. Choice is *between* Question A and Question B (i.e., answer either A or B in full).

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **grid-connected solar PV system**. List the major components and state the function of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solar module has the following specifications at STC:
