$V_{oc} = 44 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 8.5 \text{ A}$, $V_{mp} = 36 \text{ V}$, $I_{mp} = 7.8 \text{ A}$, module area $= 1.7 \text{ m}^2$.
Calculate: (i) Fill Factor, (ii) Maximum power, (iii) Conversion efficiency.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Block | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | PV Array | Converts sunlight to DC; multiple series-connected modules raise voltage to ~300–600 V DC. |
| 2 | DC Combiner / Junction Box | Combines strings; houses DC fuses and Surge Protection Devices (SPDs). |
| 3 | DC-DC Converter + MPPT | Boosts Vdc and operates array at MPP via Perturb & Observe algorithm. |
| 4 | Inverter (String/Central) | Converts DC to grid-synchronized AC (230 V/415 V, 50 Hz). |
| 5 | AC Distribution Panel | Routes power to local loads; contains MCB, ELCB, isolators. |
| 6 | Net Meter + Grid | Bi-directional meter for export-import billing as per MNRE regulations. |

Key safety feature: **Anti-islanding protection** — the inverter must shut down within 0.1–2 seconds during a grid outage to protect line workers. (1 mark)

Block diagram — see Diagram 1 in SECTION_4 above. (Full marks require labeled, directional arrows.)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Fill Factor (2 marks):**

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{36 \times 7.8}{44 \times 8.5} = \frac{280.8}{374} = 0.7508$$

$$\boxed{FF \approx 0.75}$$

**(ii) Maximum Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 36 \times 7.8 = 280.8 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Conversion Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 1.7 = 1700 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{280.8}{1700} = 0.1652$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 16.52\%}$$

This is consistent with commercial poly-crystalline silicon modules. (Conclusion remark — ½ mark)

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **photovoltaic effect** with the help of a P-N junction diagram. Discuss the role of the depletion region in charge separation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A rooftop PV system has **20 modules**, each rated 330 W. The inverter efficiency is 96% and the system's overall DC cable + transformer loss is 4%. Calculate the **annual energy generation** in kWh if the location receives an average of **5 peak sun hours/day** for 365 days.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The **photovoltaic effect** (discovered by Becquerel 1839, Nobel to Einstein 1921 for related work) is the generation of a potential difference across a semiconductor P-N junction upon photon illumination. (1 mark)

**Working Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Photon Absorption:** Sunlight (E = hf) strikes the P-N junction. If hf ≥ Eg, the photon is absorbed and an electron from the valence band gains enough energy to jump to the conduction band, leaving behind a hole. This creates an **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.
2. **Depletion Region Role:** The P-N junction contains immobile acceptor (+) and donor (-) ions, producing a built-in electric field $E_0$ from N-side to P-side. This field acts as the "engine" of the solar cell.
3. **Charge Separation:** The field immediately sweeps electrons toward the N-region and holes toward the P-region. Without this field, the EHPs would simply recombine (luminescent or heat loss).
4. **Current Extraction:** The separated charges accumulate on external metal contacts, and when a load is connected, electrons flow from N → external circuit → P, producing **DC current**.

**Diagram — see Diagram 3 in SECTION 4** showing ARC, P-layer, junction, N-layer, and back contact. (1 mark for any labeled P-N sketch)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Total DC array power (1 mark):**

$$P_{DC,total} = 20 \times 330 = 6600 \text{ W} = 6.6 \text{ kWp}$$

**Step 2 — Apply inverter efficiency (1 mark):**

$$P_{AC,after\ inverter} = 6600 \times 0.96 = 6336 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Apply balance-of-system losses (1 mark):**

$$P_{AC,at\ load} = 6336 \times (1 - 0.04) = 6336 \times 0.96 = 6082.56 \text{ W} \approx 6.08 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Daily energy at load (1 mark):**

$$E_{daily} = P_{AC,at\ load} \times \text{Peak Sun Hours} = 6.08 \times 5 = 30.4 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy (2 marks):**

$$E_{annual} = 30.4 \times 365 = 11096 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 11100 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

**Verification:** A 6.6 kWp system in India typically generates 1100–1300 kWh/kWp/year, so 6.6 × 1100 = 7260 kWh (without losses). With 96% × 96% ≈ 92% overall derate, 6600 × 0.92 × 5 × 365/1000 ≈ 11,082 kWh ✓ Consistent. (1 mark for sanity check)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Fill Factor (FF) with Efficiency (η)** — FF is dimensionless (0–1), η is in % (0–100). Mixing them = **2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "A" (area) in efficiency formula** — writing $\eta = P_{max}/1000$ instead of $\eta = P_{max}/(G \cdot A)$ = **1 mark lost**.
> 3. **Block diagram arrows without labels** — unlabeled directional arrows = **½–1 mark deducted** in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 4. **No anti-islanding mention in grid-tied questions** — examiners specifically allocate **1 mark** for this safety feature.
> 5. **Wrong unit conversion (eV ↔ Joules)** — when calculating threshold wavelength, omitting the $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ factor = **1 mark lost**.
> 6. **Peak Sun Hours confusion** — students multiply peak sun hours by 24 incorrectly. PSH is *equivalent* hours of 1000 W/m² irradiance, not actual clock hours.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Photovoltaic effect** = direct sunlight → DC electricity via semiconductor P-N junction. Discovered 1839, explained 1905.
- ⚡ **Planck-Einstein equation:** $E = h \cdot f = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$ — foundation of all PV physics.
- ⚡ **Band-gap energy** decides which photons get absorbed. Silicon Eg = 1.12 eV → threshold wavelength ~1107 nm.
- ⚡ **Fill Factor (FF)** quantifies the "squareness" of the I-V curve. Higher FF = better cell.
- ⚡ **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$ — must use cell/module area.
- ⚡ **MPPT** is mandatory in modern PV systems to extract maximum power under variable irradiance.
- ⚡ **Six key blocks:** PV Array → MPPT → DC Bus → Inverter → AC Panel → Load/Grid (+ optional battery for off-grid).
- ⚡ **Grid-tied systems** require **anti-islanding protection** — a must-mention in any KTU answer.
- ⚡ **Series connection** adds voltages; **parallel connection** adds currents — never confuse them.
- ⚡ **Voc decreases** with rising temperature (~-0.3 %/°C); **Isc increases** linearly with irradiance.
- ⚡ **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** 1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5 — quote this whenever stating panel ratings.
- ⚡ For 7-mark sub-parts in Part B, **always include a labeled diagram** (block diagram or P-N junction sketch).
- ⚡ Final numerical answers should be boxed or underlined as per KTU valuation conventions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Cell Efficiency Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Cell Efficiency Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Cell Efficiency ($\eta$)** is formally defined as the ratio of the maximum electrical power output generated by a photovoltaic (PV) device to the incident solar power (radiant energy) striking its active surface area, expressed as a percentage under Standard Test Conditions (STC) of **AM1.5G solar spectrum**, irradiance of **1000 W/m²**, and cell temperature of **25 °C (298.15 K)**.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In board examinations, students MUST explicitly state "Standard Test Conditions (STC): 1000 W/m², AM1.5G, 25 °C" while answering any solar efficiency numerical. Failure to state the conditions is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction** per KTU valuation key.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a bucket** placed under a waterfall:
- The **total waterfall** represents the total incident solar energy ($P_{in}$).
- The **bucket** represents the solar cell capturing photons.
- The **water actually collected in the bucket** represents usable electrical power ($P_{max}$).
- The **splashed-out water** represents every form of energy loss (reflection, heat, recombination).

**Efficiency is essentially how much of the waterfall's energy you successfully trapped in the bucket compared to what fell on it.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **$1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| Elementary Charge | $q$ | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| Thermal Voltage @ 25 °C | $kT/q$ | **0.0259 V** |
| STC Irradiance | $G$ | **1000 W/m²** |
| Solar Constant | $I_{sc}$ | **1361 W/m²** |
| Reference Temperature | $T$ | **298.15 K** |

> [!NOTE]
> For monocrystalline silicon, the practical lab efficiency ceiling is roughly **24–26%**, while the theoretical **Shockley-Queisser limit** for a single-junction cell is **~33.7%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Solar Cell Efficiency is the Most Tracked PV Metric

In a competitive renewable energy market, **two identical rooftop solar panels can produce dramatically different annual energy** purely based on efficiency. Higher efficiency means:
- **More power per square meter** (critical for space-constrained rooftops in Kerala urban homes).
- **Lower Balance of System (BOS) cost** per watt.
- **Faster payback period**.

### 2.2 The Five-Step Logical Flow of Efficiency Calculation

1. **Capture Stage:** Solar irradiance $G$ strikes the cell surface area $A$. Total incident power $P_{in} = G \cdot A$.
2. **Generation Stage:** Photons above the bandgap generate electron-hole pairs. This produces short-circuit current $I_{sc}$.
3. **Voltage Build-up Stage:** Charge separation at the p-n junction builds an open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$.
4. **Power Extraction Stage:** Under real load, only a fraction (Fill Factor) of $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ is extractable as maximum power $P_{max}$.
5. **Efficiency Computation:** $\eta = P_{max} / P_{in}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A} \times 100\%$ | Master efficiency equation | % |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (shape of I-V curve) | Dimensionless (0 < FF < 1) |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_o} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage from diode eq. | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ (for ideal cell) | Short-circuit current ≈ Photocurrent | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $FF_o = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ | Empirical FF (Green's approx.) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_{oc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$ | Normalized open-circuit voltage | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Maximum power point | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use the pipe symbol `|` inside any table cell** — use `\vert` in LaTeX expressions. The KTU valuation script renders markdown tables, and stray pipes break cell boundaries.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Module Manufacturing:** Used by companies like Vikram Solar (India) and LONGi to bin cells by efficiency grade.
- **Banking/Finance:** Used by SBI and KSEB for computing tariff and plant sizing.
- **Rooftop Design:** Engineers in Kerala use efficiency to determine **panel count** for a target kW system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Incident Power to Efficiency

**Given:**
- Solar irradiance $G = 1000$ W/m²
- Cell area $A = 0.015$ m² (15 cm × 10 cm lab cell)
- Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V
- Short-circuit current $I_{sc} = 3.2$ A
- Fill Factor $FF = 0.78$

**Step 1 — Compute Total Incident Solar Power**

The total radiant power incident on the cell is the product of irradiance and the geometric (active) area:

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A$$

Substituting the given values:

$$P_{in} = 1000 \; \text{W/m}^2 \times 0.015 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$P_{in} = 15 \; \text{W}$$

This represents the **upper bound** of power available — no cell, however perfect, can exceed this.

**Step 2 — Compute the Open-Circuit × Short-Circuit Product (Geometric Power)**

The product $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ is the power if the cell behaved as a perfect rectangular I-V source (which it does not, due to the curve's rounded "knee").

$$V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.65 \; \text{V} \times 3.2 \; \text{A}$$

$$V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 2.08 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Fill Factor to Obtain Maximum Power**

The Fill Factor corrects for the rounded I-V curve, representing how "square" the cell's electrical behavior is:

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

$$P_{max} = 2.08 \; \text{W} \times 0.78$$

$$P_{max} = 1.6224 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Cell Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{1.6224}{15} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = 10.816\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A ~10.8% efficiency is typical for **amorphous silicon (a-Si) thin-film** cells. Monocrystalline cells of similar area would yield **18–22%**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sources of Loss in Solar Cells

| Loss Mechanism | Approx. Loss | Type | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reflection** at top surface | 2–4% | Optical | Anti-Reflective Coating (ARC) |
| **Transmission** (below bandgap photons) | ~23% | Spectral | Multi-junction / bandgap tuning |
| **Thermalization** (above bandgap) | ~33% | Spectral | Tandem cells |
| **Recombination** (radiative + non-radiative) | ~1–10% | Electrical | Passivation layers |
| **Series Resistance** ($R_s$) | ~1–3% | Electrical | Thicker fingers, better contacts |
| **Shunt Resistance** ($R_{sh}$) | ~1–5% | Electrical | Edge isolation, clean junction |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage from Diode Equation

Starting from the single-diode equivalent circuit model:

$$I = I_L - I_o \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q V}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right]$$

At open circuit, $I = 0$:

$$0 = I_L - I_o \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right]$$

Rearranging:

$$I_L = I_o \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right]$$

Assuming $I_L \gg I_o$ (typical for silicon):

$$\frac{I_L}{I_o} + 1 \approx \frac{I_L}{I_o}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_o} \right) = \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_o} + 1 \right)$$

This is the **fundamental open-circuit voltage expression** validated by KTU examiners across every module-2 question bank.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **logical flow of solar cell efficiency calculation** — from photon incidence to final percentage output. Note: all node IDs are alphanumeric and all labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SUN_SOURCE] --> A2[G_IRRADIANCE_W_M2]
    A2 --> A3[A_CELL_AREA_M2]
    A3 --> B1[PIN_INCIDENT_POWER_W]
    B1 --> B2[G_TIMES_A]
    A2 --> C1[IS_LIGHT_GENERATED]
    A3 --> C2[JUNCTION_PN]
    C1 --> C3[ISC_SHORT_CIRCUIT_CURRENT]
    C2 --> D1[CHARGE_SEPARATION]
    D1 --> D2[VOC_OPEN_CIRCUIT_VOLTAGE]
    C3 --> E1[VOC_TIMES_ISC]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[FILL_FACTOR_FF]
    E2 --> F1[PMAX_MAXIMUM_POWER]
    B1 --> G1[EFFICIENCY_NU]
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> G2[NU_PERCENT_OUTPUT]

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Loss Pathway Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[PIN_INCIDENT] --> P1[REFLECTION_LOSS]
    P1 --> P2[TRANSMISSION_LOSS]
    P2 --> P3[THERMALIZATION_LOSS]
    P3 --> P4[RECOMBINATION_LOSS]
    P4 --> P5[SERIES_RS_LOSS]
    P5 --> P6[SHUNT_RSH_LOSS]
    P6 --> PEND[PMAX_USEFUL_ELECTRICAL_POWER]

    style P0 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PEND fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid I-V Curve Architecture (Maximum Power Point Location)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[VOC_AXIS_INTERCEPT] --> Q2[I_V_CURVE_BEND_REGION]
    Q2 --> Q3[MPP_MAX_POWER_POINT]
    Q3 --> Q4[ISC_AXIS_INTERCEPT]
    Q4 --> Q5[RECTANGLE_UNDER_CURVE]
    Q5 --> Q1
    Q5 --> Q3

    style Q3 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q5 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Diagram Interpretation Guide

- **Architecture 4.1** traces data flow: SUN → Irradiance → Area → Incident Power → Current/Voltage → Fill Factor → $P_{max}$ → $\eta$.
- **Architecture 4.2** illustrates the **cascading loss funnel** — each block is a deduction from the original $P_{in}$.
- **Architecture 4.3** clarifies that $P_{max} < V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$; the inner rectangle represents extractable power.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define solar cell efficiency. State the Standard Test Conditions used for its measurement.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PERUT637 | Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Solar cell efficiency ($\eta$) is the ratio of maximum electrical power output ($P_{max}$) to the incident solar power ($P_{in}$) on the active cell area, expressed as a percentage **(1 Mark)**.
$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$
**Standard Test Conditions (STC):** Irradiance = **1000 W/m²**, Spectrum = **AM1.5G**, Cell Temperature = **25 °C (298.15 K)** **(1 Mark)**.

---

**Q2. What is Fill Factor (FF)? Why is it less than unity?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | PERUT637 | Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Fill Factor is the ratio of maximum power to the product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current **(1 Mark)**:
$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$
FF is **less than 1** because the real I-V curve is not rectangular; it has a rounded "knee" due to internal series resistance and recombination losses **(1 Mark)**.
For a good silicon cell, FF lies between **0.70 and 0.85** **(1 Mark)**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PERUT637 | Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) of a solar cell from the single-diode model. Mention the physical significance of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solar cell has the following parameters at STC: $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A, Fill Factor $FF = 0.80$, cell area $A = 0.0125$ m². Calculate the efficiency of the cell. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from single-diode model:

$$I = I_L - I_o \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{qV}{nkT} \right) - 1 \right] \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

At open circuit, terminal current $I = 0$:

$$0 = I_L - I_o \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} \right) - 1 \right] \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Rearranging:

$$I_o \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} \right) - 1 \right] = I_L \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Assuming $I_L \gg I_o$:

$$\exp\!\left( \frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} \right) \approx \frac{I_L}{I_o} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Taking natural log:

$$\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_o} \right) \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_o} + 1 \right)} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Physical significance:**
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 = ideal, 2 = recombination-dominated).
- $I_L$ = photogenerated current proportional to irradiance.
- $I_o$ = reverse saturation current (highly temperature dependent).
- $\frac{kT}{q}$ = thermal voltage (~25.9 mV at 25 °C) **(1 Mark)**.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Incident power:

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 0.0125 = 12.5 \; \text{W} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 2:** Geometric power:

$$V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.62 \times 3.5 = 2.17 \; \text{W} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum power:

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 2.17 \times 0.80 = 1.736 \; \text{W} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{1.736}{12.5} \times 100\% = 13.888\% \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Final statement with units: **$\eta \approx 13.89\%$** **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question B** (Alternative, 14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | PERUT637 | Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the various loss mechanisms in a solar cell with a neat energy-flow diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PV module of area $1.5$ m² receives irradiance of $850$ W/m² and delivers a current of $7.8$ A at $V_{mp} = 28$ V. Calculate: (i) Input power, (ii) Output power, (iii) Module efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Loss Mechanisms (7 Marks):**

1. **Reflection Loss (1 Mark):** ~2–4% of incident light bounces off the top surface. Mitigated by ARC (Anti-Reflective Coating) like SiN or TiO₂.
2. **Transmission Loss (1 Mark):** Photons with energy below the bandgap ($E < E_g$) pass through unabsorbed (~23%).
3. **Thermalization Loss (1.5 Marks):** High-energy photons ($E \gg E_g$) waste excess energy as heat via lattice vibrations (~33%).
4. **Recombination Loss (1.5 Marks):** Generated electron-hole pairs recombine before collection (radiative + non-radiative via defects).
5. **Series & Shunt Resistance Losses (1 Mark):** Internal $R_s$ dissipates power; low $R_{sh}$ provides alternate current path, reducing voltage.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Input Power:**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 850 \; \text{W/m}^2 \times 1.5 \; \text{m}^2 = 1275 \; \text{W} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Output Power:**

$$P_{out} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 28 \; \text{V} \times 7.8 \; \text{A} = 218.4 \; \text{W} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Module Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{218.4}{1275} \times 100\% = 17.13\% \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Final Answer with STC note:** Since irradiance is **not 1000 W/m²**, efficiency is reported at **operating conditions 850 W/m²**; for STC comparison, normalization is required **(1 Mark)**.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark pitfall traps (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting STC statement** → −1 mark per question.
> 2. **Using $P_{in} = $ area only** without multiplying by irradiance → −2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ with $P_{max}$** — always multiply by FF → −2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong units** — efficiency must be in **%** with three significant figures → −1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping log derivation steps** in part (a) — every algebraic transition is **0.5–1 mark** in KTU keys.
> 6. **Non-dimensional inconsistency** — voc must be defined before using Green's approximation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Formula:** $\eta = (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF) / (G \cdot A) \times 100\%$ **(Must memorize verbatim)**.
- **STC = 1000 W/m², AM1.5G, 25 °C** — always declare in numerical answers.
- **FF lies in [0, 1]**; typical silicon FF = 0.70–0.85.
- **$V_{oc}$ equation:** $V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln(I_L/I_o + 1)$ — log-based, ideality-factor sensitive.
- **Shockley-Queisser Limit:** 33.7% for single-junction, ~68% for multi-junction concentrators.
- **Thermal voltage at 25 °C:** 25.85 mV — used extensively.
- **Loss ordering (largest to smallest):** Thermalization > Transmission > Reflection > Recombination > Resistive.
- **Voc, Isc vs. Pmax:** $P_{max} \le V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ — equality only when FF = 1 (impossible).
- **Trick question safeguard:** If irradiance ≠ 1000 W/m², mention operating condition explicitly.
- **Diagram rule:** Always draw a loss-flow block diagram for full marks in part (a) derivations.
- **Units discipline:** A in m², G in W/m², $P_{in}$ in W, $\eta$ in %.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Energy Systems - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Wind Energy Systems - Working Principle - Block Diagram ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS):** A renewable energy system that converts the **kinetic energy** of moving air masses (wind) into **mechanical energy** (via aerodynamic blades) and subsequently into **electrical energy** (via an electromechanical generator), governed by the principle of conservation of angular momentum and electromagnetic induction. A Wind Energy Conversion System is fundamentally a **fluid-to-electrical** energy transducer. The thermodynamic premise is that the atmosphere possesses a vast reservoir of kinetic energy due to differential solar heating of the Earth's surface, and a WECS intercepts a fraction of this kinetic flux using aerodynamic rotor blades. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sailboat & Water Wheel Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine a **water wheel** placed in a flowing river. Just as the wheel turns when water strikes its paddles (transferring kinetic energy from the water to the wheel's rotational axis), a wind turbine works in the exact same way — except the "river" is made of invisible moving air molecules, and the "paddles" are carefully shaped aerodynamic blades designed to "scoop" the wind efficiently. Think of it like this: - **Wind** = Moving air (invisible "river" in the sky) - **Rotor blades** = Curved paddles of a water wheel - **Hub + Shaft** = The central axle transferring rotation - **Generator** = The millstone replaced by a copper-coil electricity producer - **Tower** = Lifts the wheel into the "faster current" where wind is less turbulent ### 1.3 Foundational Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following values are **standard reference constants** used universally in KTU examinations: | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Air density (at sea level, 15°C) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² | | Betz Coefficient (Maximum) | $C_{Betz}$ | **16/27 ≈ 0.593** | dimensionless | | Specific gas constant of air | $R_{air}$ | **287** | J/(kg·K) | | Standard atmospheric pressure | $p_{atm}$ | **101325** | Pa | | Cut-in wind speed | $V_{cut-in}$ | **3–4** | m/s | | Cut-out wind speed | $V_{cut-out}$ | **25** | m/s | | Rated wind speed | $V_{rated}$ | **12–14** | m/s | | Survival wind speed | $V_{survival}$ | **60** | m/s | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme PERUT637 Module 2 specifically emphasizes the **block diagram representation** of a WECS and its **working principle**. Memorize the sequence: *Wind → Rotor → Gearbox → Generator → Power Converter → Grid.* --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physical Origin of Wind Wind is generated by the **uneven heating of the Earth's surface** by solar radiation. This creates **pressure gradients** in the atmosphere, and air naturally flows from high-pressure zones to low-pressure zones. The resulting mass movement possesses kinetic energy, which is the resource tapped by a WECS. The **kinetic energy** possessed by a parcel of air of mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ is: $$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$$ ### 2.2 The Six-Stage Working Principle (Operational Logic Breakdown) A WECS operates through a sequential six-stage energy transformation chain: 1. **Kinetic Energy Capture (Wind → Blade Rotor):** Oncoming wind strikes the airfoil-shaped blades. Due to the **Bernoulli principle** and **asymmetric pressure distribution**, a net aerodynamic **lift force** is generated, causing the rotor to spin. The blade pitch angle ($\beta$) controls the lift-to-drag ratio. 2. **Mechanical Transmission (Rotor → Gearbox):** The rotor shaft typically rotates at **15–20 RPM** (slow, high-torque). A **gearbox** steps up this speed to **1500–1800 RPM** (required for efficient electromagnetic induction in the generator). 3. **Electromechanical Conversion (Gearbox → Generator):** The high-speed shaft drives a synchronous or asynchronous generator. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, rotating conductors within a magnetic field produce an EMF: $$E = N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ 4. **Power Conditioning (Generator → Power Converter):** The generator's output is typically variable-frequency AC (depending on wind speed). An **inverter/rectifier** converts this to fixed-frequency, grid-compatible AC (50 Hz in India). 5. **Voltage Step-Up (Power Converter → Transformer):** A **step-up transformer** raises the voltage (e.g., from 690 V to 11 kV / 33 kV) to minimize transmission losses. 6. **Load Dispatch (Transformer → Grid/Load):** The conditioned, grid-matched electricity is delivered to either a local load or the utility grid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind Each Stage:** Each stage addresses a specific physical mismatch — the rotor converts *linear* momentum into *rotational* momentum; the gearbox reconciles *low-speed high-torque* with *high-speed low-torque* requirements; the generator converts *mechanical* to *electrical*; and so on. No stage is redundant. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Applicability | |---|---|---|---| | Power available in wind | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^3$ | $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³), $A$ = swept area (m²), $V$ = wind speed (m/s) | Calculates total kinetic flux through rotor disk | | Swept area of rotor | $A = \pi R^2$ | $R$ = rotor radius (m) | For a horizontal-axis turbine with $N$ blades | | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | kg/s | Mass of air passing per second | | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $n$ = RPM | Determines aerodynamic efficiency regime | | Power Coefficient | $C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}}$ | dimensionless | Actual turbine performance metric | | Maximum $C_p$ (Betz Limit) | $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | dimensionless | Theoretical upper bound, never exceeded | | Actual extracted power | $P_{out} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | Watts | Real turbine output | | Generator EMF | $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_{max} K_w$ | Volts | EMF per phase in AC generator | | Wind power density | $P_{density} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^3$ | W/m² | Power per unit swept area (site assessment) | | Weibull probability | $f(V) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{V}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{V}{c}\right)^k\right]$ | $k$ = shape factor, $c$ = scale factor (m/s) | Wind resource statistical modeling | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Table Safety:** Note that in the table above, the expression $\frac{16}{27}$ is used directly (not $\frac{16}{\vert 27 \vert}$), because division bars in fractions are rendered in LaTeX and do **not** conflict with markdown table pipe `$\vert$` delimiters. However, if you ever need absolute value or modular notation in a table cell, **always** use $\vert$ or $\mid$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The principles studied here underpin: - **Wind farm siting analysis** (using Weibull distribution) - **Microgrid integration** of renewable sources - **Hybrid energy systems** (Wind + Solar + Battery) - **Offshore wind installations** (where $\rho$ is higher → ~1.225 → 1.35 kg/m³ due to sea-level moisture) - **Smart grid frequency stabilization** challenges with intermittent wind --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I: Power Available in the Wind (The Foundation Equation) > This derivation establishes the **cubic relationship** between wind speed and available power — the single most important result in wind energy engineering. **Step 1 — Start with the kinetic energy definition for an air parcel of mass $m$:** $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$$ **Step 2 — Define the mass flow rate through the rotor's swept area:** A control volume is taken at the rotor disk of area $A$. In one second, a column of air of length $V$ (meters) and cross-section $A$ passes through the disk. $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$$ *Here, $\rho$ is the air density in kg/m³, $A$ is the swept area in m², and $V$ is the undisturbed upstream wind velocity in m/s.* **Step 3 — Power is the rate of energy flow:** Power is the time derivative of kinetic energy. The kinetic energy flowing past the disk per second is: $$P_{wind} = \frac{dE_k}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^2$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the mass flow rate expression:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho A V) \cdot V^2 \\ P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \end{aligned}$$ This is the **fundamental wind power equation**. The cubic dependence on $V$ means that doubling the wind speed results in **eight times** the available power — a critical design insight. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II: The Betz Limit (Maximum Theoretical Efficiency) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Betz Limit** (proved by German physicist Albert Betz in 1919) establishes that no wind turbine can extract more than **16/27 (≈59.3%)** of the kinetic energy in wind. This is an immutable consequence of conservation of mass and momentum. **Step 1 — Set up the actuator disk model:** Imagine the rotor as an infinitesimally thin "actuator disk" that slows the wind from upstream velocity $V_1$ to downstream velocity $V_2$. The air velocity at the disk itself is $V_d$ (where $V_2 < V_d < V_1$). **Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass:** The mass flow rate through the disk must be continuous (air cannot accumulate): $$\rho A V_d = \rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2$$ *This means $V_d$ is the average of $V_1$ and $V_2$, i.e., $V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ (by continuity for incompressible flow).* **Step 3 — Apply conservation of momentum (Newton's 2nd Law):** The thrust force $F$ on the disk equals the rate of change of momentum: $$F = \rho A V_d (V_1 - V_2)$$ **Step 4 — Compute the power extracted by the rotor:** Power extracted = Force × Velocity at the disk: $$P_{extracted} = F \cdot V_d = \rho A V_d^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$ **Step 5 — Express extracted power in terms of upstream conditions only:** Substitute $V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ into the expression: $$\begin{aligned} P_{extracted} &= \rho A \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)^2 (V_1 - V_2) \\ &= \frac{\rho A}{4} (V_1^2 - V_2^2)(V_1 + V_2) \\ &= \frac{\rho A}{4} (V_1 + V_2)^2 (V_1 - V_2) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Define the power coefficient $C_p$:** The power coefficient is the ratio of extracted power to available wind power: $$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{extracted}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3}$$ Substituting: $$C_p = \frac{\frac{\rho A}{4}(V_1 + V_2)^2(V_1 - V_2)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = \frac{(V_1 + V_2)^2(V_1 - V_2)}{2 V_1^3}$$ **Step 7 — Introduce the induction factor $a$:** Define $a = \frac{V_1 - V_d}{V_1}$, which gives $V_d = V_1(1-a)$ and $V_2 = V_1(1-2a)$. **Step 8 — Rewrite $C_p$ in terms of $a$:** $$C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$$ **Step 9 — Maximize $C_p$ by differentiation:** Take the derivative with respect to $a$ and set it to zero: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{dC_p}{da} &= 4 \cdot \frac{d}{da}\left[a(1-a)^2\right] \\ &= 4 \left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] \\ &= 4 (1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] \\ &= 4 (1-a)(1 - 3a) \end{aligned}$$ Setting $\frac{dC_p}{da} = 0$: $$(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$ This yields two solutions: $a = 1$ (trivial, wind fully stopped — zero power) or $a = \frac{1}{3}$. **Step 10 — Substitute the optimal $a = \frac{1}{3}$:** $$C_{p,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$ This proves the **Betz Limit: $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 59.3\%$**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III: Generator EMF (For Bonus Conceptual Clarity) For completeness (relevant to the WECS block diagram), the RMS EMF per phase of an AC generator is: $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot K_w$$ Where: - $f$ = frequency of generated voltage (Hz) - $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb) - $N$ = number of turns per phase - $K_w$ = winding factor (typically 0.95–0.98) This confirms why a **gearbox** is essential — without it, the low rotational speed of the rotor would produce unacceptably low-frequency output. --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional architecture** of a grid-connected horizontal-axis wind turbine system, including the **feedback control loop** that is essential for safe operation. ```mermaid flowchart TD windSource((WIND KINETIC ENERGY)) rotorBlk["ROTOR ASSEMBLY
Blades plus Hub
Captures kinetic energy"] shaftBlk["LOW SPEED SHAFT
15 to 20 RPM"] gearboxBlk["GEARBOX
Speed Multiplier"] highShaftBlk["HIGH SPEED SHAFT
1500 to 1800 RPM"] genBlk["GENERATOR
Mechanical to Electrical"] convBlk["POWER CONVERTER
AC to DC to AC
Frequency conditioning"] xfmrBlk["STEP UP TRANSFORMER
Voltage level matching"] gridLoad((GRID OR LOCAL LOAD)) ctrlBlk["CONTROL UNIT
Pitch and Yaw control
Safety shutdown"] sensorBlk["ANEMO AND VANE SENSORS
Wind speed and direction"] windSource --> rotorBlk rotorBlk --> shaftBlk shaftBlk --> gearboxBlk gearboxBlk --> highShaftBlk highShaftBlk --> genBlk genBlk --> convBlk convBlk --> xfmrBlk xfmrBlk --> gridLoad sensorBlk --> ctrlBlk ctrlBlk -. Pitch command .-> rotorBlk ctrlBlk -. Yaw command .-> rotorBlk ctrlBlk -. Trip signal .-> genBlk ``` ### 4.2 Annotated Functional Description of Each Block | Block Label | Function | Energy Domain | |---|---|---| | **WIND KINETIC ENERGY** | Atmospheric air mass motion; primary input resource | Kinetic (fluid) | | **ROTOR ASSEMBLY** | Aerofoil blades extract momentum via lift generation; hub transmits torque | Kinetic → Mechanical (rotational) | | **LOW SPEED SHAFT** | Transmits rotor torque to gearbox input | Mechanical | | **GEARBOX** | Steps up RPM (typically ratio 1:100) while reducing torque proportionally | Mechanical | | **HIGH SPEED SHAFT** | Drives generator at synchronous speed | Mechanical | | **GENERATOR** | Converts rotational mechanical energy to AC electrical energy via Faraday's law | Mechanical → Electrical | | **POWER CONVERTER** | Rectifies to DC then inverts to grid-quality 50 Hz AC | Electrical (AC↔DC↔AC) | | **STEP-UP TRANSFORMER** | Raises voltage for efficient transmission | Electrical | | **GRID / LOAD** | End consumer of electrical energy | Electrical | | **ANEMO AND VANE SENSORS** | Measure wind speed (anemometer) and direction (vane) | Measurement | | **CONTROL UNIT** | Computes optimal pitch angle, yaws rotor into wind, triggers shutdown above cut-out | Control logic | ### 4.3 Energy Transformation Summary Chart ``` WIND (Kinetic) ↓ [Aerodynamic lift] BLADES (Rotational Mechanical) ↓ [Gear ratio step-up] GENERATOR (Electrical AC) ↓ [Rectification + Inversion] GRID-READY AC (50 Hz, 11 kV/33 kV) ``` --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Q: Define the Betz limit. Why is it significant in wind energy conversion?** **Model Answer:** The **Betz Limit** is the theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy in wind that can be extracted by an ideal wind turbine, given by: $$C_{p,Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \quad (\text{i.e., } 59.3\%)$$ It is significant because: 1. It sets an **absolute theoretical upper bound** on the efficiency of any wind turbine — no design, however advanced, can exceed it. 2. It serves as a **benchmark** to evaluate the performance of real-world turbines (modern commercial turbines achieve $C_p \approx 0.45$–$0.50$). 3. It arises from the **conservation of mass and momentum** in the actuator disk model — it is a thermodynamic inevitability, not an engineering limitation. *(Word count: ~85 words — appropriate for a 3-mark answer)* --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Q: List any four components of a Wind Energy Conversion System and state their function.** **Model Answer:** | # | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Rotor (Blades + Hub)** | Captures kinetic energy of wind and converts it to rotational mechanical energy | | 2 | **Gearbox** | Increases the rotational speed from low (≈20 RPM) to high (≈1500 RPM) required by the generator | | 3 | **Generator** | Converts mechanical rotational energy into electrical energy (AC) | | 4 | **Power Converter (Inverter)** | Conditions the variable-frequency AC output to fixed 50 Hz grid-compatible AC | *(For 3 marks, listing 4 components with one-line functions is sufficient.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format) > **KTU Exam Pattern Reminder:** Module 2 typically contributes one 14-mark question in Part B. The internal choice allows the student to choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]` **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS). Discuss the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the power available in wind. A wind turbine has a blade diameter of 80 m. If the wind speed is 14 m/s and air density is 1.25 kg/m³, calculate the power available in the wind and the maximum extractable power using the Betz limit. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** A Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) converts the kinetic energy of wind into usable electrical energy through a sequence of energy transformations. **Block Diagram:** ``` WIND → ROTOR → GEARBOX → GENERATOR → POWER CONVERTER → TRANSFORMER → GRID ↑ [CONTROL UNIT] ← [SENSORS] ``` **Working Principle — Step by Step:** 1. **Wind Kinetic Energy:** Atmospheric air in motion carries kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. 2. **Rotor (Aerodynamic Conversion):** The rotor blades, shaped as airfoils, experience aerodynamic **lift** when wind flows over them. By Newton's 3rd law, the wind exerts a force on the blades, causing rotation. The pitch angle of the blades is adjustable to optimize lift-to-drag ratio across varying wind speeds. 3. **Gearbox (Speed Adaptation):** The rotor turns slowly (≈15–20 RPM) but with high torque. The gearbox multiplies this rotational speed to ≈1500 RPM (suitable for standard 4-pole 50 Hz generators) while proportionally reducing torque. 4. **Generator (Electromechanical Conversion):** The high-speed shaft drives the rotor of a synchronous or induction generator. According to Faraday's Law, conductors rotating in a magnetic field induce an EMF: $$E = N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ The output is typically variable-voltage, variable-frequency AC. 5. **Power Converter (Conditioning):** A rectifier-inverter combination first converts AC to DC (rectification), then back to AC at a fixed 50 Hz frequency and stable voltage (inversion), suitable for grid synchronization. 6. **Step-Up Transformer:** Raises the low generator voltage (typically 690 V) to medium/high voltage (11 kV / 33 kV) to reduce $I^2R$ transmission losses. 7. **Grid Connection:** The conditioned power is fed into the utility grid or a local load. **Control Loop:** Sensors (anemometer and wind vane) continuously measure wind speed and direction. The control unit adjusts blade pitch (for power regulation above rated wind speed) and yaw orientation (to face the rotor into the wind). It also issues emergency shutdown commands when wind speed exceeds the cut-out limit. --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Derivation of Power Available in Wind:** Consider a stream of air with velocity $V$ passing through the rotor's swept area $A$. The mass flow rate is: $$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$ The kinetic energy of this mass per second is the power: $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given data: - Blade diameter $D = 80$ m → Radius $R = 40$ m - Wind speed $V = 14$ m/s - Air density $\rho = 1.25$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Calculate swept area:** $$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (40)^2 = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Calculate power available in wind:** $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.25 \times 5026.55 \times (14)^3$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{wind} &= 0.625 \times 5026.55 \times 2744 \\ &= 3141.59 \times 2744 \\ &\approx 8{,}620{,}000 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 8.62 \text{ MW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate maximum extractable power (Betz limit):** $$P_{max} = C_{Betz} \times P_{wind} = \frac{16}{27} \times 8.62 \text{ MW}$$ $$P_{max} = 0.5926 \times 8.62 \approx 5.11 \text{ MW}$$ **Final Answers:** - Power available in wind ≈ **8.62 MW** - Maximum extractable power ≈ **5.11 MW** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]` **Q: (a)** Explain the Betz limit with a suitable derivation. State its numerical value and physical significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has the following specifications: Rotor diameter = 60 m, Rated wind speed = 12 m/s, Air density = 1.226 kg/m³, Generator efficiency = 90%, Gearbox efficiency = 95%. Calculate: (i) the swept area, (ii) the rated power available in wind, (iii) the rated electrical power output. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** The **Betz Limit** defines the theoretical maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that an ideal, frictionless wind turbine can extract. **Derivation Outline (Actuator Disk Model):** 1. Treat the rotor as an infinitesimally thin actuator disk of area $A$. 2. Let $V_1$ = upstream wind velocity, $V_2$ = downstream wind velocity, $V_d$ = velocity at the disk. 3. By **mass conservation:** $V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ 4. By **momentum conservation:** Thrust $F = \rho A V_d (V_1 - V_2)$ 5. **Power extracted:** $P = F \cdot V_d = \rho A V_d^2 (V_1 - V_2)$ 6. Define induction factor $a = 1 - \frac{V_d}{V_1}$, which gives $V_d = V_1(1-a)$ and $V_2 = V_1(1-2a)$. 7. Express the **power coefficient:** $$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$ 8. Maximize by setting $\frac{dC_p}{da} = 0$: $$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$ The non-trivial solution is $a = \frac{1}{3}$. 9. Substitute back: $$C_{p,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$ **Numerical Value:** $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (i.e., **59.26%**) **Physical Significance:** - It is the **upper bound** of wind energy conversion efficiency. - It is a consequence of **mass and momentum conservation** — universal and irrefutable. - It guides designers: the goal is to approach 59.3% but never expect to surpass it. - Modern three-blade turbines achieve $C_p \approx 0.45$–$0.50$, which is considered excellent. --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Given Data:** - Rotor diameter $D = 60$ m → $R = 30$ m - Rated wind speed $V = 12$ m/s - Air density $\rho = 1.226$ kg/m³ - Generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.90$ - Gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb} = 0.95$ **(i) Swept Area:** $$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (30)^2 = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ m}^2$$ **(ii) Rated Power Available in Wind:** $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.226 \times 2827.43 \times (12)^3 \\ &= 0.613 \times 2827.43 \times 1728 \\ &= 1733.22 \times 1728 \\ &\approx 2{,}995{,}000 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 2.995 \text{ MW} \approx 3.0 \text{ MW} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Rated Electrical Power Output:** Assuming $C_p = 0.45$ (typical real-world, since Betz limit is unattainable): $$P_{mech} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.45 \times 2.995 \approx 1.348 \text{ MW}$$ Apply mechanical drivetrain efficiencies (gearbox × generator): $$P_{electrical} = P_{mech} \times \eta_{gb} \times \eta_g$$ $$P_{electrical} = 1.348 \times 0.95 \times 0.90$$ $$P_{electrical} \approx 1.152 \text{ MW}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) Swept area ≈ **2827.43 m²** - (ii) Power available in wind ≈ **3.0 MW** - (iii) Electrical output ≈ **1.15 MW** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor:** In $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$, omitting the ½ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > > 2. **Mixing up diameter and radius:** Always compute $R = D/2$ before applying $A = \pi R^2$. Writing $A = \pi D^2$ directly is a common (and costly) error. > > 3. **Confusing available power with extracted power:** Students often forget to multiply by $C_p$ (or Betz coefficient $\frac{16}{27}$). The question must be read carefully — "power in wind" ≠ "power extracted by turbine". > > 4. **Wrong units:** Always end numerical answers with proper units (MW or kW or W). Answers like "2.995" without units lose at least 0.5 marks. > > 5. **Block diagram without labels:** A block diagram in KTU exams must show **arrows indicating energy/signal flow direction** AND each block must have a **label**. Unlabeled blocks get zero marks even if the structure is correct. > > 6. **Ignoring efficiencies:** When the problem gives gearbox/generator efficiencies, forgetting to apply them in part (c) of a multi-part question is a frequent 1–2 mark loss. > > 7. **Stating Betz limit without derivation:** Writing only "16/27" without showing the derivation earns partial credit. Always derive or at least sketch the actuator disk reasoning. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Summary — Read Before Entering Exam Hall:** - [ ] **Wind Power Equation:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ — **cubic in velocity** - [ ] **Swept Area:** $A = \pi R^2$ — remember $R = D/2$ - [ ] **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ (59.3%) - [ ] **Power Coefficient:** $C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}}$ - [ ] **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}$ - [ ] **Air Density (Standard):** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level - [ ] **WECS Block Diagram Sequence:** Wind → Rotor → Gearbox → Generator → Converter → Transformer → Grid (with Control Unit feedback) - [ ] **Gearbox purpose:** Speed step-up (≈1:100) from low-RPM rotor to high-RPM generator - [ ] **Power Converter purpose:** Variable-Frequency AC → Fixed 50 Hz AC (rectify + invert) - [ ] **Cut-in / Rated / Cut-out speeds:** ≈ 3–4 / 12–14 / 25 m/s - [ ] **Three-blade HAWT** is the dominant commercial design - [ ] **Generator EMF:** $E = 4.44 f N \Phi K_w$ (per phase, RMS) - [ ] **Always include units** in numerical answers - [ ] **Label every block** in diagrams and indicate arrow directions - [ ] **Apply efficiencies** when mentioned in multi-part problems - [ ] **Betz limit is a thermodynamic ceiling** — derived from momentum conservation, not engineering limitation ---

topics.title: Wind Power Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Power Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wind Power Equation** is the fundamental mathematical relationship that quantifies the amount of kinetic energy harvested from a moving air mass (wind) passing through the swept area of a wind turbine rotor. It forms the foundational governing equation of all aerogeneration calculations in wind energy engineering.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PERUT637 – Energy Systems**, the wind power equation is formally expressed as:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{3}$$

Where each symbol carries a precise physical meaning:
- **$P_{wind}$** → Available wind power in **Watts (W)**
- **$\rho$ (rho)** → Air density in **kg/m³** (Standard value at sea level = **1.225 kg/m³**)
- **$A$** → Swept area of the turbine rotor blades in **m²**
- **$V$** → Upstream wind velocity in **m/s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The cubic dependence on wind velocity ($V^3$) is the single most examined concept in this module. Doubling the wind speed increases available power by **eight (8) times**. This is why coastal Kerala (Wynd Farm style projects) prefers sites with sustained higher mean wind velocities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for Freshers)

Imagine you are standing in a river holding a wide fishing net. The river's current pushes water (and fish) into your net.

- A **faster river** (high $V$) doesn't just deliver slightly more water — it delivers **massively more** because the moving stream carries cubic energy.
- A **wider net** (large $A$) captures a bigger cross-section of moving fluid.
- **Denser water** (high $\rho$) means each cubic meter weighs more, so the kinetic punch is stronger.

Wind behaves identically to this river, except the "fluid" is invisible air. The Wind Power Equation simply asks: *"How much kinetic punch is flowing per second through the disk area swept by the rotor?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "½" appears in the formula:** Kinetic energy is $\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$. Multiplying by the mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ yields $\frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$. This is a direct consequence of **Newtonian mechanics** applied to a control volume.

---

### 1.3 The Two Tiers of Wind Power

It is critical for KTU board exams to distinguish between **Available Power** (in the wind) and **Extractable Power** (captured by turbine):

| Tier | Name | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|------|------|----------|------------------|
| Tier 1 | Available Wind Power | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | Total kinetic energy flux |
| Tier 2 | Mechanical Power Extracted | $P_{mech} = C_p \cdot P_{wind}$ | What the rotor actually harvests |

The dimensionless factor **$C_p$** is called the **Power Coefficient** (or Betz Coefficient), capped by the **Betz Limit**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Control Volume Logic (Why This Equation?)

To derive why the wind power equation takes the form $\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$, we apply an **Eulerian control volume** around the rotor disk. The logic chain proceeds in five disciplined steps:

1. **Identify the Control Volume**: An imaginary cylindrical tube with the rotor disk as its cross-section ($A$).
2. **Mass Flow Rate**: The mass of air passing through per second is $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ (density × area × velocity).
3. **Kinetic Energy per Unit Mass**: For a moving fluid, $KE/m = \frac{1}{2}V^2$ (Newtonian mechanics).
4. **Power = Energy Flow Rate**: Power equals energy per unit mass × mass flow rate:
$$P = \left(\frac{1}{2}V^{2}\right) \times (\rho A V) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$$
5. **Conclusion**: This is the **upper bound** of kinetic energy available for conversion. No turbine can extract more than this from the airstream without violating energy conservation.

### 2.2 The Power Coefficient ($C_p$) and Betz Limit

In 1919, German physicist **Albert Betz** proved that an ideal turbine can never extract more than **16/27 (≈ 59.3 %)** of the kinetic energy in wind. This is the **Betz Limit**.

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

$$P_{max,\ extractable} = \frac{16}{27} \times \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3} = \frac{8}{27}\rho A V^{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why can't we extract 100%?** If we stopped all the air completely, downstream air would pile up and no new air could enter. We must leave enough kinetic energy in the wake to "escort" the air away from the rotor. The optimum axial induction factor is **$a = 1/3$**.

### 2.3 Tip Speed Ratio (TSR, λ)

The **Tip Speed Ratio** compares the linear speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind speed:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi D n}{V}$$

Where:
- $\omega$ → Angular velocity of rotor (rad/s)
- $R$ → Rotor radius (m), $D = 2R$
- $n$ → Rotational speed (revolutions per second)

Different turbine designs are optimized at different λ values:
- **High-speed (3-blade, HAWT):** λ ≈ 6–8
- **Low-speed (multi-blade, American farm):** λ ≈ 1–2
- **Darrieus VAWT:** λ ≈ 3–5

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Rule:** For full marks on a 14-mark derivation problem, you must be able to reproduce every entry in this table from memory, including the **units**.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ | Available wind power | **W (Watts)** |
| 2 | $A = \pi R^{2} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | Swept area of rotor disk | **m²** |
| 3 | $P_{mech} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ | Mechanical power extracted by turbine | **W** |
| 4 | $C_p \le \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz limit (theoretical maximum) | **Dimensionless** |
| 5 | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi D n}{V}$ | Tip speed ratio | **Dimensionless** |
| 6 | $E_{annual} = \sum_{i} P_i \cdot t_i$ | Annual energy yield (kWh) | **kWh/year** |
| 7 | $\rho_{alt} = \rho_0 \left(1 - \frac{L h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{gM}{RL}-1}$ | Air density correction for altitude | **kg/m³** |
| 8 | $CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity factor (0.20–0.40 typical) | **Dimensionless** |
| 9 | $P_{density} = \frac{P}{\rho A V^{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$ | Alternative expression for $C_p$ | **Dimensionless** |
| 10 | $V_{hub} = V_{ref} \left(\frac{H_{hub}}{H_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Wind shear / power law exponent α | **m/s** |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** When writing absolute value, modulus, or conditional expressions inside an exam answer, use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`. Never use the bare pipe `|x|` inside markdown tables or it will break column alignment.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Wind Power Equation is the cornerstone for:

- **Wind farm site assessment** — predicting annual energy production before installation.
- **Turbine sizing** — selecting rotor diameter and rated power for a given wind regime.
- **Economic feasibility studies** — LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) calculations for IPP bids.
- **Power curve generation** — manufacturer datasheets ($P$ vs $V$) for grid integration.
- **Micro-siting and wake loss analysis** — crucial for offshore projects like the upcoming Kerala coastal belt.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Wind Power Equation

We now derive $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ from first principles using control volume analysis.

**Starting Premise (Newton's Second Law / Kinetic Energy Definition):**
The kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2}m V^{2}$$

**Step 1: Define the mass flow rate through the rotor disk.**
In time $\Delta t$, a column of air of length $V \Delta t$ passes through area $A$. The mass of this air parcel is:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot (V \Delta t)$$

**Step 2: Compute the kinetic energy of this parcel.**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \left[\rho A V \Delta t\right] V^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3} \Delta t$$

**Step 3: Power is energy per unit time.**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{KE}{\Delta t} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3} \Delta t}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4: Cancel $\Delta t$ and write the final form.**

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Reasoning Demand:** If asked to derive, always explicitly state: (i) the control volume chosen, (ii) the mass enclosed, (iii) the kinetic energy definition invoked, and (iv) the time-rate transformation. Skipping any step loses at least 1–2 marks in ESE.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Betz Limit ($C_{p,max} = 16/27$)

This is a frequently asked **14-mark derivation** in KTU papers. Below is the full step-by-step derivation expected by examiners.

**Step 1: Define upstream and downstream velocities.**
Let $V_1$ = upstream wind velocity (before rotor), $V_2$ = downstream wake velocity, and $V_{disk}$ = velocity at the rotor disk.

**Step 2: Apply continuity of mass flow.**
Mass flow rate must be conserved through the control volume:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V_1 = \rho A V_{disk} = \rho A V_2$$

**Step 3: Apply the axial induction factor $a$.**
By definition, the velocity at the disk is reduced by factor $a$:

$$V_{disk} = V_1 (1 - a)$$

**Step 4: Apply Bernoulli's equation between far upstream and far downstream.**
For an ideal, incompressible, inviscid flow with no pressure recovery:

$$V_{disk} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $V_2$ in terms of $V_1$.**
Substituting $V_{disk} = V_1(1-a)$ into the Bernoulli relation:

$$V_1(1-a) = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \implies V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$$

**Step 6: Compute the power extracted (thrust force × disk velocity).**
The thrust force on the rotor equals the rate of momentum change:

$$F = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V_{disk}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 7: Substitute $V_{disk}$ and $V_2$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho A \left[V_1(1-a)\right] \left[V_1 - V_1(1-2a)\right] \\
&= \rho A V_1^{2}(1-a)(2a)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: Power is force times disk velocity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{extracted} &= F \cdot V_{disk} \\
&= \left[\rho A V_1^{2}(1-a)(2a)\right] \cdot \left[V_1(1-a)\right] \\
&= 2\rho A V_1^{3} a (1-a)^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9: Compute the power coefficient.**

$$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A V_1^{3} a (1-a)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^{3}}$$

$$C_p = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$

**Step 10: Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$.**

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4\left[(1-a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a)$$

Setting the derivative to zero:

$$4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

This gives $a = 1$ (trivial, no power) or **$a = 1/3$** (the optimum induction factor).

**Step 11: Substitute $a = 1/3$ back into $C_p$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{p,\max} &= 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} \\
&= \frac{16}{27}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 12: Final boxed result.**

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad (59.26\%)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** The value $16/27 \approx 0.5926$ is dimensionless, lies between 0 and 1, and is universally cited in all wind energy literature (Manwell, McGowan, Rogers; Burton et al.). It is mathematically and physically correct.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind speed at hub height is 12 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate (a) the swept area, (b) the available wind power, and (c) the maximum extractable power at Betz limit.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Swept Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6400}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Part (b): Available Wind Power**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(12)^{3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{wind} &= 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \\
&= 0.6125 \times 8{,}685{,}878.4 \\
&\approx 5{,}320{,}100 \ \text{W} \approx 5.32 \ \text{MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c): Maximum Extractable Power (Betz Limit)**

$$P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \times P_{wind} = \frac{16}{27} \times 5{,}320{,}100 \approx 3{,}152{,}059 \ \text{W} \approx 3.15 \ \text{MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real turbines achieve $C_p \approx 0.35$–$0.45$ due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses. So actual output ≈ 1.86–2.39 MW for this 80 m rotor — consistent with commercial 2–3 MW utility-scale turbines.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wind Power Conversion

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the energy-flow architecture from raw wind to grid-ready electrical power, integrating every concept discussed in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Free Stream Wind Velocity V1]
    A1[Air Density rho at Hub Height]
    A2[Rotor Swept Area A = pi D squared over 4]
    B0[Available Wind Power P_wind = half rho A V cubed]
    C0{Betz Coefficient Cp Selection}
    C1[Cp at Betz Limit 16 over 27]
    C2[Cp Actual 0.35 to 0.45]
    D0[Mechanical Power Extracted P_mech = Cp times P_wind]
    E0[Gearbox Step Up Ratio]
    F0[Generator Conversion Efficiency]
    G0[Power Electronics Rectifier Inverter]
    H0[Electrical Power Output to Grid]
    I0[Tip Speed Ratio Lambda Optimization]
    J0[Yaw and Pitch Control System]

    A0 --> B0
    A1 --> B0
    A2 --> B0
    B0 --> C0
    C0 --> C1
    C0 --> C2
    C1 --> D0
    C2 --> D0
    D0 --> E0
    E0 --> F0
    F0 --> G0
    G0 --> H0
    I0 --> C0
    J0 --> A0
```

### 4.2 Detailed Process Flow: From $V_1$ to Grid Power

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Measure V1 and rho] --> P2[Step 2: Compute A from D]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: P_wind equals half rho A V cubed]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Apply Cp via TSR lambda and pitch angle]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Cp times P_wind equals P_mech]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Multiply by gearbox eta_g and generator eta_gen]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: P_electrical delivered to grid]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Integrate over 8760 hours for annual energy]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Control Volume and Velocity Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Far Upstream Wind Velocity V1] --> S2[Rotor Disk Plane Velocity Vdisk = V1 times 1 minus a]
    S2 --> S3[Far Downstream Wake Velocity V2 = V1 times 1 minus 2a]
    S4[Mass Conservation rho A V1 = rho A Vdisk = rho A V2] --> S2
    S5[Momentum Change Thrust Force F equals rho A Vdisk times V1 minus V2] --> S6[Power Extracted P equals F times Vdisk]
    S6 --> S7[Cp = P over P_wind = 4a times 1 minus a squared]
    S7 --> S8[Optimize dCp over da equals 0]
    S8 --> S9[Optimum Induction Factor a = 1 over 3]
    S9 --> S10[Betz Limit Cp max = 16 over 27]
```

> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked to "draw the schematic," examiners often accept a clear labeled block diagram (as above) showing the velocity vector transitions $V_1 \rightarrow V_{disk} \rightarrow V_2$ with the induction factor $a$. This is faster to draw and carries full credit if correctly labeled.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Wind Power Equation and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The wind power equation gives the available kinetic power in wind passing through a rotor of swept area $A$:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$$

- $\rho$ — Air density (kg/m³), standard value 1.225 kg/m³ at 15 °C sea level.
- $A$ — Swept area of the rotor disk in m² ($A = \pi D^2 / 4$).
- $V$ — Free-stream upstream wind velocity (m/s).

The cubic dependence on $V$ implies that doubling wind speed increases available power by 8 times, making site selection critical. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the Betz Limit? Why can't a wind turbine extract 100% of the kinetic energy in wind?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Betz Limit, derived by Albert Betz (1919), states that an ideal wind turbine can extract at most:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad (59.26\%)$$

of the kinetic energy in wind.

If 100% extraction were attempted, the air behind the rotor would come to a complete stop, blocking new air from entering. The wake must retain enough kinetic energy to "escort" the air mass away. The optimum axial induction factor is $a = 1/3$, beyond which extraction efficiency drops. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]*

**(a)** Derive the Wind Power Equation $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ starting from the definition of kinetic energy. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-blade horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m. At a hub-height wind speed of 10 m/s, the air density is 1.18 kg/m³. Compute:
   1. The swept area.
   2. The available wind power in kW.
   3. The maximum power that can be extracted (Betz limit) in kW.
   4. If the turbine operates at $C_p = 0.40$ with gearbox-generator efficiency of 90%, find the electrical power output in kW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumption 1:** Steady, incompressible flow of air through the rotor disk.
**Assumption 2:** Uniform velocity profile across the swept area.
**Assumption 3:** No friction or heat losses in the control volume.

Consider an air column of length $V \Delta t$ passing through swept area $A$ in time $\Delta t$. Its mass is:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot (V \Delta t)$$

Kinetic energy of this mass moving at velocity $V$:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V \Delta t \cdot V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} \Delta t$$

Power is energy per unit time:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{KE}{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$$

Hence proved. **(7 marks — 1 mark for assumptions, 3 marks for derivation steps, 2 marks for boxing and statement of significance, 1 mark for units check.)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Swept Area (2 marks)**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^{2}}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \ \text{m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Available Wind Power (2 marks)**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}(1.18)(2827.43)(10)^{3} = 0.59 \times 2827.43 \times 1000 \approx 1{,}668{,}184 \ \text{W} \approx 1668.18 \ \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Maximum Power at Betz Limit (2 marks)**

$$P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \times 1668.18 \approx 988.40 \ \text{kW}$$

**(iv) Electrical Output with Real $C_p$ (1 mark)**

$$P_{elec} = C_p \cdot \eta_{gb} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 0.90 \times 1668.18 \approx 600.54 \ \text{kW}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Retest]*

**(a)** With the help of a control volume analysis, derive the expression for the Betz power coefficient $C_p = 4a(1-a)^{2}$. Hence obtain the maximum value of $C_p$ and the corresponding axial induction factor. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.5 MW wind turbine operates at a wind site where the mean wind speed is 8 m/s and the air density is 1.225 kg/m³. The turbine has a rotor diameter of 77 m. Calculate:
   1. The swept area.
   2. The available wind power.
   3. The actual operating power coefficient $C_p$.
   4. The capacity factor if the annual energy output is 4.5 million kWh. **(6 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Derivation (8 marks)**

Apply conservation of mass through the control volume (1 mark):

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V_1 = \rho A V_{disk} = \rho A V_2$$

Axial induction factor definition (1 mark):

$$V_{disk} = V_1(1 - a)$$

Bernoulli between far upstream and far downstream (2 marks):

$$V_{disk} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \implies V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$$

Thrust force on rotor (1 mark):

$$F = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V_{disk}(V_1 - V_2) = 2\rho A V_1^{2} a(1-a)$$

Power extracted (1 mark):

$$P = F \cdot V_{disk} = 2\rho A V_1^{3} a(1-a)^{2}$$

Divide by $P_{wind}$ to obtain (1 mark):

$$C_p = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A V_1^{3} a(1-a)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^{3}} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$

Setting $dC_p/da = 0$:

$$4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \implies a = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (6 marks)**

**(i) Swept Area (1 mark)**

$$A = \frac{\pi (77)^{2}}{4} = \frac{5929\pi}{4} \approx 4656.6 \ \text{m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Available Wind Power (2 marks)**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(4656.6)(8)^{3} = 0.6125 \times 4656.6 \times 512 \approx 1{,}460{,}015 \ \text{W} \approx 1460 \ \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Actual Power Coefficient (2 marks)**

$$C_p = \frac{P_{mech}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{1500}{1460} \approx 1.027$$

**⚠ Examiner's Note:** A $C_p > 1$ is physically impossible; this indicates the **rated power** (1.5 MW) is reached by the turbine **before** $V = 8$ m/s, or wind speed is higher than reported. The student should comment on this and write: *"Since $C_p > 1$ is non-physical, the turbine is operating at rated power due to wind speeds above rated; calculation here reflects the saturation regime."* **(Full marks still awarded if properly interpreted.)**

**(iv) Capacity Factor (1 mark)**

$$CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760} = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{1500 \times 8760} = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{13{,}140{,}000} \approx 0.3425 \quad (34.25\%)$$

This is in the typical 30–40% range for onshore wind farms.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on Wind Power Equation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $V$ MUST be in m/s, $A$ in m², $\rho$ in kg/m³ to get Watts. Converting kW incorrectly loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Mis-stating Betz Limit:** Writing "$C_p$ at Betz limit = 0.59" is acceptable; but writing "$P_{wind} = \frac{16}{27} \rho A V^3$" is WRONG — the 16/27 multiplies $P_{wind}$, it does not replace the $\frac{1}{2}$.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions** in derivations: Always list incompressible flow, uniform velocity, no losses.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_p$ vs $\eta$:** $C_p$ is **aerodynamic** (Betz), $\eta$ is **mechanical-electrical** (gearbox, generator). Combined system efficiency multiplies these.
> 5. **Wrong area formula:** $A = \pi R^2 = \pi D^2/4$. Mixing radius and diameter loses 1–2 marks silently.
> 6. **Plotting errors in power curve:** Students often forget the cut-in (~3–4 m/s), rated (~12–14 m/s), and cut-out (~25 m/s) regions.
> 7. **Density at altitude:** Higher altitude sites (e.g., Idukki) have lower $\rho$; forgetting this overestimates energy yield.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — Memorize Before ESE**

- **Core Equation:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ — Watts, with $\rho$ in kg/m³, $A$ in m², $V$ in m/s.
- **Cubic Law:** Doubling wind speed → **8× power**. Halving → 1/8 power. This is the single most important fact.
- **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$, attained at axial induction factor **$a = 1/3$**.
- **Swept Area:** $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. Don't confuse with $2\pi R h$ (cylinder surface area).
- **TSR:** $\lambda = \omega R / V = \pi D n / V$. Three-blade HAWT optimized at $\lambda \approx 7$.
- **Real $C_p$:** Modern turbines achieve 0.35–0.45 (Betz loss + wake + tip losses).
- **Power Curve Stages:** Cut-in → Cubic rise → Rated plateau → Cut-out shutdown.
- **Capacity Factor:** $CF = E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \times 8760)$. Onshore typical: 0.20–0.40.
- **Density at Altitude:** $\rho$ decreases with height; for Idukki (~900 m), use ~1.10 kg/m³, not 1.225.
- **Combined System Efficiency:** $\eta_{system} = C_p \cdot \eta_{gearbox} \cdot \eta_{generator} \cdot \eta_{converter}$.
- **Kerala Context:** Coastal and Western Ghats sites (Palakkad, Idukki, Wynad) are the prime wind corridors per MNRE Wind Atlas.
- **Unit Conversions:** 1 kW = 1000 W; 1 MW = 1000 kW = 10⁶ W; annual energy in MWh or kWh.
- **The ½ in the formula:** Comes from $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$, NOT from any "halving" of efficiency.
- **Betz 1919** is the historical reference — remember the year for objective-type questions.
- **For KTU ESE:** Always box the final answer with units. Examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy from Waves and Tides - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Energy from Waves and Tides - Working Principle - Block Diagram # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Energy from Ocean Waves ### Formal Academic Definition **Wave Energy** is the form of renewable energy derived from the surface gravity waves of the ocean. These waves are generated primarily by the friction of wind blowing across the sea surface, transferring kinetic energy from atmospheric circulation to the water column. Wave energy conversion (WEC) is the process of capturing this trapped kinetic and gravitational potential energy using mechanical, hydraulic, or pneumatic systems and converting it into useful electrical power. The fundamental energy of an ocean wave is the sum of its **kinetic energy (KE)** and **gravitational potential energy (PE)**, distributed throughout the water column up to a depth of approximately half the wavelength. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The wave energy flux (power per unit crest width) is the key engineering parameter used to size wave energy converters. A typical Atlantic wave in moderate seas carries roughly **30–70 kW per metre** of crest length. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezed Sponge" Imagine holding a soaked sponge in your hand. As the ocean heaves up (the sponge swells), it traps energy; as it falls back, the sponge releases it. A wave energy converter is like a clever mechanical hand that repeatedly squeezes the sponge — each squeeze turns a turbine, and many small squeezes (across many waves) add up to a continuous flow of electricity. The ocean is the sponge; the device is the hand; the turbine is the spindle turned by your fingers. ### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Sea-water density | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Wave height (significant) | $H$ or $H_s$ | 1 – 5 m (typical) | | Wave period | $T$ | 5 – 20 s | | Wavelength | $\lambda$ | 30 – 200 m | --- ## 1.2 Energy from Ocean Tides ### Formal Academic Definition **Tidal Energy** is the renewable energy harnessed from the periodic rise and fall of sea levels caused by the combined gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun, modulated by the Earth's rotation and coastal geography. Two distinct engineering approaches are recognised by the KTU syllabus: 1. **Tidal Range Energy (Tidal Barrage / Tidal Lagoon):** Extracts **potential energy** by impounding water at high tide and releasing it through turbines at low tide. 2. **Tidal Stream Energy (Tidal Current / Underwater Kite):** Extracts **kinetic energy** from horizontal water flow through narrow channels during flood and ebb tides. > [!NOTE] > **Tidal Cycle Vocabulary:** A *flood tide* is the rising phase, an *ebb tide* is the falling phase. The vertical difference between successive high and low waters is the **tidal range**, which is largest at *spring tides* (Sun–Moon alignment) and smallest at *neap tides* (Sun–Moon perpendicular). ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Planetary Lung" Think of the ocean as a giant pair of lungs. Twice a day the Moon inhales (high tide) and exhales (low tide), and a barrage is a clever valve placed at the throat of this lung — letting water in for free on the inhale, but forcing it out through a turbine on the exhale.
