# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define energy density and power density. Why are both metrics critical when selecting an energy storage system for electric vehicles versus grid-scale applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Energy density** ($\rho_E$) is the **amount of energy stored per unit mass** (Wh/kg) or per unit volume (Wh/L). **(1 mark)**
- **Power density** ($\rho_P$) is the **rate at which energy can be delivered per unit mass** (W/kg). **(1 mark)**
- For **EVs**, **high energy density** is critical to maximize driving range per charge; weight and space are at a premium. For **grid-scale**, the priority shifts to **low cost per kWh** and **cycle life**; energy density is less important since weight is not a constraint. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four energy storage technologies and state the form of energy stored in each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 4 × 0.75 marks each):**

1. **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS):** Gravitational Potential Energy
2. **Flywheel:** Rotational Kinetic Energy
3. **Lithium-ion Battery:** Electrochemical (chemical bond) energy
4. **Supercapacitor:** Electrostatic (electrical double-layer) energy
5. *(Bonus)* **Hydrogen:** Chemical bond energy in $\text{H}_2$ molecule
6. *(Bonus)* **Molten Salt TES:** Sensible thermal energy

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** system with a neat schematic. Derive the expression for the energy stored. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PHS plant has an upper reservoir of $V = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$ at an elevation head $h = 150 \text{ m}$. The overall round-trip efficiency is 78%. If charging occurs at a constant power of 200 MW for 6 hours, find: (i) the energy stored in the reservoir, and (ii) the fraction of the reservoir capacity utilized. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Principle & Derivation (7 marks):**

A PHS system has **two water reservoirs at different elevations** connected via a **penstock** and equipped with a **reversible pump-turbine** unit. During periods of excess electricity (e.g., night-time), the surplus power drives the **motor-pump** to lift water from the lower reservoir to the upper one, converting **electrical → gravitational potential energy**. During peak demand, water flows back down through the **turbine-generator**, converting **gravitational → electrical energy**. **(3 marks)**

**Derivation:**

The mass of water available for discharge: $m = \rho V$ **(1 mark)**

Gravitational potential energy stored:

$$E_{PHS} = m \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Practical PHS accounts for round-trip efficiency $\eta_{RT}$:

$$E_{out} = \eta_{RT} \cdot E_{in} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Schematic description (text equivalent, 1 mark):

```
UPPER RESERVOIR
       |
   [penstock]
       |
   [pump-turbine] <-- reversible
       |
   [generator/motor]
       |
       ^
LOWER RESERVOIR
```

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Energy stored in the reservoir (mechanical / potential):**

Using $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $V = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$, $h = 150 \text{ m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{PHS} &= 1000 \times 2 \times 10^6 \times 9.81 \times 150 \text{ J} \\[4pt]
&= 2.943 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 817.5 \text{ MWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Energy actually stored from the grid (input side):**

Charging power $P = 200 \text{ MW}$ for $t = 6 \text{ h}$:

$$E_{in} = P \cdot t = 200 \times 6 = 1200 \text{ MWh}$$

Energy actually retained (after round-trip efficiency — but here applied on input during charging assuming charging efficiency):

$$E_{stored} = \eta_{charge} \cdot E_{in} = 0.78 \times 1200 = 936 \text{ MWh}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Fraction of reservoir capacity utilized:**

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{E_{stored}}{E_{PHS \ capacity}} = \frac{936}{817.5} = 1.145$$

Since this exceeds 1.0, it means **the reservoir cannot hold all the energy**; the charging schedule must be curtailed. Maximum chargeable energy:

$$E_{max} = 0.78 \times 817.5 / 1 \text{ (applied as charge limit)} = 637.65 \text{ MWh}$$

Corrected fraction:

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{E_{max}}{E_{PHS \ capacity}} = \frac{637.65}{817.5} = 0.78 = 78\%$$

**(3 marks)**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flywheel rotor has mass $m = 250 \text{ kg}$, radius of gyration $k = 0.4 \text{ m}$, and operates between $\omega_{min} = 4000 \text{ rpm}$ and $\omega_{max} = 8000 \text{ rpm}$. Compute: (i) the moment of inertia, (ii) the usable stored energy, and (iii) the average discharge power if fully discharged in 90 seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) FESS Principle (7 marks):**

A FESS stores energy in a **high-speed rotating mass**. During charging, a **motor** accelerates the rotor (with the help of a **vacuum housing** to eliminate air friction and **magnetic bearings** for low-loss support). During discharging, the rotor drives a **generator**, converting kinetic energy back to electricity. A **power electronics interface (PEI)** manages bidirectional AC–DC conversion. **(3 marks)**

**Advantages:**
- Very long cycle life (>100,000 cycles) — **(1 mark)**
- Sub-second response time (ideal for frequency regulation) — **(1 mark)**

**Limitations:**
- High self-discharge (~100% per hour) due to bearing drag and air resistance — **(1 mark)**
- Safety concerns at high speeds; limited energy density — **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Moment of inertia:**

$$I = m \cdot k^2 = 250 \times (0.4)^2 = 250 \times 0.16 = 40 \text{ kg·m}^2 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Usable stored energy:**

Convert angular velocities to rad/s:

$$\omega_{max} = 8000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 837.76 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{min} = 4000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 418.88 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{usable} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot I \cdot \left(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times \left[(837.76)^2 - (418.88)^2\right] \\[4pt]
&= 20 \times \left[701{,}843 - 175{,}461\right] \\[4pt]
&= 20 \times 526{,}382 = 1.053 \times 10^7 \text{ J} \\[4pt]
&\approx 10.53 \text{ MJ} = 2.92 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**(iii) Average discharge power:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{usable}}{t} = \frac{1.053 \times 10^7}{90} = 1.17 \times 10^5 \text{ W} = 117 \text{ kW}$$

**(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Per KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Converting $g$ from $\text{m/s}^2$ incorrectly, or forgetting the J-to-kWh conversion ($3.6 \times 10^6$). **−1 mark** per instance.
> 2. **Wrong formula choice:** Using $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ for batteries or $E = \rho V g h$ for flywheels. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring round-trip efficiency:** KTU strictly requires $\eta_{RT}$ to be applied **once** when computing *available discharge energy from a known charge input*. Skipping it = **−1 mark.**
> 4. **No schematic in 7-mark theory parts:** A neat labelled block diagram or component listing is **mandatory**. Missing = **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 5. **Mixing up energy density vs. power density:** These are **NOT the same**. Energy density = total capacity; Power density = how fast it can be delivered. Conflating them = **−1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist 📌

- ✅ **Six storage forms:** Mechanical, Electrical, Electrochemical, Chemical, Thermal, Electromagnetic.
- ✅ **Bulk storage champion:** PHS (>95% of global installed capacity); **fast-response champion:** Supercapacitor / Flywheel / SMES.
- ✅ **PHS energy formula:** $E = \rho V g h$ — **always convert to kWh** in final answer.
- ✅ **Flywheel energy formula:** $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ — **use rad/s, not rpm**.
- ✅ **CAES formula:** $W = p_1 V \ln(p_2/p_1)$ — **apply isothermal approximation** unless stated adiabatic.
- ✅ **Battery EMF link:** $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ — Gibbs free energy ↔ cell voltage.
- ✅ **Sensible vs. Latent TES:** $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ vs. $Q = mL$.
- ✅ **Key metric hierarchy for KTU:** $\eta_{RT}$ > cycle life > energy density > response time > cost.
- ✅ **Hydrogen:** Highest energy density (gravimetric) but **low round-trip efficiency (~35–45%)** — used for **seasonal** storage.
- ✅ **Supercapacitor:** Energy $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$; ideal for **bridging power** during voltage sags.
- ✅ **SMES:** Energy $E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$; requires **cryogenic cooling** (4.2 K) — high operational cost.
- ✅ **Li-ion nominal cell voltage:** **3.6–3.7 V**; Lead-acid: **2.0 V**; NaS: **2.0 V**.
- ✅ **KTU golden rule:** Every numerical answer must have **unit**, **prefix (k, M, G)**, and be **boxed**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressed Air Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compressed Air Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** is a large-scale, grid-connected mechanical energy storage technology in which surplus electrical energy (typically off-peak or from intermittent renewables) is used to drive **motor-compressor** units that compress ambient air into underground caverns, engineered reservoirs, or high-pressure vessels. During peak demand, the compressed air is released, optionally heated (or mixed with natural gas), and expanded through **turbine-generator** units to recover electrical energy.

**Synonyms used in KTU textbooks:** *Pneumatic Energy Storage*, *Compressed Air Storage (CAS)*, *Air-based Energy Storage System*.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine blowing air into a **balloon** and tying it shut. The stretched balloon now holds *potential mechanical energy*. If you let it go, the escaping air could spin a tiny wheel — that's stored mechanical work being released.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A CAES plant is the **grid-scale equivalent of a balloon**, but instead of a rubber membrane, the air is stored inside **massive underground salt caverns, depleted gas reservoirs, or aquifers** at pressures of **70–100 bar**. When the grid needs power, the pressurized air is released like exhaling from a giant pressurized lung.

**Why is this useful?**
- Electricity cannot be easily stored in large quantities. CAES decouples **generation time** from **consumption time**.
- Acts as a **buffer** between variable renewable sources (solar/wind) and the grid.
- Provides **load-leveling, peak shaving, frequency regulation**, and **black-start capability**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used in CAES

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Storage pressure | **40–100 bar** | Energy density increases with pressure |
| Cavern volume | **100,000 – 500,000 m³** | Determines total stored energy |
| Round-trip efficiency | **42%–70%** | Depends on cycle type (diabatic/adiabatic/isothermal) |
| Compressor discharge temperature | **≈ 600 °C** (at 70 bar, adiabatic) | Why diabatic CAES needs natural gas |
| First-law efficiency | **≈ 0.54–0.55** | Huntorf and McIntosh plants |

---

### 1.4 CAES in the KTU 2024 Scheme — Learning Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Module 3 of **PERUT637 – Energy Systems**, Compressed Air Storage is studied as one of the major **Mechanical / Thermodynamic Energy Storage** systems alongside **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** and **Flywheel Energy Storage (FES)**. The emphasis is on **thermodynamic cycle analysis, mass/energy balances, and component-level work calculations** for university exam valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The CAES cycle operates as a **closed (or semi-closed) thermodynamic loop** with three distinct phases:

#### **Phase 1 — Charging (Compression Mode)**
1. Off-peak electricity powers an **electric motor** driving a multi-stage **axial/centrifugal compressor**.
2. Ambient air (≈ 1 bar, 298 K) is compressed to high pressure (40–100 bar).
3. The compressed air is cooled (in intercoolers and aftercoolers) to reduce compressor work and improve storage safety.
4. The cooled, pressurized air is injected into an **underground storage reservoir** (salt cavern, depleted gas field, lined rock cavern, or above-ground steel tanks).

#### **Phase 2 — Storage (Standby Mode)**
1. The cavern acts as a **rigid, constant-volume pressure vessel**.
2. Air is held at high pressure; minor losses occur only due to leakage and slow thermal equilibration with surrounding rock.

#### **Phase 3 — Discharging (Generation Mode)**
1. During peak demand, high-pressure air is withdrawn from storage.
2. The air is pre-heated (recuperator or external combustor in diabatic systems).
3. The hot, high-pressure air expands through a **turbine** coupled to a **generator**, producing electricity.
4. Exhaust air is released to the atmosphere.

---

### 2.2 Classification of CAES Systems (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Heat Management | External Fuel? | Round-Trip Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diabatic CAES** | Heat from compression is vented; external natural gas combusts to reheat air before expansion | **Yes** | **≈ 42–55%** |
| **Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)** | Compression heat is captured in a TES (thermal energy storage) and reused during expansion | No | **≈ 60–70%** |
| **Isothermal CAES** | Heat is removed/added so the process is isothermal; near-ideal (Carnot-like) | No | **≈ 75–80%** (theoretical) |
| **Liquid Air Energy Storage (LAES)** | Air is liquefied (–196 °C); density ≈ 700× gaseous form | No | **≈ 50–60%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Questions in university exams typically distinguish **Diabatic vs. Adiabatic CAES** and ask for **component-wise work/energy analysis**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

All key equations for CAES analysis are consolidated below. **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any cell that requires a vertical bar.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_{1}\,\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Compressor work per kg of air (polytropic) | **J/kg** |
| 2 | $W_{turb} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_{3}\,\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{4}}{P_{3}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\right]$ | Turbine work per kg of air (polytropic) | **J/kg** |
| 3 | $\eta_{round} = \dfrac{W_{turb,\,net}}{W_{comp,\,net}}$ | Round-trip (overall) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{stored} = P_{2}\,(V_{cavern})\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)$ | Energy stored in isothermal cavern (ideal gas) | **J** |
| 5 | $m_{air} = \dfrac{P_{2}V_{2}}{R\,T_{2}}$ | Mass of air stored | **kg** |
| 6 | $W_{comp,\,rev} = R\,T_{1}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)$ | Reversible (isothermal) compressor work | **J/kg** |
| 7 | $\eta_{isentropic} = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{isentropic}}$ | Isentropic efficiency of compressor / turbine | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{2s} = T_{1}\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Isentropic discharge temperature of compressor | **K** |
| 9 | $V_{cavern} = \dfrac{m_{air}\,R\,T}{\gamma\,P_{ave}}$ | Required cavern volume | **m³** |
| 10 | $t_{discharge} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{P_{gen,\,rated}}$ | Discharge duration | **hours** |

Where:
- $n$ = polytropic index (≈ 1.4 for air, isentropic; ≈ 1.0 for isothermal)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats = **1.4** for air
- $P_{1}, P_{2}$ = inlet/outlet pressures of compressor
- $P_{3}, P_{4}$ = inlet/outlet pressures of turbine

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of CAES in Engineering

- **Huntorf CAES (Germany, 1978):** World's first commercial CAES — **290 MW**, diabatic, salt cavern.
- **McIntosh CAES (Alabama, USA, 1991):** **110 MW**, diabatic with a recuperator.
- **Pollegio A-CAES pilot (Switzerland, 2016):** First grid-scale adiabatic CAES demonstration.
- **Hydrostor Toronto Island (Canada):** Commercial underwater CAES using hydrostatic head.

> [!NOTE]
> Engineering applications: load-leveling for renewable-heavy grids, ancillary services (frequency regulation), industrial compressed-air backup (mining, manufacturing), and remote microgrids (offshore wind, island grids).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compressor Work per kg of Air (Polytropic Compression)

The First Law for an open system (control volume) undergoing steady-flow polytropic compression is:

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \dot{m}\,(h_{2} - h_{1})
$$

For an ideal gas undergoing a polytropic process $P v^{n} = \text{constant}$, the specific work input is:

$$
W_{comp} = \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} v\,dP
$$

Using $v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$ and the polytropic relation $T\,P^{\frac{1-n}{n}} = \text{constant}$:

$$
W_{comp} = \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} \dfrac{R\,T_{1}}{P_{1}}\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} dP
$$

$$
W_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_{1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

> **Logical Step:** The factor $\dfrac{n}{n-1}$ arises directly from integrating the polytropic relation. When $n \to 1$ (isothermal), the limit recovers $W_{comp} = R\,T_{1}\,\ln(P_{2}/P_{1})$, confirming consistency with the reversible-isothermal case.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Turbine Work per kg of Air (Polytropic Expansion)

Following the same logic but in the reverse direction:

$$
W_{turb} = \int_{P_{4}}^{P_{3}} v\,dP
$$

Where $P_{3}$ is the turbine inlet pressure (= cavern pressure) and $P_{4}$ is the exhaust pressure.

$$
W_{turb} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_{3}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{4}}{P_{3}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\right]
$$

> **Logical Step:** The sign convention is reversed: turbine produces positive work as gas expands from high $P_{3}$ to low $P_{4}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Stored in a Constant-Volume Cavern (Isothermal Idealization)

Assume the cavern behaves as a **rigid, thermally equilibrated container** (typical of salt caverns in deep geology). For an ideal gas:

$$
P\,V = m\,R\,T = \text{constant}
$$

The stored mechanical work available is:

$$
W_{stored} = \int_{V_{min}}^{V_{max}} P\,dV
$$

But since $V$ is **fixed** for a cavern, we instead compute the work available during **expulsion** at constant temperature:

$$
W_{stored} = \int_{P_{min}}^{P_{max}} V\,dP
$$

Because $V = mRT/P$ at isothermal conditions:

$$
W_{stored} = m\,R\,T \int_{P_{min}}^{P_{max}} \dfrac{dP}{P}
$$

$$
W_{stored} = P_{max}\,V\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)
$$

> **Logical Step:** The result shows that energy storage scales **logarithmically** with pressure ratio, which is why simply increasing pressure yields diminishing returns. Doubling the pressure from 70 to 140 bar adds only ~70% more energy, not 100%.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem Style)

**Problem:** A diabatic CAES plant compresses air from **1.0 bar, 298 K** to **75 bar** using a two-stage compressor with intercooling (assume both stages are isentropic, $n = 1.4$). The compressor overall efficiency is 0.85. The compressed air is stored in a salt cavern at **310 K**. During discharge, air expands through a turbine from **75 bar, 1000 K** (after natural-gas reheating) to **1.05 bar** with an isentropic efficiency of 0.90. Find the **round-trip efficiency** per kg of air.

**Solution Outline:**

**Step 1 — Compressor work (isentropic, single-stage equivalent):**

$$
w_{comp,\,s} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4}\,(287)\,(298)\,\left[\left(75\right)^{0.2857} - 1\right]
$$

$$
w_{comp,\,s} = 1003.9 \times \left[2.617 - 1\right] \approx 1{,}623.5 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual compressor work (with isentropic efficiency):**

$$
w_{comp,\,act} = \dfrac{w_{comp,\,s}}{\eta_{comp}} = \dfrac{1623.5}{0.85} \approx 1{,}910.0 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Turbine work:**

$$
w_{turb,\,s} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4}\,(287)\,(1000)\,\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{1.05}{75}\right)^{0.2857}\right]
$$

$$
w_{turb,\,s} = 1004.5 \times \left[1 - 0.3378\right] \approx 665.6 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual turbine work:**

$$
w_{turb,\,act} = \eta_{turb}\,w_{turb,\,s} = 0.90 \times 665.6 \approx 599.0 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Round-trip efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{w_{turb,\,act}}{w_{comp,\,act}} = \dfrac{599.0}{1910.0} \approx 0.314
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Note:** The low efficiency (≈ 31%) arises because in this simplified cycle we **ignored natural-gas fuel input**. In a real diabatic CAES plant, the natural-gas combustion contributes significantly to the turbine output, raising the **first-law efficiency** of the *whole plant* to ≈ 0.42–0.55, while the *electrical round-trip efficiency* of the air-storage loop alone remains around 30–40%.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Diabatic vs. Adiabatic vs. Isothermal)

| Property | Diabatic CAES | Adiabatic CAES | Isothermal CAES |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression heat | Wasted | Stored in TES | Continuously removed |
| Expansion heat | Added via fuel | From TES | Continuously supplied |
| External fuel | **Yes (natural gas)** | No | No |
| Round-trip efficiency | 42–55% | 60–70% | Up to ~80% (ideal) |
| CO₂ emissions | Yes (lower than peaker gas plant) | Near zero | Zero |
| Commercial maturity | **High** (Huntorf, McIntosh) | Pilot | Lab-scale |
| KTU frequency | Very High | High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Diabatic CAES Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Grid Off-Peak Power] --> B[Electric Motor]
    B --> C[Multi-Stage Compressor with Intercoolers]
    C --> D[Aftercooler Heat Exchanger]
    D --> E[Underground Salt Cavern Storage Reservoir]
    E --> F[Throttle and Pressure Regulating Valve]
    F --> G[Recuperator Waste Heat Recovery]
    G --> H[Natural Gas Combustor Reheater]
    H --> I[High Pressure Expansion Turbine]
    I --> J[Generator and Step Up Transformer]
    J --> K[Grid On-Peak Load]
    L[Compressed Air Discharge Control Valve] --> F
    M[Storage Pressure and Temperature Sensors] --> E
    E --> M
```

**Block Description for Students:**

| Block | Engineering Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | Grid supply | Energy input source |
| B | Electric motor | Converts electrical to mechanical energy |
| C | Multi-stage compressor | Raises air pressure from 1 bar to 70–100 bar |
| D | Aftercooler | Reduces discharge temperature before storage |
| E | Salt cavern / depleted reservoir | Stores compressed air at high pressure |
| F | Throttle valve | Controls discharge flow rate |
| G | Recuperator | Recovers residual heat from turbine exhaust |
| H | Combustor | Heats compressed air using natural gas |
| I | Turbine | Expands air to generate mechanical work |
| J | Generator | Converts mechanical to electrical energy |
| K | Grid | Delivers power to consumers |

---

### 4.2 Thermodynamic State-Point Flow Diagram (T–s Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State 1: Ambient Air 1 bar 298 K] --> P2[State 2: Compressor Discharge High P High T]
    P2 --> P3[State 3: Aftercooler Exit Storage Temperature]
    P3 --> P4[State 4: Cavern Outlet Before Combustor]
    P4 --> P5[State 5: Combustor Outlet Turbine Inlet]
    P5 --> P6[State 6: Turbine Exhaust Atmospheric]
    P1 -.-> P2
    P3 -.-> P4
    P5 -.-> P6
```

---

### 4.3 Charging vs. Discharging Subsystem Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHARGE_MODE[Charging Mode Off Peak Hours]
        C1[Electric Motor] --> C2[Compressor Stage 1]
        C2 --> C3[Intercooler 1]
        C3 --> C4[Compressor Stage 2]
        C4 --> C5[Aftercooler]
        C5 --> C6[Storage Cavern]
    end

    subgraph DISCHARGE_MODE[Discharging Mode Peak Hours]
        D1[Storage Cavern] --> D2[Air Expulsion Valve]
        D2 --> D3[Combustor with Natural Gas]
        D3 --> D4[Turbine High Pressure]
        D4 --> D5[Generator]
        D5 --> D6[Grid Output]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams:** When asked to draw a CAES schematic, always label **State Points 1–6**, indicate pressure and temperature at each point, and clearly separate **charging mode (motor/compressor)** from **discharging mode (turbine/generator)** components.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1a]**

**Q1.** *Define Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) and list any two real-world plant examples.*

**Model Answer:**
CAES is a mechanical energy storage technology in which off-peak electrical energy is used to compress air into underground reservoirs, then released to drive turbines for electricity generation during peak demand. Two real-world examples are **(i) Huntorf CAES plant, Germany (290 MW)** and **(ii) McIntosh CAES plant, Alabama, USA (110 MW)**. [3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each example]

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1b]**

**Q2.** *Distinguish between adiabatic and diabatic CAES systems.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Diabatic CAES | Adiabatic CAES |
|---|---|---|
| Compression heat | Released to environment | Stored in Thermal Energy Storage (TES) |
| External fuel | Natural gas used | No external fuel |
| Efficiency | 42–55% | 60–70% |
| Emissions | Lower CO₂ (fuel burned) | Near-zero CO₂ |

[3 marks: tabular differentiation + correct values]

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Q3 (Module 3 Internal Choice)]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **Diabatic Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** system. Clearly mark the state points and major components. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Air enters a two-stage compressor with intercooling at **1 bar, 300 K** and is compressed to **60 bar**. Each stage operates isentropically with equal pressure ratios. Calculate: **(i)** the total compressor work per kg of air, and **(ii)** the intermediate (intercooler) pressure. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of Diabatic CAES [7 marks]:**

1. **Charging phase:** Off-peak grid electricity powers an electric motor → drives multi-stage compressor → air compressed from ~1 bar to 60–80 bar → cooled in intercoolers/aftercooler → injected into underground salt cavern. [2 marks]
2. **Storage phase:** Cavern maintains air at high pressure; thermal mass of rock keeps temperature stable. [1 mark]
3. **Discharging phase:** During peak demand, high-pressure air is released → mixed with natural gas in a combustor → hot, high-pressure gas expands through turbine → turbine drives generator → electricity delivered to grid. [2 marks]
4. **Sketch:** Should include motor, compressor, intercoolers, cavern, throttle valve, combustor, turbine, generator, and six state points 1–6. [2 marks]

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

Given: $P_{1} = 1$ bar, $T_{1} = 300$ K, $P_{3} = 60$ bar, $n = \gamma = 1.4$.

**(i) Equal pressure ratio per stage:**

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{3}}{P_{1}}} = \sqrt{60} \approx 7.746
$$

Pressure ratio per stage $r_{p} = 7.746$.

**(ii) Intermediate (intercooler) pressure:**

$$
P_{2} = P_{1}\,r_{p} = 1 \times 7.746 = 7.746 \text{ bar}
$$

(After intercooling, $T_{2} = T_{1} = 300$ K; the second stage works from 7.746 bar, 300 K to 60 bar.)

**(iii) Work per stage (isentropic):**

$$
w_{s} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\,R\,T_{1}\left[r_{p}^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]
$$

$$
w_{s} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4}\,(287)\,(300)\,\left[(7.746)^{0.2857} - 1\right]
$$

$$
w_{s} = 1004.5 \times \left[1.667 - 1\right] = 670.0 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(iv) Total compressor work:**

$$
w_{total} = 2 \times 670.0 = 1{,}340.0 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

[7 marks: 1 mark r, 1 mark P₂, 2 marks per-stage work formula, 1 mark evaluation, 2 marks total]

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **work input to a compressor** compressing air polytropically from pressure $P_{1}$ to $P_{2}$ with inlet temperature $T_{1}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A diabatic CAES plant stores **$5 \times 10^{5}$ m³** of air in a salt cavern at **70 bar, 320 K**. The minimum operating pressure is **45 bar**. Assuming **isothermal storage**, calculate the energy stored in the cavern. Take $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Polytropic Compressor Work [7 marks]:**

Starting from the steady-flow energy equation:

$$
W_{comp} = \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} v\,dP
$$

For a polytropic process $P v^{n} = \text{constant}$, so $v = \dfrac{P_{1}^{1/n} v_{1}}{P^{1/n}}$:

$$
W_{comp} = P_{1}^{1/n} v_{1} \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} P^{-1/n}\,dP
$$

Integrating:

$$
W_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_{1}\,v_{1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

Using $P_{1}v_{1} = R\,T_{1}$:

$$
W_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_{1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

[7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for integration, 2 marks for final form with $RT_1$]

**(b) Energy Stored in Isothermal Cavern [7 marks]:**

For isothermal ideal-gas storage:

$$
E_{stored} = P_{2}\,V\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)
$$

Substituting $P_{2} = 70 \times 10^{5}$ Pa, $V = 5 \times 10^{5}$ m³, $P_{max}/P_{min} = 70/45$:

$$
E_{stored} = (70 \times 10^{5})\,(5 \times 10^{5})\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{70}{45}\right)
$$

$$
E_{stored} = 3.5 \times 10^{12} \times \ln(1.5556)
$$

$$
E_{stored} = 3.5 \times 10^{12} \times 0.4418 = 1.546 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}
$$

Converting to kWh:

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1.546 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 4.29 \times 10^{5} \text{ kWh} \approx 429 \text{ MWh}
$$

[7 marks: 2 marks formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks evaluation, 1 mark unit conversion]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting intercooling assumption:** When a problem says "two-stage compressor," always assume **intercooling to initial temperature**. Marks are routinely lost when students treat it as single-stage.
> - **Confusing $n$ (polytropic) with $\gamma$ (adiabatic index):** In isentropic air, $n = \gamma = 1.4$, but in real machines $n$ may differ (≈ 1.35). Read the question carefully.
> - **Ignoring cavern isothermal assumption:** The formula $E = PV\ln(P_{max}/P_{min})$ applies **only** under isothermal idealization. For adiabatic storage, use temperature-dependent relations.
> - **Unit mismatch:** Pressures must be in **Pa** (not bar) when energy is computed in Joules. **1 bar = $10^{5}$ Pa**.
> - **Mixing up round-trip vs. first-law efficiency:** Round-trip uses *electrical work only*; first-law includes *fuel energy input*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Compressed Air Storage (Module 3, PERUT637)**

- **CAES** stores energy as **high-pressure air** in underground reservoirs (salt caverns, depleted gas fields, aquifers) or in steel vessels.
- **Cycle phases:** Charging (compression) → Storage (constant volume) → Discharging (expansion with possible reheating).
- **Three main types:**
  - *Diabatic* (uses natural gas; 42–55% efficiency)
  - *Adiabatic* (stores compression heat in TES; 60–70%)
  - *Isothermal* (theoretical ideal; up to 80%)
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - Compressor work: $\dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_{1}\left[(P_{2}/P_{1})^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$
  - Turbine work: $\dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_{3}\left[1 - (P_{4}/P_{3})^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\right]$
  - Isothermal storage energy: $P\,V\,\ln(P_{max}/P_{min})$
- **Constants:** $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K); $\gamma = 1.4$; **1 bar = $10^{5}$ Pa**.
- **Real plants:** Huntorf (290 MW, Germany, 1978), McIntosh (110 MW, USA, 1991).
- **Engineering advantages:** Long discharge duration (8–26 h), high cycle life (>10,000 cycles), grid-scale capacity, geographic flexibility.
- **Limitations:** Requires **specific geology** (salt caverns), still partially dependent on **fossil fuels** (diabatic), lower energy density than pumped hydro.
- **Typical exam keywords:** "isothermal cavern," "polytropic compression," "diabatic vs. adiabatic," "round-trip efficiency," "intercooling," "state points 1–6," "multi-stage compressor with reheat."
- **Distinguish from related systems:** CAES ≠ PHS (uses water, not air); CAES ≠ LAES (liquefies air to –196 °C); CAES ≠ Flywheel (kinetic, not pneumatic).

> **Best of luck for your KTU Board Exams!** Remember: in CAES problems, **always state assumptions** (isentropic, isothermal, intercooling) explicitly to secure full marks on the "approach" component of valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flywheel Energy Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flywheel Energy Storage (FES)** is a **mechanical energy storage** system that stores rotational kinetic energy in a spinning mass (rotor). Energy is delivered to the flywheel by applying a torque, causing it to spin faster; energy is extracted by drawing torque from the spinning rotor, which slows it down. The stored energy is expressed mathematically as $E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the flywheel is treated as a **kinetic battery** — a device whose state of charge (SOC) is determined solely by its rotational speed. It belongs to the class of **short-duration, high-power-density** storage systems, complementing batteries (high energy) and supercapacitors (very high power, very low energy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Energy density (modern composite):** 100–200 Wh/kg
> - **Power density:** 5,000–10,000 W/kg
> - **Cycle life:** > 100,000 deep cycles
> - **Self-discharge rate:** ~10–20% per hour (vacuum-housed systems much lower)
> - **Round-trip efficiency:** 85–95%

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a child on a **bicycle wheel**. When you spin the wheel hard with your hand, it keeps rotating for a long time. That "spinning" is **stored kinetic energy**. When you grab the wheel and stop it with friction, you feel the energy leave as heat on your palm. A flywheel does the exact same thing, but with a precisely engineered steel or carbon-fiber rotor, vacuum housing, and magnetic bearings, so the energy stays for minutes instead of seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Doubling the rotation speed **quadruples** the stored energy. Doubling the mass **doubles** the energy. Doubling the radius (for a ring-shaped rotor) **quadruples** the energy. Always remember: speed squared dominates!

---

## 1.3 Classification of Flywheels (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

| Class | Material | Speed Range | Energy Density | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-speed (Steel)** | Forged/AISI steel | < 6,000 rpm | 5–30 Wh/kg | Buses, industrial UPS, gyroscopes |
| **High-speed (Composite)** | Carbon-fiber/epoxy overwrap on Al hub | 30,000–60,000 rpm | 100–200 Wh/kg | Grid regulation, hybrid vehicles, satellites |

> [!NOTE]
> The "tip speed" $v = \omega r$ is the **governing safety parameter**. Modern composite flywheels operate at $v \approx 700$–$1,000$ m/s, just below the tensile failure limit of carbon fiber.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Charging Mode:** An electric motor (or M/G set) applies torque $\tau_m$ to the rotor. Work done equals the change in rotational kinetic energy:
$$W_{in} = \int \tau_m \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}I\omega_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}I\omega_1^2$$
2. **Standby Mode:** The rotor spins in a near-vacuum chamber on **magnetic or active-electromagnetic bearings** to minimize friction. Only parasitic losses (windage, bearing drag, eddy currents) act to slow it.
3. **Discharging Mode:** The rotor is coupled to a generator. A load torque $\tau_L$ extracts power:
$$P_{out} = \tau_L \cdot \omega = -I\omega \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$
4. **State of Charge (SOC):** Defined in terms of speed:
$$SOC = \frac{\omega^2 - \omega_{min}^2}{\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2}$$
where $\omega_{min}$ is the minimum usable speed (often 50% of $\omega_{max}$) and $\omega_{max}$ is the rated top speed.
5. **Why minimum speed?** A flywheel cannot deliver power below some $\omega_{min}$ because both voltage (EMF in a generator: $E \propto \omega$) and torque (for a given current) must remain within the power electronics' operating window.

## 2.2 Governing Physics — The "Why" and the "How"

- **Why use a hollow rotor?** For a given mass $m$, the moment of inertia is maximized when the mass is placed at the **largest possible radius** (because $I \propto r^2$). Hence a thin, large-diameter rim (ring) stores more energy per kilogram than a solid disk.
- **Why use carbon fiber?** Tensile strength limits the maximum tip speed. Carbon fiber's specific strength ($\sigma/\rho$) is ~5–10× that of steel, allowing a much higher $v_{max}$, and thus a much higher $\omega_{max}$ for the same $r$ — energy goes as $\omega^2$.
- **Why vacuum housing?** Air drag scales with $\rho_{air}\omega^2 r^3$. A $10^{-6}$ mbar vacuum cuts windage losses by >$10^5\times$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU University Exams. Memorize the unit of every variable.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic energy stored | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | $E$[J], $I$[kg·m²], $\omega$[rad/s] | Fundamental equation |
| 2 | Power extracted | $P = \tau\omega$ | $P$[W], $\tau$[N·m] | Discharge power |
| 3 | Solid disk rotor | $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$ | $m$[kg], $r$[m] | Uniform disk |
| 4 | Thin ring rotor | $I = mr^2$ | — | Maximum $I$/mass ratio |
| 5 | Hollow cylinder | $I = \tfrac{1}{2}m(r_1^2 + r_2^2)$ | inner $r_1$, outer $r_2$ | Realistic rim design |
| 6 | Energy of ring | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2\omega^2$ | — | Combine (1) and (4) |
| 7 | Specific energy | $e = \tfrac{1}{2}r^2\omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}v^2$ | $e$[J/kg], $v$[m/s] | Equal to $\tfrac{1}{2}v_{tip}^2$ |
| 8 | Hoop stress | $\sigma = \rho v^2$ | $\sigma$[Pa], $\rho$[kg/m³] | Bursting limit |
| 9 | Discharge time | $t = \tfrac{E_{stored}}{P_{avg}} = \tfrac{I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}{2P_{avg}}$ | $t$[s] | With $P_{avg}$ |
| 10 | Self-discharge | $\Delta E/E = 1 - e^{-ft}$ | $f$[s⁻¹] | $f$ is friction factor |
| 11 | Round-trip eff. | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{loss}}{E_{in}}$ | — | 85–95% typical |
| 12 | SOC | $SOC = \dfrac{\omega^2 - \omega_{min}^2}{\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2}$ | dimensionless | Linear in $E$ |
| 13 | Charging power | $P_{ch} = I\omega \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | — | — |
| 14 | Tip speed | $v = \omega r$ | $v$[m/s] | Safety limit $< v_{max}$ |

> **Pitfall:** $\omega$ in the energy formula **MUST** be in **rad/s**, not rpm. Conversion: $\omega[\text{rad/s}] = 2\pi N[\text{rps}] = \dfrac{2\pi N_{rpm}}{60}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid frequency regulation** (Beacon Power, USA — 20 MW plants).
- **Regenerative braking** in Formula 1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems) and in metro rail (e.g., Bombardier MITRAC).
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** for data centers.
- **Satellite attitude control** (reaction wheels — flywheels operating in reverse as actuators).
- **Pulsed-power loads** in particle accelerators and naval railguns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Energy Stored in a Hollow Cylindrical Rotor

We derive the kinetic energy of a thin-walled hollow cylinder of mass $m$, inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, length $L$, and mass density $\rho$, rotating about its central axis at angular speed $\omega$.

**Step 1.** A differential ring of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and length $L$ has mass
$$dm = \rho \cdot (2\pi r L) \cdot dr$$

**Step 2.** The differential moment of inertia (about the axis) of that ring is
$$dI = r^2 \, dm = 2\pi \rho L r^3 \, dr$$

**Step 3.** Integrate from $r_1$ to $r_2$:
$$I = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} 2\pi \rho L r^3 \, dr = 2\pi \rho L \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = \frac{\pi \rho L}{2}(r_2^4 - r_1^4)$$

**Step 4.** Express the total mass:
$$m = \rho \cdot \pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2)L$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $\rho L$:
$$\rho L = \frac{m}{\pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2)}$$

**Step 6.** Substitute into the $I$ expression:
$$I = \frac{\pi}{2}\cdot \frac{m}{\pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2)} \cdot (r_2^4 - r_1^4)$$

**Step 7.** Factor the difference of fourth powers:
$$r_2^4 - r_1^4 = (r_2^2 - r_1^2)(r_2^2 + r_1^2)$$

**Step 8.** Cancel the common term $(r_2^2 - r_1^2)$:
$$I = \frac{1}{2}m(r_2^2 + r_1^2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This confirms Row 5 of the Formula Sheet.** For a thin rim where $r_1 \to r_2 = r$, we recover $I = mr^2$ (the thin-ring limit).

## 3.2 Derivation B: Hoop Stress in a Spinning Thin Ring

Consider a thin ring rotating at $\omega$ with tip speed $v = \omega r$. A small element $d\theta$ of the ring is subject to a centripetal force. We derive the tensile (hoop) stress.

**Step 1.** Mass of the small element subtending angle $d\theta$ at the centre:
$$dm = \frac{m}{2\pi}\, d\theta$$

**Step 2.** Centripetal force on this element (mass × centripetal acceleration):
$$dF_c = dm \cdot \omega^2 r = \frac{m\omega^2 r}{2\pi} d\theta$$

**Step 3.** Two halves of the ring each pull on the element with tensile force $T$, so the inward component of $2T$ is $2T \sin(d\theta/2) \approx T \, d\theta$ for small $d\theta$.

**Step 4.** Equating:
$$T \, d\theta = \frac{m\omega^2 r}{2\pi} d\theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T = \frac{m\omega^2 r}{2\pi}$$

**Step 5.** Cross-sectional area of the thin ring (rectangular $b \times h$): $A = bh$. Mass is $m = \rho \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot A$.

**Step 6.** Stress is force over area:
$$\sigma = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{m\omega^2 r}{2\pi A} = \frac{\rho \cdot 2\pi r A \cdot \omega^2 r}{2\pi A} = \rho r^2 \omega^2 = \rho v^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: $\sigma_{hoop} = \rho v^2$.** For steel ($\rho \approx 7,850$ kg/m³) the bursting speed limit is $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{UTS}/\rho} \approx 480$ m/s. For carbon-fiber/epoxy composite ($v_{max} \approx 1,000$ m/s), much higher energy density is feasible.

## 3.3 Derivation C: Discharge Time from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$ at Constant Power

**Step 1.** Energy balance. The energy delivered equals the drop in kinetic energy:
$$E_{del} = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega_1^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega_2^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

**Step 2.** At constant average power $P_{avg}$, the discharge time is:
$$t_{dis} = \frac{E_{del}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}{2P_{avg}}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $I = mr^2$ for a thin ring:
$$t_{dis} = \frac{mr^2(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}{2P_{avg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** If $P$ is in **kW**, convert to W before substitution. If $m$ is in **tonnes**, convert to kg. This is where students most often lose 1–2 marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy stored** | $m$, $r$, $\omega$ | $E$ [J or Wh] | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2\omega^2$ |
| **Discharge time** | $E$, $P$ | $t$ | $t = E/P$ |
| **Power for given $\Delta\omega$** | $I$, $\omega_1$, $\omega_2$, $t$ | $P$ | $P = \tfrac{I(\omega_1^2-\omega_2^2)}{2t}$ |
| **SOC calculation** | $\omega$, $\omega_{min}$, $\omega_{max}$ | $SOC$ [%] | $SOC = (\omega^2 - \omega_{min}^2)/(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2)$ |
| **Tip speed check** | $\omega$, $r$ | $v$ | $v = \omega r$ |
| **Hoop stress** | $\rho$, $v$ | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \rho v^2$ |
| **Mass for given energy** | $E$, $r$, $\omega$ | $m$ | $m = 2E/(r^2\omega^2)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Power Grid or Load] --> B[Power Electronics InverterRectifier]
    B --> C[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine acts as Motor or Generator]
    C --> D[Shaft Coupling with Magnetic Gearbox Optional]
    D --> E[High Speed Composite Rotor spinning in vacuum]
    E --> F[Vacuum Chamber housing at 10 to the power minus6 mbar]
    E --> G[Active Electromagnetic Bearings levitate the rotor]
    H[Cooling System for stator and bearings] --> C
    I[Control and Monitoring Unit measures speed temperature vacuum] --> B
    I --> G
    J[Energy Discharge Path to Load] --> A
    K[Energy Charge Path from Grid] --> A
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Charging, Storage, and Discharging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Charge Mode] --> Q1[Grid supplies AC]
    Q1 --> R1[Rectifier converts AC to DC]
    R1 --> S1[Inverter drives PMSM as Motor]
    S1 --> T1[Applies torque to Rotor]
    T1 --> U1[Rotor angular velocity increases]
    U1 --> V1[Kinetic Energy E equals half I omega squared stored]
    V1 --> W1[Storage Mode standby losses only]
    W1 --> X1[Discharge Mode Rotor slows down]
    X1 --> Y1[PMSM acts as Generator producing AC]
    Y1 --> Z1[Inverter Rectifier conditions output]
    Z1 --> AA1[Power delivered to Load]
```

## 4.3 Flywheel Rotor Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flywheel Rotor Geometry Selection] --> B[Solid Disk]
    A --> C[Thin Ring]
    A --> D[Hollow Cylinder]
    B --> B1[I equals half m r squared]
    B --> B2[Lowest I per unit mass]
    B --> B3[Used in low speed applications]
    C --> C1[I equals m r squared]
    C --> C2[Maximum I per unit mass]
    C --> C3[High energy density preferred design]
    D --> D1[I equals half m r1 squared plus r2 squared]
    D --> D2[Practical compromise for composite rotors]
    D --> D3[Allows rim mass with hub cutout]
```

## 4.4 SOC vs Angular Velocity Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[omega equals 0] --> B[SOC equals 0 percent]
    B --> C[omega equals omega min]
    C --> D[SOC equals 0 percent lower cutoff]
    D --> E[Linear region SOC proportional to omega squared]
    E --> F[omega equals omega max]
    F --> G[SOC equals 100 percent]
    G --> H[Safety trip mechanical overspeed]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Block Diagram Interpretation:** The Power Electronics Inverter/Rectifier is *bidirectional* — it functions as a rectifier during charging (AC→DC) and as an inverter during discharging (DC→AC). The Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine (PMSM) functions as a motor during charging and as a generator during discharging. The Active Electromagnetic Bearings actively control rotor position using feedback from the Control and Monitoring Unit, achieving micro-meter levitation gaps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** 
Define the term *Specific Energy* of a flywheel. Derive its expression in terms of tip speed.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Specific energy of a flywheel is the kinetic energy stored per unit mass of the rotor. It has units of J/kg (or Wh/kg).

**Derivation (2 marks):** For a thin rim, $E = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2\omega^2$. Dividing by $m$:
$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{1}{2}r^2\omega^2 = \frac{1}{2}(\omega r)^2 = \frac{1}{2}v_{tip}^2$$

Hence specific energy depends **only on the tip speed**, not on the size or mass of the rotor.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
List any three **advantages** of a flywheel over a chemical battery for short-duration energy storage.

**Model Answer (any 3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

1. **Very long cycle life** (> 100,000 deep cycles vs ~500–2,000 for Li-ion).
2. **High power density** (5,000–10,000 W/kg) for grid pulse and KERS applications.
3. **SOC is precisely known** from a single speed measurement — no complex BMS required.
4. **Wide operating temperature range** and no chemical degradation.
5. **Environmentally benign** — no heavy metals, no thermal runaway.

---

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** A flywheel energy storage system has a solid cylindrical rotor of mass **m = 250 kg** and radius **r = 0.40 m**, rotating at a maximum speed of **10,000 rpm**. Compute:
1. The moment of inertia of the rotor. **(2 marks)**
2. The kinetic energy stored at top speed, in MJ and in kWh. **(3 marks)**
3. The maximum deliverable power if it discharges from 10,000 rpm to 5,000 rpm in 60 seconds. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A composite-rim flywheel is to deliver **5 kWh** of useful energy, operating between $\omega_{max} = 50,000$ rpm and $\omega_{min} = 25,000$ rpm. The rim has $r_1 = 0.20$ m and $r_2 = 0.25$ m. Compute:
1. The required mass of the rim. **(4 marks)**
2. The tip speed at $\omega_{max}$ and comment on whether a carbon-fiber rim is necessary. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1. Convert to SI units.**
$$\omega_{max} = \frac{2\pi \times 10{,}000}{60} = 1047.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2. Moment of inertia of solid cylinder.**
$$I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(250)(0.40)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(250)(0.16) = 20.0 \text{ kg·m}^2 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 3. Kinetic energy at top speed.**
$$E_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(20.0)(1047.2)^2 = 10.0 \times 1.0966 \times 10^6 = 1.0966 \times 10^7 \text{ J} \approx 10.97 \text{ MJ}$$

Convert to kWh: 
$$E_{max} = \frac{10.97 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 3.047 \text{ kWh} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**Step 4. Power at 60 s discharge.**

Convert $\omega_{min} = 5,000$ rpm $= 523.6$ rad/s.
$$P_{avg} = \frac{I(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2)}{2t} = \frac{20.0 \times ((1047.2)^2 - (523.6)^2)}{2 \times 60}$$
$$= \frac{20.0 \times (1,096,624 - 274,159)}{120} = \frac{20.0 \times 822,465}{120} = 137,078 \text{ W} \approx 137.1 \text{ kW} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1. Energy window.**
Useful energy window (a flywheel cannot go to zero speed):
$$E_{use} = \tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2) = 5 \text{ kWh} = 5 \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 1.8 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2. Convert angular speeds.**
$$\omega_{max} = \frac{2\pi \times 50{,}000}{60} = 5235.99 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_{min} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{2} = 2617.99 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3. Difference of squares.**
$$\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2 = (5235.99)^2 - (2617.99)^2 = 27.416 \times 10^6 - 6.854 \times 10^6 = 20.562 \times 10^6 \text{ (rad/s)}^2$$

**Step 4. Required moment of inertia.**
$$I = \frac{2 E_{use}}{\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2} = \frac{2 \times 1.8 \times 10^7}{20.562 \times 10^6} = 1.751 \text{ kg·m}^2 \quad \text{[4 marks]}$$

**Step 5. Rim mass from $I = \tfrac{1}{2}m(r_2^2 + r_1^2)$.**
$$r_2^2 + r_1^2 = (0.25)^2 + (0.20)^2 = 0.0625 + 0.0400 = 0.1025 \text{ m}^2$$
$$m = \frac{2I}{r_2^2 + r_1^2} = \frac{2 \times 1.751}{0.1025} = 34.16 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6. Tip speed at $\omega_{max}$.**
$$v = \omega_{max} r_{mean} = 5235.99 \times 0.225 = 1,178 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7. Comment.** A tip speed of 1,178 m/s is **far beyond** the bursting limit of steel (≈ 480 m/s). Therefore, a **carbon-fiber composite rim is essential** — it can sustain tip speeds of 1,000+ m/s safely. **[3 marks]**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) in **charging** and **discharging** modes. List any four advantages of FESS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin-ring flywheel of mass **40 kg** and radius **0.30 m** is rated for a maximum safe tip speed of **600 m/s**.
1. Calculate the maximum safe angular velocity and the corresponding kinetic energy stored. **(3 marks)**
2. If the flywheel is to be discharged at a **constant torque of 50 N·m**, find the time taken for the speed to drop from maximum to 30% of maximum. **(4 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks):

**Block diagram (5 marks):** Draw the system with the following blocks and labeled flow arrows:

```
Grid ⇄ Rectifier/Inverter ⇄ PMSM (Motor/Generator) ⇄ Rotor (in vacuum) ⇄ Load
                                              ↑
                                   Magnetic Bearings + Control
```

**Operation:**
- **Charging:** Grid AC → Rectifier → DC bus → Inverter → PMSM acts as motor → applies torque → rotor accelerates → KE stored.
- **Discharging:** Rotor decelerates → PMSM acts as generator → produces AC → Rectifier/inverter conditions → delivers to load.

**Four advantages (1/2 mark each = 2 marks):**
1. High cycle life (>10⁵ cycles). 2. Precise SOC via speed measurement. 3. High power density. 4. Environmentally clean (no chemicals).

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1. Maximum angular velocity.**
$$\omega_{max} = \frac{v_{max}}{r} = \frac{600}{0.30} = 2000 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

**Step 2. Moment of inertia of thin ring.**
$$I = mr^2 = 40 \times (0.30)^2 = 40 \times 0.09 = 3.6 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3. Maximum kinetic energy.**
$$E_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega_{max}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(3.6)(2000)^2 = 0.5 \times 3.6 \times 4 \times 10^6 = 7.2 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 7.2 \text{ MJ} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

**Step 4. Final speed (30% of max).**
$$\omega_f = 0.30 \times 2000 = 600 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5. Angular deceleration under constant torque.**
$$\alpha = \frac{\tau}{I} = \frac{50}{3.6} = 13.889 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 6. Time to drop from 2000 to 600 rad/s.**
$$t = \frac{\omega_i - \omega_f}{\alpha} = \frac{2000 - 600}{13.889} = \frac{1400}{13.889} \approx 100.8 \text{ s} \quad \text{[4 marks]}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** $\omega$ in rpm vs rad/s is the #1 mistake. The 2π/60 conversion is **mandatory** for every energy/power calculation.
> 2. **Wrong moment of inertia formula:** A *thin ring* uses $I = mr^2$, but a *solid disk* uses $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$. Mixing these costs full marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the lower speed cutoff:** A flywheel cannot deliver energy down to $\omega = 0$. Always use $E_{use} = \tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2)$ for usable energy. Treating $E_{max}$ as $E_{use}$ is incorrect.
> 4. **Constant-torque vs constant-power discharge:** With constant torque, deceleration $\alpha$ is constant. With constant power, $\alpha$ changes as $\omega$ changes. **Read the problem wording carefully.**
> 5. **Final answer without units:** KTU examiners deduct 1/2 mark for missing units in numerical answers.

---

## **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER** (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Master formula:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$. **Energy scales with $\omega^2$**, not with $\omega$.
- [x] **Convert rpm → rad/s:** always multiply by $2\pi/60 \approx 0.10472$.
- [x] **Thin ring:** $I = mr^2$ (max energy/mass). **Solid disk:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$. **Hollow cylinder:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2}m(r_1^2 + r_2^2)$.
- [x] **Specific energy:** $e = \tfrac{1}{2}v_{tip}^2$ — depends only on tip speed.
- [x] **Hoop stress:** $\sigma = \rho v^2$ — sets the bursting limit.
- [x] **State of Charge:** $SOC = \dfrac{\omega^2 - \omega_{min}^2}{\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2}$.
- [x] **Discharge time (constant power):** $t = \dfrac{I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}{2P}$.
- [x] **Modern FESS specifics:** composite rotor, vacuum housing, magnetic bearings, PMSM, 85–95% round-trip efficiency.
- [x] **Typical tip speeds:** steel ≈ 480 m/s, carbon-fiber ≈ 1,000 m/s.
- [x] **KTU classification:** mechanical, short-duration, high-power-density energy storage.
- [x] **Applications:** grid frequency regulation, KERS, UPS, satellite reaction wheels, pulsed power.
- [x] **Pitfall summary:** always check units, always use the correct $I$ formula, never assume $\omega_{min} = 0$ for usable energy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Storage - Battery: Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Storage - Battery: Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Battery Storage — Battery: Specification

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **battery specification** is a structured, quantitative, and qualitative description of the electrical, mechanical, thermal, and longevity parameters of an electrochemical storage device. It serves as the engineering contract between the cell manufacturer, the system integrator, and the end-user, ensuring that the battery performs reliably within a defined operational envelope.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PERUT637 – Energy Systems**, "battery specification" is treated as the language of battery engineering. Just like a mechanical engineer reads a *datasheet of a centrifugal pump* (head, flow rate, NPSH, efficiency curve) before selection, a renewable energy engineer must read and interpret a battery datasheet before integrating it into a **solar PV off-grid system**, a **wind–storage hybrid**, or an **EV powertrain**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

Imagine a battery as a **water tank connected between a water source and a tap**:

| Water Tank Parameter | Battery Equivalent | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Tank capacity (liters) | **Capacity (Ah)** | Total charge the battery can deliver |
| Water pressure (bar) | **Voltage (V)** | Electrical potential driving the current |
| Flow rate (liters/sec) | **Current (A)** | Rate of charge or discharge |
| Wall thickness / material | **Chemistry (LFP, NMC, LA)** | Determines energy density & safety |
| Time before tank rusts | **Calendar Life (years)** | Operational lifespan regardless of use |
| Filling/emptying cycles | **Cycle Life** | Number of full charge–discharge cycles |
| Water lost through evaporation | **Self-Discharge (%/month)** | Capacity loss when idle |
| Tank burst pressure | **Max Safe Discharge Current (C-rate)** | Upper current limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why specification matters in KTU context:** Almost every numerical problem in the Energy Systems module — sizing a PV battery bank, choosing an EV battery pack, or calculating backup time for a microgrid — directly uses the *specification parameters* of the battery. A student who cannot interpret a battery datasheet cannot solve these problems.

### Universal Battery Specification — Master Parameter List

A professional-grade battery specification (as published by manufacturers like **CATL, BYD, LG Energy Solution, Exide, Amara Raja, or Tata Chemicals**) typically contains the following 14 parameters:

1. **Nominal Voltage (V_n)**
2. **Open-Circuit Voltage / EMF (V_oc)**
3. **Cut-off / End-of-Discharge Voltage (V_cut)**
4. **Nominal Capacity (C_n) in Ah**
5. **Nominal Energy (E_n) in Wh or kWh**
6. **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** and **Energy Density (Wh/L)**
7. **Specific Power (W/kg)** and **Power Density (W/L)**
8. **C-Rate (C)**
9. **Depth of Discharge (DoD)**
10. **State of Charge (SoC)**
11. **State of Health (SoH)**
12. **Internal Resistance (R_int)**
13. **Self-Discharge Rate (%/month)**
14. **Cycle Life (cycles) at a given DoD**
15. **Calendar Life (years)**
16. **Operating Temperature Range (°C)**
17. **Round-Trip Efficiency (η_RT, %)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold constants & standard metrics:**
> - Faraday's constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol**
> - Standard temperature reference: **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)**
> - 1 kWh = **3.6 MJ**
> - Standard cell EMF for lead-acid: **2.105 V/cell**; for LFP: **3.2 V/cell**; for NMC: **3.7 V/cell**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Voltage Specifications

A battery is not a single-voltage source. Three distinct voltages must be specified:

**(a) Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc or EMF, E):**
The terminal voltage when no load is connected. Determined by the Nernst equation from cell chemistry.

**(b) Nominal Voltage (V_n):**
The "advertised" or "rated" voltage — a useful approximation for design calculations. For a **lead-acid cell**, V_n = 2 V. For a **lithium-ion NMC cell**, V_n = 3.6–3.7 V. For **LFP**, V_n = 3.2 V.

**(c) Cut-off Voltage (V_cut):**
The minimum voltage at which discharge must be terminated to prevent irreversible damage. For lead-acid, V_cut ≈ 1.75 V/cell. For LFP, V_cut ≈ 2.5 V/cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage Under Load:** During discharge, terminal voltage drops below V_oc due to ohmic, concentration, and activation overpotentials. This drop is captured by the **internal resistance R_int**.

$$V_{terminal} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

## 2.2 Capacity Specifications

**Capacity (C)** is the total charge a fully charged battery can deliver under specified conditions (temperature, discharge rate, end voltage). Units: **Ampere-hours (Ah)**.

$$Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{[Ah]}$$

The actual deliverable charge depends on discharge current — described by **Peukert's Law** (Section 3 covers the derivation):

$$C_p = I^n \cdot t$$

where n is the Peukert exponent (≈ 1.05–1.2 for Li-ion, 1.1–1.3 for lead-acid).

**Energy Capacity (E)** is the total energy, in Wh or kWh:

$$E = V_n \cdot C_n = \int_{0}^{t_{end}} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$$

## 2.3 Energy & Power Density

These are the most critical specifications for **EV and aerospace** selection:

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{E \text{[Wh]}}{m \text{[kg]}} \quad \text{[Wh/kg]}$$

$$\text{Energy Density} = \frac{E \text{[Wh]}}{V_{vol} \text{[L]}} \quad \text{[Wh/L]}$$

$$\text{Specific Power} = \frac{P_{max} \text{[W]}}{m \text{[kg]}} \quad \text{[W/kg]}$$

$$\text{Power Density} = \frac{P_{max} \text{[W]}}{V_{vol} \text{[L]}} \quad \text{[W/L]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Ragone Plot intuition:** Specific energy (Wh/kg) is plotted on the x-axis (log) and specific power (W/kg) on the y-axis (log). **Supercapacitors** sit top-left (high power, low energy); **lithium-ion** sits middle-right; **lead-acid** sits bottom-left. This single plot is often asked in KTU vivas.

## 2.4 C-Rate

The **C-rate** normalizes current to capacity, allowing apples-to-apples comparison:

$$\text{C-rate} = \frac{I \text{[A]}}{C_n \text{[Ah]}}$$

- **1C** = full discharge in 1 hour
- **0.5C** = full discharge in 2 hours (also called the "C/2" or "2-hour" rate)
- **2C** = full discharge in 30 minutes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's note:** A 100 Ah battery discharged at 50 A = 0.5C, lasting 2 hours. A 100 Ah battery discharged at 200 A = 2C, lasting 30 minutes — but it will deliver **less than 100 Ah** due to Peukert losses.

## 2.5 Depth of Discharge (DoD) & State of Charge (SoC)

These are **complementary** parameters:

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \frac{C_{remaining}}{C_{rated}} = 1 - \text{DoD}(t)$$

$$\text{DoD}(t) = \frac{C_{discharged}}{C_{rated}}$$

For a **100 Ah battery** that has delivered 30 Ah:
- DoD = 30 %
- SoC = 70 %

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** DoD and SoC are often confused with each other. DoD = "how much has been used", SoC = "how much remains". They sum to 100 %, not multiply.

## 2.6 State of Health (SoH)

SoH quantifies degradation compared to a fresh battery:

$$\text{SoH}(\%) = \frac{C_{actual, aged}}{C_{rated, new}} \times 100$$

A battery with SoH < 80 % is generally considered **End-of-Life (EoL)** for EV applications.

## 2.7 Internal Resistance (R_int)

A non-zero resistance causes:
1. **Voltage sag** under load: $\Delta V = I \cdot R_{int}$
2. **I²R heating losses**: $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{int}$
3. **Reduced round-trip efficiency**

$$R_{int} = R_{ohmic} + R_{ct} \text{ (charge transfer)}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}} \quad \text{(maximum power transfer theorem)}$$

## 2.8 Round-Trip Efficiency (η_RT)

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$$

- **Lead-acid:** 70–80 %
- **LFP / NMC:** 90–95 %
- **Vanadium Redox Flow:** 70–80 %

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Losses contributing to η_RT < 100 %:** Joule heating, electrochemical overpotentials, side reactions, auxiliary systems (BMS, cooling), and self-discharge.

## 2.9 Self-Discharge

Charge loss when the battery is idle:

$$\text{Self-Discharge} = \frac{C_{lost}}{C_{rated} \cdot \Delta t} \quad \text{[\%/month]}$$

- **Li-ion:** 1–3 %/month
- **Lead-acid:** 4–6 %/month
- **NiMH:** 15–25 %/month

## 2.10 Cycle Life & Calendar Life

**Cycle life** depends strongly on DoD:

$$\text{Cycle Life} \propto \frac{1}{\text{DoD}^k} \quad \text{(k ≈ 1.5–2 for Li-ion)}$$

| Chemistry | Cycles at 80 % DoD | Calendar Life |
|---|---|---|
| Lead-acid (flooded) | 300–500 | 3–5 yr |
| Lead-acid (VRLA) | 500–800 | 5–7 yr |
| LFP | 2,000–5,000 | 8–15 yr |
| NMC | 1,000–2,000 | 8–10 yr |
| Sodium-sulfur (NaS) | 2,500 | 15 yr |

## 2.11 Operating Temperature Range

Temperature affects almost every specification:
- Capacity decreases below 0 °C (electrolyte freezes in lead-acid at ≈ -10 °C; Li-ion plating below 0 °C during charge).
- Capacity temporarily increases above 25 °C but accelerated degradation.
- **Arrhenius rule of thumb:** Reaction rate doubles every 10 °C → aging doubles.

## 2.12 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacity | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Ah | Battery-specific |
| 2 | Energy | $E = V_n \cdot C_n$ | Wh | Battery-specific |
| 3 | Specific Energy | $E_{spec} = E / m$ | Wh/kg | 30 (Pb-acid) – 250 (Li-ion) |
| 4 | Specific Power | $P_{spec} = P / m$ | W/kg | 50 (Pb-acid) – 3,000 (supercap) |
| 5 | C-rate | $C = I / C_n$ | h⁻¹ | 0.1C – 10C |
| 6 | SoC | $SoC = 1 - DoD$ | % | 0–100 % |
| 7 | DoD | $DoD = Q_{used} / Q_{rated}$ | % | 0–100 % |
| 8 | SoH | $SoH = C_{aged} / C_{new}$ | % | 100 % → 80 % (EoL) |
| 9 | Internal R | $R_{int} = \Delta V / I$ | Ω (or mΩ) | mΩ range for cells |
| 10 | Heat loss | $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | W | Function of I |
| 11 | Max power | $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4 R_{int})$ | W | Theoretical |
| 12 | Round-trip η | $\eta_{RT} = E_{out} / E_{in}$ | % | 70 – 95 % |
| 13 | Self-discharge | $SD = C_{lost}/(C_n \cdot \Delta t)$ | %/month | 1 – 30 % |
| 14 | Peukert | $C_p = I^n \cdot t$ | – | n = 1.05 – 1.3 |
| 15 | Nernst EMF | $E = E^0 + (RT/nF)\ln(Q)$ | V | Chemistry-specific |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These 15 parameters directly map to KTU numerical problems. A typical question: *"A 12 V, 100 Ah lead-acid battery supplies a 200 W load for 6 hours. Calculate DoD, SoC, and energy delivered."* This requires parameters 1, 2, 6, 7 above.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between C-rate and Discharge Time

We begin from the fundamental definition of capacity and C-rate.

**Step 1 — Define capacity:**
The total charge a battery delivers during a full discharge at constant current is:
$$C_n = I \cdot t_{discharge}$$

**Step 2 — Define C-rate:**
By definition, the C-rate is current normalized to capacity:
$$C_{rate} = \frac{I}{C_n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute I into the capacity equation:**
Rearranging the capacity equation for current:
$$I = C_{rate} \cdot C_n$$

Plugging this back into $C_n = I \cdot t$:
$$C_n = (C_{rate} \cdot C_n) \cdot t_{discharge}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for t_discharge:**
Dividing both sides by $C_{rate} \cdot C_n$:
$$t_{discharge} = \frac{1}{C_{rate}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A **1C** discharge → 1 hour. A **0.5C** discharge → 2 hours. A **2C** discharge → 0.5 hours (30 min). This result is independent of the absolute capacity of the battery.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Delivered vs. Charge Delivered

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power:**
For a battery delivering current I at terminal voltage V(t):
$$P(t) = V(t) \cdot I(t)$$

**Step 2 — Total energy delivered:**
Integrate power over the discharge time:
$$E_{delivered} = \int_{0}^{t_{end}} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Constant-current simplification:**
If both V and I are constant (an idealization used in KTU problems):
$$E_{delivered} = V \cdot I \cdot t = V \cdot Q$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Q = C_n (rated capacity):**
$$\boxed{E_{delivered} = V \cdot C_n \quad \text{[Wh]}}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**
A 12 V, 100 Ah battery → E = 12 × 100 = **1,200 Wh = 1.2 kWh**.
At a 100 W DC load, theoretical runtime = 1,200 / 100 = **12 hours** (neglecting efficiency).

With 90 % round-trip efficiency and 80 % DoD limit:
$$E_{usable} = 1{,}200 \times 0.9 \times 0.8 = 864 \text{ Wh}$$
$$t_{actual} = 864 / 100 = 8.64 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Peukert's Law (Capacity Loss at High Currents)

Empirically, Wilhelm Peukert (1897) observed that higher discharge currents reduce deliverable capacity.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation:**
For a constant discharge current I held to cut-off voltage:
$$C_p = I^n \cdot t$$

where C_p is the "Peukert capacity" (constant for a given battery), and n is the Peukert exponent.

**Step 2 — Solve for t:**
$$t = \frac{C_p}{I^n}$$

**Step 3 — Compute actual capacity Q = I · t:**
$$Q = I \cdot \frac{C_p}{I^n} = C_p \cdot I^{1-n}$$

**Step 4 — Linearize via logarithms:**
Taking ln of both sides:
$$\ln Q = \ln C_p + (1-n) \ln I$$

This is a straight line in the $\ln Q$ vs. $\ln I$ plane. The slope $(1-n)$ is negative, confirming that Q decreases with I.

**Step 5 — Worked example:**
A 100 Ah lead-acid battery has n = 1.25. Discharged at 25 A (0.25C):
$$Q_{25A} = 100 \times \left(\frac{25}{100}\right)^{1-1.25} = 100 \times (0.25)^{-0.25} = 100 \times 1.414 = 141.4 \text{ Ah (apparent)}$$

Wait — the convention is the opposite. Peukert's law is usually written as $I^n \cdot t = C_p$. To use it for *capacity reduction at high current*, we take:
$$t = \left(\frac{C_p}{I^n}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = I \cdot t = \frac{C_p}{I^{n-1}}$$

So at 25 A with n = 1.25 and rated C = 100 Ah at 5 A reference:
$$C_p = 5^{1.25} \cdot 20 = 100 \cdot 5^{1.25} \approx 100 \cdot 7.476 = 747.6$$

$$Q_{25A} = \frac{747.6}{25^{0.25}} = \frac{747.6}{2.236} = 334.4 \text{ Ah} \text{ (apparent)}$$

> The actual deliverable energy is reduced because the voltage also sags at high current.

## 3.4 Derivation: SoC from Coulomb Counting

**Step 1 — Define SoC:**
$$SoC(t) = \frac{Q_{remaining}(t)}{C_n} = 1 - \frac{1}{C_n}\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Discrete (sampled) form used in BMS:**
$$SoC_{k+1} = SoC_k - \frac{I_k \cdot \Delta t}{C_n} \cdot \eta_{coulombic}$$

where $\eta_{coulombic}$ accounts for charge inefficiency.

**Step 3 — Conversion to DoD:**
$$DoD(t) = 1 - SoC(t) = \frac{1}{C_n}\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Round-Trip Efficiency from Energy Bookkeeping

**Step 1 — Energy input during charge:**
$$E_{in} = \int_{0}^{t_{ch}} V_{ch}(t) \cdot I_{ch}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Energy output during discharge:**
$$E_{out} = \int_{0}^{t_{dis}} V_{dis}(t) \cdot I_{dis}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Define round-trip efficiency:**
$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Decompose losses:**
$$\eta_{RT} = \eta_{coulombic} \times \eta_{voltage}$$
where:
$$\eta_{coulombic} = \frac{\int I_{dis} \, dt}{\int I_{ch} \, dt} \quad ; \quad \eta_{voltage} = \frac{\overline{V}_{dis}}{\overline{V}_{ch}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Specifications to Applications

| Application | Dominant Specification | Typical Battery | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solar home lighting** | Specific Energy, Cost/Wh | LFP / Lead-acid | Long discharge nights, low cost |
| **EV traction** | Specific Energy + Power | NMC / NCA | Range + acceleration |
| **EV bus (heavy)** | Cycle life, Safety | LFP | High cycles, thermal stability |
| **Grid storage (4-hr)** | Cycle life, η_RT, Cost/kWh | LFP / Flow | 365 cycles/yr for 10 yr |
| **Grid storage (>8 hr)** | Calendar life, Capex | Vanadium Redox / NaS | Long-duration, decoupled energy/power |
| **UPS (data center)** | Power density, η_RT | VRLA / LFP | High surge, low impedance |
| **Satellite / aerospace** | Specific energy, Calendar | Li-ion (NCA) | Mass-critical |
| **Portable electronics** | Energy density | Li-Po (NMC) | Small volume |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 48 V, 200 Ah lithium-ion battery pack has a specific energy of 160 Wh/kg and a round-trip efficiency of 92 %. It supplies a constant 1.5 kW load for 4 hours. Compute: (i) Total energy stored, (ii) Pack mass, (iii) Energy actually delivered to load, (iv) SoC drop, (v) Heat generated per cycle.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Energy stored:**
$$E_{stored} = V \cdot C = 48 \times 200 = 9{,}600 \text{ Wh} = 9.6 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step (ii) — Mass:**
$$m = \frac{E_{stored}}{E_{spec}} = \frac{9{,}600}{160} = 60 \text{ kg}$$

**Step (iii) — Energy delivered:**
$$E_{delivered} = P \cdot t = 1{,}500 \times 4 = 6{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 6 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step (iv) — SoC drop:**
$$\Delta SoC = \frac{E_{delivered}}{E_{stored} \cdot \eta_{RT}} = \frac{6{,}000}{9{,}600} = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

Note: Inefficiency means we needed to draw more from the battery to deliver 6 kWh to the load.

**Step (v) — Heat per cycle:**
$$E_{loss} = \frac{E_{delivered}}{\eta_{RT}} - E_{delivered} = \frac{6{,}000}{0.92} - 6{,}000 = 6{,}521.7 - 6{,}000 = 521.7 \text{ Wh}$$

This 521.7 Wh per cycle is the thermal load the BMS cooling system must reject.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define the following battery specification parameters: (i) C-rate, (ii) Depth of Discharge (DoD), (iii) State of Health (SoH)."**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) C-rate:** C-rate is the normalized discharge or charge current of a battery, defined as the ratio of the current (in amperes) to the battery's nominal capacity (in ampere-hours).
>
> $$C_{rate} = \frac{I}{C_n}$$
>
> A 1C rate means the battery will be fully discharged in 1 hour; a 0.5C rate means 2 hours. C-rate allows comparison between batteries of different sizes.

> **(ii) Depth of Discharge (DoD):** DoD is the fraction of total capacity that has been removed from a fully charged battery, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$DoD = \frac{Q_{discharged}}{C_n} \times 100\%$$
>
> A DoD of 100 % means the battery is fully discharged; 0 % means fully charged.

> **(iii) State of Health (SoH):** SoH is the ratio of the battery's current maximum deliverable capacity to its rated (new) capacity, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$SoH = \frac{C_{aged}}{C_{new}} \times 100\%$$
>
> A fresh battery has SoH = 100 %; an aged battery is considered End-of-Life when SoH falls below 80 % (typical for EV applications).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between specific energy and energy density of a battery. Why is specific energy a critical specification for electric vehicle (EV) batteries?"**

**Model Answer:**

> **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Energy per unit mass. Determines how much range an EV gets per kilogram of battery weight.
>
> **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Energy per unit volume. Determines how much battery can be packed into a given volume of the vehicle chassis.
>
> **Why specific energy is critical for EVs:**
> 1. **Range:** Higher specific energy → more kWh per kg → longer driving range.
> 2. **Payload & Efficiency:** Lighter battery → less energy wasted on moving the battery itself → higher vehicle efficiency.
> 3. **Performance:** Lower mass improves acceleration and reduces tire/brake wear.
>
> Example: NMC cells (~250 Wh/kg) are preferred over LFP (~160 Wh/kg) for passenger EVs where range is paramount. LFP is chosen for city buses where safety and cycle life outweigh range needs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define the following battery specification parameters with formulas and standard units: **(7 Marks)**
   - (i) Nominal Capacity
   - (ii) Round-trip Efficiency
   - (iii) Internal Resistance and its effect on power delivery
   - (iv) Self-Discharge Rate

**(b)** A 48 V, 200 Ah LFP battery pack is used in a microgrid. The pack has a specific energy of 160 Wh/kg, a round-trip efficiency of 92 %, and an internal resistance of 20 mΩ. The connected load draws 50 A continuously. Compute: **(7 Marks)**
   - (i) Total energy stored in the pack
   - (ii) Mass of the battery pack
   - (iii) Voltage drop across internal resistance at full load
   - (iv) Power loss as heat
   - (v) Maximum theoretical power deliverable (using P_max = V_oc² / 4 R_int)
   - (vi) Actual backup time if the load is a constant 2 kW

**Model Answer for Part (a):**

> **(i) Nominal Capacity (C_n):** The total electric charge a fully charged battery can deliver under specified conditions (discharge current, temperature, end voltage) before reaching cutoff.
>
> $$C_n = I \cdot t_{discharge} \quad \text{[Ah]}$$
>
> Units: **Ampere-hour (Ah)**. Typical values: 50 Ah, 100 Ah, 200 Ah, 1000 Ah (large stationary cells).

> **(ii) Round-Trip Efficiency (η_RT):** The ratio of energy delivered during discharge to the energy input required for the preceding charge, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$$
>
> Units: **%**. Lead-acid: 70–80 %; LFP/NMC: 90–95 %.

> **(iii) Internal Resistance (R_int):** The lumped ohmic resistance inside the cell, comprising electrolyte resistance, electrode resistance, and contact resistance.
>
> $$V_{terminal} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$
>
> Units: **Ohm (Ω) or milliohm (mΩ)**. A 100 Ah Li-ion cell has R_int ≈ 0.3–1 mΩ.
>
> **Effect on power delivery:** High R_int causes voltage sag under load, reduces deliverable power, generates I²R heat, and lowers η_RT. Maximum power transfer occurs when external load = R_int.

> **(iv) Self-Discharge Rate:** The percentage of nominal capacity lost per unit time when the battery is idle (no load connected).
>
> $$\text{Self-Discharge} = \frac{\Delta C}{C_n \cdot \Delta t} \quad \text{[\%/month]}$$
>
> Li-ion: 1–3 %/month; Lead-acid: 4–6 %/month; NiMH: 15–25 %/month. Caused by parasitic side reactions (e.g., electrolyte decomposition, SEI growth).

**Model Answer for Part (b):**

> **(i) Energy stored:**
>
> $$E_{stored} = V \cdot C_n = 48 \times 200 = 9{,}600 \text{ Wh} = 9.6 \text{ kWh}$$

> **(ii) Mass of pack:**
>
> $$m = \frac{E_{stored}}{E_{spec}} = \frac{9{,}600}{160} = 60 \text{ kg}$$

> **(iii) Voltage drop at 50 A:**
>
> $$\Delta V = I \cdot R_{int} = 50 \times 0.020 = 1.0 \text{ V}$$
>
