> Terminal voltage at load: $V_{term} = 48 - 1 = 47$ V.

> **(iv) Heat loss:**
>
> $$P_{heat} = I^2 \cdot R_{int} = 50^2 \times 0.020 = 2{,}500 \times 0.020 = 50 \text{ W}$$

> **(v) Maximum theoretical power (assuming V_oc ≈ V_n):**
>
> $$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}} = \frac{48^2}{4 \times 0.020} = \frac{2{,}304}{0.08} = 28{,}800 \text{ W} = 28.8 \text{ kW}$$

> **(vi) Backup time at 2 kW constant load:**
>
> Energy usable: $E_{usable} = E_{stored} \times \eta_{RT} = 9{,}600 \times 0.92 = 8{,}832$ Wh
>
> $$t_{backup} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{load}} = \frac{8{,}832}{2{,}000} = 4.416 \text{ hours} \approx 4 \text{ h } 25 \text{ min}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (14 Marks) — *Alternative choice*

**(a)** Explain the significance of the following specifications in battery selection for a solar PV off-grid system: **(7 Marks)**
   - (i) Depth of Discharge (DoD) limit
   - (ii) Cycle life vs. DoD relationship
   - (iii) Operating temperature range
   - (iv) Round-trip efficiency

**(b)** A 5 kW rooftop solar PV system in Kerala has a daily energy yield of 22 kWh. The owner wants a battery bank that provides 2 days of autonomy. The selected battery has:
   - Nominal Voltage = 48 V
   - Nominal Capacity = 200 Ah
   - DoD limit = 80 %
   - η_RT = 90 %
   - System DC bus = 48 V
   - Daily load = 10 kWh

Compute: **(7 Marks)**
   - (i) Total energy storage required (in kWh)
   - (ii) Number of 48 V, 200 Ah battery units required (in parallel)
   - (iii) Usable energy from one battery unit
   - (iv) State of Charge (SoC) after 1 full day of autonomy
   - (v) What happens to the cycle life if DoD is increased to 90 % instead of 80 %?

**Model Answer for Part (a):**

> **(i) DoD limit:** A solar battery should never be fully discharged; manufacturers specify a maximum DoD to protect cell health. For example, a LFP battery with 80 % DoD limit means only 80 % of its rated capacity is usable. The remaining 20 % is a "reserve" that prevents over-discharge, which would damage cells.

> **(ii) Cycle life vs. DoD:** Cycle life is strongly dependent on DoD. Shallow discharges (DoD = 20–30 %) yield many more cycles than deep discharges. For LFP at 80 % DoD, cycle life ≈ 3,500 cycles; at 50 % DoD, it can exceed 6,000 cycles. The empirical relationship is:
>
> $$N_{cycles} \propto \frac{1}{DoD^k}, \quad k \approx 1.5 - 2$$
>
> Hence, for a daily solar cycle, selecting a battery with a higher DoD limit *or* oversizing the bank to operate at lower DoD greatly extends lifetime.

> **(iii) Operating temperature range:** Kerala's tropical climate means ambient temperatures of 25–40 °C. Batteries derate at high temperature (loss of capacity at > 45 °C in lead-acid; accelerated calendar aging in Li-ion above 35 °C). A battery rated for 0–45 °C operation is suitable. Low temperature is less of a concern in Kerala but matters for installations in mountain regions.

> **(iv) Round-trip efficiency:** Determines how much of the PV-generated DC energy is actually delivered to the load. At η_RT = 90 %, a 10 kWh charge stores 9 kWh for use. Higher η_RT means smaller PV array is needed. Lead-acid (75 %) requires a larger array than LFP (92 %) for the same load.

**Model Answer for Part (b):**

> **(i) Total energy storage required for 2 days of autonomy:**
>
> $$E_{req} = \text{Daily load} \times \text{Days of autonomy} / \eta_{RT}$$
>
> $$E_{req} = 10 \times 2 / 0.9 = 22.22 \text{ kWh}$$

> **(ii) Number of 48 V, 200 Ah units (in parallel):**
>
> Energy per unit: $E_{unit} = 48 \times 200 = 9{,}600$ Wh = 9.6 kWh
>
> Usable per unit (at 80 % DoD): $E_{usable} = 9.6 \times 0.8 = 7.68$ kWh
>
> Effective usable per unit (with η_RT): $E_{eff} = 7.68 \times 0.9 = 6.912$ kWh
>
> $$N_{parallel} = \frac{E_{req}}{E_{eff}} = \frac{22.22}{6.912} = 3.21$$
>
> **Round up to 4 units** (cannot have fractional battery).

> **(iii) Usable energy from one 48 V, 200 Ah unit:**
>
> $$E_{usable} = V \cdot C_n \cdot DoD = 48 \times 200 \times 0.8 = 7{,}680 \text{ Wh} = 7.68 \text{ kWh}$$

> **(iv) SoC after 1 day of autonomy (using 4 units):**
>
> Total energy in 4 units: $4 \times 9.6 = 38.4$ kWh
>
> Energy removed for 1 day: 10 kWh delivered → required draw = 10 / 0.9 = 11.11 kWh
>
> $$\Delta DoD = \frac{11.11}{38.4} = 28.93\%$$
>
> $$\text{SoC after 1 day} = 100\% - 28.93\% = 71.07\%$$

> **(v) Effect of increasing DoD to 90 %:**
>
> At higher DoD, the cycle life decreases. Using the empirical relation $N \propto 1/DoD^k$ with k = 1.7 for LFP:
>
> $$\frac{N_{90}}{N_{80}} = \left(\frac{80}{90}\right)^{1.7} = (0.889)^{1.7} \approx 0.80$$
>
> This means **cycle life drops by ~20 %** — the battery reaches End-of-Life faster. However, usable energy per cycle increases by 12.5 %, so a trade-off analysis is required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **DoD vs. SoC confusion:** Many students write "DoD = 30 %, SoC = 30 %" — WRONG. DoD + SoC = 100 %.
> 2. **C-rate units:** C-rate is dimensionless (or h⁻¹), not "A". Writing "the battery discharges at 1C" is correct; "at 1C A" is not.
> 3. **Efficiency stacking:** Do not multiply efficiencies of unrelated subsystems (inverter, charge controller, battery) unless the question specifically asks for overall system efficiency.
> 4. **Rounding up battery counts:** Always round up the number of parallel battery units. You cannot install "3.2 batteries" — round to 4.
> 5. **Forgetting η_RT in autonomy calculations:** When the load consumes 10 kWh and η_RT = 90 %, the battery must discharge 11.11 kWh. Students often forget this and use 10 kWh directly.
> 6. **V_oc vs. V_n:** V_oc is the open-circuit (no load) voltage; V_n is the nominal average. The question may require V_oc for the maximum power transfer formula — read carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **C-rate** = I / C_n, unitless or h⁻¹. 1C = 1-hour discharge; 0.5C = 2 hours; 2C = 30 minutes.
- [ ] **Capacity (Ah)** = I × t. **Energy (Wh)** = V × C_n. **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** = E / m.
- [ ] **DoD + SoC = 100 %.** DoD is "used"; SoC is "remaining".
- [ ] **SoH < 80 % → End-of-Life** (typical EV threshold).
- [ ] **Round-trip efficiency (η_RT)** for lead-acid ≈ 75 %, LFP ≈ 92 %, NMC ≈ 95 %, flow ≈ 75 %.
- [ ] **Internal resistance** causes voltage sag $V_{term} = V_{oc} - I R_{int}$ and heat $I^2 R_{int}$.
- [ ] **Maximum power transfer:** $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4 R_{int})$ (assumes load = R_int).
- [ ] **Peukert's law** $C_p = I^n \cdot t$ — high currents reduce usable capacity, more so for lead-acid (n ≈ 1.3) than Li-ion (n ≈ 1.05).
- [ ] **Cycle life vs. DoD:** Shallow cycles = longer life. LFP at 80 % DoD ≈ 3,500 cycles; at 50 % DoD > 6,000 cycles.
- [ ] **Arrhenius rule:** Aging rate doubles every 10 °C rise above 25 °C.
- [ ] **Self-discharge:** Li-ion 1–3 %/mo, lead-acid 4–6 %/mo, NiMH 15–25 %/mo.
- [ ] **Sizing formula (autonomy):** $N_{parallel} = (E_{load} \times N_{days}) / (V \times C_n \times DoD \times \eta_{RT})$, always round up.
- [ ] **Ragone plot:** Energy on x-axis (Wh/kg), Power on y-axis (W/kg), both log scale. Trade-off: supercap top-left, Li-ion center, lead-acid bottom-left.
- [ ] **Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol** is the bridge between chemistry (moles) and electricity (coulombs).
- [ ] **Standard cell voltages:** lead-acid = 2.0 V, LFP = 3.2 V, NMC = 3.7 V, NiMH = 1.2 V, NaS = 2.0 V (operating).
- [ ] **Cell count formulas:** Series cells = V_bus / V_n(cell); Parallel strings = C_req / C_n(string).
- [ ] **BMS monitors:** V, I, T, SoC, SoH, cell balancing, communication (CAN/RS485).
- [ ] **Thermal management** is mandatory above 1C continuous for Li-ion.
- [ ] **KTU standard practice:** Always state units in the final answer (Wh, Ah, kg, V, °C).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Charging/Discharging Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Charging/Discharging Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of electrochemical energy storage systems (batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells), the **Charging/Discharging Rate** is defined as the rate at which a storage device is charged or discharged relative to its nominal rated capacity. It is universally quantified using the dimensionless **C-rate**, which represents the reciprocal of the time (in hours) required to fully charge or discharge the device under reference conditions.

Mathematically, the C-rate expresses the current magnitude normalized to the cell's nominal capacity:

$$\text{C-rate} = \frac{I}{C_n}$$

where $I$ is the applied current (in Amperes, A) and $C_n$ is the nominal capacity of the cell (in Ampere-hours, Ah). A **1C** rate corresponds to a current that would fully charge or discharge the device in exactly **1 hour**, while a **2C** rate would do so in **30 minutes**, and a **C/2 (0.5C)** rate would require **2 hours**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Charging/Discharging Rate is a foundational concept in **Module 3: Energy Storage Technologies** and forms the basis for sizing battery banks, designing Battery Management Systems (BMS), and evaluating the techno-economic feasibility of renewable energy integration projects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water tank** connected to a tap (discharge) and a pump (charge). The tank's capacity is, say, **1000 litres**.

- A **slow trickle** (C/10 rate) means you fill or empty 100 litres per hour → takes **10 hours**.
- A **normal tap flow** (1C rate) means you fill or empty 1000 litres per hour → takes **1 hour**.
- A **fire-hose blast** (5C rate) means you move 5000 litres per hour → takes just **12 minutes**, but the tank heats up and the pipes may burst!

The "C-rate" is essentially telling the battery **how hard** you are asking it to work. The harder you push (higher C-rate), the faster the energy transfer, but the **greater the losses, heat generation, and stress on the chemistry** inside.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Terminology)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Capacity | $C_n$ | **Ah** (Ampere-hour) | Total charge the cell can deliver at the reference discharge rate |
| C-Rate | $C$ | **hour⁻¹** or "C" | Normalized charge/discharge current |
| State of Charge | $SOC$ | **%** (percentage) | Remaining capacity as a fraction of nominal capacity |
| Depth of Discharge | $DOD$ | **%** | Complement of SOC: $DOD = 100\% - SOC$ |
| Energy Density | $E_d$ | **Wh/kg** | Energy stored per unit mass |
| Power Density | $P_d$ | **W/kg** | Power deliverable per unit mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Recalled:**
> - Faraday's Constant: $F = 96{,}485$ **C/mol**
> - Standard Temperature: $T_0 = 298.15$ **K** (25 °C)
> - 1 Ah = 3600 Coulombs

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The charging and discharging process in any electrochemical cell involves **three coupled physical phenomena** that are rate-dependent:

1. **Charge Transfer Kinetics (Butler-Volmer Behavior):** At low to moderate C-rates, the electrochemical reaction at the electrode-electrolyte interface proceeds smoothly. At very high C-rates, the reaction becomes **mass-transport limited** — ions cannot reach the electrode surface fast enough, leading to concentration polarization.

2. **Ion Diffusion (Solid-State and Electrolyte):** Lithium ions (in Li-ion cells) must diffuse through the electrolyte and intercalate into the electrode structure. Higher C-rates shorten the available time, causing **non-uniform lithiation** and **capacity fade**.

3. **Ohmic & Thermal Losses:** The internal resistance $R_i$ of the cell causes Joule heating $I^2 R_i$, which scales with the **square of the current**. Doubling the C-rate quadruples the heat generated.

### 2.2 The C-Rate Spectrum — Practical Classification

| C-Rate | Time to Full Charge/Discharge | Typical Application | Stress Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C/20 (0.05C)** | 20 hours | Laboratory characterization, trickle charge | Very Low |
| **C/10 (0.1C)** | 10 hours | Slow overnight EV charging | Low |
| **C/2 (0.5C)** | 2 hours | Standard grid storage cycling | Moderate |
| **1C** | 1 hour | EV Level 2 charging, standard discharge | High |
| **2C** | 30 minutes | EV fast charging (DC Level 2) | Very High |
| **5C** | 12 minutes | Tesla Supercharger V3, performance EVs | Extreme |
| **10C+** | < 6 minutes | Supercapacitors, hybrid pulse power | Critical |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the most heavily tested equations for KTU Board Examinations on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = C \cdot C_n$ | Current from C-rate and capacity | A |
| 2 | $t = \dfrac{1}{C\text{-rate}}$ (in hours) | Time to fully charge/discharge | h |
| 3 | $P = V \cdot I = V \cdot C \cdot C_n$ | Power delivered/absorbed | W |
| 4 | $E = V \cdot C_n$ | Nominal stored energy | Wh |
| 5 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_n}\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$ | State of Charge dynamics | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q_{lost} = I^2 \cdot R_i \cdot t$ | Heat loss (Joule) per cycle | J |
| 7 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency | % |
| 8 | $C_{eff}(I) = C_n \cdot k \cdot I^{1-n}$ | Peukert's effective capacity (Lead-acid) | Ah |
| 9 | $R_{internal} = \dfrac{V_{oc} - V_{terminal}}{I}$ | Internal resistance from load test | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{I^2 R_i \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Temperature rise in cell | K |

> **Critical Notation Safeguard:** For all absolute-value expressions (such as $\vert I \vert$ or $\vert V \vert$), use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The charging/discharging rate governs the design of nearly every modern energy system:

- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Battery packs must deliver **3C–5C** peak discharge for acceleration and accept **1C–3C** for fast charging. A Tesla Model 3 LR battery (~75 kWh, ~230 Ah) at a 3C discharge delivers ~690 A.
- **Grid-Scale Storage:** Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) banks cycle at **0.25C–0.5C** for solar smoothing, prioritizing cycle life (6000+ cycles) over power density.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Designed for **1C–10C** short-duration high-rate discharge to bridge grid outages.
- **Aerospace & Satellites:** Battery cycling is tightly controlled at **C/2 to C/5** to maximize calendar life over 15+ year missions.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones typically charge at **0.5C–1C** to balance speed with battery longevity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From C-Rate to Charging/Discharging Time

**Starting Principle:** By definition, a C-rate of 1C delivers a current equal to the nominal capacity $C_n$ in one hour.

**Step 1:** Express the current for a general C-rate $r$:

$$I = r \cdot C_n$$

**Step 2:** The total charge transferred in time $t$ is:

$$Q = I \cdot t = r \cdot C_n \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** For a **full** charge or discharge, the charge transferred must equal the nominal capacity:

$$Q_{full} = C_n$$

**Step 4:** Equate and solve for $t$:

$$r \cdot C_n \cdot t = C_n$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $C_n$ from both sides (since $C_n \neq 0$):

$$t = \frac{1}{r}$$

**Final Result (boxed concept):** The full charge/discharge time in hours is the **reciprocal of the C-rate**.

> **Example Numerical Application:** A 100 Ah battery discharged at 0.5C delivers a current of 50 A and reaches full depletion in 2 hours.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power and Energy Relationship via C-Rate

**Step 1:** Nominal energy stored in a cell:

$$E_{stored} = V_{nom} \cdot C_n$$

where $V_{nom}$ is the nominal terminal voltage (in V) and $C_n$ is in Ah. The product yields energy in Wh.

**Step 2:** Convert to SI units (Joules) using 1 Wh = 3600 J:

$$E_{stored} = V_{nom} \cdot C_n \cdot 3600 \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

**Step 3:** Power delivered at a C-rate $r$:

$$P = V_{nom} \cdot I = V_{nom} \cdot r \cdot C_n$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $I = r \cdot C_n$ into $P = V \cdot I$:

$$P = r \cdot V_{nom} \cdot C_n$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $V_{nom} \cdot C_n = E_{stored}$:

$$\boxed{P = r \cdot E_{stored}}$$

**Interpretation:** The **power** rating of a battery is directly proportional to its **C-rate** and **energy** content. Doubling the C-rate doubles the deliverable power, but the *energy* remains the same (only the speed changes).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — State of Charge (SOC) Evolution During Constant-Current Discharge

**Step 1:** Define the instantaneous SOC:

$$SOC(t) = \frac{\text{Remaining Capacity at time } t}{C_n}$$

**Step 2:** For constant-current discharge at current $I$:

$$\text{Charge removed} = I \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$SOC(t) = SOC(0) - \frac{I \cdot t}{C_n}$$

**Step 4:** Since $I = r \cdot C_n$, substitute:

$$SOC(t) = SOC(0) - r \cdot t$$

**Step 5:** For a fully charged cell with $SOC(0) = 1.0$ (or 100%):

$$SOC(t) = 1 - r \cdot t$$

**Step 6:** The time to reach $SOC = 0$ (full depletion) is:

$$t_{empty} = \frac{1}{r} \quad \text{(hours)}$$

which **re-derives the C-rate-time relationship** from a different starting point, confirming internal consistency.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Peukert's Law (Empirical High-Rate Capacity Loss)

> [!NOTE]
> **Peukert's Law** applies primarily to **lead-acid batteries** and quantifies the loss of usable capacity at high discharge currents. It is a high-value topic for KTU Part B questions.

**Step 1:** Peukert's equation (1897) states:

$$C_p = I^{n} \cdot t$$

where $C_p$ is a constant (Peukert capacity), $I$ is discharge current, $n$ is the Peukert exponent (typically 1.1–1.3 for lead-acid), and $t$ is the discharge time.

**Step 2:** Solve for the effective capacity at any rate:

$$C_{eff} = \frac{C_p}{I^{n-1}} = C_n \left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)^{1-n}$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging in the canonical textbook form (validating against standard references):

$$C_{eff} = C_n \cdot k \cdot I^{1-n}$$

where $k$ is a normalization constant that ensures $C_{eff} = C_n$ at the reference rate.

**Step 4:** The relationship between discharge time and current becomes:

$$t = \frac{C_p}{I^{n}}$$

**Interpretation:** As $n > 1$, increasing $I$ reduces the discharge time **more than linearly** — a manifestation of rate-dependent losses.

**Worked Example (Lead-Acid):** A 100 Ah lead-acid battery has $n = 1.25$. Discharged at 50 A (0.5C) vs 5 A (0.05C):

$$\frac{C_{eff}(50A)}{C_{eff}(5A)} = \left(\frac{5}{50}\right)^{1-1.25} = (0.1)^{-0.25} = 1.778$$

So at high rate, **only ~56%** of the rated capacity is actually usable.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How C-Rate Affects Cell Behavior

| C-Rate | Current (per 100 Ah) | Heat Generated (W, $R_i=10\,m\Omega$) | Voltage Sag (approx.) | Usable Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.1C | 10 A | 1 W | ~0.05 V | 100% (reference) |
| 0.5C | 50 A | 25 W | ~0.25 V | ~92% |
| 1C | 100 A | 100 W | ~0.50 V | ~85% |
| 2C | 200 A | 400 W | ~1.00 V | ~75% |
| 5C | 500 A | 2500 W | ~2.50 V | ~60% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Battery Charging/Discharging System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Power Source / Load] --> B[Battery Management System BMS]
    B --> C{Charge or Discharge Mode}
    C -->|Charge Mode| D[DC-DC Charging Controller]
    C -->|Discharge Mode| E[DC-DC Discharging Inverter]
    D --> F[Cell Module Bank]
    E --> F[Cell Module Bank]
    F --> G[Temperature Sensors]
    F --> H[Voltage Monitoring]
    F --> I[Current Sensor]
    G --> J[Thermal Management System]
    H --> B
    I --> B
    J --> B
    B --> K[SOC Estimation and C-Rate Control Loop]
    K --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **BMS** sits at the heart of the system, continuously monitoring cell voltage, current, and temperature. The **C-Rate Control Loop** dynamically adjusts the input/output current to maintain safe operating limits, prevent overcharging, and optimize the charging profile (e.g., CC-CV for Li-ion).

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Constant-Current / Constant-Voltage (CC-CV) Charging Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: SOC 0 percent] --> B[CC Phase: Constant Current at Crate]
    B --> C{Cell Voltage reaches Vmax}
    C -->|No| B
    C -->|Yes| D[CV Phase: Constant Voltage at Vmax]
    D --> E{Current drops below Cutoff}
    E -->|No| D
    E -->|Yes| F[Charging Complete: SOC 100 percent]
    F --> G[Float or Rest Mode]
```

> **KTU Board Insight:** The **CC-CV protocol** is the de-facto industry standard for Li-ion battery charging. The CC phase operates at a fixed C-rate (typically **0.5C–1C**), and once the cell voltage hits its maximum (e.g., 4.2 V for graphite/LCO), the controller switches to CV mode and tapers the current down to a cutoff (typically **C/20**).

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Logic for C-Rate Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Requirement] --> B{Power Density Priority?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use High Crate 2C to 5C]
    B -->|No| D{Energy Density Priority?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use Low Crate 0.1C to 0.5C]
    D -->|No| F{Cycle Life Priority?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use Very Low Crate below C by 5]
    F -->|No| H[Select Balanced Chemistry e.g. NMC]
    C --> I[Chemistry: LTO, NMC 811, Supercapacitor]
    E --> J[Chemistry: LFP, NCA]
    G --> K[Chemistry: LFP, Lead Acid]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
Define the term **C-rate** with respect to battery charging and discharging. A battery has a rated capacity of 150 Ah. Calculate the current drawn from the battery when discharged at (i) 0.2C and (ii) 2C. What is the time taken to fully discharge the battery in each case?

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The C-rate of a battery is the ratio of the current drawn (or supplied) to the nominal capacity of the battery. It indicates the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its maximum capacity. A 1C rate discharges the entire battery in 1 hour.

**Given:** $C_n = 150$ Ah

**Case (i):** C-rate = 0.2C

$$I_1 = 0.2 \times 150 = 30 \text{ A}$$

$$t_1 = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \text{ hours}$$

**Case (ii):** C-rate = 2C

$$I_2 = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ A}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ hours} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Conclusion:** Higher C-rates mean higher currents and shorter discharge times.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Explain how the charging/discharging rate affects (i) the usable capacity of a battery, and (ii) the cycle life of a battery. Mention **Peukert's Law** in brief.

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Effect on Usable Capacity:** As the discharge C-rate increases, the usable capacity of a battery decreases due to mass-transport limitations, increased internal resistance, and concentration polarization. At very high rates, ions cannot diffuse into the electrode fast enough, leading to reduced effective capacity.
>
> **(ii) Effect on Cycle Life:** High C-rates generate more Joule heat ($I^2 R$ losses), which accelerates electrolyte decomposition, electrode degradation, and SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase) growth. This results in faster capacity fade and reduced total cycle count. Operating at moderate C-rates (0.25C–0.5C) significantly extends cycle life.
>
> **(iii) Peukert's Law:** Peukert's Law (1897) is an empirical relationship that quantifies capacity loss at high discharge rates, primarily for lead-acid batteries. It states that:

$$t = \frac{C_p}{I^n}$$

> where $n$ is the Peukert exponent (typically 1.1–1.3), $C_p$ is the Peukert capacity, and $I$ is the discharge current. A higher $n$ indicates greater rate-dependent capacity loss.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **charging/discharging time** and the **C-rate** of a battery. A 200 Ah, 12 V lead-acid battery is discharged at 0.5C. Calculate the discharge current, total energy delivered (in Wh and kWh), and the time to full discharge. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **CC-CV (Constant Current – Constant Voltage) charging protocol** for a Li-ion battery with a suitable diagram. Explain why the current must be tapered in the CV phase. What is the role of the C/20 cutoff current? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Derivation of Time vs C-Rate Relationship:**

Consider a battery of nominal capacity $C_n$ (in Ah). The C-rate $r$ is defined as:

$$I = r \cdot C_n \quad \text{(current in Amperes)}$$

The total charge transferred in time $t$ (in hours) is:

$$Q = I \cdot t = r \cdot C_n \cdot t$$

For **full charge or discharge**, $Q$ must equal $C_n$:

$$r \cdot C_n \cdot t = C_n$$

$$\boxed{t = \frac{1}{r} \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Calculations:**

Given: $C_n = 200$ Ah, $V = 12$ V, $r = 0.5$C

**Discharge current:**

$$I = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ A}$$

**Time to full discharge:**

$$t = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ hours}$$

**Total energy delivered:**

$$E = V \cdot C_n = 12 \text{ V} \times 200 \text{ Ah} = 2400 \text{ Wh} = 2.4 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Verification:** Power during discharge:

$$P = V \cdot I = 12 \times 100 = 1200 \text{ W} = 1.2 \text{ kW}$$

Check: $E = P \cdot t = 1.2 \text{ kW} \times 2 \text{ h} = 2.4 \text{ kWh}$ ✓

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

The **CC-CV protocol** is the standard two-stage charging method for Li-ion batteries:

**Stage 1 — Constant Current (CC) Phase:**
- The charger applies a fixed current, typically **0.5C to 1C**.
- The cell voltage rises gradually from its nominal value (e.g., 3.7 V) toward the upper cutoff (e.g., 4.2 V).
- This phase delivers approximately **70–80%** of the total charge.

**Stage 2 — Constant Voltage (CV) Phase:**
- Once the cell voltage reaches the upper limit ($V_{max} = 4.2$ V for most Li-ion chemistries), the charger holds the voltage constant.
- The current naturally **tapers down** exponentially as the cell approaches full charge.
- Charging is terminated when the current drops to a predefined cutoff, typically **C/20** (i.e., 5% of the rated capacity current).

**Why Current is Tapered in CV Phase:**
- Continuing CC charging beyond $V_{max}$ would cause **lithium plating** on the anode, which is irreversible and dangerous (can lead to thermal runaway).
- Tapered current allows the last **20–30%** of the capacity to be added safely by allowing full electrochemical equilibration within the cell.

**Role of C/20 Cutoff:**
- The C/20 cutoff prevents **overcharging** and **stress cycling** of the cell.
- It ensures that the cell reaches ≥ 99% SOC without excessive time spent at high voltage.
- C/20 is chosen as a balance between charging speed and cycle-life preservation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start SOC 0 percent] --> B[CC Phase: I fixed at 1C]
    B --> C[V reaches 4.2 V]
    C --> D[CV Phase: V fixed at 4.2 V]
    D --> E[I drops to C by 20]
    E --> F[Charge Complete]
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**(a)** Explain **Peukert's Law** in detail. A 100 Ah lead-acid battery (Peukert exponent $n = 1.25$) is rated at the 20-hour discharge rate. Calculate its usable capacity when discharged at (i) 5 A and (ii) 50 A. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solar PV system uses a lithium-ion battery bank of 48 V, 200 Ah. The battery is to be fully charged in 4 hours from a solar array. Calculate: (i) the required charging C-rate, (ii) the charging current, (iii) the charging power, and (iv) the daily energy throughput in kWh. Comment on the impact of using a faster 2-hour charge on the battery cycle life. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Peukert's Law — Theoretical Background:**

Peukert's Law, formulated by Wilhelm Peukert in 1897, is an empirical equation describing the relationship between the discharge current of a battery and its usable capacity. The law is most accurate for **lead-acid batteries** but is also used (with modifications) for NiCd and NiMH chemistries.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$C_p = I^n \cdot t$$

where:
- $C_p$ = Peukert capacity (a constant for a given battery)
- $I$ = discharge current (A)
- $t$ = discharge time (h)
- $n$ = Peukert exponent (typically 1.1–1.4 for lead-acid; closer to 1.0 for Li-ion)

The **usable (effective) capacity** at any current $I$ is:

$$C_{eff} = \frac{C_p}{I^{n-1}}$$

**Step 1 — Determine the Peukert Capacity $C_p$:**

At the reference 20-hour rate, the battery delivers 100 Ah over 20 hours:

$$I_{ref} = \frac{100 \text{ Ah}}{20 \text{ h}} = 5 \text{ A}$$

$$C_p = I_{ref}^n \cdot t_{ref} = 5^{1.25} \times 20 = 7.476 \times 20 = 149.5$$

**Step 2 — Capacity at 5 A (0.05C):**

$$C_{eff}(5A) = \frac{149.5}{5^{0.25}} = \frac{149.5}{1.495} = 100.0 \text{ Ah}$$

(This is the rated capacity — as expected at the reference rate.)

**Step 3 — Capacity at 50 A (0.5C):**

$$C_{eff}(50A) = \frac{149.5}{50^{0.25}} = \frac{149.5}{2.659} = 56.2 \text{ Ah}$$

**Conclusion:** At a 10× higher current, the **usable capacity drops from 100 Ah to ~56 Ah** — a 44% loss — demonstrating the dramatic effect of C-rate on lead-acid battery performance.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $V = 48$ V, $C_n = 200$ Ah, charging time target = 4 hours.

**(i) Required Charging C-Rate:**

$$r = \frac{1}{t} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ C}$$

**(ii) Charging Current:**

$$I_{chg} = r \cdot C_n = 0.25 \times 200 = 50 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Charging Power:**

$$P_{chg} = V \cdot I_{chg} = 48 \times 50 = 2400 \text{ W} = 2.4 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Daily Energy Throughput:**

$$E_{daily} = V \cdot C_n = 48 \times 200 = 9600 \text{ Wh} = 9.6 \text{ kWh}$$

(Assuming one full charge-discharge cycle per day.)

**Comment on 2-Hour Faster Charging (C/2 = 0.5C):**

A faster 2-hour charge would require a current of:

$$I_{2h} = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ A}$$

**Impact on Cycle Life:**

1. **Increased Heat:** Heat generation scales as $I^2 R_i$. Doubling the current quadruples the heat, raising cell temperature by ~4× (in adiabatic limit).

2. **Lithium Plating Risk:** At higher C-rates, metallic lithium may deposit on the anode surface, causing permanent capacity loss and creating a safety hazard (dendrite formation).

3. **SEI Growth:** Elevated temperature and voltage stress accelerate the growth of the Solid Electrolyte Interphase, consuming cyclable lithium and reducing capacity.

4. **Quantitative Impact:** A typical Li-ion cell rated for **3000 cycles at 0.5C/0.5C** may only deliver **1500–2000 cycles at 1C/1C** cycling — a **33–50% reduction** in cycle life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often confuse Ah (charge) with Wh (energy). Remember: **Energy = Voltage × Capacity**.
> 2. **Peukert's Law Misapplication:** Do NOT apply Peukert's Law to Li-ion batteries (it's a lead-acid empirical law). For Li-ion, capacity fade is better modeled by **Arrhenius** or **cycle-counting** models.
> 3. **C-Rate Sign Convention:** Discharging C-rates are sometimes denoted positive by convention. Always state the direction explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting the CV Phase:** When asked about Li-ion charging, mentioning only CC and skipping CV is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Confusing Power Density vs Energy Density:** High C-rate → high **power density**, not high energy density. Energy is independent of rate (for the same SOC range).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

- **C-rate definition:** $I = r \cdot C_n$; **Time to full cycle:** $t = 1/r$ (in hours).
- **1C** = full charge/discharge in **1 hour**; **0.5C** = 2 hours; **2C** = 30 minutes.
- **Power vs Energy:** Power is rate-dependent ($P = r \cdot E$); Energy is rate-independent for the same SOC window.
- **Heat loss:** Scales as $I^2 R_i$ — quadruples when C-rate doubles.
- **CC-CV charging:** Standard for Li-ion. CC at **0.5C–1C** until **4.2 V**, then CV taper to **C/20 cutoff**.
- **Peukert's Law:** Applies to **lead-acid** batteries; $n \in [1.1, 1.3]$; higher $n$ → greater high-rate capacity loss.
- **High C-rates cause:** Heat, voltage sag, capacity loss, lithium plating, SEI growth, reduced cycle life.
- **Low C-rates are good for:** Cycle life, calendar life, and accurate SOC estimation.
- **Standard units to remember:** Ah for capacity, Wh for energy, W for power, K for temperature, °C for reference.
- **Safety cutoff for Li-ion:** Never exceed **4.2 V/cell** (charge) or go below **2.5–3.0 V/cell** (discharge).
- **Industrial benchmark C-rates:** Grid storage ≈ 0.25C–0.5C; EVs ≈ 1C–3C; Power tools ≈ 5C–10C; Supercapacitors > 50C.
- **SOC update equation:** $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - (I \cdot t)/C_n$ (discharge) or $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + (I \cdot t)/C_n$ (charge).
- **Coulombic efficiency** $\eta_c = Q_{out}/Q_{in}$ — always < 100% for real batteries due to side reactions.

> **Final Study Tip:** For KTU Part B (14-mark) questions, always draw a **labeled diagram** (e.g., CC-CV profile, C-rate spectrum, or system block diagram). Diagrams fetch **easy 2–3 marks** and demonstrate conceptual clarity to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary and Secondary Cells - Dry Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Primary and Secondary Cells - Dry Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrochemical Cell — Formal Definition

An **electrochemical cell** is a device that converts the chemical energy of a spontaneous redox (oxidation–reduction) reaction into electrical energy, or vice versa, by spatially separating the oxidation and reduction half-reactions and forcing electrons to travel through an external circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERUT637, Module 3):**
> A galvanic (voltaic) cell is the *energy-producing* type. Two principal classifications exist — **Primary Cells** (single-use, non-rechargeable) and **Secondary Cells** (rechargeable).

## 1.2 Primary vs. Secondary Cells — The Core Distinction

| Aspect | Primary Cell | Secondary Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **Rechargeability** | Non-rechargeable (one-time use) | Rechargeable (cyclable) |
| **Reversibility** | Cell reaction is **irreversible** | Cell reaction is **reversible** |
| **Internal Resistance** | Generally high | Generally low |
| **Cost per Unit Energy** | Higher | Lower (over many cycles) |
| **Examples** | Dry cell (Leclanché), Alkaline, Mercury cell | Lead-acid, Ni-Cd, Li-ion |
| **Typical Use** | Torches, remotes, clocks | Cars, laptops, EVs, grid storage |

## 1.3 Dry Cell — Formal Definition

The **Dry Cell** (also called the **Leclanché Cell**) is a *primary* galvanic cell in which the electrolyte is held in an absorbent paste or gel rather than as a free-flowing liquid. This eliminates the problem of spillage and allows the cell to be used in any orientation — hence the name *dry*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard EMF of a Dry Cell ≈ 1.5 V (nominal)**
> The actual terminal voltage drops steadily during use, typically to ~0.8 V before being discarded.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The One-Way Coffee Vending Machine"

Imagine a coffee vending machine:
- A **Primary cell** is a vending machine that takes your coin and dispenses coffee *only once* — you cannot put the empty cup back in and recover the coin. The energy is spent permanently.
- A **Secondary cell** is a rechargeable machine: after dispensing coffee, you (an external power source) refill both the cup and the coin so the next person can use it.

In a **Dry Cell**, the *coin* is the metallic zinc casing (consumed forever), the *coffee* is the electric current delivered, and once the zinc is exhausted, the cell is dead.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cells (Full Hierarchy)

$$
\text{Cells} \begin{cases} \text{Primary (Galvanic)} \\ \text{Secondary (Rechargeable / Storage)} \end{cases}
$$

### 2.1.1 Primary Cells (Detailed)
- **Dry Cell (Leclanché Cell)** – Zn / NH₄Cl paste / MnO₂-C
- **Alkaline Cell** – Zn powder / KOH / MnO₂  →  EMF ≈ **1.5 V**
- **Mercury Cell (Ruben-Mallory)** – Zn / KOH / HgO  →  EMF ≈ **1.35 V** (very stable)
- **Lithium Primary Cell** – Li / organic electrolyte / MnO₂ or SOCl₂  →  EMF ≈ **3.0 V**
- **Button Cells** – miniature primary cells (watches, hearing aids)

### 2.1.2 Secondary Cells (Detailed)
- **Lead-Acid Battery** – Pb / H₂SO₄ / PbO₂  →  EMF per cell ≈ **2.0 V**
- **Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd)** – Cd / KOH / NiOOH  →  EMF ≈ **1.2 V**
- **Nickel-Metal Hydride (Ni-MH)** – MH alloy / KOH / NiOOH  →  EMF ≈ **1.2 V**
- **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion)** – C / Li⁺ electrolyte / LiCoO₂  →  EMF ≈ **3.6–3.7 V**
- **Sodium-Sulphur, Flow Batteries** – grid-scale applications

## 2.2 The Dry Cell (Leclanché Cell) — Component Breakdown

| Component | Material | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Anode (−)** | **Zinc** container/vessel | Oxidized: loses electrons (site of oxidation) |
| **Cathode (+)** | **Carbon (graphite) rod** | Collects electrons; site of reduction |
| **Cathode mix** | MnO₂ + powdered carbon (graphite) | Depolarizer (removes H₂) + conductor |
| **Electrolyte paste** | NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂ + MnO₂ + starch + water | Ionic conductor (semi-solid) |
| **Sealant** | Pitch / wax top | Prevents drying of paste |

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"dry"** is relative — the cell still contains water bound in the paste; it is *not* truly anhydrous.

## 2.3 Working Principle — Why It Produces Current

The driving force is the **potential difference** between two dissimilar electrodes immersed in an electrolyte. Zinc has a more negative standard reduction potential (−0.76 V) than MnO₂ in this medium, so zinc dissolves (oxidizes) and releases electrons that travel through the external load to the carbon rod.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cell EMF | $E$ or $\varepsilon$ | $E = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | **V (volt)** | $E^{\circ}$ = standard reduction potential |
| Faraday's Law (mass dissolved) | $m$ | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$ |
| Charge quantity | $Q$ | $Q = I \cdot t$ | C (coulomb) | — |
| Energy delivered | $W$ | $W = E \cdot Q = E \cdot I \cdot t$ | J (joule) | — |
| Capacity of a cell | $C_{\text{cell}}$ | $C_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M}$ | C or **A·h** | 1 A·h = 3600 C |
| Energy density | $\rho_E$ | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E \cdot C_{\text{cell}}}{m_{\text{cell}}}$ | Wh/kg | Higher for Li primary cells |
| Nernst Equation | $E_{\text{cell}}$ | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V | $Q$ = reaction quotient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Dry Cell Use:**
> 1. Current drain must be **low / intermittent** (≤ 250 mA continuous).
> 2. Operating temperature: **5 °C – 35 °C** (paste may freeze or dry outside this range).
> 3. Voltage drops steadily; cell is considered dead at **~0.8 V** under load.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Dry cells power **low-drain, portable, intermittent-load devices** where weight, cost, and shelf-life dominate over cycle-life. They are also the pedagogical "first cell" taught worldwide because their components are inexpensive, the chemistry is intuitive, and the half-reactions are easy to balance on an exam paper — exactly the kind of question KTU sets in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Half-Cell Reactions in the Dry Cell (with Explanations)

The two electrodes are **Zn** (anode) and the **MnO₂–C composite** (cathode). The electrolyte supplies **NH₄⁺** and **Cl⁻** ions.

### Step 1 — Oxidation at the Anode (Zinc dissolves)

$$
\text{Zn}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}
$$

**Logic:** Zinc, being more electropositive (lower reduction potential), is oxidized. Each zinc atom releases **2 electrons** to the external circuit, increasing the oxidation state from **0 → +2**.

### Step 2 — Reduction at the Cathode (Manganese dioxide is reduced)

Manganese in MnO₂ is in the **+4 oxidation state**. In the presence of ammonium ions (NH₄⁺) from the dissociated NH₄Cl paste, the reduction produces **Mn₂O₃** (Mn in +3 state) and releases **NH₃** and **H₂O**.

$$
2\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{4(aq)}^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Mn}_{2}\text{O}_{3(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{3(g)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
$$

**Logic:** Two Mn⁴⁺ atoms share the **2 electrons** from the anode reaction, each reducing by one unit to Mn³⁺. The NH₄⁺ supplies the hydrogen that forms water, and the nitrogen leaves as ammonia gas.

### Step 3 — Overall Cell Reaction (Add Step 1 + Step 2)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Zn}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \\
+ \quad & 2\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{4(aq)}^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Mn}_{2}\text{O}_{3(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{3(g)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)} \\
\hline
&\text{Zn}_{(s)} + 2\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{4(aq)}^{+} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + \text{Mn}_{2}\text{O}_{3(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{3(g)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Electrons cancel on both sides. The NH₄⁺ is the actual electrolyte ion consumed — it comes from the dissociation **NH₄Cl → NH₄⁺ + Cl⁻**. The Zn²⁺ immediately combines with Cl⁻ to form ZnCl₂ in the paste.

### Step 4 — Net Reaction (Including ZnCl₂ Formation)

$$
\text{Zn}_{(s)} + 2\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{4}\text{Cl}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{ZnCl}_{2(aq)} + \text{Mn}_{2}\text{O}_{3(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{3(g)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
$$

**Logic:** The ammonia produced is partly absorbed by the paste (to form Zn(NH₃)₂Cl₂) and partly escapes if the seal is imperfect — this is why an old dry cell may smell of ammonia.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Faraday's Law)

**Question:** A dry cell delivers a constant current of **0.5 A** for **6 hours**. Calculate:
(a) Total charge transferred in coulombs and ampere-hours.
(b) Mass of zinc dissolved, given $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.4\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$.

### Part (a) — Charge

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 0.5\ \text{A} \\
t &= 6\ \text{h} = 6 \times 3600\ \text{s} = 21{,}600\ \text{s} \\
Q &= I \cdot t \\
Q &= 0.5 \times 21{,}600 \\
Q &= 10{,}800\ \text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

In ampere-hours:

$$
Q = 0.5\ \text{A} \times 6\ \text{h} = 3.0\ \text{A·h}
$$

### Part (b) — Mass of Zinc Dissolved

Using **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \\
m &= \frac{65.4 \times 0.5 \times 21{,}600}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
m &= \frac{706{,}320}{192{,}970} \\
m &\approx 3.66\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **3.66 g** of zinc is consumed in delivering 3 A·h — this matches the empirical capacity of a standard D-size dry cell.

## 3.3 Polarization and the Role of MnO₂ (Depolarizer)

A naive cell with only NH₄Cl would suffer **hydrogen polarization**: H⁺ would be reduced at the carbon rod, forming H₂ bubbles that:
1. Coat the cathode, increasing internal resistance.
2. Set up a back-EMF opposing the cell.

The **depolarizer** is **MnO₂**, which oxidizes the nascent hydrogen before it can accumulate:

$$
\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow \text{MnO}_{(s)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
$$

MnO₂ hence *depolarizes* the cell, sustaining the EMF close to **1.5 V** even under moderate load.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Dry Cell (Leclanché) | Alkaline Cell | Lead-Acid Secondary |
|---|---|---|---|
| EMF (V) | 1.5 | 1.5 | 2.0 (per cell) |
| Rechargeable? | No | No | Yes (200–1000 cycles) |
| Internal Resistance | High (~0.5 Ω) | Lower | Very low (~0.01 Ω) |
| Shelf Life | 1–2 years | 5–7 years | 3–6 months (needs top-up) |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | High upfront, low per cycle |
| Performance at High Current | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Pollution Concern | Mn, Zn (low) | Mn, KOH (low) | Pb, H₂SO₄ (high) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dry Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ZINC ANODE - Negative terminal - Consumed during discharge"]
    B["ELECTROLYTE PASTE - NH4Cl + ZnCl2 + MnO2 - Ionic conductor semi solid"]
    C["CATHODE MIX - MnO2 + Carbon powder - Depolarizer and conductor"]
    D["CARBON ROD - Positive terminal - Electron collector inert"]
    E["SEALANT - Pitch or wax top - Prevents moisture loss"]
    F["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT - Load e.g. torch bulb"]
    G["IONIC PATH - NH4+ and Cl- through paste"]
    H["ELECTRONIC PATH - e- travel through wire from Zn to C rod"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    A --> F
    F --> D
    B --> G
    F --> H
    E -.- A
    E -.- D
```

## 4.2 Energy / Electron Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OXIDATION["ANODE - Oxidation site"]
        A1["Zn(s) → Zn2+ + 2e-"]
    end

    subgraph CIRCUIT["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT - Useful work"]
        B1["Electrons flow Zn → Carbon rod through load"]
    end

    subgraph REDUCTION["CATHODE - Reduction site"]
        C1["2MnO2 + 2NH4+ + 2e- → Mn2O3 + 2NH3 + H2O"]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1
    C1 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional View (Functional Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["SEALANT LAYER - Wax or pitch"]
    ROD["CENTRAL CARBON ROD - Positive current collector"]
    MIX["CATHODE MIX - MnO2 + Graphite powder (depolarizer)"]
    PASTE["ELECTROLYTE PASTE - NH4Cl + ZnCl2 + starch + H2O"]
    WALL["ZINC CAN - Negative electrode and container"]
    BOT["METAL BOTTOM CAP - Negative terminal"]

    TOP --> ROD
    ROD --> MIX
    MIX --> PASTE
    PASTE --> WALL
    WALL --> BOT
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Discharge of a Dry Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["START - Fresh dry cell EMF approx 1.5 V"]
    S2["Step 1: Zn oxidized to Zn2+ releasing 2e- per atom"]
    S3["Step 2: e- flow through external load to carbon rod"]
    S4["Step 3: At cathode MnO2 + NH4+ + e- form Mn2O3 + NH3 + H2O"]
    S5["Step 4: NH3 partially absorbed, partially escapes as gas"]
    S6["Step 5: Zn2+ combines with Cl- to form ZnCl2 in paste"]
    S7["Step 6: Paste dries, internal resistance rises, voltage drops"]
    S8["END - Cell dead when V_terminal falls below 0.8 V"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between primary and secondary cells. Give two examples of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Primary Cell | Secondary Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **Reversibility** | Cell reaction is **irreversible**; cannot be recharged | Reaction is **reversible**; can be recharged by passing reverse current |
| **Internal resistance** | High | Low |
| **Examples** | (i) Dry cell (Leclanché), (ii) Alkaline / Mercury cell | (i) Lead-acid battery, (ii) Nickel-Cadmium / Li-ion |

(1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction + 1 mark examples → **3 marks**)

### Q2. Why is the Leclanché cell called a "dry" cell even though it contains water? What is the role of MnO₂? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The cell is called **"dry"** because the electrolyte is held in an **absorbent paste** (NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂ + starch + water) rather than a free-flowing liquid; this prevents spillage and allows use in any orientation. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **MnO₂ acts as a depolarizer.** It oxidizes the H₂ gas that would otherwise form at the carbon cathode (polarization), preventing the formation of bubbles, reducing back-EMF, and keeping the terminal voltage near 1.5 V. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Dry Cell (Leclanché cell)**. Write the half-reactions at each electrode and derive the overall cell reaction. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A dry cell delivers **0.25 A for 20 hours**. Calculate:
   1. The total charge in coulombs and ampere-hours.
   2. The mass of zinc consumed.
   3. The total energy delivered in watt-hours, assuming the average terminal voltage is 1.3 V.
   *(Given: M_Zn = 65.4 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol)* *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**

| Component | Material |
|---|---|
| Anode (−) | **Zinc container** — also acts as the cell vessel |
| Cathode (+) | **Carbon (graphite) rod** at the centre |
| Cathode mix | MnO₂ + powdered carbon (graphite) packed around the rod |
| Electrolyte | **Paste of NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂ + MnO₂ + starch** in water, filling the space between cathode mix and zinc wall |
| Seal | **Pitch or wax** at the top |

**Working and reactions (4 marks):**

*Anode reaction (oxidation):*
$$
\text{Zn}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}
$$

*Cathode reaction (reduction):*
$$
2\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{4(aq)}^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Mn}_{2}\text{O}_{3(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{3(g)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
$$

*Overall reaction:*
$$
\text{Zn}_{(s)} + 2\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{4}\text{Cl}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{ZnCl}_{2(aq)} + \text{Mn}_{2}\text{O}_{3(s)} + 2\text{NH}_{3(g)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
$$

The cell EMF is **≈ 1.5 V**. As discharge proceeds, the zinc casing is gradually consumed, the paste dries, internal resistance rises, and the terminal voltage decays.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $I = 0.25\ \text{A}$, $t = 20\ \text{h} = 72{,}000\ \text{s}$, $V_{\text{avg}} = 1.3\ \text{V}$, $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.4\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$.

**Step 1 — Total charge (2 marks):**
$$
Q = I \cdot t = 0.25 \times 72{,}000 = 18{,}000\ \text{C}
$$
$$
Q = 0.25 \times 20 = 5.0\ \text{A·h}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of zinc (3 marks):**
$$
m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{65.4 \times 0.25 \times 72{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$
$$
m = \frac{1{,}177{,}200}{192{,}970} \approx 6.10\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy delivered (2 marks):**
$$
W = V_{\text{avg}} \cdot I \cdot t = 1.3 \times 0.25 \times 20 = 6.5\ \text{Wh}
$$

**Final Answers:** $Q = 18{,}000\ \text{C} = 5.0\ \text{A·h}$; $m_{\text{Zn}} \approx 6.10\ \text{g}$; $W = 6.5\ \text{Wh}$.

---

### Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Question) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **primary and secondary cells** in a tabular form covering at least six parameters. State **three disadvantages** of a dry cell. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With relevant equations, explain the phenomenon of **polarization** in a dry cell. How does MnO₂ act as a depolarizer? Why is the dry cell **unsuitable for continuous heavy-current applications**? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks — give at least six parameters):**

| Parameter | Primary Cell | Secondary Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Irreversible | Reversible |
| Rechargeable | No | Yes |
| Internal resistance | High | Low |
| Initial cost | Low | Higher |
| Life | Single use | Hundreds–thousands of cycles |
| Energy density | Moderate | Higher (especially Li-ion) |
| Examples | Dry cell, alkaline | Lead-acid, Ni-Cd, Li-ion |
| Maintenance | None | Periodic (e.g., water top-up in lead-acid) |

**Three disadvantages of a dry cell (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Cannot be recharged** — once Zn is consumed, the cell must be discarded.
2. **Hydrogen polarization** occurs under continuous load, reducing voltage and producing NH₃ gas.
3. **Voltage drops steadily** during use and the cell is unsuitable for sustained heavy current.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Polarization (3 marks):**
When a dry cell discharges, NH₄⁺ ions migrate to the carbon cathode. Ideally NH₄⁺ accepts electrons to form NH₃ and H, and the H should be oxidized. However, due to slow kinetics, **H atoms combine to form H₂ gas**, which:
- coats the cathode surface, blocking the reaction,
- produces a **back-EMF** that opposes the cell EMF,
- increases the internal resistance.

**Depolarizer (MnO₂) action (2 marks):**
MnO₂ is a strong oxidizing agent that oxidizes the nascent H to water:
$$
\text{MnO}_{2(s)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow \text{MnO}_{(s)} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)}
$$
(or, in the full cell reaction, MnO₂ itself is reduced to Mn₂O₃). This keeps the cathode surface free of H₂ bubbles and maintains a stable EMF.

**Why unsuitable for heavy current (2 marks):**
- The dry cell has **high internal resistance** (≈ 0.5 Ω for small sizes), causing large $IR$ voltage drop at high current.
- The **depolarizer MnO₂ is a solid** with limited reaction surface; it cannot consume H₂ fast enough at high current drains, so polarization sets in and the cell "dies" prematurely.
- The **paste electrolyte has low ionic conductivity** compared with liquid electrolytes (e.g., H₂SO₄ in lead-acid).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the NH₄⁺ in the cathode half-reaction.** A common error is writing $2\text{MnO}_2 + 2e^- \to \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{O}^{2-}$ — this is **not** what happens in a Leclanché cell. The electrolyte supplies the protons via NH₄⁺.
> 2. **Omitting the depolarizer role of MnO₂.** Marks are reserved for explaining *why* MnO₂ is present, not just that it is present.
> 3. **Unit conversion mistakes** in Faraday's-law problems: forgetting to convert hours → seconds. $1\ \text{A·h} = 3600\ \text{C}$, NOT 60 C.
> 4. **Confusing anode and cathode** — Zn is the *negative* terminal (anode, oxidation), carbon is the *positive* terminal (cathode, reduction). Spelling out the rule earns the 0.5 mark that students routinely lose.
> 5. **Stating "dry = no water"** — false; the paste contains water. Worth at least 1 mark in Part A.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Primary cell** = non-rechargeable, reaction **irreversible**; **Secondary cell** = rechargeable, reaction **reversible**.
- Dry cell = a **primary** galvanic cell with electrolyte in a **paste form** (not truly dry).
- **Anode (–):** Zn container — oxidized to Zn²⁺.
- **Cathode (+):** Carbon rod surrounded by MnO₂ + graphite.
- **Electrolyte:** Paste of **NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂** (with starch binder and water).
- **Standard EMF:** **≈ 1.5 V**.
- **Overall cell reaction:** $\text{Zn} + 2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \to \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **MnO₂ = depolarizer** — it removes H₂ that would otherwise polarize the cathode.
- **NH₃** is partly absorbed in the paste as $\text{Zn(NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2$ and partly escapes (the smell of old cells).
- **Cut-off voltage:** ~**0.8 V** under load — below this the cell is considered dead.
- **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$, with $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$.
- **Energy delivered:** $W = E_{\text{avg}} \cdot I \cdot t$ (in Wh) or $W = E_{\text{avg}} \cdot Q$ (in J).
- **Disadvantages** to memorize: not rechargeable, voltage drops, polarization, low current capability, short shelf life, NH₃ leakage.
- **Applications** (memorize 2–3): torchlights, transistor radios, remote controls, wall clocks, children's toys — all **low-drain intermittent** devices.
- **High-yield formula triplet for KTU:** $E = E^{\circ}_{\text{cath}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ ; $Q = I t$ ; $m = MIt / (nF)$.
- The dry cell is the *classic pedagogical example* of a primary cell — expect at least **one full 14-mark question** on it in every KTU PERUT637 cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lead Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lead Acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lead-Acid Battery (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **secondary (rechargeable) electrochemical energy storage device** that converts chemical energy into electrical energy through reversible electrochemical reactions between **lead dioxide (PbO₂) as the positive active material**, **spongy lead (Pb) as the negative active material**, and a **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) aqueous solution as the electrolyte**.

It is classified under the **"Conversion Type"** electrochemical storage system, where the active materials undergo a true chemical phase change (dissolution–precipitation mechanism) during each charge–discharge cycle, as opposed to **"intercalation type"** systems (like Li-ion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Significance:** Invented by **Gaston Planté in 1859**, the lead-acid battery is the **oldest rechargeable battery technology** still in commercial use. It remains dominant in **automotive SLI (Starting, Lighting, Ignition)** and **stationary backup power** applications due to its low cost per watt-hour, high surge current capability, and excellent recyclability (over **97%** of lead-acid batteries are recycled globally).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Two Buckets and a River" Analogy:**
> Imagine two buckets (the electrodes) connected by a river (the electrolyte).
> - **Bucket A (Positive Plate – PbO₂)** is filled with brown "rusty lead" (lead dioxide).
> - **Bucket B (Negative Plate – Pb)** is filled with soft, spongy grey lead.
> - The **river water (H₂SO₄)** carries sulfate ions back and forth.
> - When the **floodgate opens (discharge)**, sulfate ions rush out of the river and stick to the lead in both buckets, turning both into **lead sulfate (PbSO₄)** — a whitish, dead, inert sludge. The river becomes weaker (diluted acid, lower specific gravity). Once both buckets are clogged, the battery is "dead."
> - When we **push water back upstream using an external pump (charger)**, the sulfate is forcibly ripped out of the buckets and returned to the river, restoring the original brown PbO₂ and grey Pb. The river regains its strength.
> - This is the essence of the **"double sulfate theory"** of lead-acid chemistry.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Cell Voltage** | **2.0 V (Volts DC)** | Standard open-circuit voltage per cell |
| **Full-Charge Specific Gravity** | **1.26 – 1.28 g/cm³** at 27 °C | Density of H₂SO₄ electrolyte (Battery-grade) |
| **Discharged Specific Gravity** | **1.10 – 1.12 g/cm³** | Indicates state of charge < 20% |
| **Electrolyte Concentration** | **~37% H₂SO₄ by weight (full charge)** | Approx. 4.5 – 5.0 Molar |
| **Energy Density (Practical)** | **30 – 50 Wh/kg** | Much lower than Li-ion (~150 Wh/kg) |
| **Round-Trip Efficiency** | **70 – 85%** | Energy out ÷ Energy in |
| **Self-Discharge Rate** | **3 – 20% per month** | Depends on temperature & type |
| **Operating Temperature Range** | **−20 °C to +50 °C** | With derating above 40 °C |
| **Recyclability Rate** | **> 97%** | Highest of all battery chemistries |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Temperature (SRT):** All specific gravity readings in KTU problems are normalized to **27 °C (80 °F)**. A temperature correction factor of **±0.0007 per °C** must be applied for readings taken at other temperatures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Components (Architecture)

A lead-acid cell is constructed from the following key parts, each engineered for a specific electrochemical or mechanical role:

### 2.1.1 Positive Plate (Cathode during discharge)
- **Material:** Lead Dioxide (**PbO₂**) — dark brown in color.
- **Two crystal forms:**
  - **α-PbO₂ (orthorhombic):** Formed in alkaline/electrolytic pastes, denser, more stable, gives longer life.
  - **β-PbO₂ (tetragonal):** Formed in acidic pastes, higher initial capacity, but degrades faster.
- **Grid support:** Cast lead-antimony (Pb-Sb, 4–6% Sb) or lead-calcium (Pb-Ca) alloy grid holds the active material mechanically and conducts current.
- **Function:** Site of **reduction reaction** during discharge (gains electrons).

### 2.1.2 Negative Plate (Anode during discharge)
- **Material:** Sponge lead (**Pb**) — grey, porous, high surface area.
- **Expanders added** (lignosulfonate, barium sulfate) to prevent shrinkage and improve cycle life.
- **Grid:** Same alloy as positive plate, but may have different design.
- **Function:** Site of **oxidation reaction** during discharge (loses electrons).

### 2.1.3 Electrolyte
- **Aqueous Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** of specific gravity **1.21 – 1.28 g/cm³**.
- Serves **three simultaneous roles:**
  1. **Ionic conductor** (H⁺ and SO₄²⁻ ions carry charge between plates).
  2. **Active mass participant** (sulfate ions are consumed during discharge).
  3. **Heat sink** (absorbs heat generated during operation).

### 2.1.4 Separators
- Made of **microporous polyethylene (PE), rubber, or AGM (Absorbent Glass Mat)**.
- Prevent physical contact between +ve and −ve plates (short circuit) while permitting ionic flow.
- Must be **acid-resistant, electrically insulating, and ion-permeable**.

### 2.1.5 Container
- **Hard rubber, polypropylene, or ABS plastic** for flooded types.
- **Sealed steel can** (with pressure-relief valve) for VRLA types.
- Cell caps with vent plugs (flooded) or pressure-regulating valves (VRLA).

---

## 2.2 Electrochemical Working Principle — The Double Sulfate Theory

The lead-acid cell operates on the **Double Sulfate Theory** proposed independently by **Gladstone and Tribe (1882)**. The key insight is that **both electrodes convert to lead sulfate (PbSO₄) during discharge**, and the electrolyte's acid concentration decreases.

### 2.2.1 Discharge Reactions

> [!NOTE]
> **During DISCHARSE — Galvanic Mode (Spontaneous, ΔG < 0):**

**At the Negative Plate (Anode, Oxidation):**

$$Pb_{(s)} + SO_4^{2-}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow PbSO_{4(s)} + 2e^-$$

**At the Positive Plate (Cathode, Reduction):**

$$PbO_{2(s)} + SO_4^{2-}_{(aq)} + 4H^+_{(aq)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow PbSO_{4(s)} + 2H_2O_{(l)}$$

**Net Cell Reaction:**

$$Pb_{(s)} + PbO_{2(s)} + 2H_2SO_{4_{(aq)}} \longrightarrow 2PbSO_{4(s)} + 2H_2O_{(l)}$$

**Observations during discharge:**
- Both electrodes convert to PbSO₄ (whitish solid).
- H₂SO₄ is **consumed**; H₂O is **produced** → Specific gravity **decreases**.
- Current flows in external circuit from + terminal to − terminal.

### 2.2.2 Charge Reactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **During CHARGE — Electrolytic Mode (Non-spontaneous, External DC source required, ΔG > 0):**

The cell reactions are **exactly reversed** when a DC source greater than the cell EMF is applied.

**At the Negative Plate (Cathode, Reduction):**

$$PbSO_{4(s)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow Pb_{(s)} + SO_4^{2-}_{(aq)}$$

**At the Positive Plate (Anode, Oxidation):**

$$PbSO_{4(s)} + 2H_2O_{(l)} \longrightarrow PbO_{2(s)} + SO_4^{2-}_{(aq)} + 4H^+_{(aq)} + 2e^-$$

**Net Cell Reaction:**

$$2PbSO_{4(s)} + 2H_2O_{(l)} \longrightarrow Pb_{(s)} + PbO_{2(s)} + 2H_2SO_{4_{(aq)}}$$

**Observations during charge:**
- PbSO₄ on both plates is **decomposed** back to original active materials.
- H₂O is **consumed**; H₂SO₄ is **regenerated** → Specific gravity **increases**.
- Gassing (H₂ at negative, O₂ at positive) occurs near end of charge.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Energy Calculations

> [!TIP]
> **From Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis, the theoretical capacity of a lead-acid cell is governed by the mass of active material and the electrochemical equivalent of lead.**

The **theoretical specific capacity** (Ampere-hours per kg) is given by:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M \cdot 3.6} \;\; \text{Wh/kg (with voltage)}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction (= 2 for Pb / PbO₂)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $M$ = Molecular weight of active material (kg/kmol)

For the lead-acid system, combining the masses of **207 g of Pb + 239 g of PbO₂ = 446 g** (the active mass per equivalent of discharge), the theoretical specific energy is:

$$E_{th} = \frac{nFE_{cell}}{M} = \frac{2 \times 96485 \times 2.04}{0.446 \times 3600} \approx 245 \;\; \text{Wh/kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The **practical specific energy is only 30–50 Wh/kg** — i.e., a **utilization factor of just 12–20%** of the theoretical value! The remainder is "dead weight" (grid alloy, container, electrolyte water, separators, terminals).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = 0.85 + \text{SG}$ (approx.) | Approx. open-circuit voltage from specific gravity (SG in g/cm³) | Volts |
| 2 | $E_{cell}^{0} = 1.928 + 0.059 \log a_{H^+}^2 \cdot a_{SO_4^{2-}}$ | Nernst equation (full) for lead-acid | Volts |
| 3 | $SG_{27°C} = SG_T + 0.0007 \times (T - 27)$ | Temperature correction of hydrometer | g/cm³ |
| 4 | $\eta_{Ah} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Ampere-hour (coulombic) efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{Wh} = \dfrac{V_{avg,d} \cdot Q_{out}}{V_{avg,c} \cdot Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Watt-hour (energy) efficiency | % |
| 6 | $E_{stored} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Stored energy | Wh |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{t_h}$ | Peukert-derived current for discharge | A |
| 8 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{E^2}{4r_{int}}$ | Maximum power transfer theorem | W |
| 9 | $\text{State of Charge (SOC)} = \dfrac{SG_{current} - SG_{min}}{SG_{max} - SG_{min}} \times 100\%$ | SOC from specific gravity | % |
| 10 | $\text{Depth of Discharge (DOD)} = 100\% - \text{SOC}$ | DOD definition | % |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive SLI Batteries:** Capital cost is ~$50–$100/kWh (cheapest of all rechargeable chemistries).
- **Backup Power (UPS, Telecom):** Dominates the >$10B stationary market; provides 5–15 minute ride-through at 100–500 kW.
- **Forklifts, Golf Carts:** Deep-cycle traction batteries deliver 1000+ cycles at 80% DOD.
- **Off-Grid Solar:** Lead-acid remains preferred in developing markets for cost reasons.
- **Hybrid Vehicles (Micro-hybrid Start-Stop):** Enhanced Flooded Batteries (EFB) and AGM units handle 300,000+ micro-cycles per year.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cell EMF from Nernst Equation

The cell EMF of a lead-acid battery is a function of **H₂SO₄ activity** in the electrolyte. We derive the Nernst equation step-by-step as required in KTU derivations.

### Step 1 — Identify Half-Reactions and Standard Potentials

**Negative electrode (Pb ⇌ PbSO₄):**

$$Pb + SO_4^{2-} \rightleftharpoons PbSO_4 + 2e^- \quad E_{-}^{0} = -0.358 \;\; \text{V vs SHE}$$

**Positive electrode (PbO₂ ⇌ PbSO₄):**

$$PbO_2 + SO_4^{2-} + 4H^+ + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons PbSO_4 + 2H_2O \quad E_{+}^{0} = +1.685 \;\; \text{V vs SHE}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Nernst Equation to Each Half-Cell

For the **positive electrode (4-electron reduction)**:

$$E_{+} = E_{+}^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{PbO_2} \cdot a_{SO_4^{2-}} \cdot a_{H^+}^4}{a_{PbSO_4} \cdot a_{H_2O}^2}\right)$$

Since pure solids have $a = 1$ and pure water has $a_{H_2O} \approx 1$:

$$E_{+} = 1.685 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(a_{SO_4^{2-}} \cdot a_{H^+}^4\right)$$

For the **negative electrode (2-electron oxidation)**:

$$E_{-} = -0.358 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{a_{Pb} \cdot a_{SO_4^{2-}}}{a_{PbSO_4}}\right) = -0.358 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}})$$

### Step 3 — Compute Net Cell EMF

$$E_{cell} = E_{+} - E_{-}$$

$$E_{cell} = \left[1.685 - (-0.358)\right] + \frac{RT}{2F}\left[\ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}} a_{H^+}^4) - \ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}})\right]$$

The $a_{SO_4^{2-}}$ terms cancel logarithmically:

$$E_{cell} = 2.043 + \frac{RT}{2F}\left[2\ln(a_{H^+}^2) + \ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}}) - \ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}}) + \ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}})\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> After careful book-keeping, the simplified Nernst expression for the lead-acid cell is:

$$E_{cell} = 2.043 + \frac{RT}{F}\ln a_{H^+} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln a_{SO_4^{2-}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Standard Constants at 25 °C (298 K)

Using $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.02569$ V and converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ ($\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$):

$$E_{cell} = 2.043 + 0.0592 \log(a_{H^+}^2 \cdot a_{SO_4^{2-}}^{1/2})$$

For a fully charged cell with $a_{H_2SO_4} \approx 4.5$ M:
- $a_{H^+} \approx 8.0$ (from dissociation of strong acid)
- $a_{SO_4^{2-}} \approx 3.5$

Substituting: $E_{cell} \approx 2.05$ V, consistent with the empirical 2.04 V full-charge EMF.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Peukert's Law (Capacity Fade with Discharge Rate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peukert's Law** describes why a battery delivers **less capacity at high discharge currents** than at low rates. It is essential for KTU numerical problems involving automotive cranking amps.

Peukert empirically found (1897) that for a lead-acid battery:

$$I^{n} \cdot t = C_{p} = \text{constant}$$

Where:
- $I$ = discharge current (Amperes)
- $t$ = discharge time to cutoff voltage (hours)
- $n$ = Peukert exponent (typically **1.1 – 1.3** for lead-acid; close to 1 for low-rate, up to 1.4 for thick-plate traction)
- $C_p$ = Peukert capacity (A·h)

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Let the rated capacity be $C_R$ delivered at rated current $I_R$ over time $t_R$. By definition:

$$I_R^{n} \cdot t_R = C_p$$

**Step 2:** At any other discharge current $I$, the actual delivered time $t$ satisfies:

$$I^{n} \cdot t = C_p = I_R^{n} \cdot t_R$$

**Step 3:** The **actual delivered capacity** is $C = I \cdot t$. Solving for $t$:

$$t = \frac{I_R^{n} \cdot t_R}{I^{n}}$$

$$\boxed{C = I \cdot t = I_R^{n} \cdot t_R \cdot I^{1-n} = C_R \left(\frac{I_R}{I}\right)^{n-1}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
A 100 Ah battery rated at the 20-hour rate (i.e., $I_R = 5$ A, $t_R = 20$ h) has Peukert exponent $n = 1.25$. Find the capacity delivered at a 50 A discharge rate:

$$C = 100 \times \left(\frac{5}{50}\right)^{1.25-1} = 100 \times (0.1)^{0.25} = 100 \times 0.5623 \approx 56.2 \;\; \text{Ah}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The battery delivers only **56.2 Ah** at 50 A instead of the rated 100 Ah — a **43.8% loss**! This is why KTU examiners love Peukert problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Charging Time and Energy Inefficiency

**Given:** A 200 Ah lead-acid battery at 50% SOC is charged at a constant current of 40 A at an average charging voltage of 2.4 V/cell. A 12 V (6-cell) battery is used. The charge efficiency is 80%. Find (a) charging time, (b) energy supplied, and (c) energy efficiency.

### Solution

**(a) Charge to be delivered:**

The deficit charge = 50% of $C_R$ = $0.5 \times 200 = 100$ Ah. Accounting for charge efficiency $\eta_{Ah} = 0.80$:

$$Q_{in} = \frac{Q_{deficit}}{\eta_{Ah}} = \frac{100}{0.80} = 125 \;\; \text{Ah}$$

Charging time:

$$t = \frac{Q_{in}}{I} = \frac{125}{40} = 3.125 \;\; \text{hours}$$

**(b) Energy supplied by charger:**

$$E_{in} = V_{avg,charge} \times Q_{in} = (2.4 \times 6) \times 125 = 14.4 \times 125 = 1800 \;\; \text{Wh} = 1.8 \;\; \text{kWh}$$

**(c) Energy efficiency:**

Energy recovered on discharge at $V_{avg,discharge} = 2.0$ V/cell:

$$E_{out} = V_{avg,dis} \times Q_{out} = (2.0 \times 6) \times 100 = 1200 \;\; \text{Wh}$$

$$\eta_{Wh} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \frac{1200}{1800} = 66.67\%$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Flooded vs. VRLA (AGM vs. Gel)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a frequently-tested 7-mark comparison in KTU ESE papers.**

| Parameter | **Flooded (VLA)** | **AGM (VRLA)** | **Gel (VRLA)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrolyte state** | Free liquid | Absorbed in glass mat | Immobilized in silica gel |
| **Specific gravity (full)** | 1.26 – 1.28 | 1.30 – 1.32 | 1.24 – 1.27 |
| **Maintenance** | Top-up water needed | Sealed, no top-up | Sealed, no top-up |
| **Orientation** | Upright only | Any orientation | Any orientation |
| **Venting** | Vented (gas escape) | Valve-regulated (0.5–2 psi) | Valve-regulated |
| **Cycle life (80% DOD)** | 200 – 500 | 400 – 800 | 500 – 1000 |
| **Float life (25 °C)** | 6 – 10 yr | 8 – 12 yr | 10 – 15 yr |
| **Internal resistance** | Highest | Lowest (~30% lower) | Medium |
| **Cold-cranking amps (CCA)** | High | Highest | Lower |
| **Cost (per kWh)** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Thermal runaway risk** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Typical applications** | Automotive SLI, traction | Start-stop, UPS, military | Solar storage, telecom |

---

## 3.5 Sulfation and Stratification — Key Failure Modes

> [!WARNING]
> **Sulfation (Hard Sulfation):**
> When a lead-acid battery is **left in a discharged state** for an extended period, the PbSO₄ crystals on both plates grow into **large, hard, non-conductive crystals**. These crystals:
> - **Cannot be converted back** to active material by normal charging.
> - Reduce active surface area → loss of capacity.
> - Increase internal resistance.
>
> **Prevention:** Keep SOC > 60%, apply periodic **equalization charge** (controlled overcharge at 2.5–2.7 V/cell for 2–4 h).

> [!WARNING]
> **Acid Stratification:**
> In flooded cells, the **denser H₂SO₄** produced during charge **sinks to the bottom** of the cell, while **lighter water** floats on top. The top of the plates sees **weak acid** (slows reaction), and the bottom sees **strong acid** (causes corrosion and sulfation).
>
> **Prevention:** Occasional overcharge to generate gas bubbles that stir the electrolyte; use of tall plates carefully; AGM/Gel designs eliminate this entirely.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cell Construction — Cross-Sectional View

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Vent Cap with Flame Arrestor] --> nodeB[Cell Cover]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Positive Plate Group PbO2]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Negative Plate Group Pb Sponge]
    nodeC --- nodeE[Microporous Separator]
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Electrolyte H2SO4 SG 1.26]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Positive Terminal Post]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Negative Terminal Post]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Container Hard Rubber or PP]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Sediment Space for shedded active material]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single 2 V lead-acid cell contains a stack of alternating positive and negative plates (typically 5–17 plates per cell), interleaved with separators, all immersed in sulfuric acid electrolyte within a robust acid-resistant container. The vent cap allows gas escape during overcharge and provides access for electrolyte maintenance in flooded types.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Charge/Discharge Cycle Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DISCHARGE[DISCHARSE MODE Galvanic]
        nodeA1[Pb Negative Plate] -->|Releases 2e-| nodeB1[External Load]
        nodeB1 -->|Returns 2e-| nodeC1[PbO2 Positive Plate]
        nodeD1[SO4 2- Ions] -->|Migrate to both plates| nodeA1
        nodeD1 -->|Migrate to both plates| nodeC1
        nodeA1 -->|Forms| nodeE1[PbSO4 Lead Sulfate]
        nodeC1 -->|Forms| nodeE1
        nodeF1[H2SO4 Strong Acid] -->|Consumed becomes| nodeG1[H2O Water]
    end

    subgraph CHARGE[CHARGE MODE Electrolytic]
        nodeA2[DC Power Source] -->|Forces current opposite| nodeB2[PbSO4 Negative Plate]
        nodeB2 -->|Reduced to| nodeC2[Pb Sponge Lead]
        nodeA2 -->|Forces current opposite| nodeD2[PbSO4 Positive Plate]
        nodeD2 -->|Oxidized to| nodeE2[PbO2 Lead Dioxide]
        nodeF2[H2O] -->|Regenerated to| nodeG2[H2SO4 Strong Acid]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Logic:** Discharge is a **spontaneous chemical reaction** that releases electrical energy. Charging is the **exact reverse** — an external DC source forces current backward, restoring the original active materials. The same electrode sites alternate between being anodes and cathodes depending on mode.

---

## 4.3 State-of-Charge vs. Specific Gravity — Reference Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SOC_SCALE[STATE OF CHARGE SCALE]
        nodeSOC100[SOC 100 percent Full Charge]
        nodeSOC75[SOC 75 percent]
        nodeSOC50[SOC 50 percent]
        nodeSOC25[SOC 25 percent]
        nodeSOC0[SOC 0 percent Fully Discharged]
    end

    nodeSOC100 -->|SG 1.265| nodeSG_High[Electrolyte Dense]
    nodeSOC75 -->|SG 1.225| nodeSG_MidHigh[Moderate Density]
    nodeSOC50 -->|SG 1.190| nodeSG_Mid[Weak Acid]
    nodeSOC25 -->|SG 1.155| nodeSG_MidLow[Very Weak]
    nodeSOC0 -->|SG 1.120| nodeSG_Low[Essentially Water]

    nodeSG_High --> nodeNOTE1[Fully charged clear H2SO4]
    nodeSG_Low --> nodeNOTE2[Cloudy white from PbSO4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** A hydrometer reading below **1.225 g/cm³** indicates the battery needs recharging. A reading below **1.120 g/cm³** signals deep discharge with risk of permanent sulfation. A reading **above 1.30** in service may indicate overcharging and water loss.

---

## 4.4 Voltage Profile During Discharge Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeStart[Start at 2.10 to 2.15 V] --> nodeFlat[Flat Plateau 2.05 V for 70 to 80 percent of cycle]
    nodeFlat --> nodeKnee[Knee Region 1.95 to 1.85 V]
    nodeKnee --> nodeSlope[Rapid Voltage Drop]
    nodeSlope --> nodeCutoff[Cutoff Voltage 1.75 V per cell]
    nodeCutoff --> nodeDamage[Below 1.75 V Risk of Sulfation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discharge Termination Rule:** For lead-acid cells, discharge **MUST be stopped at 1.75 V/cell** (i.e., 10.5 V for a 12 V battery). Continuing below this threshold causes **irreversible sulfation** and plate damage. Never confuse this with Li-ion cutoff (~2.7 V/cell) — different chemistry, different rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the term "Double Sulfation Theory" as applied to lead-acid batteries and write the net cell reaction during discharge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The Double Sulfation Theory, proposed by Gladstone and Tribe (1882), states that **both the positive active material (PbO₂) and the negative active material (Pb) are converted into lead sulfate (PbSO₄)** during the discharge of a lead-acid cell, while the **sulfuric acid electrolyte is consumed** and its concentration decreases.

**Net cell reaction during discharge (2 marks):**

$$Pb_{(s)} + PbO_{2(s)} + 2H_2SO_{4(aq)} \longrightarrow 2PbSO_{4(s)} + 2H_2O_{(l)} \quad E_{cell}^{0} = 2.04 \;\; \text{V}$$

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is meant by "specific gravity" of the electrolyte in a lead-acid battery? Mention the standard full-charge value at 27 °C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
