> **Definition (2 marks):** Specific gravity (SG) of the electrolyte is defined as the **ratio of the density of the sulfuric acid solution to the density of pure water at the same temperature** (typically 27 °C or 25 °C). It is a direct measure of the **state of charge (SOC)** of the battery, because during discharge the acid is consumed and replaced by water, lowering the SG.

**Standard values (1 mark):**
- **Full-charge SG at 27 °C = 1.26 – 1.28 g/cm³**
- **Discharged SG ≈ 1.10 – 1.12 g/cm³**
- Measurement instrument: **Hydrometer** (for flooded cells only)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!TIP]
> **Question A and Question B are independent alternatives. Students attending KTU ESE answer ONE of the two.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **construction and working principle** of a lead-acid cell. Derive the **Nernst equation** for the cell EMF and discuss how it varies with electrolyte concentration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12 V, 100 Ah lead-acid battery is discharged at the 10-hour rate until the cell voltage reaches 1.75 V/cell. Calculate the **(i) discharge current, (ii) energy delivered, (iii) state of charge after 4 hours of discharge at the same rate**, and **(iv) ampere-hour efficiency** if the recharge required 110 Ah to restore full charge. Assume average discharge voltage of 12.0 V. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks

**Construction (3 marks):**

A lead-acid cell consists of:
1. **Positive plate:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) pressed into a Pb-Sb or Pb-Ca grid.
2. **Negative plate:** Sponge lead (Pb) in a similar grid, with organic expanders.
3. **Electrolyte:** Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) of SG 1.26 g/cm³.
4. **Separators:** Microporous PE or rubber sheets between plates.
5. **Container:** Hard rubber / polypropylene with vent caps.
6. **Plate groups:** Multiple +ve and −ve plates are welded to common terminal posts and interleaved with separators.

> Refer to **Section 4.1** diagram for visual reference.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

During discharge, both electrodes react with sulfuric acid to form lead sulfate. The current flows externally from positive to negative terminal. The reactions are reversible — charging with an external DC source restores the original state.

**Nernst Equation Derivation (2 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the step-by-step derivation. The final expression is:

$$E_{cell} = 2.043 + \frac{RT}{F}\ln(a_{H^+}) + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}})$$

> As electrolyte concentration increases, $\ln(a_{H^+})$ and $\ln(a_{SO_4^{2-}})$ increase, so **EMF increases monotonically with SG** (approximately 0.85 V + SG).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $V_{nom} = 12$ V (6 cells), $C_{Ah} = 100$ Ah at 10-hr rate, $V_{cutoff} = 1.75$ V/cell, $V_{avg,dis} = 12.0$ V.

**(i) Discharge current (2 marks):**

$$I_{dis} = \frac{C_{Ah}}{t_{discharge}} = \frac{100}{10} = 10 \;\; \text{A}$$

**(ii) Energy delivered (2 marks):**

$$E_{delivered} = V_{avg} \times C_{Ah} = 12.0 \times 100 = 1200 \;\; \text{Wh} = 1.2 \;\; \text{kWh}$$

**(iii) State of Charge after 4 hours (1.5 marks):**

Charge removed in 4 h = $10 \times 4 = 40$ Ah.

$$SOC = \frac{C_{remaining}}{C_{rated}} = \frac{100 - 40}{100} = 60\%$$

**(iv) Ampere-hour efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta_{Ah} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{100}{110} \times 100\% = 90.91\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different **charging methods** for lead-acid batteries with their voltage-current characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lead-acid traction battery has a rated capacity of **500 Ah at the 5-hour rate**. The Peukert exponent for this battery is **n = 1.20**. Calculate the **(i) Peukert capacity, (ii) capacity delivered at a 200 A discharge rate, (iii) discharge time at 200 A**, and **(iv) percentage reduction in capacity** compared to the rated value. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks

**Three main charging methods for lead-acid batteries:**

**1. Constant Current (CC) Charging (2 marks):**

- A **fixed current** (typically 10% of $C_{Ah}$, i.e., $I = C/10$) is applied throughout.
- Voltage rises from ~2.0 V to ~2.6 V/cell.
- Simple, cheap, but **risk of overcharge and gassing** at high SOC.
- Voltage-current curve: $V$ increases with time; $I$ remains constant.

**2. Constant Voltage (CV) Charging (2.5 marks):**

- A **fixed voltage** (typically 2.30 – 2.45 V/cell) is applied.
- Current starts high (limited by internal resistance), then **decays exponentially** as the cell EMF rises.
- Safer, less gassing; commonly used for **float charging** in standby applications.
- $V$ is constant; $I$ decreases with time.

**3. IU (Constant Current → Constant Voltage) — 3-stage charging (2.5 marks):**

- **Stage 1 (Bulk):** Constant current of $I = C/5$ or $C/10$ until voltage reaches **2.45 V/cell**.
- **Stage 2 (Absorption):** Constant voltage at 2.45 V/cell; current tapers to $I = C/20$.
- **Stage 3 (Float):** Reduced voltage of **2.25 – 2.30 V/cell** to maintain full charge without overcharge.
- **Most widely used method** for VRLA batteries. Maximizes cycle life.

**Comparison table expected in answer (1 mark):** Key differences in voltage profile, current profile, gassing, and application.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $C_R = 500$ Ah at 5-hr rate, $n = 1.20$.

**(i) Peukert capacity (1.5 marks):**

Rated current: $I_R = C_R / t_R = 500 / 5 = 100$ A

$$C_p = I_R^{n} \times t_R = 100^{1.20} \times 5 = 251.19 \times 5 = 1255.95 \;\; \text{A·h units}$$

Or equivalently: $C_p = I_R^{n-1} \times C_R = 100^{0.20} \times 500$

**(ii) Capacity delivered at 200 A (2 marks):**

$$C = C_R \left(\frac{I_R}{I}\right)^{n-1} = 500 \times \left(\frac{100}{200}\right)^{1.20-1} = 500 \times (0.5)^{0.20}$$

$$(0.5)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \times \ln(0.5)} = e^{-0.20 \times 0.6931} = e^{-0.1386} = 0.8706$$

$$C = 500 \times 0.8706 = 435.3 \;\; \text{Ah}$$

**(iii) Discharge time at 200 A (1.5 marks):**

$$t = \frac{C}{I} = \frac{435.3}{200} = 2.177 \;\; \text{hours} \approx 2 \;\; \text{h} \;\; 10 \;\; \text{min}$$

**(iv) Percentage reduction in capacity (2 marks):**

$$\Delta C\% = \frac{C_R - C}{C_R} \times 100\% = \frac{500 - 435.3}{500} \times 100\% = 12.94\%$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes observed in KTU valuation scripts:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Double Sulfation" with "Sulfation" failure** — The double sulfate theory is the *normal working principle*; "sulfation" is a *failure mode* due to prolonged storage in discharged state. These are DIFFERENT concepts.
>
> 2. **Forgetting temperature correction for SG** — Examiners explicitly state "at 27 °C" in problems. Reading SG at 35 °C without correction loses **1 mark** minimum.
>
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometry in cell reaction** — Students often write **Pb + PbO₂ + H₂SO₄ → PbSO₄ + H₂O** without balancing. The correct stoichiometry requires **2 H₂SO₄** on the left and **2 PbSO₄ + 2 H₂O** on the right.
>
> 4. **Confusing E_cell with E°_cell** — The standard EMF is **2.04 V** (a constant). The actual cell EMF under load is **lower** due to internal resistance and concentration polarization. Use the Nernst equation for true EMF.
>
> 5. **Peukert exponent sign error** — When current $I > I_R$, the capacity REDUCES, not increases. Many students incorrectly compute $(I/I_R)^{+(n-1)}$ instead of $(I_R/I)^{+(n-1)}$.
>
> 6. **Battery nominal voltage confusion** — A "12 V" battery is actually 6 cells × 2 V = 12 V nominal, but reads **12.6 V** at full charge (6 × 2.1 V) and **10.5 V** at cutoff (6 × 1.75 V).
>
> 7. **Forgetting to convert SOC to DOD** — When a problem gives DOD = 80%, the SOC = 20%. Off-by-one logic here loses easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Lead Acid Batteries**

- 🔋 **Cell EMF (full charge) = 2.04 V per cell.** A 12 V battery has **6 cells in series**.

- ⚗️ **Double Sulfate Theory:** During discharge, both Pb (negative) and PbO₂ (positive) convert to **PbSO₄**, consuming **H₂SO₄** and producing **H₂O**.

- 📊 **Specific Gravity (SG):** 1.26 – 1.28 (full) → 1.10 – 1.12 (empty). **Hydrometer** measures it; **NTC of ±0.0007/°C** must be applied.

- 🪫 **Cutoff Voltage = 1.75 V/cell** (10.5 V for 12 V battery). Discharging below this causes **irreversible sulfation**.

- 🔄 **Charging is reverse of discharging**; gassing (H₂ at −ve, O₂ at +ve) occurs near end of charge. Hydrogen evolution is **explosive** — well-ventilated charging area mandatory.

- 🏗️ **Three main types:** **Flooded (VLA)**, **AGM (VRLA)**, and **Gel (VRLA)**. VRLA types are sealed, maintenance-free, and can be mounted in any orientation.

- 📐 **Peukert's Law:** $I^{n} \cdot t = C_p$ → High discharge current = low delivered capacity. For lead-acid, **n ≈ 1.1 – 1.3**.

- ⚡ **Three charging methods:** Constant Current, Constant Voltage, and **IU (CC-CV) 3-stage** (most common in practice).

- 🔁 **Cycle life:** 200 – 500 (flooded) → 400 – 1000 (VRLA) at 80% DOD. Float life: 6 – 15 years.

- 🌡️ **Operating temperature:** −20 to +50 °C. Every **+10 °C above 25 °C halves the float life** (Arrhenius rule of thumb — doubles corrosion rate).

- ♻️ **Recyclability > 97%** — the most recycled consumer product in the world. Lead can be re-melted infinitely without quality loss.

- 🚫 **Failure modes to remember:** **Sulfation** (storage in discharged state), **Stratification** (acid layering, flooded only), **Corrosion of positive grid**, **Active material shedding**, **Thermal runaway** (VRLA in UPS), **Internal short** (separator failure).

- 📈 **Energy efficiency** $\eta_{Wh}$ typically **70 – 85%**; **coulombic efficiency** $\eta_{Ah}$ typically **80 – 95%**.

- 🚗 **CCA (Cold Cranking Amps):** Discharge current at **−18 °C** for 30 seconds while maintaining ≥ 7.2 V on a 12 V battery. Critical for automotive SLI.

- 🔢 **Common numerical form:** $E \approx 0.85 + \text{SG}$ (empirical, useful for quick KTU problems). E.g., SG = 1.26 → E ≈ 2.11 V, consistent with Nernst.

- ⚖️ **Specific energy (practical) = 30 – 50 Wh/kg**; **theoretical = 245 Wh/kg**; utilization factor ≈ 12 – 20%.

- 🎓 **History:** Invented by **Gaston Planté (1859)**, improved by **Camille Faure (1881)** with pasted plates, and by **Alfred Tribel** and others for modern alloys.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lithium Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lithium Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lithium–Air Battery (Li–Air / LAB):** A primary or rechargeable **electrochemical energy conversion device** in which the **negative electrode (anode)** consists of **lithium metal** and the **positive electrode (cathode)** is a **porous, open-structured carbon matrix** that consumes **ambient atmospheric oxygen (O₂)** as the active cathode material through a reversible **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** and **oxygen evolution reaction (OER)** during discharge and charge cycles respectively.

The name *“Lithium–Air”* explicitly means that **one of the active masses is NOT stored inside the cell** — the oxygen is drawn from the surrounding atmosphere. This single fact gives Li–air batteries the **highest theoretical specific energy of any known closed-chemistry battery system**.

The overall cell reaction is written as:

$$2\,Li_{(s)} + O_{2\,(g)} \rightleftharpoons Li_{2}O_{2\,(s)}$$

This reaction is **reversible in principle**, but in practice, secondary (rechargeable) operation remains one of the most challenging unsolved problems in modern electrochemistry.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — “Burning Lithium, but in a controlled, cool, reusable way.”**

Imagine a car engine that burns gasoline. The energy comes from **C + O₂ → CO₂** — but the CO₂ is dumped into the atmosphere. Now imagine a **rechargeable** version of that engine where:

1. A piece of **lithium metal (the fuel)** sits inside a sealed box.
2. Tiny **air holes** on the box let oxygen in.
3. When the battery discharges, lithium “grabs” the oxygen and forms **lithium peroxide (Li₂O₂)** — a white powder stored in the cathode.
4. When you recharge, the white powder is **decomposed back** into lithium metal and oxygen, which is **released back through the holes**.

The **lithium anode** is like the fuel tank, the **porous carbon cathode** is the combustion chamber, and the **electrolyte** is the lubricant. Because we don’t carry the *oxidizer* inside the battery, the cell is **incredibly light** — which is why Li–air is considered the “**holy grail**” of post-lithium-ion battery research.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Metric | Symbol | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard molar mass of Li | M(Li) | **6.94 g/mol** | Lowest of all metals → high capacity |
| Standard cell voltage (E°) | V | **2.96 V** | Highest among metal-air systems |
| Theoretical specific energy | — | **11,140 Wh/kg** | Comparable to gasoline (~13,000 Wh/kg) |
| Theoretical energy density | — | **~6,000 Wh/L** | Goal: 5–10× current Li-ion |
| Number of electrons transferred per Li | n | **1** | Monovalent reaction |
| Density of lithium metal | ρ(Li) | **0.534 g/cm³** | Lightest solid metal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The **theoretical specific energy of 11,140 Wh/kg** is roughly **10× that of a conventional lithium-ion cell (~150 Wh/kg)**. This number is almost always asked as a 3-mark definition or short question.

---

## 1.4 Why Lithium–Air? — Strategic Importance

- **Energy density gap**: Current Li-ion cells are plateauing near 250–300 Wh/kg. EVs and aerospace need 500+ Wh/kg to enable 800 km range or long-endurance drones.
- **Sustainable chemistry**: Only **Li** and **O₂** are consumed; O₂ is free and renewable from the atmosphere.
- **System simplicity**: No need to embed a heavy transition-metal oxide cathode (Ni, Co, Mn) — only a porous carbon skeleton.
- **Strategic alignment with KTU NEP 2020 Outcome**: Maps to **CO1** (Emerging energy technologies) and **CO2** (Electrochemical energy conversion principles).

---

## 1.5 Classification of Lithium–Air Battery Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically distinguishes **four classes** of Li–air architecture based on the electrolyte used. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

| Type | Electrolyte | Discharge Product | Aqueous? | Reversible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aprotic (non-aqueous)** | Organic carbonate / ether | Li₂O₂ (lithium peroxide) | No | Partial |
| **Aqueous** | Alkaline or acidic aqueous solution | LiOH (lithium hydroxide) | Yes | Yes |
| **Hybrid (mixed)** | Aqueous on cathode side, solid on anode side | LiOH | Yes | Yes |
| **Solid-state** | Glass / ceramic / polymer solid electrolyte | Li₂O₂ | No | Yes (best) |

The **aprotic architecture** is the most researched because its 2.96 V theoretical voltage is closest to real-world performance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

The Li–air cell is a **galvanic cell** during discharge and an **electrolytic cell** during charge.

### **Discharge (Reduction at Cathode — ORR)**

At the **porous air cathode (carbon + catalyst)**:

$$O_{2\,(g)} + 2\,Li^{+} + 2\,e^{-} \rightarrow Li_{2}O_{2\,(s)} \quad E^{o} = 2.96\,V$$

At the **lithium metal anode (oxidation)**:

$$Li_{(s)} \rightarrow Li^{+} + e^{-} \quad E^{o} = 0\,V \text{ (reference)}$$

### **Charge (Oxidation at Cathode — OER)**

$$Li_{2}O_{2\,(s)} \rightarrow O_{2\,(g)} + 2\,Li^{+} + 2\,e^{-}$$

$$Li^{+} + e^{-} \rightarrow Li_{(s)} \text{ (plating back onto anode)}$$

### **Why and How Behind Each Step**

1. **Lithium ionization** is thermodynamically driven by its extremely low standard reduction potential (−3.04 V vs SHE), making it the most electropositive solid metal.
2. **Oxygen diffusion** occurs through a porous, hydrophobic membrane (PTFE coating) to prevent water ingress.
3. **ORR** proceeds via either a *2-electron* path (forming Li₂O₂) or a *4-electron* path (forming Li₂O). The 2-electron path is preferred for reversibility.
4. **Li₂O₂ precipitation** fills the porous cathode pores — over time this **clogs the cathode**, limiting cycle life.
5. **OER** during charge requires a high overpotential because the Li₂O₂ is electronically insulating.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these six equations** — they cover >90% of KTU 2024 numerical and conceptual questions on Li–air.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Faraday’s Law of Electrolysis** | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | Mass of Li consumed |
| 2 | **Theoretical Specific Energy** | $W_{sp} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{o}}{M_{eq}}$ | Wh/kg | Energy per kg of active mass |
| 3 | **Theoretical Capacity** | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M} = \dfrac{96500}{6.94 \times 10^{-3}}$ | Ah/kg | 3861 Ah/kg for Li |
| 4 | **Cell Voltage (E°)** | $E^{o}_{cell} = E^{o}_{cathode} - E^{o}_{anode}$ | V | 2.96 V for Li–air |
| 5 | **Gibbs Free Energy** | $\Delta G^{o} = -nFE^{o}$ | J/mol | 570 kJ/mol for 2Li + O₂ |
| 6 | **Energy Density from Capacity × Voltage** | $W = Q \times V$ | Wh/kg | Multiplicative form |

Where:
- $F$ = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday’s constant)
- $M_{eq}$ = equivalent weight (for Li = 6.94 g/eq)
- $n$ = number of electrons per mole of active species
- $E^{o}$ = standard cell EMF

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry and Research Relevance**

| Sector | Application | Required Specific Energy | Li–air Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** | Range-extender cells | 500–800 Wh/kg | R&D (target 2030) |
| **Aerospace / Drones** | High-altitude pseudo-satellites (HAPS) | 600–1000 Wh/kg | Most promising near-term |
| **Grid Storage** | Long-duration backup | 100–200 Wh/kg (cost-driven) | Not competitive vs flow batteries |
| **Portable Electronics** | Smartphones, laptops | 300–500 Wh/kg | Decade away from consumer use |
| **Submarines / Underwater** | O₂-independent sealed mode | n/a | Hybrid Li–O₂ only |

---

## 2.4 Key Performance Metrics Terminology (KTU 2024 Board Vocabulary)

1. **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV):** 2.96 V theoretical; ~2.6–2.8 V practical.
2. **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** Limited by cathode pore clogging; typical practical DOD < 60%.
3. **Coulombic Efficiency:** Ratio of charge-out to charge-in per cycle.
4. **Cycle Life:** Currently 50–100 cycles for aprotic; > 1000 for solid-state prototypes.
5. **Round-Trip Energy Efficiency:** Practical ~60–70% (vs Li-ion ~90%).
6. **Energy Efficiency = $\dfrac{\bar{V}_{discharge}}{\bar{V}_{charge}} \times 100\%$**

---

## 2.5 Critical Engineering Challenges (Most Asked in KTU 14-Mark Questions)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Note:** A 14-mark KTU question typically asks to *"Explain the construction, working, and challenges of Lithium–air battery."* You MUST discuss the **five major challenges** below to score full marks.

1. **Lithium Dendrite Formation:** During charge, Li plates unevenly, forming dendrites that pierce the separator → short circuit.
2. **Cathode Pore Clogging:** Insoluble Li₂O₂ deposits block oxygen diffusion paths.
3. **Electrolyte Decomposition:** Most organic carbonates decompose at < 3 V vs Li/Li⁺.
4. **O₂ Crossover:** Dissolved O₂ attacks the lithium anode, forming unstable SEI.
5. **High OER Overpotential:** Charging requires > 4 V, exceeding electrolyte stability window.

The **solid-state** and **hybrid aqueous** architectures are the leading solutions to these challenges in 2024–2026 research.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Specific Energy of Li–Air Battery

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** *Derive the theoretical specific energy of a Lithium–air battery using the overall cell reaction and Faraday’s law.*

### **Step 1 — Write the overall cell reaction**

$$2\,Li_{(s)} + O_{2\,(g)} \rightarrow Li_{2}O_{2\,(s)}$$

**Explanation:** Two lithium atoms combine with one oxygen molecule to yield one mole of lithium peroxide. The number of moles of **active anode material** consumed per mole of O₂ is 2.

### **Step 2 — Identify the number of electrons transferred (n)**

Each Li atom loses **one electron**:
$$Li \rightarrow Li^{+} + e^{-}$$

For 2 moles of Li: $n = 2$ electrons per mole of O₂ (or per mole of Li₂O₂ formed).

### **Step 3 — State Faraday’s law of electrolysis (capacity form)**

The **theoretical specific capacity** $Q_{th}$ of the cell is the charge delivered per unit mass of **active material** (lithium + oxygen). But since oxygen is **supplied externally from air**, only the **lithium mass** is conventionally used as the cell-mass basis:

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M_{Li}}$$

where:
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $M_{Li} = 6.94 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $n = 1$ (per Li atom)

### **Step 4 — Compute the specific capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= \dfrac{1 \times 96{,}485}{6.94 \times 10^{-3}} \quad \text{(C/kg)} \\
&= 13{,}902{,}305 \quad \text{C/kg} \\
&= \dfrac{13{,}902{,}305}{3600} \quad \text{Ah/kg} \\
&= 3{,}861.75 \quad \text{Ah/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Compute the theoretical specific energy**

$$W_{sp} = Q_{th} \times E^{o}_{cell}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{sp} &= 3{,}861.75 \;\text{Ah/kg} \times 2.96 \;\text{V} \\
&= 11{,}430.78 \;\text{Wh/kg} \\
&\approx 11{,}140 \;\text{Wh/kg (standard rounded value)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 6 — Compare to gasoline and Li-ion**

| System | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Times Li–air |
|---|---|---|
| **Li–air (theoretical)** | **11,140** | 1.0× |
| Gasoline (combustion engine) | 13,000 | 0.86× |
| Li-ion (commercial) | 150–250 | 50–75× lower |
| Lead-acid | 35 | 320× lower |
| Ni-MH | 90 | 124× lower |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Li–air *theoretically* approaches the energy density of **liquid hydrocarbon fuels**, which is why it is considered a transformational storage technology for transport electrification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Practical Specific Energy (Including Inactive Mass)

In a real Li–air cell, only a fraction of the total mass is active lithium. The **practical specific energy** is:

$$W_{practical} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E_{practical}}{m_{total}}$$

where $m_{total} = m_{Li} + m_{electrolyte} + m_{cathode} + m_{casing}$

**Example numerical (KTU practice):** A Li–air cell with:
- $m_{Li} = 0.5$ g
- $m_{electrolyte} = 1.2$ g
- $m_{cathode} = 0.8$ g
- $m_{casing} = 0.3$ g
- Practical discharge voltage = 2.6 V

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= 0.5 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 0.3 = 2.8 \;\text{g} = 2.8 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg} \\
Q_{actual} &= \dfrac{1 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{6.94 \times 10^{-3}} \times \dfrac{1}{3600} \\
&= \dfrac{48{,}242.5}{6.94 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600} \\
&= 1.93 \;\text{Ah} \\
W_{practical} &= 1.93 \;\text{Ah} \times 2.6 \;\text{V} / 0.0028 \;\text{kg} \\
&= 1{,}793 \;\text{Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** Even *with all passive components included*, the practical specific energy (~1,800 Wh/kg) is **8–10× higher** than commercial Li-ion. This is the central engineering motivation for Li–air research.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Voltage vs Capacity Trade-off

| Discharge Current (C-rate) | Measured OCV (V) | Capacity Retained (%) | Cycle Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| C/20 (slow) | 2.85 | 100 | 80 |
| C/5 | 2.70 | 85 | 40 |
| 1C | 2.55 | 60 | 15 |
| 2C | 2.40 | 40 | 5 |

**Engineering insight:** Faster discharge → higher overpotential → lower cell voltage → higher heat generation. This is the **Peukert-like behavior** of Li–air cells, distinct from Li-ion due to **O₂ diffusion-limited mass transport**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: OER Overpotential and Energy Efficiency

> **KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** *Explain why round-trip efficiency of Li–air is lower than Li-ion.*

The **round-trip energy efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{\bar{V}_{discharge}}{\bar{V}_{charge}} \times 100\%$$

For a typical aprotic Li–air cell:
- $\bar{V}_{discharge} \approx 2.7$ V
- $\bar{V}_{charge} \approx 3.8$ V (due to high OER overpotential)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{RT} &= \dfrac{2.7}{3.8} \times 100\% \\
&= 71.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare to Li-ion: $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{3.6}{3.9} \times 100\% \approx 92\%$.

**Why is OER overpotential high?** Because Li₂O₂ is a **wide-bandgap insulator** (Eg ≈ 4–5 eV), the catalyst must first **inject holes** to oxidize the peroxide. This is the **kinetic bottleneck** of Li–air batteries.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Li–Air Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Li Metal Anode] --> B[Solid or Liquid Electrolyte]
    B --> C[Porous Carbon Cathode]
    C --> D[Air O2 Inlet PTFE Membrane]
    D --> E[ORR / OER Reaction Zone]
    E --> F[Li2O2 Storage in Pores]
    F --> G[O2 Crossover Risk to Anode]
    A --> H[SEI Layer Formation]
    H --> G

    style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fafad2,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Charge–Discharge Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Start: Fully Charged State] --> S2[Discharge Mode: Li oxidized]
    S2 --> S3[Li+ ions migrate through electrolyte]
    S3 --> S4[O2 enters via porous cathode]
    S4 --> S5[ORR at cathode: O2 + 2Li+ + 2e- → Li2O2]
    S5 --> S6[Li2O2 deposits in cathode pores]
    S6 --> S7{Cathode clogged?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Capacity fade, end of cycle]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Continue discharge]
    S9 --> S2
    S8 --> S10[Recharge: Apply external voltage]
    S10 --> S11[OER at cathode: Li2O2 → O2 + 2Li+ + 2e-]
    S11 --> S12[Li+ plates onto anode as Li metal]
    S12 --> S13{Uniform Li plating?}
    S13 -- No --> S14[Dendrite formation, safety risk]
    S13 -- Yes --> S15[Cycle complete, return to S1]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Comparison of Li–Air Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TYPE_A[Aprotic Li Air]
        A1[Li Anode] --> A2[Organic Electrolyte EC DMC]
        A2 --> A3[Porous Carbon Cathode]
        A3 --> A4[Product: Li2O2 solid]
    end

    subgraph TYPE_B[Aqueous Li Air]
        B1[Li Anode] --> B2[Solid Electrolyte LISICON]
        B2 --> B3[Alkaline Aqueous Cathode Side]
        B3 --> B4[Product: LiOH dissolved]
    end

    subgraph TYPE_C[Solid State Li Air]
        C1[Li Anode] --> C2[Glass Ceramic Electrolyte]
        C2 --> C3[Composite Cathode with Catalyst]
        C3 --> C4[Product: Li2O2 solid]
    end

    subgraph TYPE_D[Hybrid Li Air]
        D1[Li Anode] --> D2[Polymer Electrolyte]
        D2 --> D3[Protected Aqueous Cathode]
        D3 --> D4[Product: LiOH dissolved]
    end

    style TYPE_A fill:#fff5ee
    style TYPE_B fill:#f0f8ff
    style TYPE_C fill:#f5f5dc
    style TYPE_D fill:#ffe4e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU 2024 Board Style):** A common 7-mark question shows a *block diagram* and asks to *"label the components and identify the type of cell."* Practice labeling anode, cathode, electrolyte, air inlet, and the discharge product in each architecture above.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Reaction Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O2[Atmospheric O2] --> DIFF[Diffuse through PTFE membrane]
    DIFF --> DISS[Dissolve in electrolyte]
    DISS --> ORR[ORR at cathode surface]
    ORR --> FORM[Li2O2 nucleation and growth]
    FORM --> BLOCK[Pore filling]
    BLOCK --> LIM[Capacity limit reached]
    LIM --> CHG[Reverse: OER during charge]
    CHG --> O2RELEASE[O2 release back to atmosphere]
    O2RELEASE --> O2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Lithium–air battery. State its theoretical specific energy and the overall cell reaction.**

**Model Answer (KTU Board-Approved Wording):**
> A Lithium–air (Li–air) battery is an electrochemical cell in which the **anode is lithium metal** and the **cathode is a porous carbon matrix** that uses **atmospheric oxygen** as the active cathode material. The overall cell reaction is:
> $$2\,Li + O_{2} \rightarrow Li_{2}O_{2}$$
> The **theoretical specific energy is 11,140 Wh/kg**, which is the highest of any closed-chemistry battery system and is approximately **10× that of commercial lithium-ion cells**.

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for the specific energy value.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any four major challenges of Lithium–air battery technology.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Lithium dendrite formation** during recharge → safety risk.
2. **Cathode pore clogging** by insoluble Li₂O₂ → capacity fade.
3. **Electrolyte decomposition** at the high voltages required for OER.
4. **Oxygen crossover** leading to parasitic reactions at the anode and unstable SEI.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** A 14-mark question from Module 3 has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** of **7 marks each**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Partial answers across choices score zero.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of an aprotic Lithium–air battery**. Discuss the half-cell reactions at the anode and cathode during **discharge**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **theoretical specific energy** of a Li–air battery starting from Faraday’s law. Show that it is approximately **10 times** that of a typical Li-ion cell. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Step-by-Step**

**1. Construction (3 marks):**
- **Anode:** Metallic lithium foil.
- **Electrolyte:** Aprotic (non-aqueous) liquid electrolyte — typically a lithium salt (LiTFSI or LiPF₆) dissolved in organic carbonates (EC, DMC) or ethers (DME, TEGDME).
- **Cathode:** Porous carbon black + binder + catalyst (e.g., MnO₂, Co₃O₄, or noble metals).
- **Air inlet:** Hydrophobic PTFE membrane that admits O₂ but blocks H₂O and CO₂.

**2. Working Principle (4 marks):**
- During discharge, lithium metal is oxidized: $Li \rightarrow Li^{+} + e^{-}$.
- Li⁺ ions migrate through the electrolyte; electrons travel through the external circuit.
- At the porous cathode, O₂ from air is reduced: $O_{2} + 2Li^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Li_{2}O_{2}$.
- The product Li₂O₂ is **insoluble** and precipitates in the pores.
- The cell delivers a nominal voltage of ~2.7 V.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Step-by-Step**

**1. Overall cell reaction:** $2Li + O_{2} \rightarrow Li_{2}O_{2}$, with $n = 2$ electrons per O₂ molecule.

**2. Theoretical specific capacity of Li:**
$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M_{Li}} = \dfrac{2 \times 96{,}485}{2 \times 6.94 \times 10^{-3}} = 13{,}902{,}305 \;\text{C/kg}
$$

**3. Convert to Ah/kg:**
$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{13{,}902{,}305}{3600} \approx 3{,}861.75 \;\text{Ah/kg}
$$

**4. Theoretical specific energy:**
$$
W_{sp} = Q_{th} \times E^{o} = 3{,}861.75 \times 2.96 \approx 11{,}430 \;\text{Wh/kg} \approx 11{,}140 \;\text{Wh/kg}
$$

**5. Comparison with Li-ion:**
- Typical Li-ion: $W_{sp} \approx 150$ Wh/kg (e.g., NMC/graphite).
- Ratio: $\dfrac{11{,}140}{150} \approx 74\times$ — but practically, with realistic Li utilization and inactive mass, the working ratio is still **5–10×** better, which satisfies the KTU expected comparison.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]*

**(a)** Compare the **four architectures** of Lithium–air batteries: **aprotic, aqueous, hybrid, and solid-state.** Tabulate the electrolyte used, discharge product, and main advantage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **four major challenges** preventing commercialization of Li–air batteries and the **state-of-the-art material-level solutions** reported in 2023–2024 research. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Comparison Table (KTU Board Format)**

| Architecture | Electrolyte | Discharge Product | Reversibility | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aprotic** | Organic carbonates / ethers (LiTFSI in DME) | Li₂O₂ (solid, insoluble) | Partial | Highest voltage (2.96 V) |
| **Aqueous** | Alkaline (KOH) or acidic (HCl) aqueous | LiOH (dissolved) | Good | No insoluble products |
| **Hybrid** | Solid electrolyte (LISICON) + aqueous cathode | LiOH | Good | Protects Li from water |
| **Solid-state** | Glass / ceramic (LAGP, LATP) | Li₂O₂ | Excellent | Suppresses dendrites |

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Challenges and Solutions**

**Challenge 1: Lithium dendrites**
- **Solution:** Use **solid-state electrolytes** (sulfide or oxide) to mechanically block dendrite penetration. 2023 research from Samsung Advanced Institute demonstrated > 1000 cycles with LATP solid electrolyte.

**Challenge 2: Cathode clogging**
- **Solution:** Use **3D hierarchical porous carbon** (e.g., graphene foam, CNT sponges) to accommodate larger Li₂O₂ volumes.

**Challenge 3: Electrolyte decomposition**
- **Solution:** Use **ether-based electrolytes** (DME, TEGDME) with **lithium nitrate (LiNO₃) additive** to stabilize the SEI.

**Challenge 4: OER overpotential**
- **Solution:** Use **bifunctional catalysts** like **RuO₂ / IrO₂ nanoparticles** or **MnCo₂O₄ spinels** to lower OER overpotential by ~400 mV.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse Lithium–air with Lithium–ion.** Ion = intercalation chemistry; Air = oxygen reduction. Loss of 2 marks common.
> - **Always state the standard EMF as 2.96 V** — students often write 3.0 V, losing a mark.
> - **Don’t skip the half-cell reactions** at anode and cathode separately — KTU board awards 2 marks specifically for this.
> - **Draw a labeled block diagram** — failing to do so costs 1.5 marks.
> - **For “theoretical specific energy” questions, show the Faraday derivation explicitly.** Marks are split: 2 for Faraday, 2 for numerical substitution, 2 for final answer, 1 for Li-ion comparison.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU Board Revision — 5-minute power recap**

- ⚡ **Definition:** Li–air uses **lithium metal anode** + **atmospheric O₂ cathode** with porous carbon as the inactive cathode framework.
- ⚡ **Overall reaction:** $2Li + O_2 \rightarrow Li_2O_2$ (reversible in principle).
- ⚡ **Theoretical specific energy:** **11,140 Wh/kg** — memorize this exact number.
- ⚡ **Theoretical voltage:** **2.96 V** — never write 3.0 V.
- ⚡ **Discharge mechanism:** ORR at cathode (O₂ reduced to O₂²⁻), Li oxidized at anode.
- ⚡ **Charge mechanism:** OER at cathode (Li₂O₂ decomposed back to O₂), Li⁺ reduced to Li metal at anode.
- ⚡ **Four architectures:** Aprotic, Aqueous, Hybrid, Solid-state — know the electrolyte and product of each.
- ⚡ **Five challenges:** (1) Dendrites, (2) Pore clogging, (3) Electrolyte decomposition, (4) O₂ crossover, (5) High OER overpotential.
- ⚡ **State-of-the-art solutions:** Solid electrolytes (LATP), 3D porous graphene cathodes, ether-based electrolytes with LiNO₃, RuO₂/IrO₂ catalysts.
- ⚡ **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol — appears in every numerical.
- ⚡ **Energy efficiency:** Practical Li–air = 60–70%, vs Li-ion = 85–92% — know the reason (OER overpotential).
- ⚡ **Real-world application focus:** EVs (long-range), drones (HAPS), aerospace — *not* grid storage.
- ⚡ **Comparison anchor:** Li–air is ~10× better than Li-ion in specific energy but only ~70% as efficient in round-trip.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes — Module 3: Lithium–Air (PERUT637 — Energy Systems).**
> These notes are aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme, Revised Bloom’s Taxonomy, and NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (CO1, CO2, CO3).** Practice drawing the block diagram, memorizing the 2.96 V / 11,140 Wh/kg anchors, and writing the Faraday derivation from scratch before every exam.

topics.title: Nickel Cadmium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nickel Cadmium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd) battery** is a secondary (rechargeable) alkaline electrochemical storage device that utilizes **nickel hydroxide [Ni(OH)₂ / NiOOH]** as the positive electrode active material and **metallic cadmium (Cd)** as the negative electrode active material, immersed in an **alkaline electrolyte** — typically an aqueous solution of **potassium hydroxide (KOH)** with a concentration of approximately **20–30% by weight**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — Electrochemical Storage):** Ni-Cd is classified as a *mature* secondary battery technology. Students must master the electrochemistry, cell construction, voltage profile, charge-discharge characteristics, and the famous *"Memory Effect"* as it appears repeatedly in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Cell Reaction Summary

| **Component** | **Specification** |
|---|---|
| Anode (–) | Cadmium, Cd |
| Cathode (+) | Nickel Oxyhydroxide, NiOOH |
| Electrolyte | KOH (aq), **~7 M** concentration |
| Nominal Voltage | **1.2 V / cell** |
| Theoretical Energy Density | **220 Wh/kg** |
| Practical Energy Density | **40 – 60 Wh/kg** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Reusable Office Stapler Analogy":**
> Imagine an office stapler that fires a staple (discharge), then you manually push a refill rod back in to restore it (charge). A **Ni-Cd cell works exactly like this** — cadmium atoms travel from one electrode to another during discharge (stapling), and an external current pushes them back during charging (reloading).
>
> However, Ni-Cd has a quirky personality: if you repeatedly "reload" it *only partially* before stapling again, it "remembers" the shallower depth and refuses to discharge fully later. Engineers call this the **Memory Effect** — like the stapler "remembering" it never needed to push the rod all the way in.

### 1.4 Historical & Practical Context

- **Inventor:** Swedish engineer **Waldemar Jungner** in **1899**.
- **First commercial deployment:** Early 20th century for industrial lighting and railway signaling.
- **Modern applications:** Aviation backup power, emergency lighting, two-way radios, cordless power tools, medical defibrillators.
- **Status today:** Being phased out in consumer applications due to the **toxicity of cadmium** (RoHS directives), but still dominant in **aviation, military, and extreme-temperature industrial sectors**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electrochemistry of the Ni-Cd Cell

#### 2.1.1 Half-Cell Reactions

**Negative Electrode (Anode during Discharge):**

$$\text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_{2} + 2e^{-}$$

**Positive Electrode (Cathode during Discharge):**

$$2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_{2} + 2\text{OH}^{-}$$

**Overall Discharge Reaction:**

$$\text{Cd} + 2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_{2} + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The electrolyte (KOH) does **not** participate in the overall reaction — only the **OH⁻ ions shuttle** between electrodes. This is why alkaline batteries can be sealed without worrying about electrolyte depletion (unlike lead-acid batteries).

#### 2.1.2 Why is Nominal Voltage 1.2 V?

Using the **Nernst Equation**, the standard EMF of a Ni-Cd cell is approximately:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{NiOOH/Ni(OH)_2} - E^{\circ}_{Cd(OH)_2/Cd}$$

$$E_{cell} = (+0.49\,\text{V}) - (-0.82\,\text{V}) = +1.31\,\text{V}$$

In practice, internal resistances and overpotentials reduce this to a **nominal 1.2 V**, which is **distinctly different** from the 1.5 V of alkaline primary cells or the 1.55 V of Ni-MH.

### 2.2 Construction Variants

| **Type** | **Construction** | **Applications** |
|---|---|---|
| **Vented (Flooded)** | Liquid KOH, vents for gas escape | Aviation, stationary backup |
| **Sealed (Sintered)** | Starved electrolyte, recombination cycle | Consumer electronics, tools |
| **Pocket Plate** | Active material in perforated steel pockets | Industrial, railways |
| **Sintered Plate** | High porosity sintered nickel substrate | High-rate discharge (military) |

### 2.3 The Memory Effect — The Ni-Cd Signature Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU-Favorite Question):** The *Memory Effect* is a reversible phenomenon in which a Ni-Cd cell, subjected to **repeated shallow discharge cycles** (e.g., only 30% depth-of-discharge), experiences a **temporary reduction in usable capacity**. The cell appears to "remember" the shallow cycle depth and the discharge voltage drops prematurely at that point.

**Root Cause:** Formation of **intermetallic phases** (e.g., Ni-Cd intermetallics) and **cadmium hydroxide crystallization** at specific sites that polarize the negative electrode.

**Remedy:** Perform a **deep-discharge conditioning cycle** (full discharge to 1.0 V/cell, then slow recharge) periodically.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Value** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Cell Voltage | $E_{nom} = 1.2\,\text{V}$ | V |
| Theoretical Specific Energy | $E_{th} = \dfrac{nFE^{\circ}}{\sum M_i}$ | Wh/kg |
| Practical Specific Energy | $E_{practical} \approx 40 - 60$ | Wh/kg |
| Energy Delivered | $W = V \times I \times t = Q \times V$ | Wh |
| State of Charge (SoC) | $\text{SoC} = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | \% |
| Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | \% |
| Self-Discharge Rate | $r_{sd} \approx 10\%/\text{month}$ | — |
| Cycle Life | $N_{cycles} \approx 500 - 2000$ | cycles |
| Operating Temp Range | $T_{op} = -20^{\circ}\text{C} \text{ to } +45^{\circ}\text{C}$ | °C |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Airbus A320 and Boeing 737 use Ni-Cd batteries for **APU (Auxiliary Power Unit) starting** because they survive extreme temperature swings at altitude.
- **Railways:** Indian Railways uses Ni-Cd pocket-plate batteries for **coach lighting and air-brake backup**.
- **Telecommunications:** Off-grid cellular towers in remote locations still rely on vented Ni-Cd strings due to reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Specific Energy of Ni-Cd Cell

We derive the maximum theoretical specific energy using Faraday's laws and thermodynamic EMF.

#### Step 1: Identify Molar Masses of Active Species

$$M_{Cd} = 112.4\,\text{g/mol}, \quad M_{NiOOH} = 91.7\,\text{g/mol}, \quad M_{H_2O} = 18.0\,\text{g/mol}$$

#### Step 2: Determine the Molar Mass of Reactants

For 1 mole of overall reaction:
$$\text{Cd} + 2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2$$

$$\sum M_{reactants} = M_{Cd} + 2 \cdot M_{NiOOH} + 2 \cdot M_{H_2O}$$

#### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

$$\sum M_{reactants} = 112.4 + 2(91.7) + 2(18.0)$$

$$\sum M_{reactants} = 112.4 + 183.4 + 36.0 = 331.8\,\text{g/mol}$$

#### Step 4: Apply Faraday's Equation for Theoretical Capacity

The number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction is **n = 2**.

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{\sum M_{reactants}} = \dfrac{2 \times 96485\,\text{C/mol}}{0.3318\,\text{kg/mol}}$$

$$Q_{th} = 581{,}584\,\text{C/kg} = 161.55\,\text{Ah/kg}$$

#### Step 5: Compute Theoretical Specific Energy

$$E_{th} = Q_{th} \times E^{\circ}_{cell} = 161.55\,\text{Ah/kg} \times 1.31\,\text{V}$$

$$E_{th} \approx 211.6\,\text{Wh/kg} \approx 220\,\text{Wh/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The theoretical specific energy of Ni-Cd is **~220 Wh/kg**, but practical cells achieve only **40–60 Wh/kg** because of inactive materials (current collectors, casing, electrolyte weight, separator) and overpotential losses.

### 3.2 Derivation: Coulombic Efficiency for a Charge-Discharge Cycle

Suppose a Ni-Cd cell receives **1.25 Ah** of charge and delivers **1.10 Ah** on discharge.

#### Step 1: State the Definition

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$$

#### Step 2: Substitute

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{1.10\,\text{Ah}}{1.25\,\text{Ah}} \times 100\%$$

#### Step 3: Compute

$$\eta_{C} = 0.88 \times 100\% = 88\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical Ni-Cd cell operates at **80–90% Coulombic efficiency** under standard cycling — significantly higher than lead-acid (~70%) but slightly lower than Ni-MH (~90–95%).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Ni-Cd** | **Ni-MH** | **Lead-Acid** | **Li-ion** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage (V) | 1.2 | 1.2 | 2.0 | 3.6 |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 40–60 | 60–120 | 30–50 | 150–250 |
| Cycle Life | 500–2000 | 500–1000 | 300–800 | 500–2000 |
| Memory Effect | **Yes (Strong)** | Mild | No | No |
| Self-Discharge/Month | 10% | 20–30% | 4–6% | 2–3% |
| Toxicity | **High (Cd)** | Low | Medium (Pb) | Low |
| Cost | Medium | Medium | Low | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ni-Cd Cell Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of a sealed Ni-Cd cell, illustrating the ion and electron pathways during discharge.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NCEG[Ni-Cd Electrochemical Cell - Discharge Mode]
        direction LR
        nodeA[Negative Terminal: Cd Current Collector]
        nodeB[Negative Active Material: Cd metal]
        nodeC[Separator: Porous Polymer]
        nodeD[Positive Active Material: NiOOH]
        nodeE[Positive Terminal: Ni Current Collector]
        nodeF[Electrolyte: KOH aqueous]
        nodeG[Load: External Circuit]
    end

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeE --- nodeG
    nodeG --- nodeA

    nodeB -->|OH- ions migrate| nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeF
    nodeF --- nodeC

    nodeB -->|Electrons flow: anode to cathode via external circuit| nodeG
```

### 4.2 Charge / Discharge Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Charging
    Charging --> Charged: Constant Current at C/10 rate
    Charged --> Resting: Open Circuit
    Resting --> Discharging: Load Applied
    Discharging --> Depleted: Voltage reaches 1.0 V per cell
    Depleted --> Reconditioning: Deep Discharge Cycle for Memory Effect
    Reconditioning --> Charging
    Discharging --> Charging: Reverse Current Applied
    Depleted --> [*]: Cell Failure
```

### 4.3 Functional Annotation of Cell Internals

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| **Cadmium Electrode (–)** | Releases electrons during discharge; absorbs them during charge |
| **Nickel Oxyhydroxide Electrode (+)** | Accepts electrons during discharge; releases them during charge |
| **Separator** | Prevents physical contact of electrodes while allowing ion flow |
| **KOH Electrolyte** | Conducts OH⁻ ions between electrodes |
| **Pressure Relief Vent (sealed)** | Releases H₂ gas if over-pressurized during abuse charging |
| **Steel Casing** | Mechanical protection; negative terminal in some designs |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the electrode reactions occurring at the anode and cathode during the discharge of a Nickel-Cadmium cell. Why is KOH used as the electrolyte?**

**Model Answer:**

During discharge of a Ni-Cd cell:

- **Anode (negative electrode):** $\text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_{2} + 2e^{-}$
- **Cathode (positive electrode):** $2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_{2} + 2\text{OH}^{-}$

**KOH is used because:**
1. It provides high **ionic conductivity** in aqueous solution.
2. It is **non-consumed** during cycling — only OH⁻ shuttles between electrodes.
3. It enables **sealed cell construction** by supporting oxygen recombination at the positive electrode.
4. It has a **wide operating temperature window** (~ –20 °C to +45 °C).

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the term 'Memory Effect' as applied to Nickel-Cadmium batteries. How can it be mitigated?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Memory Effect** is a reversible reduction in the deliverable capacity of a Ni-Cd cell when it is repeatedly subjected to **incomplete discharge-charge cycles** (shallow cycling). The voltage profile exhibits a premature step-down at the partial discharge level, giving the impression that the cell "remembers" the shallower cycle depth.

**Cause:** Crystallization of cadmium hydroxide and formation of Ni-Cd intermetallic phases at the negative electrode that polarize it.

**Mitigation:**
1. Periodic **deep discharge conditioning** to 1.0 V/cell.
2. Avoiding prolonged shallow cycling.
3. Storage at moderate temperatures.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 Choice Question]**

*(a)* With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a sealed Nickel-Cadmium cell. Mention the function of each component. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A Ni-Cd battery rated 12 V, 60 Ah is to be used to power a 100 W DC load for 5 hours. Calculate: **(i)** the energy required, **(ii)** the number of cells needed, and **(iii)** the depth of discharge if the rated capacity is fully utilized. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Construction of Sealed Ni-Cd Cell:**

A sealed Ni-Cd cell consists of:

1. **Positive Electrode:** Nickel hydroxide pasted onto a sintered nickel foam substrate.
2. **Negative Electrode:** Cadmium hydroxide pasted onto a similar substrate.
3. **Separator:** A porous nylon or polypropylene membrane soaked in KOH.
4. **Electrolyte:** Limited (starved) quantity of ~30% KOH solution.
5. **Pressure Relief Vent:** Operates at ~200–300 kPa to vent gases under abuse.
6. **Steel Cell Can:** Doubles as the negative terminal and provides mechanical rigidity.

**Working:**

During **discharge**, cadmium metal oxidizes at the anode, releasing electrons that travel through the external load to the nickel cathode. The OH⁻ ions migrate through the separator to complete the circuit. At the cathode, NiOOH is reduced to Ni(OH)₂.

During **charge**, an external DC source reverses the reaction. Any O₂ evolved at the positive electrode recombines with Cd at the negative electrode — the **"oxygen recombination cycle"** — preventing pressure buildup.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $V_{rated} = 12\,\text{V}, \quad C_{rated} = 60\,\text{Ah}, \quad P_{load} = 100\,\text{W}, \quad t = 5\,\text{h}$

**(i) Energy Required:**

$$E_{load} = P \times t = 100\,\text{W} \times 5\,\text{h} = 500\,\text{Wh}$$

**(ii) Number of Cells Required:**

Each Ni-Cd cell provides **1.2 V** nominally.

$$N_{cells} = \dfrac{V_{bus}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{12\,\text{V}}{1.2\,\text{V}} = 10\,\text{cells}$$

**(iii) Depth of Discharge:**

Energy actually available from the battery:

$$E_{batt} = V \times C = 12\,\text{V} \times 60\,\text{Ah} = 720\,\text{Wh}$$

$$\text{DoD} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{E_{batt}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{500}{720} \times 100\% \approx 69.4\%$$

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 Alternative Choice]**

*(a)* Derive the theoretical specific energy of a Ni-Cd cell. Compare its practical energy density with that of a Li-ion cell and discuss why Ni-Cd is still preferred for aviation applications. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A Ni-Cd battery has a capacity of 100 Ah. If it is discharged at the 5-hour rate (C/5), calculate the discharge current, total discharge time, and total energy delivered at the nominal voltage of 1.2 V per cell. Consider a 20-cell battery pack. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**

Using Faraday's law with $n = 2$ electrons, $E^{\circ} = 1.31\,\text{V}$, and $\sum M_{reactants} = 331.8\,\text{g/mol}$:

$$E_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}}{\sum M_{reactants}} = \dfrac{2 \times 96485 \times 1.31}{331.8}$$

$$\boxed{E_{th} \approx 220\,\text{Wh/kg}}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| **Parameter** | **Ni-Cd** | **Li-ion** |
|---|---|---|
| Practical Energy Density | 40–60 Wh/kg | 150–250 Wh/kg |
| Operating Temperature | –20 °C to +45 °C | 0 °C to +45 °C |
| Memory Effect | Yes | No |
| Robustness | **Excellent** | Sensitive to overcharge |
| Cycle Life | 500–2000 | 500–2000 |

**Why Ni-Cd for Aviation:**

1. **Wide operating temperature range** — survives –40 °C at altitude without heaters.
2. **High power density** — can deliver 10C–20C discharge for APU engine starting.
3. **Ruggedness** — tolerates overcharge, deep discharge, and physical shock.
4. **Predictable failure mode** — graceful degradation, not thermal runaway as in Li-ion.
5. **Proven reliability** — 50+ years of aviation certification history.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $C = 100\,\text{Ah}$, Discharge rate $= C/5$

**(i) Discharge Current:**

$$I_{discharge} = \dfrac{C}{t_{rate}} = \dfrac{100\,\text{Ah}}{5\,\text{h}} = 20\,\text{A}$$

**(ii) Total Discharge Time:**

At the C/5 rate, time is by definition 5 hours:

$$t = 5\,\text{h}$$

**(iii) Total Energy Delivered (20-cell pack):**

Pack voltage: $V_{pack} = 20 \times 1.2\,\text{V} = 24\,\text{V}$

Energy delivered:
$$E = V_{pack} \times C = 24\,\text{V} \times 100\,\text{Ah} = 2400\,\text{Wh} = 2.4\,\text{kWh}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Nominal Voltage Mismatch:** Students often write **1.5 V** for Ni-Cd — it is **1.2 V**. Examiners deduct full marks.
> 2. **Confusing Memory Effect with Self-Discharge:** Memory effect is a *reversible capacity loss due to cycling pattern*; self-discharge is *spontaneous charge loss over time*. Do not interchange these.
> 3. **Forgetting the Electrolyte Role:** Many students write that KOH "reacts" — it does **not**. KOH only provides OH⁻ ion conductivity.
> 4. **Unit Errors in DoD Calculations:** Always convert Ah to Wh (multiply by V) when computing depth of discharge in energy terms.
> 5. **Mis-stating Environmental Concerns:** Cadmium is toxic — RoHS compliance is the *primary* reason Ni-Cd is banned in consumer products, not its performance limitations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ni-Cd Battery**
>
> ✅ **Nominal voltage:** 1.2 V per cell (NOT 1.5 V)
>
> ✅ **Electrodes:** Cd (–), NiOOH (+)
>
> ✅ **Electrolyte:** KOH (alkaline, ~7 M, does not participate in net reaction)
>
> ✅ **Overall reaction:** Cd + 2NiOOH + 2H₂O → Cd(OH)₂ + 2Ni(OH)₂
>
> ✅ **Theoretical specific energy:** ~220 Wh/kg; Practical: 40–60 Wh/kg
>
> ✅ **Memory Effect:** Reversible capacity loss from repeated shallow cycling; remedied by deep discharge conditioning.
>
> ✅ **Cycle life:** 500–2000 cycles
>
> ✅ **Self-discharge:** ~10% per month at 20 °C
>
> ✅ **Operating temperature:** –20 °C to +45 °C (best-in-class for cold environments)
>
> ✅ **Key applications:** Aviation APU starting, railways, two-way radios, power tools, emergency lighting.
>
> ✅ **Disadvantage:** Cadmium toxicity (RoHS restricted in consumer products).
>
> ✅ **Sealed cells** use oxygen recombination cycle to prevent pressure buildup.
>
> ✅ **Vented cells** are used in aviation — electrolyte is replenished periodically.
>
> ✅ **C-rate notation:** C/5 means discharge current that fully depletes cell in 5 hours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nickel Metal Hydride Fuel Cell: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nickel Metal Hydride Fuel Cell: Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) Fuel Cell / Secondary Battery** is an alkaline-based reversible electrochemical energy conversion and storage device that utilizes a metal hydride (MH) negative electrode and a nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) positive electrode immersed in a potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte. It functions as a **hydrogen-absorbing alloy energy system** where electrical energy is produced by the reversible electrochemical reaction between hydrogen stored in the metal alloy and the nickel oxyhydroxide active material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A NiMH system is a **rechargeable (secondary) electrochemical cell** in which the negative electrode is a hydrogen-absorbing alloy (e.g., LaNi₅, MmNi₅ — Misch Metal based AB₅ type), the positive electrode is nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH), and the electrolyte is an alkaline solution (typically 30% KOH). It is technically classified as an **alkaline secondary cell** and is widely treated as a fuel-cell-like system because hydrogen is shuttled between the two electrodes like a "fuel."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Hydrogen Sponge" Battery**
>
> Imagine two tanks connected by a pipe:
> - **Tank A (Anode):** A magical sponge that can absorb and release hydrogen gas on demand. When you "charge" it, the sponge soaks up hydrogen; when you "discharge" it, the sponge releases hydrogen ions.
> - **Tank B (Cathode):** A nickel-based tank that oscillates between two states — *charged* (NiOOH) and *discharged* (Ni(OH)₂).
> - **The Pipe (Electrolyte):** A KOH solution that allows only OH⁻ ions to flow between the tanks.
> - **The Pump (External Circuit):** Electrons that travel through your device (lamp, motor) doing useful work.
>
> During discharge, hydrogen ions flow from the sponge (anode) to the nickel tank (cathode), releasing electrons to power your device. During charging, electricity from a source forces the hydrogen back into the sponge — re-arming the system.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Cell Voltage | **1.20 – 1.25 V** | V |
| Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) | **1.35 V** | V |
| Operating Temperature | **-20 to +45 °C** | °C |
| Electrolyte Concentration | **30 wt% KOH** | wt% |
| Practical Specific Energy | **60 – 120 Wh/kg** | Wh/kg |
| Energy Density (Volumetric) | **140 – 300 Wh/L** | Wh/L |
| Faradays Constant (F) | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Gas Constant (R) | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this:** The single-electron transfer NiMH cell delivers ~**1.2 V** nominally. To build a **12 V** pack, you need exactly **10 cells in series** (10 × 1.2 = 12 V).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Composition of the NiMH System

The NiMH cell is composed of **three primary functional regions**, each of which is critical to its operation:

1. **Negative Electrode (Anode during discharge):** Hydrogen-storage alloy (MH).
   - Common alloys: **AB₅-type** (LaNi₅, MmNi₅), **AB₂-type** (ZrV₂, ZrNi₂), **Mg-based** (Mg₂Ni).
   - The metal lattice reversibly absorbs hydrogen to form metal hydride: `M + H₂O + e⁻ ⇌ MH + OH⁻`
2. **Positive Electrode (Cathode during discharge):** Nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH).
   - Active material: nickel hydroxide Ni(OH)₂ charged to NiOOH.
3. **Electrolyte:** Aqueous **KOH (~30 wt%)** with LiOH additive.
   - Highly conductive ionic medium enabling OH⁻ transport.
4. **Separator:** Polypropylene or non-woven nylon — prevents physical contact between electrodes while allowing ionic flow.
5. **Current Collectors:** Nickel-plated steel for both electrodes (corrosion-resistant in alkaline medium).

## 2.2 Working Principle — Discharge Process

During **discharge**, the cell delivers electrical energy. The reactions are:

- **Anode (Oxidation – Metal Hydride releases hydrogen):**

$$MH + OH^- \rightarrow M + H_2O + e^-$$

- **Cathode (Reduction – NiOOH accepts proton/electron):**

$$NiOOH + H_2O + e^- \rightarrow Ni(OH)_2 + OH^-$$

- **Net Cell Reaction (Overall Discharge):**

$$MH + NiOOH \rightarrow M + Ni(OH)_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this happen spontaneously?**
> The metal hydride has a higher chemical potential for hydrogen than the NiOOH electrode. When an external circuit is provided, electrons prefer to flow from the hydride (lower work function) through the circuit to the NiOOH electrode, releasing free energy (~237 kJ per mole of electrons at standard conditions).

## 2.3 Working Principle — Charge Process

During **charging**, an external DC source forces the reverse reaction:

- **Anode (Reduction – Metal absorbs hydrogen):**

$$M + H_2O + e^- \rightarrow MH + OH^-$$

- **Cathode (Oxidation – Ni(OH)₂ converts back to NiOOH):**

$$Ni(OH)_2 + OH^- \rightarrow NiOOH + H_2O + e^-$$

- **Net Charging Reaction:**

$$M + Ni(OH)_2 \rightarrow MH + NiOOH$$

The charging is essentially **forced electrolysis** of water, where hydrogen is stored in the alloy lattice rather than being released as gas.

## 2.4 Why the "Fuel Cell" Terminology?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The NiMH is sometimes called a "fuel cell" in the KTU syllabus because:
> 1. **Hydrogen is the working fuel** — shuttled between two electrodes.
> 2. The architecture mirrors a **hydrogen-air fuel cell** in concept (H₂ → H⁺ + e⁻).
> 3. It is a **closed-loop hydrogen system** (hydrogen is regenerated internally, unlike a true PEMFC where H₂ is externally supplied).
>
> Strictly speaking, it is an **alkaline secondary battery** with fuel-cell-like working principles.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Process | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | OH⁻ migrates from cathode to anode | Completes internal ionic circuit, maintains charge neutrality |
| 2 | MH desorbs H atom → H⁺ + e⁻ | Releases hydrogen to participate in electrochemical reaction |
| 3 | e⁻ travels via external load | Delivers usable electrical work |
| 4 | H⁺ combines with OH⁻ → H₂O at cathode | Forms water as a byproduct |
| 5 | NiOOH reduces to Ni(OH)₂ | Stores the electron, completing reduction half-reaction |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E^° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | **Nernst Equation** (General form) | V |
| 2 | $E^° = 1.35\ \text{V}$ | Standard EMF of NiMH | V |
| 3 | $V_{nominal} = 1.2\ \text{V}$ | Practical nominal voltage | V |
| 4 | $E_{specific} = \frac{V \times Q}{m}$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg |
| 5 | $\Delta G° = -nFE^°$ | Gibbs free energy change | J/mol |
| 6 | $\eta = E_{thermo} - E_{operating}$ | Overpotential / polarization loss | V |
| 7 | $C = \frac{It}{3600}$ | Capacity (Ah) from current and time | Ah |
| 8 | $P = V \times I$ | Instantaneous power output | W |
| 9 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \frac{Q_{dis}}{Q_{ch}}$ | Charge/discharge efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{State of Charge (SOC)} = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}}$ | SOC indicator | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use the vertical bar `\|` symbol for absolute value in markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to avoid breaking table formatting.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The NiMH system dominates several engineering sectors:

1. **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs):** Toyota Prius, Honda Insight (1st-3rd gen).
2. **Consumer Electronics:** Older digital cameras, cordless phones, power tools.
3. **Space Applications:** Hubble Space Telescope, International Space Station (as backup).
4. **Medical Devices:** Portable ventilators, infusion pumps.
5. **Grid Storage:** Distributed energy storage for renewable integration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Cell EMF Using Nernst Equation

The Nernst equation for the NiMH cell is derived from first principles of electrochemical thermodynamics.

### Step 1: Write the Overall Discharge Reaction

$$MH + NiOOH \rightarrow M + Ni(OH)_2$$

This reaction transfers **n = 1 electron** per mole of active material (a single-electron transfer process).

### Step 2: Define the Reaction Quotient Q

For the reaction, the reaction quotient is:

$$Q = \frac{a_M \cdot a_{Ni(OH)_2}}{a_{MH} \cdot a_{NiOOH}}$$

For pure solids, activities of solids = 1, so the expression simplifies to:

$$Q = 1$$

But considering partial states of charge, we use:

$$Q = \frac{[\text{Ni(OH)}_2]}{[\text{NiOOH}][H_2]}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Nernst Equation

$$E = E^° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$$

### Step 4: Substitute Standard Values

For NiMH at standard conditions (T = 298.15 K, n = 1, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol):

$$E^° = 1.35\ \text{V}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Thermal Voltage Term

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.02569\ \text{V} \approx 25.69\ \text{mV}$$

### Step 6: Simplified Nernst Expression for NiMH

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = 1.35 - 0.02569 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{[Ni(OH)_2]}{[NiOOH]}\right)\ \text{V}}$$

This form is useful for estimating **state-of-charge** by measuring the open-circuit voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gravimetric Energy Density of NiMH

### Step 1: Define the Theoretical Energy Per Mole

The Gibbs free energy of the cell reaction determines the maximum electrical work:

$$\Delta G° = -nFE^°$$

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values (n = 1, F = 96,485 C/mol, E° = 1.35 V)

$$\Delta G° = -(1)(96485)(1.35) = -130,254.75\ \text{J/mol}$$

$$\Delta G° = -130.25\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Watt-hours (1 Wh = 3600 J)

$$W_{theoretical} = \frac{130,254.75}{3600} = 36.18\ \text{Wh/mol}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Molar Mass of Active Materials

For LaNi₅H₆ (a typical AB₅ hydride):
- La: 138.91 g/mol
- Ni: 58.69 × 5 = 293.45 g/mol
- H: 1.008 × 6 = 6.05 g/mol
- **Total ≈ 438.41 g/mol**

For NiOOH:
- Ni: 58.69
- O: 16.00 × 2 = 32.00
- H: 1.008
- **Total ≈ 91.70 g/mol**

For Ni(OH)₂:
- Ni: 58.69
- O: 16.00 × 2 = 32.00
- H: 1.008 × 2 = 2.016
- **Total ≈ 92.71 g/mol**

Total active mass per mole of reaction:

$$m_{active} = 438.41 + 91.70 = 530.11\ \text{g/mol}$$

### Step 5: Compute Theoretical Specific Energy

$$E_{sp,theoretical} = \frac{36.18\ \text{Wh/mol}}{0.530\ \text{kg/mol}} = 68.26\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

### Step 6: Account for Practical Overpotential and Inactive Mass

Real-world efficiency ~70% of theoretical, and inactive components (current collectors, separator, casing) add mass:

$$E_{sp,practical} = 0.70 \times 68.26 = 47.78\ \text{Wh/kg (conservative)}$$

Modern commercial NiMH cells reach **60 – 120 Wh/kg** with optimized alloys (La-Ce-Nd based) and thinner separators.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — State of Charge (SOC) from Open-Circuit Voltage

The NiMH cell has a relatively flat discharge curve, but the OCV-SOC relationship is:

### Step 1: Reformulate the Nernst Equation

Using the linearized approximation for small changes around the nominal point:

$$V_{OCV}(SOC) = V_{nom} + k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1 - SOC}{SOC}\right)$$

Where k is an empirical slope coefficient (~0.05 V for NiMH).

### Step 2: Example Calculation

For SOC = 0.5 (50% charged):

$$V_{OCV} = 1.20 + 0.05 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{0.5}{0.5}\right) = 1.20 + 0 = 1.20\ \text{V}$$

For SOC = 0.2 (20% charged, near end of discharge):

$$V_{OCV} = 1.20 + 0.05 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{0.8}{0.2}\right) = 1.20 + 0.05 \cdot \ln(4) = 1.20 + 0.069 = 1.269\ \text{V}$$

This is the basis for **battery management system (BMS)** algorithms in HEVs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NiMH vs Other Systems

| Property | NiMH | Li-ion | Lead-Acid | NiCd |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage (V) | **1.20** | 3.60 | 2.00 | 1.20 |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | **60 – 120** | 100 – 265 | 30 – 50 | 40 – 60 |
| Cycle Life | **500 – 2000** | 500 – 3000 | 200 – 1000 | 1000 – 2000 |
| Memory Effect | Mild | None | None | Severe |
| Toxicity | Low | Medium | High (Pb) | High (Cd) |
| Self-Discharge/Month | **15 – 30%** | 2 – 5% | 4 – 20% | 10 – 20% |
| Cost (relative) | Medium | High | Low | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> The NiMH sits as a **bridge technology** between the toxic NiCd and the high-energy Li-ion — non-toxic, robust, and proven, but with higher self-discharge and lower specific energy than Li-ion.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of NiMH Cell During Discharge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    anodeS["ANODE: Metal Hydride Alloy MH"] -->|"Oxidation: MH + OH- = M + H2O + e-"| anodeI["Releases H+ and e-"]
    anodeI -->|"e- flows through external load"| externalLoad["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT: Lamp / Motor / Electronics"]
    externalLoad -->|"e- arrives at cathode"| cathodeR["CATHODE: NiOOH accepts e-"]
    cathodeR -->|"Reduction: NiOOH + H2O + e- = Ni(OH)2 + OH-"| cathodeP["NiOOH reduced to Ni(OH)2"]
    electrolyteK["ELECTROLYTE: 30 percent KOH aqueous"] -->|"OH- ion migration from cathode to anode"| anodeS
    cathodeR -->|"OH- produced in cathode reaction"| electrolyteK
    sep["SEPARATOR: Polypropylene non-woven fabric"] -.->|"Prevents short circuit, allows OH- flow"| electrolyteK
    caseA["STEEL NICKEL PLATED CASING"] -.->|"Hermetic seal, pressure relief vent"| anodeS
    caseB["SAFETY VENT"] -.->|"Releases H2 gas if overpressure occurs"| anodeI
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of NiMH Pack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cell["Single NiMH Cell"]
        A1["MH Negative Electrode"] --> B1["KOH Electrolyte"]
        B1 --> C1["NiOOH Positive Electrode"]
        A1 --> D1["Nickel Current Collector Anode"]
        C1 --> E1["Nickel Current Collector Cathode"]
    end
    Cell --> F1["Series Connection: 10 cells for 12V Pack"]
    F1 --> G1["Battery Management System BMS"]
    G1 --> H1["Thermal Management Module"]
    G1 --> I1["State of Charge SOC Estimator"]
    G1 --> J1["Protection Circuit: Overcharge, Overdischarge, Short Circuit"]
    J1 --> K1["Output Terminals: Positive and Negative"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Charge/Discharge Cycle States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Charged
    Charged --> Discharging: External Load Connected
    Discharging --> Discharged: MH and NiOOH converted to M and NiOH2
    Discharged --> Charging: External DC Source Applied
    Charging --> Charged: M and NiOH2 converted back to MH and NiOOH
    Charged --> [*]
    note right of Discharging: Voltage drops from 1.35V to 1.20V
    note left of Charging: Voltage rises to 1.45V at end of charge
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of Physical Cell

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Layout (Cross-Section View)**
>
> 1. **Outer Casing:** Nickel-plated steel can, hermetically sealed with a rubber vent (ruptures at ~3 MPa to release H₂ in abuse conditions).
> 2. **Positive Electrode:** Sintered or pasted Ni(OH)₂/NiOOH on nickel foam substrate, coated with cobalt additive for conductivity.
> 3. **Separator:** Polypropylene non-woven fabric soaked in KOH — physical insulator, ionic conductor.
> 4. **Negative Electrode:** Hydrogen-absorbing alloy (LaNi₅-type) coated on nickel-plated steel substrate.
> 5. **Electrolyte:** 30% KOH + 1% LiOH (LiOH improves cycle life).
> 6. **Gasket & Vent:** EPDM rubber gasket with resealable vent.
> 7. **Current Collectors:** Nickel-plated steel tabs welded to each electrode.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q: Define the Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) cell and identify its three primary components. Write the overall discharge reaction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) cell** is an alkaline secondary (rechargeable) electrochemical cell in which hydrogen is the working species, stored reversibly in a metal alloy at the negative electrode.

**Three primary components:**
1. **Negative electrode:** Hydrogen-absorbing alloy such as LaNi₅ (AB₅ type) that forms metal hydride MH during charge.
2. **Positive electrode:** Nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) that converts to Ni(OH)₂ during discharge.
3. **Electrolyte:** 30 wt% aqueous KOH solution enabling OH⁻ ion transport.

**Overall discharge reaction:**

$$MH + NiOOH \rightarrow M + Ni(OH)_2$$

The nominal cell voltage is **1.2 V** and the theoretical OCV is **1.35 V**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Why is the NiMH system considered superior to the NiCd system for consumer applications? Mention two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Environmental non-toxicity:** NiMH uses a hydrogen-absorbing alloy in place of toxic **cadmium (Cd)**, making disposal and recycling safer and compliant with RoHS directives. Cadmium is a heavy metal carcinogen.

2. **Higher specific energy:** NiMH delivers **60–120 Wh/kg** compared to NiCd's **40–60 Wh/kg**, providing ~2× the runtime for the same mass — critical for portable electronics and EVs.

3. **(Bonus) No "memory effect" in modern low-self-discharge NiMH:** Unlike NiCd, contemporary NiMH cells do not suffer significant capacity loss if repeatedly partially discharged, simplifying user behavior.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Common mistakes students make in this topic:
> 1. **Confusing charging vs discharging reactions** — students often write the charge reaction as if it were discharge, losing 4 marks instantly.
> 2. **Wrong stoichiometry of H₂O** — the H₂O molecule must appear on the correct side of each half-reaction.
> 3. **Forgetting the n = 1 electron transfer** — the NiMH is a single-electron cell, not two-electron like lead-acid.
> 4. **Misstating the electrolyte** — it is KOH, not H₂SO₄ (that would be lead-acid).
> 5. **Ignoring the role of the metal alloy** — many students think the anode is just "hydrogen gas," missing the alloy's role in reversible storage.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

> **Q (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the construction and working principle of a Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) cell with neat sketches of charge and discharge reactions. Derive the overall cell reaction.

> **Q (b) [7 marks]:** A NiMH cell has a theoretical specific energy of 68 Wh/kg. If the practical specific energy is 50% of theoretical due to overpotential and inactive mass, calculate the energy stored in a 1.5 Ah, 1.2 V cell of mass 35 g. Also, calculate the runtime if it discharges at 0.5 A constant current.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Construction (2 marks):**

The NiMH cell consists of:
- **Positive electrode:** Nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) active material coated on a nickel foam or sintered nickel substrate, with cobalt hydroxide additive for better conductivity.
- **Negative electrode:** Hydrogen-absorbing alloy (e.g., LaNi₅, MmNi₅) in AB₅-type structure, coated on a nickel-plated steel substrate.
- **Electrolyte:** 30 wt% aqueous KOH + 1% LiOH (LiOH improves cycle life).
- **Separator:** Non-woven polypropylene fabric soaked in electrolyte.
- **Current collectors:** Nickel-plated steel for both electrodes.

**Working Principle — Discharge (2.5 marks):**

During discharge, the cell delivers electrical energy:
- **Anode (oxidation):** $MH + OH^- \rightarrow M + H_2O + e^-$
- **Cathode (reduction):** $NiOOH + H_2O + e^- \rightarrow Ni(OH)_2 + OH^-$
- **Overall:** $MH + NiOOH \rightarrow M + Ni(OH)_2$

**Working Principle — Charge (2.5 marks):**

During charging, an external DC source reverses the reaction:
- **Anode (reduction):** $M + H_2O + e^- \rightarrow MH + OH^-$
- **Cathode (oxidation):** $Ni(OH)_2 + OH^- \rightarrow NiOOH + H_2O + e^-$
- **Overall:** $M + Ni(OH)_2 \rightarrow MH + NiOOH$

Hydrogen is stored in the metal alloy lattice during charge, and released during discharge — the cell behaves as a "hydrogen shuttle."

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1: Calculate the practical specific energy (1 mark):**

$$E_{sp,practical} = 0.50 \times 68\ \text{Wh/kg} = 34\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the energy stored in the cell (2 marks):**

$$E_{stored} = E_{sp,practical} \times m_{cell}$$

$$E_{stored} = 34\ \text{Wh/kg} \times 0.035\ \text{kg} = 1.19\ \text{Wh}$$

**Step 3: Verify using capacity × voltage (2 marks):**

$$E_{stored} = V \times Q = 1.2\ \text{V} \times 1.5\ \text{Ah} = 1.8\ \text{Wh}$$

(Note: The 1.8 Wh theoretical and 1.19 Wh practical differ because the practical cell is 35g, suggesting lower actual specific energy ~51 Wh/kg, very close to typical NiMH values.)

**Step 4: Calculate the runtime at 0.5 A discharge (2 marks):**

$$t = \frac{Q}{I} = \frac{1.5\ \text{Ah}}{0.5\ \text{A}} = 3.0\ \text{hours}$$

$$\boxed{t = 3\ \text{hours (or 180 minutes)}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]`

> **Q (a) [7 marks]:** Compare the NiMH cell with the Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) cell in terms of: (i) electrode materials, (ii) specific energy, (iii) memory effect, (iv) environmental impact, and (v) cycle life.

> **Q (b) [7 marks]:** A NiMH battery pack for a hybrid electric vehicle consists of 168 cells in series, each with a capacity of 6.5 Ah and nominal voltage of 1.2 V. Calculate: (i) the total pack voltage, (ii) the total pack energy in kWh, (iii) the pack energy if specific energy is 70 Wh/kg and total pack mass is 35 kg.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

| Parameter | NiMH | NiCd | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Electrode materials | MH alloy (LaNi₅) + NiOOH | Cd + NiOOH | 1.5 |
| (ii) Specific energy | 60–120 Wh/kg (higher) | 40–60 Wh/kg (lower) | 1.5 |
| (iii) Memory effect | Minimal in modern LSD-NiMH | Severe — must fully discharge before recharge | 1.5 |
| (iv) Environmental impact | Non-toxic, RoHS compliant | Toxic Cd — carcinogen, restricted | 1.5 |
| (v) Cycle life | 500–2000 cycles | 1000–2000 cycles (NiCd slightly better) | 1.0 |

**Conclusion:** NiMH is the **environmentally preferred choice** with higher specific energy, at a small cost in cycle life.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1: Total pack voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{pack} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell} = 168 \times 1.2 = 201.6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Total pack energy in kWh (2.5 marks):**

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = 201.6 \times 6.5 = 1310.4\ \text{Wh}$$

$$E_{pack} = 1.310\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3: Pack energy from specific energy (3 marks):**

$$E_{pack,mass} = E_{sp} \times m_{pack} = 70\ \text{Wh/kg} \times 35\ \text{kg} = 2450\ \text{Wh}$$

$$E_{pack,mass} = 2.45\ \text{kWh}$$
