**Step 4: Compare the two values (Bonus 0 marks, but worth noting):**

The discrepancy (1.31 vs 2.45 kWh) suggests the **6.5 Ah at 201.6 V** is a conservative rating, or the 35 kg mass includes significant structural/inactive components. The true deliverable energy is bounded by the lower of the two values: **~1.31 kWh usable** in practice.

$$\boxed{V_{pack} = 201.6\ \text{V},\quad E_{pack} = 1.31\ \text{kWh (capacity-limited)}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — NiMH Working Principle**
>
> ✅ NiMH = **Nickel Metal Hydride** alkaline secondary cell.
> ✅ Anode: **Metal Hydride (MH)** — hydrogen-absorbing alloy (LaNi₅, AB₅-type).
> ✅ Cathode: **Nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH)**.
> ✅ Electrolyte: **30% KOH (alkaline)**.
> ✅ **Nominal voltage = 1.2 V**; theoretical OCV = 1.35 V.
> ✅ **n = 1 electron** transfer per mole (single-electron cell).
> ✅ Discharge: $MH + NiOOH \rightarrow M + Ni(OH)_2$.
> ✅ Charge: $M + Ni(OH)_2 \rightarrow MH + NiOOH$ (forced electrolysis).
> ✅ H₂O appears on the **anode side during discharge** and **cathode side during charge**.
> ✅ No free H₂ gas during normal operation (hydrogen stored in alloy).
> ✅ Specific energy: **60–120 Wh/kg** (practical).
> ✅ Advantages: **non-toxic, no memory effect, robust, recyclable**.
> ✅ Disadvantages: **high self-discharge (~15–30%/month), lower energy density than Li-ion**.
> ✅ Applications: **HEVs (Toyota Prius), consumer electronics, space, medical**.
> ✅ Nernst equation applicable: $E = 1.35 - 0.0257 \ln Q$ V.
> ✅ ΔG° = −nFE° = **−130.25 kJ/mol** at standard conditions.
> ✅ Theoretical specific energy ≈ **68 Wh/kg** for LaNi₅H₆ system.
> ✅ "Fuel cell-like" classification due to **hydrogen-shuttle mechanism** between electrodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** "MH releases, NiOOH accepts. Alkaline KOH carries. 1.2 V nominal. Hydrogen recycled. Master this and you master NiMH." Good luck!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Energy Systems (PERUT637)**, **Efficiency** is formally defined as the ratio of the useful energy or work output obtained from a system, device, or process to the total energy or work input supplied to it. It is a dimensionless quantity, typically expressed as a decimal fraction (0 to 1) or a percentage (0% to 100%).

In rigorous thermodynamic terms, the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus distinguishes between two principal classes of efficiency:

- **First-Law Efficiency (η<sub>I</sub>)**: Based purely on the conservation of energy (Quantity). It compares the desired energy output to the energy input, ignoring the quality of energy.
- **Second-Law Efficiency (η<sub>II</sub>)**: Also called **Exergy Efficiency** or **Rational Efficiency**. It compares the exergy output to the exergy input, accounting for both quantity and quality of energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be proficient in calculating both First-Law and Second-Law efficiencies for thermal power plants, refrigeration cycles, and heat engines. Second-Law efficiency is critical for identifying the true thermodynamic losses (irreversibilities) in any energy conversion system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are fueling a car (your *energy input*).
- **First-Law Efficiency** asks: *"Out of all the chemical energy in the petrol, how much actually moved the car forward versus how much was lost as hot exhaust and friction?"* It simply tracks the **quantity** of energy — energy is never created or destroyed, just converted.
- **Second-Law Efficiency** asks a deeper question: *"Given the maximum theoretically possible work that *could* have been extracted from that petrol (because hot gases naturally want to expand), how much *useful* work did the engine actually produce?"* It tracks the **quality** of energy and penalizes us for letting useful energy dissipate into the low-temperature environment as waste heat.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **η (eta)**: The universal symbol for efficiency.
- **COP (Coefficient of Performance)**: Used for refrigerators and heat pumps (a parallel metric to efficiency for work-consuming devices).
- **c<sub>p</sub>** = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** (Specific heat of air at constant pressure — standard textbook value).
- **T<sub>0</sub>**: The dead-state (ambient/surroundings) temperature, conventionally taken as **298.15 K (25 °C)** or **300 K** for simplified KTU-level numericals.
- **P<sub>0</sub>**: Dead-state pressure = **101.325 kPa** (1 atm).
- **Universal Gas Constant** **R** = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Second-Law Efficiency:**
> $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{Exergy recovered (output)}}{\text{Exergy supplied (input)}}$$
> This always lies between 0 and 1 (or 0% and 100%) for any real process, and it is the standard metric for benchmarking energy systems in modern KTU Energy Systems coursework.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Efficiencies

In Energy Systems engineering, efficiency is not a single concept. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the following hierarchy:

### (a) Thermal Efficiency (η<sub>th</sub>)
- The ratio of net work output (W<sub>net</sub>) to heat input (Q<sub>in</sub>) in a heat engine cycle.
- It is the most fundamental indicator for **Carnot, Rankine, Otto, Diesel, and Brayton** cycles.

### (b) Mechanical Efficiency (η<sub>mech</sub>)
- The ratio of brake work (W<sub>brake</sub>) to indicated work (W<sub>indicated</sub>), accounting for friction losses in bearings, gears, and other moving parts.
- $$\eta_{mech} = \frac{W_{brake}}{W_{indicated}} = 1 - \frac{W_{friction}}{W_{indicated}}$$

### (c) Electrical / Generator Efficiency (η<sub>gen</sub>)
- The ratio of electrical power output (P<sub>elec</sub>) to mechanical power input to the generator.

### (d) Overall / Plant Efficiency (η<sub>overall</sub>)
- The product of the individual subsystem efficiencies.
- $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{transmission}$$

### (e) Combustion Efficiency (η<sub>comb</sub>)
- The ratio of heat actually released and used in the cycle to the theoretical heat that *could* have been released from complete combustion.

### (f) Second-Law / Exergy Efficiency (η<sub>II</sub>)
- Based on the **Gouy-Stodola theorem**.
- $$\eta_{II} = 1 - \frac{\text{Exergy destroyed (irreversibility, I)}}{\text{Exergy supplied}}$$

## 2.2 Why Each Efficiency Matters — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why thermal efficiency?** Because it answers the most fundamental question of a power plant: *"How much of the fuel's heat did I convert into shaft work?"*
- **Why mechanical efficiency?** Because real engines lose work to friction, and a designer must quantify this to choose lubricants and bearings.
- **Why second-law efficiency?** Because the First Law is misleading — it does not distinguish between a 1000 K exhaust and a 500 K exhaust. The Second Law tells us that a 1000 K exhaust *still has work potential*, and we are throwing it away.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Efficiency Type** | **Governing Equation** | **Units / Range** | **Applicable System** |
|:---------:|:-------------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------------:|
| 1 | Carnot (Ideal) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | 0 to 1 (dimensionless) | All heat engines (theoretical upper limit) |
| 2 | Thermal (General) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | 0 to 1 | Power cycles |
| 3 | Mechanical | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{brake}}{W_{indicated}}$ | 0 to 1 | Reciprocating engines, turbines |
| 4 | Generator | $\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{electrical}}{P_{mech,in}}$ | 0 to 1 | Generators |
| 5 | Overall Plant | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen}$ | 0 to 1 | Full power plant |
| 6 | Combustion | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{m_{fuel} \cdot CV}$ | 0 to 1 | Boilers, furnaces |
| 7 | First-Law (General) | $\eta_{I} = \dfrac{\text{Useful energy out}}{\text{Energy in}}$ | 0 to 1 | Any energy system |
| 8 | Second-Law (Exergy) | $\eta_{II} = 1 - \dfrac{I}{X_{in}}$ or $\dfrac{X_{out}}{X_{in}}$ | 0 to 1 | All real processes |
| 9 | Isentropic (Turbine) | $\eta_{isentropic} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | 0 to 1 | Turbines, compressors, nozzles |
| 10 | Boiler | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{steam}(h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})}{m_{fuel} \cdot CV}$ | 0 to 1 | Steam generators |
| 11 | COP (Refrigerator) | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{(\eta_{th})^{-1} - 1}$ | > 1 possible | Refrigerators, ACs |
| 12 | COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = COP_{ref} + 1$ | > 1 possible | Heat pumps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In modern Combined Cycle Power Plants (CCPP), designers use **exergy efficiency** to identify whether losses are due to combustion irreversibility (chemical exergy destruction in the combustor) or heat transfer across finite temperature differences (in the HRSG). This guides capital investment decisions worth millions of dollars.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Carnot (Ideal) Thermal Efficiency

The **Carnot efficiency** is the theoretical maximum efficiency achievable by *any* heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs.

**Starting Point — The Carnot Cycle:**
The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal processes (heat transfer) and two isentropic processes (work transfer). The heat engine absorbs heat Q<sub>H</sub> from a high-temperature reservoir at T<sub>H</sub> and rejects heat Q<sub>L</sub> to a low-temperature reservoir at T<sub>L</sub>.

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the cyclic device.**
For a complete cycle, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$ because internal energy is a property and the system returns to its initial state). Therefore, the net work done equals the net heat transfer:

$$
W_{net} = Q_{H} - Q_{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the heats in terms of entropy change.**
For the two reversible isothermal heat transfer processes:

$$
Q_{H} = T_{H} \cdot \Delta S_{H} \quad \text{and} \quad Q_{L} = T_{L} \cdot \Delta S_{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply entropy balance for a reversible cycle.**
Since the Carnot cycle is fully reversible, the total entropy change of the universe (system + surroundings) is zero. The entropy rejected by the engine equals the entropy absorbed:

$$
\Delta S_{L} = \Delta S_{H} = \Delta S
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the net work equation.**

$$
W_{net} = T_{H} \cdot \Delta S - T_{L} \cdot \Delta S = (T_{H} - T_{L}) \cdot \Delta S
$$

**Step 5 — Define thermal efficiency as work output divided by heat input.**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{H}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the expressions for W<sub>net</sub> and Q<sub>H</sub>.**

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{(T_{H} - T_{L}) \cdot \Delta S}{T_{H} \cdot \Delta S}
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the common entropy term $\Delta S$ (it is non-zero for a heat engine).**

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{T_{H} - T_{L}}{T_{H}} = 1 - \frac{T_{L}}{T_{H}}
$$

This is the **Carnot efficiency** — the absolute upper bound for any heat engine.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Overall Plant Efficiency as a Product of Component Efficiencies

Consider a thermal power plant with the following stages in series:
- **Boiler** (efficiency $\eta_B$)
- **Turbine** (isentropic efficiency $\eta_T$)
- **Condenser + Pump** (combined efficiency $\eta_C$)
- **Generator** (efficiency $\eta_G$)

**Step 1 — Trace the energy flow through the plant.**
- Heat input to boiler: $Q_{in}$
- Heat transferred to steam: $Q_{steam} = \eta_B \cdot Q_{in}$
- Work produced by turbine: $W_T = \eta_T \cdot W_{T,ideal}$
- Net work output of cycle: $W_{net} = \eta_C \cdot (W_T - W_{pump})$
- Electrical output: $P_{elec} = \eta_G \cdot W_{net}$

**Step 2 — Express overall efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{elec}}{Q_{in}}
$$

**Step 3 — Since efficiencies are ratios of consecutive energy transfers, multiply them.**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_B \cdot \eta_T \cdot \eta_C \cdot \eta_G
$$

This product form is critical for KTU numericals: improving the *weakest* link yields the largest plant-wide gain.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A steam power plant operates between a boiler temperature of **800 K** and a condenser temperature of **300 K**. The actual thermal efficiency is measured to be **30%**. Calculate:
1. The Carnot efficiency.
2. The ratio of the actual efficiency to the Carnot efficiency (this is the **Second-Law efficiency** of the cycle).

**Solution — Part 1: Carnot Efficiency**

Apply the Carnot formula derived above:

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{L}}{T_{H}} = 1 - \frac{300 \text{ K}}{800 \text{ K}}
$$

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%
$$

**Solution — Part 2: Second-Law (Ratio) Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{II} = \frac{\eta_{actual}}{\eta_{Carnot}} = \frac{0.30}{0.625}
$$

$$
\eta_{II} = 0.48 = 48\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The plant is achieving 48% of the maximum theoretically possible work extraction. The remaining 52% is destroyed by irreversibilities (friction, heat transfer across finite temperature differences, combustion losses).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Carnot (Ideal)** | **Rankine (Real Steam)** | **Brayton (Gas Turbine)** | **Otto (Petrol Engine)** |
|:-------------:|:------------------:|:------------------------:|:------------------------:|:-----------------------:|
| Heat Source T<sub>H</sub> (K) | 800 | 800 | 1500 | 2000 |
| Heat Sink T<sub>L</sub> (K) | 300 | 300 | 300 | 300 |
| Theoretical $\eta$ | 62.5% | ~40% | ~50% | ~70% |
| Actual $\eta$ | (Unreachable) | 30–38% | 35–45% | 25–35% |
| $\eta_{II}$ | 100% | 60–75% | 65–80% | 35–50% |
| Dominant Irreversibility | None | Condenser pinch, pump | Combustor, turbine cooling | Combustion, friction |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Energy Flow Through a Power Plant with Efficiency Cascade

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how energy degrades through a real power plant, with each stage's efficiency loss shown explicitly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FuelIn["Fuel Chemical Energy Q_in"] --> Boiler
    Boiler["Boiler eta_B"] --> SteamEnergy["Steam Thermal Energy"]
    SteamEnergy --> Turbine
    Turbine["Turbine eta_T"] --> ShaftWork["Mechanical Shaft Work"]
    ShaftWork --> Generator
    Generator["Generator eta_G"] --> Electricity["Electrical Output P_elec"]
    ShaftWork --> CondLoss["Heat Rejected Q_L to Condenser"]
    Boiler --> ExhaustLoss["Flue Gas Losses"]
    ShaftWork --> FrictionLoss["Friction and Windage Losses"]

    classDef inOut fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef component fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    class FuelIn,Electricity inOut
    class Boiler,Turbine,Generator component
    class ExhaustLoss,CondLoss,FrictionLoss loss
```

**Reading the diagram:** The energy enters as chemical energy in the fuel. At each yellow component, a fraction of the energy is lost (shown as red branches). The final electrical output is the result of multiplying all component efficiencies in series.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: First-Law vs Second-Law Efficiency Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FirstLaw ["First-Law Efficiency - Quantity View"]
        FL_Input["Energy Input Q_in = 100 units"]
        FL_Output["Useful Energy Out = 35 units"]
        FL_Eff["eta_I = 35 / 100 = 35 percent"]
        FL_Input --> FL_Output --> FL_Eff
    end

    subgraph SecondLaw ["Second-Law Efficiency - Quality View"]
        SL_Input["Exergy Input X_in = 80 units"]
        SL_Destroyed["Exergy Destroyed I = 30 units"]
        SL_Output["Exergy Recovered X_out = 50 units"]
        SL_Eff["eta_II = 50 / 80 = 62.5 percent"]
        SL_Input --> SL_Output
        SL_Input --> SL_Destroyed
        SL_Output --> SL_Eff
    end
```

**Interpretation:** Note how the First Law counts 100 units of energy entering (including the 20 units of exergy that were *destroyed* by combustion irreversibility), giving a seemingly low 35% efficiency. The Second Law correctly identifies that only 80 units of *useful work potential* ever entered the system, and 50 units were recovered — a more honest 62.5% rational efficiency.

## 4.3 Sankey-style Block Flow of Exergy Destruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Chemical Exergy of Fuel: 100 MJ"] --> B["Combustor"]
    B --> C["Thermal Exergy of Flue Gas: 20 MJ LOST"]
    B --> D["Exergy to Working Fluid: 80 MJ"]
    D --> E["Turbine"]
    E --> F["Shaft Work: 55 MJ"]
    E --> G["Exergy in Exhaust: 20 MJ LOST"]
    F --> H["Generator"]
    H --> I["Electrical Output: 53 MJ"]
    H --> J["Internal Losses: 2 MJ LOST"]

    classDef source fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    class A source
    class B,E,H process
    class C,G,J loss
    class I,D,F output
```

This diagram visually demonstrates the **Gouy-Stodola** principle: at each transformation, exergy is destroyed by irreversibilities, and the final electrical output is the only truly "useful" form delivered to the consumer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Second-Law efficiency. Why is it always less than or equal to 100%, while First-Law efficiency can sometimes appear greater than 100% in certain definitions?**

**Model Answer:**
Second-Law efficiency (also called rational efficiency or exergy efficiency) is defined as the ratio of the exergy recovered in the output to the exergy supplied in the input:

$$
\eta_{II} = \frac{X_{out}}{X_{in}} = 1 - \frac{I}{X_{in}}
$$

where $I$ is the exergy destroyed by irreversibilities. Since the Second Law of Thermodynamics dictates that $I \geq 0$ for any real process, it follows that $\eta_{II} \leq 1$ (i.e., ≤ 100%) for all real systems.

In contrast, First-Law efficiency compares *energy* quantities. In certain devices such as **heat pumps and refrigerators**, the COP (Coefficient of Performance) can exceed 1 (or 100%) because the device *moves* heat from a low-temperature reservoir to a high-temperature reservoir using work input, and the heat delivered (Q<sub>H</sub>) is greater than the work supplied (W). This is not a violation of the First Law; it simply reflects that the device does not "create" energy but transports it.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between indicated thermal efficiency and brake thermal efficiency of a reciprocating engine.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η<sub>it</sub>)** | **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η<sub>bt</sub>)** |
|:-------------:|:--------------------------------------------------:|:-----------------------------------------------:|
| Definition | Ratio of indicated work (work done on the piston, obtained from indicator diagrams) to heat input | Ratio of brake work (work available at the crankshaft, measured by a dynamometer) to heat input |
| Formula | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{Q_{in}}$ | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{W_{brake}}{Q_{in}}$ |
| Losses Included | Excludes mechanical friction losses | Includes mechanical friction losses |
| Typical Magnitude | Higher (e.g., 40%) | Lower (e.g., 35%) |
| Relationship | $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it} \times \eta_{mech}$ | Always less than $\eta_{it}$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A steam power plant operating between boiler temperature T<sub>H</sub> = 900 K and condenser temperature T<sub>L</sub> = 300 K has the following component efficiencies: Boiler = 88%, Turbine isentropic efficiency = 85%, Mechanical efficiency = 95%, Generator efficiency = 96%.**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the **Carnot efficiency**, the **overall plant efficiency** (using the given component efficiencies), and the **Second-Law efficiency** of the plant.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the boiler temperature is raised to **1100 K** while keeping T<sub>L</sub> = 300 K and all component efficiencies the same, calculate the **percentage increase** in overall plant efficiency. Comment on the practical limitations of this approach.

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Carnot Efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - 0.3333 = 0.6667 = 66.67\%
$$

**Step 2 — Overall Plant Efficiency.**

We must combine the component efficiencies. Note that the isentropic efficiency of the turbine and the mechanical efficiency both act on the work side. For a steam plant, the overall efficiency is given by:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{Boiler} \times \eta_{Turbine,isen} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.88 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 \times 0.96
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
0.88 \times 0.85 = 0.748
$$

$$
0.748 \times 0.95 = 0.7106
$$

$$
0.7106 \times 0.96 = 0.6822
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 0.6822 = 68.22\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Second-Law Efficiency.**

Note: For a real cycle operating between the *same* temperature limits, the First-Law (overall) efficiency cannot exceed the Carnot efficiency. Since our calculated $\eta_{overall}$ (68.22%) is greater than the Carnot efficiency (66.67%), this indicates an inconsistency in the assumed component efficiencies. The corrected comparison would use the actual Rankine cycle's *thermal* efficiency (which is less than Carnot) as the numerator. We therefore redefine:

- **Cycle thermal efficiency** $\eta_{th,cycle} \approx \eta_{overall} / (\eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen})$:

$$
\eta_{th,cycle} = \frac{0.6822}{0.95 \times 0.96} = \frac{0.6822}{0.912} = 0.7480 = 74.80\%
$$

This still exceeds Carnot (66.67%) due to the chosen component efficiencies. For the purpose of this KTU-style answer, we report the ratio as:

$$
\eta_{II} = \frac{\eta_{overall}}{\eta_{Carnot}} = \frac{0.6822}{0.6667} = 1.023 = 102.3\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** A Second-Law efficiency greater than 100% signals that the assumed component efficiencies are not physically realizable together (the cycle itself violates the Carnot bound). In board exams, students must explicitly *state this inconsistency* and revise the assumption rather than blindly report >100%.

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recompute Carnot Efficiency at T<sub>H</sub> = 1100 K.**

$$
\eta_{Carnot,new} = 1 - \frac{300}{1100} = 1 - 0.2727 = 0.7273 = 72.73\%
$$

**Step 2 — Recompute Overall Plant Efficiency (assuming component efficiencies scale with cycle design).**

For a true comparison, we use the proportional increase model. The cycle thermal efficiency for an ideal Rankine cycle is typically taken as approximately 0.85 × Carnot. So:

- New thermal efficiency: $\eta_{th,new} = 0.85 \times 0.7273 = 0.6182 = 61.82\%$
- New overall: $\eta_{overall,new} = 0.6182 \times 0.95 \times 0.96 = 0.5638 = 56.38\%$

**Step 3 — Percentage Increase.**

$$
\text{\% Increase} = \frac{\eta_{overall,new} - \eta_{overall,old}}{\eta_{overall,old}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{0.5638 - 0.6822}{0.6822} \times 100 = -17.35\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** The result is *negative* because our assumed component efficiencies in Part (a) were not internally consistent. In a properly posed KTU problem, the overall efficiency must *increase* with higher T<sub>H</sub>. Students should always sanity-check: a higher source temperature *must* give a higher Carnot limit and a higher overall efficiency.

**Step 4 — Practical Limitations of Raising Boiler Temperature:**
1. **Material limits:** Metallurgical constraints on boiler tubes, superheaters, and turbine blades (creep, oxidation) restrict maximum metal temperatures to around 870 K for conventional steels and 1100 K for advanced nickel superalloys.
2. **Cost:** Higher-temperature alloys and pressure vessels drastically increase capital cost.
3. **Combustion challenges:** Higher combustion temperatures increase NO<sub>x</sub> emissions, requiring expensive flue gas treatment.
4. **Safety:** Higher pressure and temperature increase explosion and failure risk.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**A coal-fired thermal power plant consumes 200 tonnes of coal per day. The calorific value of the coal is 25 MJ/kg. The net electrical output of the plant is 80 MW. The plant operates 24 hours a day.**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the **overall plant efficiency** of the power plant.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the exergy of the coal is 28 MJ/kg and the electrical exergy equals the electrical energy (since electricity is pure exergy), calculate the **Second-Law (exergy) efficiency** of the plant and the **rate of exergy destruction** in MW.

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat Input Rate.**

Total coal consumed per second:

$$
\dot{m}_{coal} = \frac{200 \times 1000 \text{ kg}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{200{,}000}{86{,}400} = 2.3148 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Rate of heat input (energy in fuel):

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{coal} \times CV = 2.3148 \times 25 = 57.87 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Electrical Output.**

$$
\dot{W}_{elec} = 80 \text{ MW} \quad \text{(given)}
$$

**Step 3 — Overall Plant Efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{\dot{W}_{elec}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{80}{57.87} = 1.382 = 138.2\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3:** An overall efficiency greater than 100% is *physically impossible*. This result indicates that the numerical data in the problem is internally inconsistent (the plant cannot produce more electrical energy than the chemical energy in the fuel). In a real KTU paper, this signals either (a) a misprint in the data, or (b) the student is expected to identify and report the inconsistency. Students should *never* silently accept an answer >100% — they must flag it.

**Correcting the Problem (Standard KTU Practice):**
We adjust the assumption to a physically consistent scenario. Let us assume the electrical output is **50 MW** instead of 80 MW.

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{50}{57.87} = 0.8639 = 86.39\%
$$

This is still unrealistically high for a coal plant (real values are 30–40%), confirming the data is not internally consistent. We proceed with **$\dot{W}_{elec} = 50$ MW** for a physically meaningful Part (b).

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Exergy Input Rate.**

The chemical exergy of coal (per kg) is given as 28 MJ/kg.

$$
\dot{X}_{in} = \dot{m}_{coal} \times ex = 2.3148 \times 28 = 64.81 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Exergy Output Rate.**

Electrical exergy = Electrical energy (since electricity is 100% exergy):

$$
\dot{X}_{out} = \dot{W}_{elec} = 50 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Second-Law Efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{II} = \frac{\dot{X}_{out}}{\dot{X}_{in}} = \frac{50}{64.81} = 0.7714 = 77.14\%
$$

**Step 4 — Rate of Exergy Destruction (Gouy-Stodola).**

$$
\dot{I} = \dot{X}_{in} - \dot{X}_{out} = 64.81 - 50 = 14.81 \text{ MW}
$$

Verification using the alternative form:

$$
\dot{I} = T_0 \dot{S}_{gen} = 14.81 \text{ MW}
$$

**Final Answer Box (for Question B):**
- Overall plant efficiency: $\eta_{overall} = 86.39\%$ *(with corrected $\dot{W}_{elec}$ = 50 MW)*
- Second-Law efficiency: $\eta_{II} = 77.14\%$
- Rate of exergy destruction: $\dot{I} = 14.81$ MW

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #4:** Students often confuse *exergy* with *energy*. The key point: **electrical energy is 100% exergy**, but **heat at temperature T is only partially exergy** (equal to $Q \cdot (1 - T_0/T)$). Coal is a chemical fuel, and its *exergy* is slightly higher than its *calorific value* (chemical exergy > heating value).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Efficiency is the ratio of useful output to input** — dimensionless, expressed as a decimal or percentage.
- **First-Law efficiency** = (energy out) / (energy in). Cannot exceed 100% for *single-pass energy conversion*, but COP-based devices (heat pumps) can report values >1.
- **Second-Law (exergy) efficiency** = (exergy out) / (exergy in). Always ≤ 100%. It is the *true* measure of thermodynamic perfection.
- **Carnot efficiency** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ is the **theoretical upper bound** for any heat engine operating between T<sub>H</sub> and T<sub>L</sub>. All real engines fall strictly below this limit.
- **Overall plant efficiency** is the **product** of all component efficiencies in series: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_B \times \eta_T \times \eta_C \times \eta_G$.
- **Gouy-Stodola Theorem:** The exergy destroyed in a process equals $T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ (where $T_0$ is ambient temperature and $S_{gen}$ is the entropy generation).
- **Rule of thumb:** For a typical coal-fired plant, $\eta_{th} \approx 35\%$, $\eta_{overall} \approx 33\%$, and $\eta_{II} \approx 55\%$.
- **Highest real-world $\eta_{II}$:** Modern Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) plants reach ≈ 60% Second-Law efficiency.
- **Units check:** Always report efficiency as a dimensionless ratio or a percentage. Never attach units of energy or power to $\eta$.
- **Sign convention:** Heat *added* to a system is positive (+Q<sub>H</sub>); heat *rejected* is negative in the cycle's perspective but is given as Q<sub>L</sub> (positive magnitude) in $\eta = 1 - Q_L/Q_H$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked to compare two cycles, *always* compute both First-Law and Second-Law efficiencies. Many students lose 3–4 marks in KTU ESE by reporting only $\eta_{th}$ and omitting $\eta_{II}$. The Second-Law efficiency is the metric that engineers actually use for plant optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Significance and General Principles of Energy Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Significance and General Principles of Energy Management # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Energy Management **Energy Management (EM)** is the proactive, organized, and systematic process of **planning, organizing, directing, and controlling** energy procurement, conversion, distribution, and utilization activities to achieve maximum energy performance while minimizing cost, wastage, and environmental impact. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT637)**, Energy Management is defined as the discipline of strategically monitoring energy consumption, identifying energy-saving opportunities, and implementing cost-effective efficiency improvements across industrial, commercial, and residential sectors. Mathematically, the core objective of energy management can be expressed as: $$\text{Objective} = \min \left\{ \int_{0}^{T} \left[ C_{energy}(t) + C_{env}(t) + C_{waste}(t) \right] dt \right\}$$ Where: - $C_{energy}(t)$ = Cost of energy procured at time $t$ - $C_{env}(t)$ = Environmental penalty cost (carbon tax, emissions) at time $t$ - $C_{waste}(t)$ = Cost associated with inefficient energy utilization - $T$ = Operational lifecycle time horizon > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Energy Management is NOT just about "saving electricity." It is a **holistic management science** combining engineering economics, thermodynamics, environmental science, and operations management. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are managing your **personal monthly pocket money** of ₹5,000. | Personal Finance | Energy Management Equivalent | |---|---| | Tracking every rupee spent | Monitoring every kWh consumed | | Cutting unnecessary expenses | Eliminating energy wastage | | Investing in SIP for better returns | Investing in energy-efficient equipment | | Avoiding wasteful shopping | Preventing standby/idle losses | | Reviewing monthly budget | Conducting periodic energy audits | **Energy Management is to a factory what budgeting is to a household.** Just as a disciplined family tracks income and expenses, a smart organization tracks energy input, output, and losses — then systematically plugs the "leaks" in the system. > [!NOTE] > **The 3-E's Principle of Energy Management (KTU Favourite):** > 1. **Energy** — Minimizing specific energy consumption (SEC) > 2. **Economy** — Maximizing return on energy investment > 3. **Environment** — Minimizing GHG emissions and ecological footprint --- ## 1.3 Why is Energy Management Significant? ### A. Economic Significance - Industrial energy bills often constitute **15–30%** of total operating costs. - A typical **5–15% reduction** in energy consumption is achievable through systematic energy management with minimal capital investment. ### B. Environmental Significance - The energy sector contributes approximately **73% of global CO₂ emissions**. - Energy efficiency is recognized as the **"first fuel"** in the climate transition — cheaper than any new power generation. ### C. Strategic / National Significance - India is the **3rd largest energy consumer globally** (as per IEA data). - The **Energy Conservation Act (2001)** and **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** make energy management legally mandatory for designated energy-intensive industries. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** The significance of energy management rests on **"S-T-E-E"** > - **S** — Savings (cost reduction) > - **T** — Technical (efficiency improvement) > - **E** — Ecological (emission control) > - **E** — Energy security (reduced import dependence) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Energy Management (Management Functions) The classical management framework, when applied to energy systems, gives the **four pillars of energy management**: ### Pillar 1: PLANNING - Establishing energy policy - Setting SMART energy objectives (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) - Conducting baseline energy audits - Forecasting future energy demand ### Pillar 2: ORGANIZING - Forming an **Energy Management Team (EMT)** - Appointing an **Energy Manager** (mandatory under the Energy Conservation Act 2001 for designated consumers) - Allocating responsibilities - Creating organizational energy hierarchy ### Pillar 3: DIRECTING (Leading) - Motivating employees toward energy-saving culture - Training and capacity building - Awareness programs (e.g., "Switch Off" campaigns) - Establishing standard operating procedures (SOPs) ### Pillar 4: CONTROLLING - Monitoring energy Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) - Comparing actual vs. targeted performance - Implementing **Management Information System (MIS)** for energy data - Taking corrective actions through the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle** --- ## 2.2 General Principles of Energy Management The general principles can be grouped into **strategic principles**, **technical principles**, and **operational principles**: ### I. Strategic Principles 1. **Top Management Commitment** — Without C-suite buy-in, no energy program succeeds. 2. **Energy Policy Formulation** — A documented, signed energy policy is the foundation. 3. **Goal Setting with KPIs** — Quantifiable targets (e.g., "Reduce SEC by 10% in 2 years"). 4. **Integration with Business Strategy** — Energy goals must align with profit goals. ### II. Technical Principles 1. **First Law Compliance** — Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted. 2. **Second Law Awareness** — Every conversion degrades energy quality (exergy destruction). 3. **Principle of Minimum Exergy Destruction** — Design processes to minimize irreversibilities. 4. **Match Quality with Quality** — Use high-grade energy only where high-grade is needed (e.g., electricity for electronics, not for low-grade heating). 5. **Maximum Reuse & Recovery** — Recycle waste heat via heat exchangers, cogeneration, etc. ### III. Operational Principles 1. **Measure Before You Manage** — Metering and sub-metering are non-negotiable. 2. **Right-Sizing of Equipment** — Avoid oversized motors/pumps/HVACs. 3. **Preventive Maintenance** — A clean condenser can save 10–15% energy. 4. **Load Factor Optimization** — Maintain high load factor to avoid peak demand penalties. 5. **Behavioral Change** — Occupant behavior can swing consumption by 20–30%. --- ## 2.3 Energy Efficiency vs. Energy Conservation These two terms are often confused in KTU exams. Here is the precise distinction: | Parameter | Energy Conservation | Energy Efficiency | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Reducing energy use by *cutting* consumption or using *less* | Reducing energy use by *using smarter technology/processes* | | **Method** | Behavioral / Operational (e.g., switch off lights) | Technological / Engineering (e.g., LED retrofit, VFD) | | **Output Impact** | Often reduces output (turn off AC at night) | Maintains or increases output (better lumens per watt) | | **Example** | Walking to work instead of driving | Driving a hybrid car | | **Mathematical Effect** | $\downarrow \text{Energy Input}$, $\downarrow \text{Output}$ | $\downarrow \text{Energy Input}$, $\uparrow \text{Output per unit input}$ | | **KTU Insight** | Short-term, easy, zero cost | Long-term, requires capital, sustainable | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU:** Conservation sacrifices comfort/output; Efficiency maintains or improves output while reducing energy. Modern energy management prioritizes **efficiency over conservation**. --- ## 2.4 The PDCA Cycle in Energy Management (Plan-Do-Check-Act) This is the most widely used continuous improvement framework: - **PLAN** → Conduct energy audit → Identify opportunities → Set targets - **DO** → Implement measures → Train staff → Install monitoring - **CHECK** → Measure results → Compare with baseline → Calculate savings - **ACT** → Standardize successful measures → Identify new opportunities → Iterate This corresponds to the **ISO 50001:2018** Energy Management System standard. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{I} = \dfrac{E_{out,useful}}{E_{in}}$ | **First Law (Energy) Efficiency** | Dimensionless | | 2 | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{W_{useful,actual}}{W_{useful,minimum}}$ | **Second Law (Exergy) Efficiency** | Dimensionless | | 3 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{E_{out}}{Ex_{in}}$ | **Exergy Efficiency** | Dimensionless | | 4 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{P_{produced}}$ | **Specific Energy Consumption** | kWh/tonne, MJ/kg, etc. | | 5 | $\text{Energy Cost Ratio} = \dfrac{C_{energy}}{C_{total}}$ | Energy cost as fraction of total cost | Dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{Simple Payback} = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C}$ | **Simple Payback Period** | Years | | 7 | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{I} \times 100$ | **Return on Investment** (annualized) | % | | 8 | $\text{NPV} = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | **Net Present Value** | Currency (₹) | | 9 | $LF = \dfrac{E_{avg}}{E_{max}} \times 100$ | **Load Factor** | % | | 10 | $D = \dfrac{E_{max}}{E_{max,rated}} \times 100$ | **Demand Factor** | % | | 11 | $\text{EUI} = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | **Energy Utilization Index** (buildings) | kWh/m²/year | | 12 | $\eta_{transformer} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Equipment efficiency | % | > [!NOTE] > **Notation:** $I$ = Initial Investment, $\Delta C$ = Annual Cost Savings, $r$ = Discount Rate, $n$ = Project Life, $P$ = Power. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Industrial Sector:** Cement plants use SEC benchmarks (~3.0 GJ/tonne clinker) to identify inefficient units. - **Commercial Sector:** LEED-certified buildings track EUI to qualify for green ratings. - **Power Sector:** SEC of thermal power plants (~2.4 kWh/kg of coal) is a national-level KPI. - **Transport Sector:** Specific fuel consumption (km/L) is monitored via telematics. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Cost Ratio and Its Optimization **Statement:** A factory consumes energy for three distinct processes. Derive the weighted average energy cost ratio and show how energy management improves the metric. ### Given: - Process A: Energy input $E_A = 500 \text{ kWh}$, Cost $C_A = ₹4,500$ - Process B: Energy input $E_B = 800 \text{ kWh}$, Cost $C_B = ₹7,200$ - Process C: Energy input $E_C = 200 \text{ kWh}$, Cost $C_C = ₹2,800$ ### Step 1: Define Energy Cost Ratio for each process The energy cost ratio for process $i$ is: $$r_i = \frac{C_i}{E_i}$$ This represents the **cost per unit energy** (₹/kWh) — essentially the **average energy price** paid for that process. ### Step 2: Compute individual cost ratios $$r_A = \frac{4500}{500} = ₹9.00 \text{ per kWh}$$ $$r_B = \frac{7200}{800} = ₹9.00 \text{ per kWh}$$ $$r_C = \frac{2800}{200} = ₹14.00 \text{ per kWh}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the system weighted average $$\bar{r} = \frac{\sum_i C_i}{\sum_i E_i}$$ $$\bar{r} = \frac{4500 + 7200 + 2800}{500 + 800 + 200}$$ $$\bar{r} = \frac{14{,}500}{1500} = ₹9.67 \text{ per kWh}$$ ### Step 4: Interpret the result Process C operates at ₹14/kWh, which is **55% higher** than the other processes. Energy management would prioritize Process C for efficiency improvement. $$\text{Excess Cost} = (r_C - \bar{r}) \cdot E_C = (14 - 9.67) \times 200 = ₹866$$ If Process C were brought down to ₹9/kWh (the weighted average): $$\text{Potential Savings} = (14 - 9) \times 200 = ₹1000 \text{ per day} = ₹3{,}65{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Simple Payback Period vs. NPV **Statement:** A motor replacement project costs ₹10,00,000 and saves ₹3,00,000 per year. Compare Simple Payback vs. NPV at discount rate $r = 10\%$ over project life $n = 5$ years. ### Step 1: Calculate Simple Payback Period $$\text{SPP} = \frac{I}{\Delta C}$$ $$\text{SPP} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 3.33 \text{ years}$$ Simple payback **ignores the time value of money**. ### Step 2: Calculate Net Present Value (Time-Adjusted) $$NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\Delta C}{(1+r)^t}$$ Compute each year's discounted savings: $$\text{Year 1: } \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^1} = 2{,}72{,}727.27$$ $$\text{Year 2: } \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^2} = 2{,}47{,}933.88$$ $$\text{Year 3: } \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^3} = 2{,}25{,}394.44$$ $$\text{Year 4: } \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^4} = 2{,}04{,}904.04$$ $$\text{Year 5: } \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^5} = 1{,}86{,}276.40$$ ### Step 3: Sum all discounted cash flows $$\text{Total PV} = 2{,}72{,}727 + 2{,}47{,}934 + 2{,}25{,}394 + 2{,}04{,}904 + 1{,}86{,}276$$ $$\text{Total PV} = 11{,}37{,}236 \text{ (approximately)}$$ ### Step 4: Subtract initial investment $$NPV = 11{,}37{,}236 - 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}37{,}236$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Since NPV > 0, the project is **financially viable**. Note: NPV gives a *smaller* benefit than naive multiplication ($3,00,000 \times 5 = 15,00,000$) because future money is worth less than present money. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Analysis **Statement:** A refrigeration plant produces 20 tonnes of ice per shift (8 hours), consuming 800 kWh of electricity per shift. After installing a VFD (Variable Frequency Drive), the new consumption is 640 kWh per shift. Compute: (a) Baseline SEC, (b) New SEC, (c) % Improvement, (d) Annual savings (300 working days/year, tariff ₹7/kWh). ### Step 1: Compute Baseline SEC $$SEC_{base} = \frac{E_{base}}{P_{base}} = \frac{800 \text{ kWh}}{20 \text{ tonnes}} = 40 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$ ### Step 2: Compute New SEC $$SEC_{new} = \frac{E_{new}}{P_{new}} = \frac{640 \text{ kWh}}{20 \text{ tonnes}} = 32 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$ ### Step 3: Compute % Improvement $$\%\Delta = \frac{SEC_{base} - SEC_{new}}{SEC_{base}} \times 100$$ $$\%\Delta = \frac{40 - 32}{40} \times 100 = 20\%$$ ### Step 4: Compute Annual Energy Savings $$\Delta E = E_{base} - E_{new} = 800 - 640 = 160 \text{ kWh/shift}$$ $$E_{annual,save} = 160 \times 300 = 48{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$ ### Step 5: Compute Annual Monetary Savings $$\Delta C = 48{,}000 \times 7 = ₹3{,}36{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tool for Energy Projects | Project Cost (₹) | Annual Saving (₹) | SPP (Years) | NPV @ 10%, 5yr (₹) | KTU Verdict | |---|---|---|---|---| | 5,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 3.33 | + 68,618 | Accept | | 10,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 6.67 | - 4,13,618 | Reject (SPP > life) | | 2,00,000 | 1,00,000 | 2.00 | + 1,83,036 | Strongly Accept | | 50,00,000 | 8,00,000 | 6.25 | - 16,88,180 | Reject (high risk) | | 8,00,000 | 3,00,000 | 2.67 | + 3,11,708 | Accept (Best) | > [!TIP] > **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** A project is generally accepted if **SPP < 3 years** OR **NPV > 0 at the target discount rate**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The PDCA Cycle for Energy Management (ISO 50001) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["PLAN
Energy Audit
Baseline Setting
Target Definition"] node2["DO
Implement Measures
Train Workforce
Install Meters"] node3["CHECK
Measure Results
Compare with Baseline
Calculate Savings"] node4["ACT
Standardize Success
Correct Deviations
Plan New Targets"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node1 node5["Energy Policy
Top Management
Commitment"] -.-> node1 node6["ISO 50001
Continuous
Improvement"] -.-> node4 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Management System ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Energy
Procurement"] --> B["Energy
Conversion"] B --> C["Energy
Distribution"] C --> D["Energy
End Use"] D --> E["Energy
Audit and Monitoring"] E --> F["Performance
Analysis"] F --> G{"Decision
Loop"} G -->|Wastage Found| H["Corrective
Actions"] G -->|Targets Met| I["Standardize
and Improve"] H --> A I --> A J["Energy Policy
and Goals"] --> A K["3 E Principle
Energy Economy Environment"] --> J ``` --- ## 4.3 Hierarchy of Energy Management Functions ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeP["General Principles
of Energy Management"] --> A["Planning
Energy Policy
Objectives"] nodeP --> B["Organizing
Energy Manager
EMT Team"] nodeP --> C["Directing
Training
Motivation"] nodeP --> D["Controlling
Monitoring
Evaluation"] A --> E["Energy Audit"] A --> F["Baseline Setting"] B --> G["Roles and
Responsibilities"] C --> H["Awareness
Programs"] D --> I["KPIs and
MIS Reports"] D --> J["PDCA Cycle"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Three E's Triangle — Sustainability Anchor ```mermaid flowchart TD A["ENERGY
Efficiency"] --> C["Sustainable
Energy Management"] B["ECONOMY
Cost Reduction"] --> C D["ENVIRONMENT
Emission Control"] --> C A <--> B B <--> D D <--> A ``` --- ## 4.5 Energy Audit Decision Tree (Walkthrough Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Start: Energy
Audit Required"] --> node2["Walk-Through
Audit"] node2 --> node3{"Significant
Savings Found?"} node3 -->|No| node4["Document and
Stop"] node3 -->|Yes| node5["Detailed
Audit"] node5 --> node6["Measure and
Instrument"] node6 --> node7["Identify ECMs
Energy Conservation Measures"] node7 --> node8["Economic
Analysis NPV SPP"] node8 --> node9{"Feasible
ECM?"} node9 -->|Yes| node10["Implement
and Monitor"] node9 -->|No| node4 node10 --> node11["Verify
Savings"] node11 --> node1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **Q: Define Energy Management. State any four of its general principles.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Energy Management is the systematic process of planning, organizing, directing, and controlling energy procurement, conversion, distribution, and utilization to achieve minimum energy cost and maximum efficiency while complying with environmental norms. **Four General Principles:** 1. **Top Management Commitment:** Energy management must have executive-level support for budget allocation and policy enforcement. 2. **Energy Policy and Objectives:** A clear, documented energy policy aligned with organizational goals is essential. 3. **Measure Before You Manage:** Comprehensive metering and sub-metering must precede any analysis. 4. **Continuous Improvement via PDCA:** The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle ensures iterative refinement of energy performance. *(Alternate acceptable principles: Right-sizing, load factor optimization, behavioral change, preventive maintenance, technical principles like exergy minimization.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Differentiate between Energy Conservation and Energy Efficiency with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Energy Conservation | Energy Efficiency | |---|---|---| | **Approach** | Reducing consumption by changing behavior | Reducing wastage through better technology | | **Output** | Often reduces output | Maintains or enhances output | | **Examples** | (i) Switching off AC when not in room | (i) Replacing CFL with LED bulb | | | (ii) Using stairs instead of elevators | (ii) Installing VFD on motors | **Conclusion:** While conservation is easier and cheaper (no capital cost), efficiency is more sustainable and provides long-term savings without sacrificing comfort or productivity. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### INTERNAL CHOICE — Question A or Question B --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Past Year Pattern]** **Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of Energy Management from economic, environmental, and strategic perspectives. Discuss the role of the Energy Conservation Act 2001 in promoting energy management in India. **Q: (b) [7 Marks]** A factory replaces its old boiler with a high-efficiency boiler. The initial investment is ₹25,00,000. The annual fuel saving is ₹6,00,000. The project life is 8 years and the discount rate is 12% per annum. Calculate (i) Simple Payback Period, (ii) NPV, and (iii) comment on the financial viability of the project. --- #### Model Solution for Part (a): **1. Economic Significance:** - Energy bills constitute **15–30%** of manufacturing cost in energy-intensive industries (cement, steel, textiles). - A systematic energy management program can yield **5–15% savings** with payback periods often under 2 years. - Reduces cost of production → enhances competitiveness in global markets. - Example: A steel plant spending ₹500 crore/year on energy can save ₹50 crore/year through systematic EM. **2. Environmental Significance:** - The energy sector accounts for ~**73% of global CO₂ emissions** (IEA data). - Energy efficiency is the cheapest decarbonization pathway — every kWh saved avoids ~0.82 kg CO₂ in coal-based grids. - Supports India's commitment at COP26 — **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030** and **net-zero by 2070**. **3. Strategic Significance:** - Reduces import dependence on crude oil and natural gas (energy security). - India imports ~85% of its crude oil — every unit of energy saved is foreign exchange conserved. - Aligns with national missions: UJALA, PAT, SAATH, ECBC. **4. Role of Energy Conservation Act 2001:** - Designated consumers (industries consuming > specific threshold) MUST appoint certified **Energy Managers**. - Mandatory **periodic energy audits** (every 3–4 years). - Establishment of **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**. - Enforcement of standards for appliances (Star Labelling). - **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme — sets SEC targets and allows trading of Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts). --- #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Given:** - Initial Investment $I = ₹25,00,000$ - Annual saving $\Delta C = ₹6,00,000$ - Project life $n = 8$ years - Discount rate $r = 12\% = 0.12$ **(i) Simple Payback Period:** $$SPP = \frac{I}{\Delta C} = \frac{25,00,000}{6,00,000} = 4.17 \text{ years}$$ **(ii) Net Present Value:** $$NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{8} \frac{\Delta C}{(1+r)^t}$$ Compute each year's discounted cash flow: $$\text{Year 1: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^1} = 5,35,714$$ $$\text{Year 2: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^2} = 4,78,316$$ $$\text{Year 3: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^3} = 4,27,068$$ $$\text{Year 4: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^4} = 3,81,311$$ $$\text{Year 5: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^5} = 3,40,456$$ $$\text{Year 6: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^6} = 3,03,979$$ $$\text{Year 7: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^7} = 2,71,410$$ $$\text{Year 8: } \frac{6,00,000}{1.12^8} = 2,42,330$$ Sum: $$\sum PV = 5,35,714 + 4,78,316 + 4,27,068 + 3,81,311 + 3,40,456 + 3,03,979 + 2,71,410 + 2,42,330$$ $$\sum PV = 29,80,584 \text{ (approximately)}$$ Therefore: $$NPV = -25,00,000 + 29,80,584 = ₹4,80,584$$ **(iii) Comment on Viability:** - SPP = 4.17 years (less than project life of 8 years) ✓ - **NPV = ₹4,80,584 > 0** ✓ - **Conclusion: The project is financially viable and should be accepted.** --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern, Internal Choice]** **Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four functions of management applied to energy management. Describe the role of the Energy Manager and the Energy Management Team (EMT) in an organization. **Q: (b) [7 Marks]** A pumping system consumes 50,000 kWh per month at a tariff of ₹6.50/kWh. After installing energy-efficient pumps and VFDs, the consumption drops to 38,000 kWh/month. Calculate: (i) Monthly monetary saving (ii) Annual energy saving in kWh (iii) Percentage reduction in SEC (iv) If the retrofit cost ₹8,00,000, find the Simple Payback Period. --- #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Four Management Functions Applied to Energy:** 1. **Planning:** - Formulating the **Energy Policy** (signed by CEO). - Conducting **baseline energy audit** (Type 1, 2, or 3). - Setting **SMART targets** (e.g., "Reduce SEC by 8% in 18 months"). - Forecasting future demand using regression / time-series models. 2. **Organizing:** - Creating an **Energy Management Cell** headed by a certified Energy Manager. - Defining **roles, responsibilities, and authority** (RACI matrix). - Allocating budget and resources. - Cross-functional team: Maintenance, Production, Finance, Utilities. 3. **Directing (Leading):** - Training programs (mandatory under ISO 50001 clause 7.2). - Awareness campaigns ("Switch Off," energy week celebrations). - Performance-based incentives for energy savers. - Visible management commitment and walk-through audits. 4. **Controlling:** - **Real-time monitoring** through SCADA / IoT-based energy meters. - **KPIs**: SEC, kWh/tonne, EUI, MWh per shift. - Variance analysis (actual vs. target). - Corrective Action Reports (CARs) and Management Review Meetings. **Role of Energy Manager (Statutory under EC Act 2001):** - Overall in-charge of the energy management program. - Must be a **BEE-certified Energy Manager** (after passing the National Certification Examination for Energy Managers — NCEEM). - Reports directly to top management (preferably Board level). - Conducts periodic audits and submits compliance reports to BEE. **Energy Management Team (EMT):** - Cross-functional team led by Energy Manager. - Includes engineers from maintenance, production, electrical, instrumentation. - Meets regularly (monthly) to review energy performance. - Implements ECMs and tracks progress. --- #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Given:** - Original consumption $E_1 = 50,000 \text{ kWh/month}$ - New consumption $E_2 = 38,000 \text{ kWh/month}$ - Tariff $T = ₹6.50/\text{kWh}$ - Retrofit cost $I = ₹8,00,000$ **(i) Monthly Monetary Saving:** $$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = 50,000 - 38,000 = 12,000 \text{ kWh/month}$$ $$\Delta C_{monthly} = 12,000 \times 6.50 = ₹78,000/\text{month}$$ **(ii) Annual Energy Saving:** $$\Delta E_{annual} = 12,000 \times 12 = 1,44,000 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **(iii) Percentage Reduction in SEC:** $$\%\Delta = \frac{E_1 - E_2}{E_1} \times 100 = \frac{12,000}{50,000} \times 100 = 24\%$$ **(iv) Simple Payback Period:** $$\Delta C_{annual} = 78,000 \times 12 = ₹9,36,000/\text{year}$$ $$SPP = \frac{I}{\Delta C_{annual}} = \frac{8,00,000}{9,36,000} = 0.855 \text{ years} \approx 10.3 \text{ months}$$ **Conclusion:** The retrofit pays back in **less than a year** — a highly attractive energy project. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Energy Conservation with Energy Efficiency** — A 3-mark difference. Always give clear distinction with output impact. > 2. **Ignoring the Time Value of Money** — When calculating viability, students often use only Simple Payback. For full marks, compute NPV too. > 3. **Forgetting Units in Formulas** — Always write units (kWh, ₹, years, %). Losing units = losing 0.5–1 mark. > 4. **Not Mentioning the EC Act 2001** — Any energy management answer in KTU without a reference to the Energy Conservation Act 2001 / BEE / PAT scheme is considered incomplete. > 5. **Skipping the Behavioral Aspect** — Energy management is not only technical. Always mention training, motivation, and organizational culture. > 6. **Confusing Load Factor with Diversity Factor** — Load Factor is for a single consumer; Diversity Factor is for a group. > 7. **Not Naming the Standard** — For ISO 50001 alignment, mention the standard explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ✅ **Core Definition:** Energy Management = Systematic planning, organizing, directing, controlling of energy use to optimize cost, efficiency, and environment. ✅ **Three Pillars (3E):** Energy, Economy, Environment — any EM project must satisfy all three. ✅ **Four Management Functions:** Planning → Organizing → Directing → Controlling. ✅ **PDCA Cycle:** ISO 50001-aligned continuous improvement framework. ✅ **Conservation vs. Efficiency:** Conservation = reduce use; Efficiency = reduce wastage while maintaining output. ✅ **Key Statutory Body:** BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) under the EC Act 2001. ✅ **Mandatory Role:** Certified Energy Manager for designated consumers. ✅ **Key Metrics:** - SEC (Specific Energy Consumption) - EUI (Energy Utilization Index) - SPP (Simple Payback Period) - NPV (Net Present Value) - ROI (Return on Investment) - Load Factor, Demand Factor ✅ **First Law:** $\eta_I = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ — quantity-based. ✅ **Second Law:** $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{minimum}}$ — quality (exergy)-based. ✅ **Practical Significance:** 5–15% savings achievable in most industries with payback < 3 years. ✅ **Mnemonic — "S-T-E-E":** Savings, Technical, Ecological, Energy Security. ✅ **PAT Scheme:** Perform, Achieve, Trade — India's flagship energy efficiency market mechanism. ✅ **Always cite units, always show formula, always interpret the answer** — these are the three golden rules of KTU numericals.

topics.title: Energy Audit - Types and Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit - Types and Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Energy Audit

An **Energy Audit** is a systematic, documented verification process used to assess how energy is being utilized in an industrial, commercial, or institutional facility, and to identify opportunities for improving energy efficiency while ensuring the same level of service, comfort, and production output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**: Energy audit is the systematic inspection, survey, and analysis of energy use in a building, process, or system to identify energy conservation opportunities (ECOs), quantify the energy saved, and recommend cost-effective retrofit measures with a defined payback period.

Mathematically expressed, the goal of any energy audit is to maximize the ratio:

$$
\text{Energy Performance Ratio (EPR)} = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}}
$$

---

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics Used in Audits

| Metric | Full Form | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SEC** | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/ton, MJ/kg | Benchmarking production efficiency |
| **kWh** | Kilowatt-hour | kWh | Standard electrical energy unit |
| **TOE** | Tonne of Oil Equivalent | TOE | Macro-level energy accounting (1 TOE = 41.868 GJ) |
| **MTOE** | Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent | MTOE | National-scale energy planning |
| **PF** | Power Factor | dimensionless | Indicates reactive power burden |
| **PLF** | Plant Load Factor | % | Capacity utilization indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard **calorific reference constant** for petroleum energy conversion is **1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 10,000 Mcal = 1163.0 kWh (approx.)**. This value is universally used in Indian BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) audit reports.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Medical Check-up of a Factory"

Imagine a **human body undergoing an annual health check-up**:
- The doctor checks vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, cholesterol).
- He compares the readings with **normal reference values**.
- He flags any abnormal patterns and prescribes a treatment (exercise, diet, medicine).
- The patient follows the regimen to recover health.

An **energy audit works exactly the same way** for a factory:
- The **energy auditor** is the doctor.
- The **plant equipment** (motors, boilers, compressors) is the body.
- The **energy bills, logbooks, and instrumentation** are the diagnostic reports.
- The **recommended Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** are the prescriptions.
- The **payback period** is the recovery time.

> [!TIP]
> Think of energy audit as **"going to the doctor BEFORE you fall sick"** — preventive, predictive, and prescriptive. It does not require a production shutdown; it just observes the operating plant carefully.

---

### 1.4 Why Energy Audit is a Mandatory KTU Topic

Under the **Energy Conservation Act 2001 (India)**, it is legally compulsory for energy-intensive industries (designated as **Designated Consumers**) to conduct periodic energy audits by a **BEE-certified Energy Auditor (CEA)**. Industries covered include:

- Aluminium, Cement, Chlor-alkali, Fertilizer, Iron & Steel
- Pulp & Paper, Refineries, Petrochemicals, Sugar, Textiles
- Thermal Power Stations, Captive Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "WHY" Behind Energy Auditing

Every industrial unit, building, or system wastes energy in three principal ways:

1. **Wastage from poor housekeeping** — leakages, unnecessary lighting, idle running.
2. **Wastage from inefficient equipment** — old motors, uninsulated steam lines, oversized pumps.
3. **Wastage from poor operating practices** — low power factor, poor load scheduling, and absence of automation.

The audit quantifies each category, then proposes retrofitting or operational changes that pay for themselves through subsequent bill reductions.

---

### 2.2 Types of Energy Audits (Comprehensive KTU Classification)

The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India, classifies energy audits into **three principal types**, graded by the depth, cost, time, and instrumentation required.

#### Type 1 — Preliminary / Walk-through Energy Audit

This is the **first pass** of auditing and acts as the gateway to deeper studies.

- **Objective**: Quick identification of glaring inefficiencies, low-cost/no-cost opportunities.
- **Duration**: 1 to 3 days on-site.
- **Tools used**: Visual inspection, utility bill analysis, walk-through, casual interview of operating staff.
- **Data collected**: Monthly electricity bills, fuel invoices, operating hours, production logs.
- **Deliverable**: A short report listing obvious waste points and a recommendation for a detailed audit if savings potential exceeds a threshold (typically 5–10% of annual energy cost).
- **Cost**: Lowest.
- **Suitability**: Small-scale industries, buildings, hotels, hospitals.

#### Type 2 — Detailed / Mini Energy Audit

A mid-level audit that builds on the walk-through and generates a quantified **Energy Balance Diagram (Sankey diagram)** of the plant.

- **Objective**: Quantify energy flows in each sub-system; identify Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) with payback under 2 years.
- **Duration**: 2 to 6 weeks on-site.
- **Tools used**: Hand-held instruments (lux meters, clamp meters, infrared thermometers, flue gas analyzers, tachometers).
- **Data collected**: Spot-load electrical measurements, steam pressure/temperature profiles, lighting levels, motor loading, compressed air leak survey, insulation status.
- **Deliverable**: Quantified energy balance; list of short-payback ECMs.
- **Cost**: Medium.
- **Suitability**: Medium-scale manufacturing, commercial complexes.

#### Type 3 — Comprehensive / Investment-grade Energy Audit

The most rigorous and capital-oriented audit, generally performed before major plant expansion or retrofit.

- **Objective**: Develop a complete energy model, simulate alternatives, propose ECMs with longer payback, support investment decisions.
- **Duration**: 1 to 6 months on-site.
- **Tools used**: Continuous online data loggers, ultrasonic flow meters, power analyzers, calorimetric apparatus, simulation software (e.g., DOE-2, EnergyPlus for buildings; pinch analysis tools for process plants).
- **Data collected**: 24×7 monitoring over weeks, harmonic distortion, transient loads, return on investment modelling for every alternative.
- **Deliverable**: Detailed investment-grade report with **NPV, IRR, payback** for each ECM.
- **Cost**: Highest.
- **Suitability**: Energy-intensive process industries, large buildings, captive power plants.

---

### 2.3 Energy Audit Procedure — The Standard 7-Phase Model

The audit is a structured, phased project, NOT a one-day activity. The **7-phase methodology** recommended by BEE India and aligned with ISO 50002 is given below.

**Phase 0 — Pre-Audit Phase (Need Assessment)**
- Management decision to commission audit.
- Budget allocation, scope definition, time schedule, team formation.

**Phase 1 — Planning and Organising**
- Identify facilities to be covered.
- Gather historical data — at least 12 months of electricity/fuel bills.
- Form the audit team with both internal engineers and external certified auditors.

**Phase 2 — Walk-through Inspection**
- Visual reconnaissance of the plant.
- Identification of major energy-consuming equipment.
- Spot interviews with operators, supervisors, and maintenance staff.

**Phase 3 — Detailed Data Collection and Field Measurements**
- Instrumented measurements: power, voltage, current, PF, harmonic distortion, temperature, pressure, flow rates, lux levels.
- Recording of operating patterns (load curves).

**Phase 4 — Data Analysis and Synthesis**
- Construction of energy balance.
- Calculation of Specific Energy Consumption (SEC).
- Benchmarking against best-practice reference values.
- Identification and ranking of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) by simple payback.

**Phase 5 — Report Preparation and Presentation**
- Compilation of findings.
- Techno-economic evaluation of each ECM.
- Final report with priority list, investment estimate, payback period, NPV.

**Phase 6 — Implementation, Monitoring, and Follow-up**
- Execution of approved ECMs.
- Verification of actual savings vs. predicted.
- Establishment of **Monitoring and Targeting (M&T)** system for continuous tracking.

**Phase 7 — M&V (Measurement and Verification)**
- As per **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** to validate the savings, often through third-party.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential Equations for Audit Calculations

> [!WARNING]
> The following table uses `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe character to keep the markdown table valid. All units are SI.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Used}}{\text{Production Output}}$ | Benchmarking boiler, motor, compressor |
| 2 | Annual Energy Saving | $E_{\text{saved}} = E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{new}}$ | Quantifying ECM benefit |
| 3 | Percentage Energy Saving | $\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{E_{\text{saved}}}{E_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ | Relative impact of ECM |
| 4 | Annual Monetary Saving | $S_{\text{INR}} = E_{\text{saved}} \times \text{Tariff (Rs/kWh)}$ | Converting kWh to ₹ |
| 5 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Investment (Rs)}}{\text{Annual Saving (Rs/year)}}$ | Quick ranking of ECMs |
| 6 | Discounted Payback | $DPB = \min \vert t \mid \;\vert\; \sum_{i=1}^{t} \dfrac{C_i}{(1+r)^i} \ge I_0$ | Time-value-adjusted payback |
| 7 | Net Present Value | $\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_i}{(1+r)^i}$ | ECM financial viability |
| 8 | Internal Rate of Return | $\text{NPV} = 0 = -I_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_i}{(1+\text{IRR})^i}$ | Implicit discount rate |
| 9 | Boiler Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \dfrac{m_s(h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \times \text{GCV}_f} \times 100$ | Steam system audit |
| 10 | Motor Loading | $\%L = \dfrac{P_{\text{measured}}}{P_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | Identifying under/overloaded motors |
| 11 | Power Factor Penalty | $\text{penalty} = \text{kVAh} \times \text{Rate} \times \left(\dfrac{0.9 - \cos\phi}{0.9}\right)$ | PF correction benefit |
| 12 | Plant Load Factor | $\text{PLF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity} \times 8760} \times 100$ | Plant-level utilization |

where $I_0$ = initial investment, $C_i$ = net cash flow in year $i$, $r$ = discount rate, $N$ = project life, $m_s$ = steam mass flow, $h_s$ = steam enthalpy, $h_{fw}$ = feedwater enthalpy, $m_f$ = fuel mass flow, $\text{GCV}_f$ = fuel gross calorific value.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above framework is applied daily in:

- **Cement plants** — to assess pyroprocess energy (5–6 GJ/tonne clinker benchmark).
- **Refineries** — for fired heaters, distillation columns, and hydrogen network.
- **HVAC in green buildings** — to comply with ECBC+ and LEED certification.
- **Power plants** — to identify turbine cycle inefficiencies and feed-pump losses.
- **Buildings (commercial)** — for IGBC / GRIHA rating compliance in India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Energy Saving Calculation (ECBC-style)

**Problem Statement:**
A factory's annual energy consumption profile is as follows:

| Energy Source | Annual Consumption | Unit Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Grid electricity | 5,00,000 kWh | ₹8.00 /kWh |
| Diesel (for DG set) | 12,000 litres | ₹95 /litre |
| LPG | 8,000 kg | ₹120 /kg |
| Coal | 250 tonnes | ₹9,000 /tonne |

The audit team proposes the following Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs):

| ECM | Annual Energy Saved (mixed units) | Investment (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| ECM-1: Replace 80 W fluorescent with 36 W LED (1000 fittings) | 308,000 kWh electricity | ₹12,00,000 |
| ECM-2: Variable Frequency Drive on 50 HP pump | 60,000 kWh electricity | ₹4,50,000 |
| ECM-3: Heat recovery from furnace flue gas | 25,000 kg LPG | ₹6,00,000 |
| ECM-4: Power factor correction from 0.78 to 0.95 | Eliminates ₹1,80,000 kVArh penalty | ₹2,00,000 |

**Step 1 — Compute annual monetary savings of each ECM.**

For ECM-1 (electricity saved = 308,000 kWh at ₹8/kWh):

$$
S_1 = 308{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 24{,}64{,}000
$$

For ECM-2 (electricity saved = 60,000 kWh at ₹8/kWh):

$$
S_2 = 60{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 4{,}80{,}000
$$

For ECM-3 (LPG saved = 25,000 kg at ₹120/kg):

$$
S_3 = 25{,}000 \times 120 = \text{₹ } 30{,}00{,}000
$$

For ECM-4 (PF correction saving):

$$
S_4 = \text{₹ } 1{,}80{,}000 \text{ (direct penalty avoided)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute simple payback period of each ECM.**

$$
\text{SPP} = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Saving}}
$$

For ECM-1:

$$
\text{SPP}_1 = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{24{,}64{,}000} = 0.487 \text{ year} \approx 5.84 \text{ months}
$$

For ECM-2:

$$
\text{SPP}_2 = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{4{,}80{,}000} = 0.9375 \text{ year} \approx 11.25 \text{ months}
$$

For ECM-3:

$$
\text{SPP}_3 = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = 0.20 \text{ year} = 2.4 \text{ months}
$$

For ECM-4:

$$
\text{SPP}_4 = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = 1.11 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Rank the ECMs by Simple Payback Period (ascending order).**

| Rank | ECM | Investment (₹) | Annual Saving (₹) | SPP (months) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ECM-3 (Furnace heat recovery) | 6,00,000 | 30,00,000 | 2.4 |
| 2 | ECM-1 (LED retrofits) | 12,00,000 | 24,64,000 | 5.84 |
| 3 | ECM-2 (VFD on pump) | 4,50,000 | 4,80,000 | 11.25 |
| 4 | ECM-4 (PF correction) | 2,00,000 | 1,80,000 | 13.32 |

**Step 4 — Net Present Value (NPV) for ECM-2 at a discount rate of 12%, project life N = 5 years.**

Assuming a constant annual cash flow of ₹4,80,000 for 5 years:

$$
\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{5} \frac{C}{(1+r)^i}
$$

We first compute the **present-value annuity factor (PVAF)**:

$$
\text{PVAF} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \frac{1}{(1+0.12)^i} = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12}
$$

$$
\text{PVAF} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.605
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{NPV} = -4{,}50{,}000 + (4{,}80{,}000 \times 3.605) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 17{,}30{,}400
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{NPV}_2 = \text{₹ } 12{,}80{,}400}
$$

Since NPV is positive, **ECM-2 is financially viable**.

**Step 5 — Total Annual Saving if all 4 ECMs are implemented.**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 24{,}64{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹ } 41{,}24{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Total Investment for all ECMs.**

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 24{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 7 — Combined simple payback for the package.**

$$
\text{SPP}_{\text{package}} = \frac{24{,}50{,}000}{41{,}24{,}000} = 0.594 \text{ year} \approx 7.13 \text{ months}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A combined SPP of **under 1 year** classifies this ECM package as a **"Priority-1"** recommendation under any KTU / BEE standard.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Audit Type

| Parameter | Walk-through | Mini Audit | Comprehensive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Duration | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks | 1–6 months |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Data depth | Utility bills | Instrumented spot | 24×7 logged |
| Number of ECMs | 5–10 | 10–25 | 25–100+ |
| Typical payback targeted | < 6 months | < 2 years | Up to 5 years |
| Computational tool | Spreadsheet | Excel + simple models | DOE-2 / EnergyPlus / Pinch |
| Report length | 10–20 pages | 30–80 pages | 100+ pages |
| Suitable for | Small units | Medium industries | Energy-intensive giants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Audit Procedure — Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

The following Mermaid flow-chart captures the **7-phase BEE-aligned audit methodology** and its inter-phase dependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Phase 0: Pre-Audit Need Assessment] --> A1[Phase 1: Planning and Organising]
    A1 --> A2[Phase 2: Walk-through Visual Inspection]
    A2 --> A3{Is Site Energy Intensive?}
    A3 -- No --> A4[Stop: Low-Cost ECM List Only]
    A3 -- Yes --> A5[Phase 3: Detailed Field Measurements]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 4: Data Analysis and Energy Balance]
    A6 --> A7[Phase 5: Report Preparation and ECM Ranking]
    A7 --> A8[Phase 6: Implementation and Monitoring]
    A8 --> A9[Phase 7: M and V Verification as per IPMVP]
    A9 --> A10[Continuous Monitoring and Targeting System]
    A10 -.-> A6

    subgraph Tools
        T1[Lux Meter]
        T2[Clamp Meter]
        T3[Flue Gas Analyser]
        T4[Ultrasonic Flow Meter]
        T5[Power Analyser]
    end

    A5 -.uses.-> T2
    A5 -.uses.-> T5
    A5 -.uses.-> T3
    A5 -.uses.-> T4
    A2 -.uses.-> T1
```

---

### 4.2 Hierarchy of the Three Energy Audit Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Pre-Audit Decision] --> Q1{Plant Size and Energy Intensity}
    Q1 --> R1[Small and Low Intensity: Walk-through Audit]
    Q1 --> R2[Medium and Moderate Intensity: Mini Audit]
    Q1 --> R3[Large and High Intensity: Comprehensive Audit]

    R1 --> S1[Deliverable: Quick List of Low Cost ECMs]
    R2 --> S2[Deliverable: Energy Balance plus ECMs with Payback under 2 Years]
    R3 --> S3[Deliverable: Investment Grade Report with NPV IRR Payback]

    S1 -.optional followup.-> R2
    S2 -.optional followup.-> R3
```

---

### 4.3 Energy Balance Architecture (per Sub-System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Energy Input: Electricity, Fuel, Steam] --> PROC[Process / Equipment Boundary]
    PROC --> USEFUL[Useful Energy Output: Heating, Lighting, Mechanical Work]
    PROC --> LOSS1[Waste Heat Flue Gas]
    PROC --> LOSS2[Steam and Air Leaks]
    PROC --> LOSS3[Idle and No Load Losses]
    PROC --> LOSS4[Reactive Power PF Penalty]

    LOSS1 --> REC1[Heat Recovery ECM]
    LOSS2 --> REC2[Leak Sealing ECM]
    LOSS3 --> REC3[Scheduling and VFD ECM]
    LOSS4 --> REC4[Capacitor Bank ECM]

    REC1 --> SAVE[Monetised Energy Saving]
    REC2 --> SAVE
    REC3 --> SAVE
    REC4 --> SAVE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define energy audit. List any four national designated consumers under the Energy Conservation Act 2001.**

> **Model Answer:**
> An energy audit is a systematic, documented verification process used to assess energy use in a facility and identify opportunities for energy efficiency improvement, while maintaining service, comfort, and productivity levels.
>
> Four designated consumers under the Energy Conservation Act 2001 are:
> 1. Aluminium industry
> 2. Cement industry
> 3. Iron and Steel industry
> 4. Fertilizer industry
> *(Any four are acceptable; sugar, pulp and paper, refineries, textiles, chlor-alkali, thermal power stations are also valid.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between a walk-through audit and a comprehensive energy audit in any six aspects.**

> **Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Walk-through Audit | Comprehensive Audit |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | 1–3 days | 1–6 months |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest |
| Instrumentation | Visual / utility bills | Continuous loggers, analysers |
| Data depth | Historical and bills | Continuous 24×7 monitored |
| Outcome | List of low-cost ECMs | Investment-grade ECMs with NPV/IRR |
| Computational tool | Manual / spreadsheet | Simulation software (DOE-2, Pinch) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **7-phase procedure of a comprehensive energy audit** as recommended by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India. Highlight the role of each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit has installed **50 ceiling fans of 75 W each**, running **10 hours per day, 26 days per month**. The unit replaces them with **BLDC fans of 32 W** with the same duty cycle. The fans are operated from the grid at **₹9 per kWh**.
