# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. *Never* state "thermal imager" without specifying **emissivity** — automatic 1-mark penalty in BEE-style questions.
> 2. *Never* write "PF = kW / kVA" without a $\cos\phi$ symbol or note — that is *displacement* PF, not *true* PF, when harmonics exist.
> 3. *Always* mention **calibration validity** in instrument selection answers — marks reserved for it in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List any three portable instruments used during a walk-through energy audit of a thermal power plant. State one specific use of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Infrared (IR) Thermometer** — Used for non-contact measurement of steam pipe surface temperature to estimate *heat loss* and verify insulation adequacy. (1 mark)
2. **Clamp-on Power Meter** — Used to measure line current and derive the *kW load* of HT/LT motors without breaking the circuit, ideal for live ID/FD fan motors. (1 mark)
3. **Portable Flue Gas Analyser** — Used to measure O₂, CO, and flue temperature in the boiler stack to calculate *combustion efficiency* and *excess air*. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define emissivity. Why is it critical when using an IR thermometer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Emissivity $\varepsilon$ is the *ratio* of radiation emitted by a real surface to the radiation emitted by a black body at the same temperature, ranging from 0 (perfect reflector) to 1 (black body). (1.5 marks)

It is critical because the IR thermometer determines temperature from the *radiated* energy, and this energy is *directly proportional* to $\varepsilon$. If the operator enters a wrong $\varepsilon$ — e.g., 0.95 instead of the actual 0.05 of a polished copper pipe — the instrument will massively under-read (because the pipe actually emits far less than the assumed value). (1.5 marks)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> The Module-4 ESE paper in KTU 2024 Scheme typically offers a 14-mark choice. Two alternatives are given below — students answer either.

---

### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the working principle of a **portable flue gas analyser**. List the parameters it measures and explain the **Siegert formula** for combustion efficiency. (7 marks)

**b)** Describe the working of a **thermal imaging camera** and list **four** typical energy-audit applications of thermography in an industrial plant. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
Sample probe → Particulate filter → Moisture trap → Pump → Sensor block (O₂: zirconia; CO, NOₓ, SO₂: electrochemical; CO₂: NDIR) → Microcontroller → Display & memory → Data port.

**Working principle (2 marks):** A slip-stream of flue gas is drawn by an internal pump. Each electrochemical / NDIR cell produces a current or voltage proportional to the gas concentration. The microprocessor converts these to % or ppm and computes the **air-fuel ratio**, **excess air**, and **combustion efficiency**.

**Siegert formula derivation (2 marks):** For natural gas firing, an empirical relation correlates flue temperature, ambient temperature, and flue O₂ to stack heat loss:

$$\eta_{comb} = 100 - \dfrac{T_{flue} - T_{air}}{21 - 0.0625 \cdot O_2\,(\%)}$$

(Valid for natural gas with stoichiometric O₂ ≈ 21%. For other fuels, the constant 0.0625 changes to 0.085 for LPG, 0.094 for furnace oil, etc.)

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Principle (3 marks):** A thermal imaging camera uses a **focal-plane array (FPA)** of microbolometers (typically amorphous silicon or vanadium oxide) cooled only by ambient air. Each microbolometer pixel absorbs IR in the **8–14 µm band**, heating up and changing its resistance. This resistance change is read by a per-pixel ROIC and mapped to apparent temperature using the **Stefan–Boltzmann law**:

$$E_b = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

The on-board software applies emissivity, reflected temperature, atmospheric humidity, and distance corrections to generate a **false-colour thermogram** at typical resolutions of 320×240 or 640×480 pixels, with thermal sensitivity (NETD) of **< 50 mK**.

**Four industrial applications (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Refractory inspection** in rotary kilns — hot spots on the outer shell indicate lining wear.
2. **Steam trap and valve diagnostics** — cold traps show unopen valves; hot pipes downstream of traps show leakage.
3. **Electrical panel inspection** — loose connections show as hot spots before failure; preventive maintenance of MCCs.
4. **Insulation audit of furnace / boiler** — cold bands confirm *missing or wet insulation*; quantified heat loss in kW is calculated as $Q = U A \Delta T$.

---

### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**a)** Explain the working of a **clamp-on power quality analyzer** with reference to the quantities it measures (V, I, P, Q, S, PF, THD, kWh). Show how active power is calculated for a balanced 3-phase load. (7 marks)

**b)** Describe the working of an **ultrasonic flow meter** and an **anemometer**. State two industrial applications of each. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Working (4 marks):** The instrument uses *split-core current transformers* (CTs) for current and direct *voltage probes* for potential. The current and voltage signals are sampled by a 16-bit ADC at ≥ 128 samples/cycle (≥ 6.4 kHz for 50 Hz). A DSP computes:

- **V_rms** and **I_rms** (per phase, line-to-line / line-to-neutral).
- **Active power** $P = V I \cos\phi$ (instantaneous product, averaged over N cycles).
- **Reactive power** $Q = V I \sin\phi$ (using 90° shifted voltage).
- **Apparent power** $S = V I$.
- **Power factor** $\cos\phi$.
- **THD** via FFT: $\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{n} I_h^{2}}}{I_1}\times 100$.
- **Energy** $E = \int P\,dt$ integrated over logging interval (e.g., 1, 5, 15, 30 min) and stored on SD card.

**Active power for balanced 3-φ (3 marks):**
For a balanced star load with phase voltage $V_{ph}$ and phase current $I_{ph}$ at angle $\phi$:

$$P_{total} = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$

Using line quantities $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph}$ and $I_L = I_{ph}$ (star):

$$P_{total} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

(For a delta, $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{ph}$ and $V_L = V_{ph}$, leading to the same result.) The analyzer applies this formula internally to display the *total* three-phase quantity when all three CTs are connected.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Ultrasonic flow meter (3.5 marks):** Two piezo-electric transducers A and B are clamped on a clean pipe at angle $\theta$ to the flow axis. A transmits an ultrasound pulse that B receives after transit time $t_1$; then B transmits back to A with transit time $t_2$. The *transit-time difference* $\Delta t = t_1 - t_2$ is proportional to the average flow velocity $v$:

$$v = \dfrac{L^{2}}{2 D \cos\theta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta t}{t_1 t_2}$$

where $L$ is the diagonal path length and $D$ is the inner diameter. Volume flow: $Q = v \cdot \pi D^{2}/4$. The pipe wall and lining are accounted for by *transit-time corrections* based on sound speed in steel/HDPE/PVC.

Two applications: (i) chilled water in HVAC chiller lines, (ii) condensate measurement in boiler auxiliaries.

**Anemometer (3.5 marks):** A **vane anemometer** has a low-mass propeller whose rotational speed (measured by a Hall-effect sensor) is proportional to flow velocity; a calibration curve converts rpm to m/s. A **hot-wire anemometer** uses a fine tungsten wire heated to ~200 °C; airflow cools it, and the current needed to *reheat* it to constant temperature follows **King's Law**:

$$E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{v}$$

Two applications: (i) HVAC duct balancing, (ii) face velocity measurement at fume hoods / cleanroom laminar flow stations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Instruments for Energy Auditing)**

- ☐ Every KTU answer that mentions an IR instrument **must** mention *emissivity* and *reflected temperature compensation*.
- ☐ Lux meter formula: $E_v = I_v \cos\theta / r^{2}$; IS 3646 specifies 300 lx for offices, 500 lx for precision work.
- ☐ Flue gas analyser: electrochemical cells wear out — typical life **1–2 years**; re-calibrate before every BEE audit.
- ☐ Power analyser formulas to remember: $P_{3\phi}=\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L\cos\phi$, $\text{PF}=\cos\phi=P/S$, $\text{THD}$ definition via RMS of harmonics divided by fundamental.
- ☐ Ultrasonic flow meter is **non-invasive**; works only on *clean, fully filled* pipes.
- ☐ Hot-wire anemometer obeys **King's Law** $E^2 = A + B\sqrt{v}$; needs periodic *zero-flow* calibration.
- ☐ SPL uses **A-weighting** with $p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa; OSHA limit 90 dB(A) for 8 h exposure.
- ☐ Photo tachometer needs **reflective tape**; stroboscope must be checked against hazardous rotating machinery protocols.
- ☐ Calibration certificate validity: **≤ 12 months** for BEE Grade-1 / ISO 50002 audits.
- ☐ Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-4}$ — must be cited for *any* radiation instrument question.
- ☐ Siegert formula (natural gas): $\eta = 100 - (T_f - T_a)/(21 - 0.0625\,O_2)$ — modify constant for other fuels.
- ☐ Remember the **two-wattmeter formula** identity: $P = W_1 + W_2$ and $Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)$.
- ☐ In thermal imager answers, mention **NETD** (typically ≤ 50 mK), **spectral band (8–14 µm)**, and **FPA microbolometer**.
- ☐ Lux meter: always hold in the *plane of work*; shadow of the auditor is a common error.
- ☐ Clamp meters: select *true-RMS* for VFDs, SMPS, LED drivers — average-responding types under-read.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Economics: Traditional Types of Rates - Single-Part Rates - Two-Part Rates - Three-Part Rates - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Energy Economics: Traditional Types of Rates - Single-Part Rates - Two-Part Rates - Three-Part Rates - Numerical Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an Electricity Tariff? In electrical power engineering, a **tariff** is the schedule of rates or charges framed by an electricity supply utility (e.g., KSEB – Kerala State Electricity Board) under which it sells electrical energy to its various categories of consumers (domestic, commercial, industrial). The tariff must ensure the **recovery of costs** (generation, transmission, distribution, capital, operation, maintenance) plus a **reasonable profit**, while remaining fair to the consumer. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT637 Module 4):** > *An electricity tariff is a structured pricing mechanism that recovers fixed costs (independent of energy used), variable costs (proportional to energy), and demand costs (proportional to peak loading) from the end consumer.* ### 1.2 Why Are There Different Types of Rates? A single uniform rate cannot be fair to all consumers because: - A domestic consumer with a low **Load Factor (LF)** should not pay the same average rate as an industrial consumer with a high LF. - A consumer with a poor **Power Factor (PF)** stresses the system (higher current, more losses) and must be penalised. - A consumer whose **Maximum Demand (MD)** is high must contribute to the standing costs (transformer, line capacity). Hence, traditional rates are classified into: | Rate Type | Recovery Basis | Complexity | |-----------|----------------|------------| | **Single-Part Rate** | Energy (kWh) only | Simplest | | **Two-Part Rate** | Fixed (per kW) + Energy (per kWh) | Moderate | | **Three-Part Rate** | Fixed + Energy + Demand/PF penalty | Most comprehensive | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Billing" Analogy Imagine you walk into a restaurant: - **Single-Part Rate** → You pay only for the food you ate (₹ per plate). The kitchen, chef, and gas costs are absorbed uniformly. - **Two-Part Rate** → You pay a "table reservation fee" (fixed, regardless of how much you eat) **plus** the price of the food (per plate). The reservation fee covers the cost of keeping the table ready. - **Three-Part Rate** → You pay a reservation fee (fixed) + price per plate (energy) + a "peak-hour surcharge" if you ordered too much during the rush (demand charge). The surcharge recovers the extra effort the restaurant had to put in when it was busiest. The **"restaurant"** is the power utility, the **"food"** is electrical energy, the **"table"** is the reserved capacity (kW/kVA), and the **"peak-hour surcharge"** is the demand or power-factor penalty. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Principle:** A well-designed tariff should satisfy the **Krishna Murthy Equations** of tariff fixation: > 1. Revenue = Cost of production + Profit > 2. Tariff must be fair (proportional to cost of service) > 3. Tariff must encourage optimal use (penalise low LF, low PF, and high peak MD). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Foundational Concepts (Pre-Requisite Vocabulary) Before tackling the rate structures, the following engineering quantities **must be crystal clear**, since they appear in every numerical problem: **a) Connected Load (L):** Sum of the rated capacities (in **kW** or **kVA**) of all apparatus installed at the consumer's premises. $$L \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{i} \quad \text{[kW or kVA]}$$ **b) Maximum Demand (MD or $\text{D}_{\max}$):** The greatest load (in **kW** or **kVA**) that the consumer imposes on the supply system during a given period (typically a billing cycle of 30 days). **c) Average Load or Average Power (Avg. P):** Total energy consumed divided by the period hours (H = 30 × 24 = 720 h for a month). $$P_{avg} \;=\; \frac{E}{H} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ where $E$ = energy consumed in kWh, $H$ = hours of use. **d) Load Factor (LF):** Ratio of average load to maximum demand. $$\text{LF} \;=\; \frac{P_{avg}}{MD} \;=\; \frac{E}{MD \times H}$$ A high LF means the consumer uses energy steadily; a low LF means energy is used in sharp bursts. **e) Demand Factor (DF):** Ratio of maximum demand to connected load. $$\text{DF} \;=\; \frac{MD}{L}$$ **f) Diversity Factor (DivF):** Ratio of sum of individual maximum demands to the simultaneous maximum demand on the system. $$\text{DivF} \;=\; \frac{\sum MD_{i}}{MD_{system}}$$ **g) Power Factor (PF or cos $\phi$):** Ratio of true power (kW) to apparent power (kVA). $$\text{PF} \;=\; \cos\phi \;=\; \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}} \;=\; \frac{P}{S}$$ **h) Bill Factor (BF):** Sometimes used to link MD to connected load — ratio of average load to connected load. $$\text{BF} \;=\; \frac{P_{avg}}{L} \;=\; \text{LF} \times \text{DF}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Hack:** Memorise the triangle **LF × DF = BF** — it saves time in derivation-based problems. ### 2.2 Type 1 — Single-Part Rate (Flat Rate / Uniform Rate) **Definition:** The total bill is a single product — a flat rate (₹/kWh) multiplied by the units of energy consumed. There is **no separate charge** for demand, fixed cost, or power factor. **Bill Equation:** $$B_{1} \;=\; r \times E$$ where $r$ = flat rate in ₹ per kWh (or "unit") and $E$ = energy consumed in kWh. **Operational Logic:** - Consumer A (uses 200 units) pays exactly half of Consumer B (uses 400 units) — no regard to peak demand or time of use. - **Pros:** Simplest possible billing; easy for the consumer to understand. - **Cons:** Highly **unfair** to the utility. A consumer with a tiny LF forces the utility to install transformer capacity for a high peak, but pays the same rate per unit. Encourages wasteful peaks. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world use:** KSEB uses single-part rates only for very small domestic consumers (e.g., BPL — Below Poverty Line) where installing a separate demand meter is uneconomical. ### 2.3 Type 2 — Two-Part Rate (Hopkinson Tariff / Doherty Rate) **Definition:** The bill has two components: 1. **Fixed Charge** — proportional to **Maximum Demand** (₹/kW of MD per month). Recovers standing/capacity costs. 2. **Running Charge (Semaphore Rate)** — proportional to **energy** consumed (₹/kWh). Recovers running/fuel costs. **Bill Equation:** $$B_{2} \;=\; R_{kW} \times MD \;+\; R_{kWh} \times E$$ where $R_{kW}$ = fixed rate per kW of MD per month (₹/kW/month) and $R_{kWh}$ = running rate per kWh. **Why it works:** The fixed charge forces the consumer to **declare or control their MD**, because the bill goes up the moment they raise their peak. This naturally disciplines demand. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Hopkinson Tariff** is the most commonly used two-part tariff globally. KSEB's LT-VI (industrial), HT-I (industrial), and most commercial tariffs are variants of the two-part structure. **Effective (Average) Rate per Unit:** $$r_{eff} \;=\; \frac{B_{2}}{E} \;=\; R_{kW} \times \frac{MD}{E} \;+\; R_{kWh} \;=\; \frac{R_{kW}}{(\text{LF} \times H)} \;+\; R_{kWh}$$ As **LF ↑**, $r_{eff}$ ↓ → consumer is rewarded for higher load factor. ### 2.4 Type 3 — Three-Part Rate (Maximum Demand Tariff / Wright Tariff) **Definition:** The bill has three explicit components: 1. **Fixed Charge** — based on **Connected Load** (₹/kW of L per month). Recovers infrastructure investment. 2. **Variable / Running Charge** — based on energy (₹/kWh). Recovers fuel. 3. **Demand / Reactive Charge** — based on **Maximum Demand in kVA** OR a penalty for low **Power Factor**. **Bill Equation (Most General Form):** $$B_{3} \;=\; R_{L} \times L \;+\; R_{kWh} \times E \;+\; R_{kVA} \times MD_{kVA}$$ OR (alternate form with PF penalty): $$B_{3} \;=\; R_{L} \times L \;+\; R_{kWh} \times E \;+\; C_{PF}$$ where $R_{L}$ = ₹/kW of connected load/month, $R_{kVA}$ = ₹/kVA of MD/month, and $C_{PF}$ = penalty for operating below a reference PF (typically 0.90 lag). **Why it works:** - Connected-load charge ensures the utility recovers capital tied up in transformer + line installation per consumer, regardless of whether they use it. - kVA-based demand charge penalises poor PF because the transformer must be sized for kVA, not kW. - kWh charge recovers fuel. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the "demand charge" in Two-Part (based on kW of MD) with the "demand charge" in Three-Part (often based on kVA of MD). The kVA form implicitly penalises low PF, which is the whole point of the third part. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Single-Part Bill | $B_{1} = r \cdot E$ | ₹ | Flat rate, no demand charge | | 2 | Two-Part Bill (Hopkinson) | $B_{2} = R_{kW} \cdot MD + R_{kWh} \cdot E$ | ₹ | MD in **kW** | | 3 | Three-Part Bill (Wright) | $B_{3} = R_{L} \cdot L + R_{kWh} \cdot E + R_{kVA} \cdot MD_{kVA}$ | ₹ | MD in **kVA** | | 4 | Average Load | $P_{avg} = E / H$ | kW | $H = 720$ h for 30 days | | 5 | Load Factor | $LF = P_{avg} / MD = E / (MD \cdot H)$ | pu (0–1) | High LF → consumer-friendly | | 6 | Demand Factor | $DF = MD / L$ | pu (0–1) | Usually $< 1$ | | 7 | Bill Factor | $BF = LF \times DF = P_{avg} / L$ | pu | $BF = LF \cdot DF$ | | 8 | kVA ↔ kW | $kVA = kW / \cos\phi$ | — | $\cos\phi$ lagging | | 9 | Effective rate (Two-Part) | $r_{eff} = R_{kW} / (LF \cdot H) + R_{kWh}$ | ₹/kWh | Decreases with LF | | 10 | Diversity Factor | $DivF = \sum MD_i / MD_{system}$ | pu $\geq 1$ | System planning | | 11 | PF Penalty (KSEB rule) | Penalty $\propto (0.90 - \text{actual PF})$ | ₹/kVAh | Triggered below 0.90 lag | | 12 | Energy charges (slab) | Slab 1: 0–100, Slab 2: 101–200, … | ₹/unit | Tiered domestic | > [!TIP] > **KTU Frequently Asked Derivation:** *Show that in a two-part tariff, the average rate per unit decreases as the load factor increases.* Keep this template handy — it is a guaranteed 5- to 7-mark question. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Two-Part Tariff: Average Rate vs. Load Factor **Statement to Prove:** *In a Hopkinson (two-part) tariff, the average cost per unit (effective rate) is inversely proportional to the load factor.* **Starting Point — Hopkinson Bill:** $$B \;=\; R_{kW} \cdot MD \;+\; R_{kWh} \cdot E$$ **Step 1:** Recall the definition of Load Factor: $$\text{LF} \;=\; \frac{E}{MD \times H} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E \;=\; LF \cdot MD \cdot H$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $E$ into the bill equation: $$B \;=\; R_{kW} \cdot MD \;+\; R_{kWh} \cdot (LF \cdot MD \cdot H)$$ $$B \;=\; MD \cdot \left[\, R_{kW} \;+\; R_{kWh} \cdot LF \cdot H \,\right]$$ **Step 3:** Define the average (effective) rate per unit: $$r_{eff} \;=\; \frac{B}{E} \;=\; \frac{MD \cdot \left[\, R_{kW} \;+\; R_{kWh} \cdot LF \cdot H \,\right]}{LF \cdot MD \cdot H}$$ **Step 4:** Cancel $MD$ from numerator and denominator: $$r_{eff} \;=\; \frac{R_{kW} \;+\; R_{kWh} \cdot LF \cdot H}{LF \cdot H}$$ **Step 5:** Split the fraction: $$r_{eff} \;=\; \frac{R_{kW}}{LF \cdot H} \;+\; R_{kWh}$$ **Step 6:** Observation — since $R_{kW}$, $R_{kWh}$, and $H$ are constants for a given consumer, $r_{eff}$ is a hyperbolic function of LF (decreases as LF increases). $$\boxed{\; r_{eff} \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{R_{kW}}{H}}_{\text{fixed}} \cdot \frac{1}{LF} \;+\; R_{kWh} \;}$$ Hence, as LF increases (consumer uses energy more steadily), the average rate drops. This is the **engineering justification for the two-part tariff**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Marking Insight:** Examiners specifically look for the clean split in Step 5 and the boxed final expression. Awarding 1 extra mark is common for stating the conclusion explicitly ("…hence $r_{eff}$ decreases monotonically as LF increases"). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Three-Part Tariff Total Bill (kVA Demand Variant) **Given:** A consumer has connected load $L$ kW, Maximum Demand $MD_{kW}$ kW at Power Factor $\cos\phi$, and consumes $E$ kWh in $H$ hours. **Step 1:** Maximum Demand in kVA: $$MD_{kVA} \;=\; \frac{MD_{kW}}{\cos\phi}$$ **Step 2:** Three-part bill: $$B_{3} \;=\; R_{L} \cdot L \;+\; R_{kWh} \cdot E \;+\; R_{kVA} \cdot MD_{kVA}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $MD_{kVA}$: $$B_{3} \;=\; R_{L} \cdot L \;+\; R_{kWh} \cdot E \;+\; R_{kVA} \cdot \frac{MD_{kW}}{\cos\phi}$$ **Step 4:** Effective rate per unit: $$r_{eff} \;=\; \frac{B_{3}}{E} \;=\; \frac{R_{L} \cdot L}{E} \;+\; R_{kWh} \;+\; \frac{R_{kVA} \cdot MD_{kW}}{E \cdot \cos\phi}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $E = P_{avg} \cdot H = LF \cdot MD_{kW} \cdot H$: $$r_{eff} \;=\; \frac{R_{L} \cdot L}{LF \cdot MD_{kW} \cdot H} \;+\; R_{kWh} \;+\; \frac{R_{kVA}}{LF \cdot H \cdot \cos\phi}$$ $$\boxed{\; r_{eff} \;=\; R_{kWh} \;+\; \frac{R_{L}}{H} \cdot \frac{1}{LF \cdot DF} \;+\; \frac{R_{kVA}}{H} \cdot \frac{1}{LF \cdot \cos\phi} \;}$$ The second term shows dependence on **Bill Factor** ($LF \cdot DF$) and the third on **Power Factor**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Improving power factor (cos $\phi$ ↑) directly reduces the third term, so a consumer who installs capacitor banks (PFC) saves money on every kWh — a classic engineering trade-off analysis. --- ### 3.3 Solved Numerical Problems (Board Exam Pattern) #### **Problem 1: Single-Part Tariff (5 marks)** A domestic consumer consumes **360 kWh per month** under a flat-rate tariff of **₹6.50 per unit**. Calculate: (a) The monthly electricity bill. (b) The cost per kWh. **Solution:** $$B_1 \;=\; r \times E \;=\; 6.50 \times 360 \;=\; \mathbf{2340.00 \; ₹}$$ $$r_{eff} \;=\; B_1 / E \;=\; 2340 / 360 \;=\; \mathbf{6.50 \; ₹/kWh}$$ In a single-part tariff, $r_{eff}$ is **identical** to the flat rate. *(Verification only — not a step required by KTU.)* --- #### **Problem 2: Two-Part (Hopkinson) Tariff (7 marks)** A factory has a maximum demand of **240 kW** and consumes **1,08,000 kWh per month** (30 days). Tariff: **₹350 per kW per month** (fixed) + **₹4.20 per kWh** (running). (a) Calculate the monthly bill. (b) Calculate the average rate per kWh. (c) Find the new average rate if the consumer's load factor increases from 0.625 to 0.80 (same MD, same rates). **Solution:** **(a) Monthly Bill:** $$B_2 \;=\; R_{kW} \cdot MD + R_{kWh} \cdot E$$ $$B_2 \;=\; (350)(240) + (4.20)(1{,}08{,}000)$$ $$B_2 \;=\; 84{,}000 + 4{,}53{,}600 \;=\; \mathbf{₹ \; 5{,}37{,}600}$$ **(b) Average rate per kWh:** $$r_{eff} \;=\; B_2 / E \;=\; 5{,}37{,}600 / 1{,}08{,}000 \;=\; \mathbf{₹4.9778/kWh}$$ **(c) New average rate at LF = 0.80:** Same MD = 240 kW. New energy: $$E_{new} \;=\; LF \cdot MD \cdot H \;=\; 0.80 \times 240 \times 720 \;=\; 1{,}38{,}240 \text{ kWh}$$ $$B_{2,new} \;=\; (350)(240) + (4.20)(1{,}38{,}240) \;=\; 84{,}000 + 5{,}80{,}608 \;=\; \mathbf{₹ \; 6{,}64{,}608}$$ $$r_{eff,new} \;=\; 6{,}64{,}608 / 1{,}38{,}240 \;=\; \mathbf{₹4.8077/kWh}$$ **Interpretation:** A higher load factor reduces the average rate from ₹4.978 to ₹4.808 (a saving of ~₹0.17/unit), even though the **absolute bill rises** because more energy is consumed. The bill rise is slower than the energy rise. > [!TIP] > **Exam Trick:** When asked *"will the bill rise or fall?"*, the answer is **bill always rises** (more units), but **average rate falls** (better utilisation). Always distinguish the two. --- #### **Problem 3: Three-Part Tariff with PF Penalty (14 marks — full board pattern)** A small-scale industry has: - **Connected Load:** 500 kW - **Maximum Demand:** 320 kW - **Power Factor:** 0.80 lagging - **Energy consumed:** 1,50,000 kWh in 30 days **Tariff (KSEB HT-IV Industrial):** - Fixed charge: **₹250 per kW of connected load per month** - Running charge: **₹4.80 per kWh** for the first 1,00,000 kWh, **₹5.20 per kWh** for the balance - Demand charge: **₹450 per kVA of maximum demand per month** - PF penalty: **1% of the demand charge for every 0.01 fall in PF below 0.90 lag** Calculate the **total monthly bill** and the **average rate per kWh**. **Solution:** **Step 1: Hours in month** $H = 30 \times 24 = 720$ h **Step 2: Convert MD to kVA** $$MD_{kVA} \;=\; \frac{MD_{kW}}{\cos\phi} \;=\; \frac{320}{0.80} \;=\; 400 \text{ kVA}$$ **Step 3: Fixed Charge (Component 1)** $$F_1 \;=\; R_L \times L \;=\; 250 \times 500 \;=\; \mathbf{₹1{,}25{,}000}$$ **Step 4: Energy Charge (Component 2 — Slab)** - First 1,00,000 kWh @ ₹4.80 = ₹4,80,000 - Balance 50,000 kWh @ ₹5.20 = ₹2,60,000 $$F_2 \;=\; 4{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}60{,}000 \;=\; \mathbf{₹7{,}40{,}000}$$ **Step 5: Demand Charge (Component 3)** $$F_3 \;=\; R_{kVA} \times MD_{kVA} \;=\; 450 \times 400 \;=\; \mathbf{₹1{,}80{,}000}$$ **Step 6: Power Factor Penalty (Component 4)** - Reference PF = 0.90 - Actual PF = 0.80 - Fall = 0.90 − 0.80 = 0.10 = 10 × 0.01 steps - Penalty rate = 1% of demand charge per 0.01 step - Total penalty = 10 × 1% = **10% of ₹1,80,000** $$F_4 \;=\; 0.10 \times 1{,}80{,}000 \;=\; \mathbf{₹18{,}000}$$ **Step 7: Total Bill** $$B_{total} \;=\; F_1 + F_2 + F_3 + F_4 \;=\; 1{,}25{,}000 + 7{,}40{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 18{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\; B_{total} \;=\; \mathbf{₹10{,}63{,}000} \;}$$ **Step 8: Average Rate** $$r_{eff} \;=\; B_{total} / E \;=\; 10{,}63{,}000 / 1{,}50{,}000 \;=\; \mathbf{₹7.087/kWh}$$ **Bonus: Saving from PF Improvement to 0.95** If the consumer installs capacitors and improves PF to 0.95: - New $MD_{kVA} = 320 / 0.95 = 336.84$ kVA - New $F_3 = 450 \times 336.84 = ₹1,51,579$ - New $F_4 = 0$ (PF now above 0.90) - Net saving on demand + penalty = (1,80,000 + 18,000) − 1,51,579 = **₹46,421/month** - Plus, capacitor cost recovery can be shown in a payback period analysis (typically 8–14 months for industrial PF correction). > [!NOTE] > This problem demonstrates the **complete three-part structure** as asked in KTU 14-mark ESE questions. The PF penalty is the "kicker" that makes it a true three-part tariff. --- #### **Problem 4: Comparative Numerical — Choosing the Best Tariff (7 marks)** A consumer has two tariff options from the same utility: | Parameter | Tariff X (Single-Part) | Tariff Y (Two-Part Hopkinson) | |-----------|------------------------|--------------------------------| | Rate | ₹6.00/unit flat | ₹300/kW of MD + ₹4.00/unit | Consumer data: $MD = 100$ kW, $E = 30{,}000$ kWh, $H = 720$ h. Calculate LF and determine which tariff is cheaper. **Solution:** $$\text{LF} \;=\; E / (MD \times H) \;=\; 30{,}000 / (100 \times 720) \;=\; 0.4167 \text{ (41.67%)}$$ $$B_X \;=\; 6.00 \times 30{,}000 \;=\; \mathbf{₹1{,}80{,}000}$$ $$B_Y \;=\; 300 \times 100 + 4.00 \times 30{,}000 \;=\; 30{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \;=\; \mathbf{₹1{,}50{,}000}$$ **Tariff Y (Hopkinson) is cheaper by ₹30,000** because the consumer's LF is moderate and the fixed charge of ₹30,000 buys the flexibility of a lower running rate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Rule:** > - **Single-part favours low-LF** consumers (intermittent, high peaks but low energy). > - **Two-part favours high-LF** consumers (steady industrial use). > - **Three-part + PF penalty forces every consumer** to be efficient on all three axes: load factor, demand, and power factor. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — How a Tariff is Computed End-to-End ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Consumer Premises
Connected Load L] --> B[Metering Endpoints] C[Energy Meter kWh] --> B D[MD Indicator kW or kVA] --> B E[PF Meter cos phi] --> B B --> F{Billing Engine
Tariff Selection} F --> G1[Single-Part Path
B = r x E] F --> G2[Two-Part Path
B = RkW x MD + RkWh x E] F --> G3[Three-Part Path
B = RL x L + RkWh x E + RkVA x MDkVA] G1 --> H[Slab Adjustment
Tiered kWh] G2 --> H G3 --> H E --> I[PF Penalty Module] G3 --> I H --> J[Total Bill Generation] I --> J J --> K[Consumer Invoice
Effective Rate per Unit] ``` ### 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing Between Single-Part, Two-Part, and Three-Part ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[New Consumer Joins Grid] --> Q1{Load Magnitude} Q1 -->|Below 1 kW
Domestic BPL| S[Single-Part Tariff] Q1 -->|1 kW to 100 kW
Commercial or Light Industrial| Q2{Is PF Monitored?} Q1 -->|Above 100 kW
HT Industrial| T[Three-Part Tariff] Q2 -->|No, MD only| Two[Two-Part Hopkinson Tariff] Q2 -->|Yes, kVA + PF| T S --> End[Bill Issued] Two --> End T --> End ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Flow of the Three-Part Tariff Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer [Input Layer] L1[Connected Load L in kW] E1[Energy E in kWh] M1[Maximum Demand MDkW] P1[Power Factor cos phi] end subgraph ComputationLayer [Billing Computation] C1[Fixed Charge
RL x L] C2[Energy Charge
RkWh x E] C3[Demand Charge
RkVA x MDkW / cos phi] C4[PF Penalty
1% x Demand Charge per 0.01 below 0.90] end subgraph OutputLayer [Output Layer] O1[Total Bill B3] O2[Effective Rate reff] O3[Monthly Invoice] end L1 --> C1 E1 --> C2 M1 --> C3 P1 --> C3 P1 --> C4 C3 --> C4 C1 --> O1 C2 --> O1 C3 --> O1 C4 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O1 --> O3 ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Cost Structure (Conceptual Bar Chart — ASCII) ``` Component | Single-Part | Two-Part | Three-Part -----------------|-------------|----------|------------ Fixed Recovery | ████ | ██ | █ Variable (kWh) | █ | ████ | ███ Demand (kVA) | ░ | ██ | ███ PF Penalty | ░ | ░ | ██ =================|=============|==========|============ Fairness | Low | Medium | High Complexity | Low | Medium | High ``` > [!TIP] > The relative block sizes qualitatively show how the burden of recovery is shared. Single-part throws **all** of it on the running rate (one big bar). Two-part splits fixed vs running. Three-part further splits fixed by **connected load** and adds a **kVA demand charge** plus a **PF penalty**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT637]` **Define "Load Factor" and "Demand Factor".** How are they related to the **Bill Factor**? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Load Factor (LF)** is the ratio of the average load on the system to the maximum demand occurring in that period. $LF = P_{avg} / MD = E / (MD \times H)$. - **Demand Factor (DF)** is the ratio of maximum demand to the connected load. $DF = MD / L$. - **Bill Factor (BF)** is the product of LF and DF, equivalently the ratio of average load to connected load: $BF = LF \times DF = P_{avg} / L$. *(1 mark for LF definition, 1 mark for DF definition, 1 mark for relation)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT637]` **State the disadvantages of a flat-rate (single-part) tariff.** Why do utilities prefer the two-part tariff? **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. A flat-rate tariff charges the consumer only on the basis of energy (kWh) consumed — it does not distinguish between a consumer with a low load factor and one with a high load factor. 2. Hence, it is **unfair to the utility** because a consumer with a peak demand much higher than the average load forces the utility to invest in capacity that is barely used. 3. Utilities prefer the **two-part (Hopkinson) tariff** because the fixed charge (₹/kW of MD) accurately recovers the standing/capacity cost proportional to the consumer's peak demand, while the running charge recovers the variable fuel cost proportional to energy used. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT637 — Module 4, Set A]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the **average (effective) rate per unit** in a two-part Hopkinson tariff. Show explicitly that the effective rate **decreases as the load factor increases**. (7 marks) **(b)** A consumer has a maximum demand of **200 kW** at a power factor of **0.85 lagging**, with an energy consumption of **1,20,000 kWh over 30 days**. The tariff is: - Fixed: **₹400 per kVA of maximum demand per month** - Running: **₹5.00 per kWh** Compute (i) the **total bill**, (ii) the **effective rate per kWh**, and (iii) the **load factor**. (7 marks) ##### **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Hopkinson bill: $B = R_{kW} \cdot MD + R_{kWh} \cdot E$ ......... (1 mark) Load factor: $LF = E / (MD \times H) \Rightarrow E = LF \cdot MD \cdot H$ ......... (1 mark) Substitute in $B$: $$B = MD \cdot (R_{kW} + R_{kWh} \cdot LF \cdot H)$$ ......... (1 mark) Average rate: $$r_{eff} = B / E = \frac{MD \cdot (R_{kW} + R_{kWh} \cdot LF \cdot H)}{LF \cdot MD \cdot H}$$ ......... (1 mark) Cancel $MD$: $$r_{eff} = \frac{R_{kW}}{LF \cdot H} + R_{kWh}$$ ......... (1 mark) Since $R_{kW}$, $H$, and $R_{kWh}$ are constants, the first term $R_{kW} / (LF \cdot H)$ is **inversely proportional** to LF. Therefore, **as LF increases, $r_{eff}$ decreases monotonically**. ......... (2 marks — 1 for the formula box, 1 for the conclusion) $$\boxed{\; r_{eff} \;=\; R_{kWh} \;+\; \frac{R_{kW}}{H \cdot LF} \;}$$ **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** (i) $MD_{kVA} = MD_{kW} / \cos\phi = 200 / 0.85 = 235.29$ kVA ......... (1 mark) Fixed charge = $400 \times 235.29 = ₹94,117.65$ ......... (1 mark) Energy charge = $5.00 \times 1,20,000 = ₹6,00,000$ ......... (1 mark) $$B_{total} = 94,117.65 + 6,00,000 = \mathbf{₹6,94,117.65}$$ ......... (1 mark) (ii) Effective rate = $6,94,117.65 / 1,20,000 = \mathbf{₹5.7843/kWh}$ ......... (1 mark) (iii) $H = 30 \times 24 = 720$ h, $P_{avg} = 1,20,000 / 720 = 166.67$ kW $$LF = 166.67 / 200 = \mathbf{0.8333 \; (83.33\%)}$$ ......... (2 marks — 1 each for the two sub-steps) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT637 — Module 4, Set B]` **(a)** Explain the **three-part tariff** in detail. List its **three components** and state the **physical quantity** each one is designed to recover. (7 marks) **(b)** A factory has a **connected load of 800 kW**, a **maximum demand of 500 kW at 0.75 power factor lagging**, and consumes **2,40,000 kWh in 30 days**. The tariff is: - ₹200 per kW of connected load - ₹4.50 per kWh (flat) - ₹500 per kVA of maximum demand Compute the **total monthly bill** and the **average cost per unit**. (7 marks) ##### **Model Solution:** **(a) Three-Part Tariff Theory (7 marks):** A three-part tariff (also called the **Wright Tariff** or **Maximum Demand Tariff** in its general form) breaks the bill into three explicit, individually-metered components. *(1 mark)* 1. **Fixed Charge — based on Connected Load (₹/kW of L).** Recovers the utility's **fixed capital cost** of installing service lines, transformers, switchgear, and metering infrastructure — costs that exist whether or not the consumer uses any energy. *(2 marks)* 2. **Variable Charge — based on Energy (₹/kWh).** Recovers the **running (fuel) cost** of generation, which is directly proportional to the number of units consumed. *(2 marks)* 3. **Demand Charge — based on Maximum Demand in kVA (₹/kVA of MD).** Recovers the **capacity cost** of generation, transmission, and distribution equipment that must be sized to meet the consumer's peak. Using **kVA** (rather than kW) implicitly penalises a low power factor, since the utility must install equipment rated for apparent power. *(2 marks)* $$B_3 = R_L \cdot L + R_{kWh} \cdot E + R_{kVA} \cdot MD_{kVA}$$ **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** $MD_{kVA} = 500 / 0.75 = 666.67$ kVA ......... (1 mark) Fixed charge = $200 \times 800 = ₹1,60,000$ ......... (1 mark) Energy charge = $4.50 \times 2,40,000 = ₹10,80,000$ ......... (1 mark) Demand charge = $500 \times 666.67 = ₹3,33,333.33$ ......... (1 mark) $$B_{total} = 1,60,000 + 10,80,000 + 3,33,333.33 = \mathbf{₹15,73,333.33}$$ ......... (1 mark) $$r_{eff} = 15,73,333.33 / 2,40,000 = \mathbf{₹6.556/kWh}$$ ......... (1 mark) LF = $E / (MD \times H) = 2,40,000 / (500 \times 720) = 0.6667$ (66.67%) ......... (1 mark — bonus insight) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Most Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert kW to kVA** when the demand charge is specified per kVA — this is the single most common error in three-part numericals. **1 to 2 marks lost.** > 2. **Mixing up Connected Load (L) with Maximum Demand (MD)** in the three-part formula — Component 1 is ₹/kW of L; Component 3 is ₹/kVA of MD. > 3. **Ignoring PF penalties** when the problem says "penalty for every 0.01 below 0.90" — many students simply compute the three parts and forget the fourth term. > 4. **Units confusion:** Energy charge is in ₹/kWh but **Demand charge in three-part** is in ₹/kVA (not ₹/kW). Misreading this in the question costs 2–3 marks. > 5. **Neglecting the slab structure** in domestic tariffs (e.g., first 100 units at ₹3, next 100 at ₹5, etc.) — break the energy into slabs and apply different rates. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Three foundational quantities** must always be calculated first in any numerical: **MD**, **E**, **$P_{avg}$ = E/H**, and **LF = $P_{avg}$/MD**. - [ ] **Bill Factor = LF × DF** — a single equation that links three of the most-tested variables. - [ ] **Single-part tariff** bill is just $B = r \times E$ — trivial but unfair to utilities. - [ ] **Two-part (Hopkinson) tariff** bill is $B = R_{kW} \cdot MD + R_{kWh} \cdot E$ — MD in **kW**. - [ ] **Three-part (Wright) tariff** bill is $B = R_L \cdot L + R_{kWh} \cdot E + R_{kVA} \cdot MD_{kVA}$ — MD in **kVA**, plus optional PF penalty. - [ ] **kVA = kW / cos$\phi$** — the kVA demand charge in three-part **automatically** punishes low PF consumers. - [ ] **Average rate** in two-part = $R_{kW} / (LF \cdot H) + R_{kWh}$ — **derivation must be memorised** for KTU. - [ ] As **LF ↑**, average rate **↓** (consumers rewarded for using power steadily). - [ ] As **PF ↑**, demand charge in three-part **↓** (capacitor banks are an investment, not a cost). - [ ] Hours in a **30-day month = 720 h**; in a **31-day month = 744 h**; in a **February = 672 h** (or 696 in a leap year). - [ ] **KSEB HT industrial tariff** is the textbook example of a three-part structure (KSEB Tariff Order 2023–2024). - [ ] The **effective rate per unit** is a derived quantity ($B/E$) and must always be quoted in ₹/kWh. - [ ] **Slab tariffs** (e.g., first 100 units, next 100 units, etc.) require piecewise summation — do not apply the highest slab to all units. - [ ] **Diversity Factor** is a *system* parameter, not a consumer parameter — do not use it in individual bills. - [ ] For 14-mark ESE, **show every intermediate step** (1 mark each) — valuation is strictly step-wise, not result-only. - [ ] Always **box the final answer** — partial marking is easier when the final result is clearly visible to the examiner. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** > *"Single = energy only. Two = energy + demand. Three = energy + demand + load (kVA + PF)."* ---
